topics.title: Block Chain, Public Ledgers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Block Chain, Public Ledgers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Blockchain

A **Blockchain** is a distributed, decentralized, append-only data structure that maintains a continuously growing list of records (called *blocks*) which are linked together using cryptographic hashes. Each block contains a cryptographic hash of the previous block, a timestamp, and transaction data, making the chain inherently resistant to modification of historical data.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, blockchain is classified as a type of **Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)** where the ledger is replicated, shared, and synchronized across multiple nodes in a peer-to-peer (P2P) network without a central administrator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECCT522 M1):** The module introduces the *foundational architecture* of blockchain — the very first step before diving into consensus, mining, or smart contracts. Treat this topic as the *conceptual bedrock* of the entire course.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of a Public Ledger

A **Public Ledger** is a permissionless, open, and transparent distributed ledger where:
- **Anyone** can read the data (read permission is public)
- **Anyone** can submit transactions (write permission is public, subject to consensus rules)
- **Anyone** can participate in the consensus process (validation permission is public)

The most famous example of a public ledger is the **Bitcoin blockchain**, conceptualized by the pseudonymous **Satoshi Nakamoto** in the 2008 whitepaper *"Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System"*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Community Notice Board That No One Owns**

Imagine a **transparent glass notebook** placed in the middle of a village square:
1. Every villager has an **identical copy** of this notebook.
2. Whenever someone wants to record a transaction ("Alice gives ₹100 to Bob"), they announce it.
3. All villagers **verify** the transaction independently (do they have enough money?).
4. Once verified, every villager **adds the same new page (block)** to their notebook.
5. Each new page contains a **unique fingerprint (hash)** of the previous page — so if a fraudster tries to rip out page 7 from one notebook, the fingerprints won't match, and the fraud is instantly detected.

**The glass notebook = Public Ledger**
**Each page = Block**
**The chain of pages = Blockchain**

| Property | Traditional Centralized Ledger (e.g., Bank) | Public Ledger (Blockchain) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Control** | Single authority (Bank/Government) | No single authority |
| **Transparency** | Restricted / Private | Fully transparent |
| **Trust** | Trusted third party required | Trustless (cryptographic proof) |
| **Access** | Permissioned | Permissionless |
| **Immutability** | Records can be edited by admin | Records are practically immutable |

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **Block time (Bitcoin): ~10 minutes** (mean time to produce a new block)
- **Block time (Ethereum): ~12–15 seconds** (post-Merge)
- **Bitcoin block size limit: 1 MB** (post-SegWit: ~4 MB effective)
- **Hash output (SHA-256): 256 bits** (i.e., **64 hexadecimal characters**)
- **Bitcoin Genesis block timestamp: 03 January 2009, 18:15:05 UTC**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Genesis Block** is the very first block (Block #0) in any blockchain. In Bitcoin, it contains the now-famous coinbase message: *"The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks."* This is a political commentary embedded immutably in the chain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core 'Why' Behind Blockchain

Traditional systems suffer from the **"Double-Spend Problem"** and require **trusted intermediaries** (banks, payment gateways). Blockchain solves this by combining four pillars:

1. **Cryptography** → Secures transactions (hashes, digital signatures)
2. **Distributed Systems** → No single point of failure
3. **Consensus Algorithms** → All nodes agree on the chain's state
4. **Economic Incentives** → Miners/validators are rewarded for honest behavior

### 2.2 Network Topologies: Centralized vs Distributed vs Decentralized

Understanding the topology is *critical* — KTU frequently tests this with diagrams.

| Topology | Description | Example | Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Centralized** | One central node controls all | Bank server, Google | Single point of failure |
| **Distributed** | Multiple nodes, but often one coordinator | Cloud data centers (AWS) | Coordinator failure = system halt |
| **Decentralized** | All nodes equal, no coordinator | Bitcoin, Ethereum, BitTorrent | Harder to attack, but slower |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Differentiate between distributed and decentralized networks."* Use the rule: a **distributed** system *may* have a coordinator; a **decentralized** system has **no coordinator at all** — every node is a peer.

### 2.3 Anatomy of a Block

Every block in a blockchain has two main parts:

**(A) Block Header** (metadata)
- **Previous Block Hash** — links to the parent block
- **Merkle Root Hash** — a single hash summarizing all transactions in the block
- **Timestamp** — Unix time of block creation
- **Difficulty Target** — current mining difficulty
- **Nonce** — a number miners vary to find a valid hash
- **Version** — protocol version

**(B) Block Body** (data)
- A list of validated transactions (often organized as a **Merkle Tree**)

### 2.4 How Blocks Are Chained — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** Transactions are broadcast to the P2P network.
- **Step 2:** Validators/Miners collect valid transactions into a candidate block.
- **Step 3:** The candidate block's data is hashed repeatedly (e.g., SHA-256) along with a changing nonce until the hash is below a target threshold (Proof-of-Work).
- **Step 4:** The new block includes the hash of the previous block's header → this is the *link* in the chain.
- **Step 5:** The new block is broadcast; if accepted by consensus, every full node appends it.

> **Why this is tamper-proof:** Changing *any* historical block changes its hash → breaks the link with the next block → invalidates all subsequent blocks → makes tampering computationally infeasible.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cryptographic Hash | $H(m) = h$ where $h$ is a fixed-length output | SHA-256: **256 bits**; deterministic, one-way |
| Block Hash | $B_i = H(\text{Header}_i \mid \text{Nonce}_i)$ | Concatenation then hashed |
| Chain Link | $B_i.\text{prev\_hash} = H(B_{i-1})$ | Ensures immutability |
| Merkle Root | $R = H(H(T_1 \mid T_2) \mid H(T_3 \mid T_4)\mid \ldots)$ | Binary tree of tx hashes |
| Digital Signature | $\sigma = \text{Sign}(SK, H(m))$ | Sender's private key $SK$ |
| Signature Verify | $\text{Valid} \iff \text{Verify}(PK, \sigma, m) = 1$ | Uses sender's public key $PK$ |
| Mining Difficulty $D$ | $H(\text{Block}) < \frac{2^{256}}{D}$ | Higher $D$ = harder to mine |
| Target Adjustment | $\text{NewTarget} = \text{OldTarget} \times \frac{\text{ActualTime}}{\text{DesiredTime}}$ | Recalibrated every 2016 blocks in Bitcoin |
| Block Reward | $R_{\text{block}} = R_{\text{subsidy}} + \sum \text{TxFees}$ | Halves every 210,000 blocks in Bitcoin |
| Blockchain Height | $\text{Height} = n$ | $n$ = number of blocks since genesis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** The symbol $\mid$ denotes **concatenation** (NOT bitwise OR), a KTU paper convention.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Finance:** Cross-border payments (Ripple, Stellar), DeFi
- **Supply Chain:** Walmart uses Hyperledger to trace food origin in seconds (took 7 days earlier)
- **Healthcare:** Patient records (MediLedger)
- **Government:** Land registries (Georgia, Sweden), e-voting pilots
- **Intellectual Property:** Royalty distribution for artists
- **Cybersecurity:** Decentralized Identity (DID), PKI replacement
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Building the Chain of Hashes (Linkage Logic)

Let us derive mathematically how a chain of $n$ blocks forms an *immutable* linked structure.

**Step 1 — Define a generic block**

$$
B_i = \{\,H_{\text{header}}(B_i),\, \text{Nonce}_i,\, \text{TxList}_i \,\}
$$

where $H_{\text{header}}$ is the header hash of block $i$.

**Step 2 — Define the hash function output**

$$
h_i = H(B_i)
$$

This is the *unique fingerprint* of block $i$, produced using a cryptographic hash function (e.g., SHA-256).

**Step 3 — Embed the previous hash into the new block header**

The header of block $i+1$ is constructed as:

$$
\text{Header}_{i+1} = \big\langle h_i,\; \text{MerkleRoot}_{i+1},\; \text{TS}_{i+1},\; \text{Difficulty}_{i+1},\; \text{Nonce}_{i+1} \big\rangle
$$

Notice that $h_i$ is **embedded inside** the next block's header. The first block (Genesis, $i=0$) has $h_{-1} = 0$ (a constant, since no parent exists).

**Step 4 — Form the chain link**

$$
h_{i+1} = H\big(\text{Header}_{i+1}\big) = H\big(\big\langle H(B_i),\; \text{MerkleRoot}_{i+1},\;\ldots \big\rangle\big)
$$

This single equation is the **mathematical essence of the blockchain**. Because $H$ is a one-way function with the **avalanche property** (any tiny change in input flips ~50% of output bits), any modification to $B_i$ changes $h_i$, which in turn changes $\text{Header}_{i+1}$, which changes $h_{i+1}$, and so on — cascading through the entire chain.

**Step 5 — Cost of tampering with block $k$**

To tamper with block $k$, an attacker must:
1. Recompute block $k$ → new $h_k$
2. Recompute block $k+1$ → new $h_{k+1}$
3. Continue up to the current tip → $n - k$ recomputations

For a chain of $n$ blocks and Proof-of-Work at difficulty $D$:

$$
\text{TamperCost}(k) = (n - k) \times T_{\text{hash}} \times D
$$

where $T_{\text{hash}}$ is the time to compute one hash. As $n$ grows, the cost grows linearly. With $D \approx 2^{67}$ on Bitcoin (as of 2024), this is computationally infeasible.

**Step 6 — Comparison: The probabilistic immutability**

The probability that an attacker (with fraction $q$ of total hash power) catches up with $z$ confirmations is approximated by:

$$
P_{\text{success}} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{z} \frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!}\left(1 - \left(\frac{q}{p}\right)^{z-k}\right)
$$

where $\lambda = z \cdot \frac{q}{p}$ and $p$ is the honest network hash power fraction. As $z \to \infty$, $P_{\text{success}} \to 0$. This is the formal proof that **more confirmations = exponentially stronger finality**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Merkle Root from Transactions

Given transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$, the Merkle root is:

$$
R = H\big(H(T_1 \mid T_2) \mid H(T_3 \mid T_4)\big)
$$

**Why this matters:** To prove $T_2$ is in the block, a node provides only $H(T_1 \mid T_2)$ and $H(T_3 \mid T_4)$ — a **Merkle proof** of length $O(\log_2 n)$ — without revealing other transactions. This is foundational for **Simplified Payment Verification (SPV)** in light wallets.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Properties of a Public Ledger

| Property | Description | Mechanism Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Decentralization** | No central authority | P2P gossip protocol |
| **Transparency** | All transactions publicly visible | Public read API, block explorers |
| **Immutability** | Past records cannot be altered | SHA-256 chaining + PoW |
| **Anonymity / Pseudonymity** | Users identified by addresses, not real names | Public-key cryptography (no KYC at protocol level) |
| **Consensus** | All honest nodes agree on the same chain | PoW, PoS, PBFT, etc. |
| **Auditability** | Anyone can verify the full history | Merkle proofs, full node replication |
| **Open Source** | Protocol code is publicly auditable | MIT/Apache licensed clients |

### 3.4 Three Generations of Blockchains (Analytical Evolution)

| Generation | Era | Key Feature | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1.0** | 2008–2013 | Digital currency / payments | Bitcoin |
| **2.0** | 2014–2019 | Smart contracts / DApps | Ethereum, NEO |
| **3.0** | 2020–present | Scalability, Interoperability, DApps (scaled) | Polkadot, Cosmos, Solana |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Network Topology Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Centralized]
    A1 --> A2[Server HUB]
    A2 --> A3[Client 1]
    A2 --> A4[Client 2]
    A2 --> A5[Client 3]
    A2 --> A6[Client 4]

    B1[Distributed]
    B1 --> B2[Coordinator]
    B2 --> B3[Node 1]
    B2 --> B4[Node 2]
    B2 --> B5[Node 3]
    B2 --> B6[Node 4]

    C1[Decentralized Blockchain]
    C1 --> C2[Node A]
    C1 --> C3[Node B]
    C1 --> C4[Node C]
    C1 --> C5[Node D]
    C1 --> C6[Node E]
    C2 --- C3
    C3 --- C4
    C4 --- C5
    C5 --- C6
    C6 --- C2
```

> **Reading Guide:** In *Centralized*, all traffic flows through one server. In *Distributed*, a coordinator exists but nodes hold copies. In *Decentralized (blockchain)*, every node is a peer and the topology forms a mesh.

### 4.2 Block Structure — Detailed Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BlockHeader[BLOCK HEADER]
        h1[Version Number]
        h2[Previous Block Hash 256 bits]
        h3[Merkle Root Hash 256 bits]
        h4[Timestamp 32 bits]
        h5[Difficulty Target 32 bits]
        h6[Nonce 32 bits]
    end
    subgraph BlockBody[BLOCK BODY]
        b1[Transaction Counter]
        b2[Transaction TX1]
        b3[Transaction TX2]
        b4[Transaction TXn]
    end
    h2 -.links to.-> h1
```

### 4.3 Blockchain as a Linked List of Blocks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Block 0 Genesis]
    B1[Block 1]
    B2[Block 2]
    B3[Block 3]
    B4[Block N Current]

    B0 -- Hash 0 --> B1
    B1 -- Hash 1 --> B2
    B2 -- Hash 2 --> B3
    B3 -- Hash 3 --> B4
```

> **Key Insight:** Each arrow represents a **cryptographic pointer** (a hash of the previous block's header). Tampering with any block breaks all arrows to its right.

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Public Ledger System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U1[User 1 Wallet] --> Net
    U2[User 2 Wallet] --> Net
    U3[User 3 Wallet] --> Net

    subgraph Net[Public P2P Network Layer]
        N1[Full Node 1]
        N2[Full Node 2]
        N3[Full Node 3]
        N1 <--> N2
        N2 <--> N3
        N3 <--> N1
    end

    Net --> Cons[Consensus Layer PoW PoS PBFT]
    Cons --> Val[Validation Rules ScriptSig UTXO]
    Val --> Chain[Append to Blockchain]
    Chain --> Dist[Replicate to All Nodes]
    Dist --> U1
    Dist --> U2
    Dist --> U3
```

> **Explanation:** A transaction is broadcast by the user → propagated across the P2P network → validated by consensus → appended to the chain → replicated to all nodes → visible to all users.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. Define a public ledger. List any four of its key characteristics.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **public ledger** is a distributed, open, and permissionless data structure that records transactions across a peer-to-peer network, where anyone can read, write (subject to consensus), and participate in validation without needing prior authorization from a central authority.

**Four key characteristics:**

1. **Decentralized** — No central authority controls the ledger; it is maintained collectively by nodes.
2. **Transparent** — All transactions are publicly visible to any participant.
3. **Immutable** — Once a transaction is confirmed and added, it cannot be altered retroactively without re-computing all subsequent block hashes.
4. **Consensus-driven** — All nodes must agree on the validity of new entries through protocols like Proof-of-Work (PoW) or Proof-of-Stake (PoS).

*Exam Tip: Students often forget to mention "permissionless" or "consensus" — these are the two most-tested keywords.*

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between centralized, distributed, and decentralized networks with one real-world example for each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Centralized | Distributed | Decentralized |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Control** | Single central authority | Multiple nodes, may have coordinator | All nodes equal; no coordinator |
| **Failure Point** | Single point of failure | Coordinator failure = partial halt | No single point of failure |
| **Example** | Bank server, Google search | Cloud data centers (AWS), CDNs | Bitcoin blockchain, BitTorrent |
| **Trust Model** | Trust the central party | Trust the system operator | Trustless (cryptographic) |

*Valuation Tip: A 3-mark question expects a 3-row table OR three short paragraphs. Do not exceed 1 page.*

---

### **Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ONE)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the architecture of blockchain technology with a neat diagram. Discuss the key features that make blockchain a disruptive technology.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Blockchain is a distributed, decentralized, append-only ledger that records transactions in *blocks* linked via cryptographic hashes and secured through consensus mechanisms.

**Architecture (Layered Model):**

1. **Application Layer** — End-user applications (wallets, DApps, DeFi front-ends).
2. **Execution Layer (Smart Contract Layer)** — Smart contracts and business logic (Ethereum's EVM).
3. **Consensus Layer** — Protocol to agree on the next valid block (PoW, PoS, PBFT).
4. **Network Layer (P2P Layer)** — Gossip protocol for propagating transactions and blocks.
5. **Data Layer** — The actual chain of blocks, Merkle trees, and cryptographic primitives.

```
[ Application Layer ]
        ↓
[ Smart Contract Layer ]
        ↓
[ Consensus Layer ]  ← Proof of Work / Stake
        ↓
[ P2P Network Layer ] ← Gossip Protocol
        ↓
[ Data Layer ]  ← Chain of Blocks + Merkle Trees
```

**Key Features (any 6, 1 mark each):**

- **Decentralization** — No single point of control.
- **Immutability** — Tampering requires recomputing the entire chain.
- **Transparency** — Public verifiability of all transactions.
- **Cryptographic Security** — Hashing (SHA-256) and digital signatures (ECDSA).
- **Consensus** — All honest nodes agree on the canonical chain.
- **Traceability** — Every transaction is auditable from genesis to tip.
- **Open Source** — Protocol code is publicly auditable.

**(b) Explain the structure of a block in detail. How are blocks cryptographically linked to form an immutable chain? Illustrate with a diagram.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Structure (with field sizes):**

- **Block Header (80 bytes in Bitcoin):**
  - Version (4 bytes)
  - Previous Block Hash (32 bytes / 256 bits)
  - Merkle Root (32 bytes)
  - Timestamp (4 bytes)
  - Difficulty Target (4 bytes)
  - Nonce (4 bytes)
- **Block Body:**
  - Transaction Counter
  - List of transactions (organized as Merkle tree)

**Linking Mechanism:**

The previous block's header hash $h_{i-1} = H(\text{Header}_{i-1})$ is embedded as the *previous block hash* field in block $i$'s header. The new block hash is:

$$
h_i = H(\text{Header}_i) = H(\langle h_{i-1},\; \text{MerkleRoot}_i,\; \text{TS}_i,\; \text{Difficulty}_i,\; \text{Nonce}_i \rangle)
$$

**Immutability proof:** Any modification to block $i-1$ cascades and changes $h_{i-1}$, which changes $\text{Header}_i$, which changes $h_i$, and so on. The attacker must redo the Proof-of-Work for *all* subsequent blocks — infeasible at scale.

```
[B0 Genesis] →hash0→ [B1] →hash1→ [B2] →hash2→ [B3] ... →hashN→ [Bn Current Tip]
   (header)            (header)         (header)        (header)
```

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative to Q.A)**

**(a) What is a public ledger? How does it differ from a private (permissioned) ledger? Provide two real-world examples of each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Public Ledger:**
A permissionless, open distributed ledger where anyone can read, write, and participate in consensus without authorization. Examples: **Bitcoin**, **Ethereum**.

**Private (Permissioned) Ledger:**
A restricted distributed ledger controlled by a single organization or consortium, where only authorized nodes can validate and view transactions. Examples: **Hyperledger Fabric** (Linux Foundation), **R3 Corda** (banking consortium).

| Feature | Public Ledger | Private Ledger |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Access | Permissionless | Permissioned |
| Read | Anyone | Authorized only |
| Write | Anyone (with consensus) | Authorized only |
| Consensus | PoW, PoS (slow, expensive) | PBFT, Raft (fast, cheap) |
| Throughput | Low (Bitcoin ~7 TPS) | High (Hyperledger ~3000+ TPS) |
| Transparency | Fully public | Confidential |
| Example | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Hyperledger, Corda |

**(b) Describe the history and evolution of blockchain technology. Explain the three main types of blockchain networks (public, private, consortium).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Historical Evolution:**

- **2008:** Satoshi Nakamoto publishes the Bitcoin whitepaper.
- **2009:** Genesis block of Bitcoin mined; first transaction between Satoshi and Hal Finney.
- **2013:** Vitalik Buterin proposes Ethereum, introducing general-purpose smart contracts.
- **2015:** Ethereum mainnet launches.
- **2017:** Initial Coin Offering (ICO) boom; Hyperledger Fabric 1.0 released.
- **2020:** Ethereum 2.0 Beacon Chain launches (move to PoS begins).
- **2022:** Ethereum Merge — successful transition from PoW to PoS.
- **2024 onwards:** Mass adoption of Layer-2 rollups (Optimism, Arbitrum, zkSync).

**Three Types of Blockchain Networks:**

1. **Public Blockchain (Permissionless):** Open to all; fully decentralized; uses PoW/PoS. Examples: Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana.
2. **Private Blockchain (Permissioned):** Controlled by a single organization; used for internal record-keeping. Examples: Hyperledger Besu (private deployment), Multichain.
3. **Consortium Blockchain (Federated):** Controlled by a group of organizations; semi-decentralized. Examples: Hyperledger Fabric, R3 Corda, Energy Web Chain.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "distributed" with "decentralized"** in diagrams — *lose 1–2 marks*.
> 2. **Forgetting the Merkle Root** when listing block fields — *lose 1 mark*.
> 3. **Not writing the equation** $h_i = H(\text{Header}_i)$ for the chaining — *lose 2 marks* in the 7-mark sub-part.
> 4. **Giving the year 2008 but missing Nakamoto's name** — *lose 0.5 mark*.
> 5. **Writing "block chain" as two words** in a definition — *frowned upon*, always use "blockchain" (one word) per IEEE/NIST convention.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU):**

- [x] **Definition:** Blockchain = Distributed + Decentralized + Append-only + Cryptographically-linked ledger of blocks.
- [x] **Genesis Block:** First block (Block #0); Bitcoin genesis timestamp = **03 Jan 2009**.
- [x] **Block Header fields (6):** Version, Previous Hash, Merkle Root, Timestamp, Difficulty, Nonce.
- [x] **Block Header size (Bitcoin):** 80 bytes.
- [x] **Hash function:** SHA-256 produces **256-bit (64 hex char)** output.
- [x] **Chaining equation:** $h_i = H(\text{Header}_i \mid \text{Nonce}_i)$, with $h_i$ stored in $\text{Header}_{i+1}$.
- [x] **Public Ledger:** Permissionless, open read/write, transparent, immutable. Examples: Bitcoin, Ethereum.
- [x] **Private Ledger:** Permissioned, restricted access. Examples: Hyperledger, Corda.
- [x] **Consortium:** Semi-decentralized, group-controlled.
- [x] **Centralized vs Distributed vs Decentralized:** Authority, failure point, and example for each.
- [x] **Merkle Root:** Single hash summarizing all transactions in a block; enables SPV.
- [x] **Generations:** 1.0 (Bitcoin) → 2.0 (Ethereum/Smart Contracts) → 3.0 (Interop & Scale).
- [x] **Three pillars of blockchain:** Cryptography, Distributed Systems, Game Theory/Incentives.
- [x] **Immutability cost:** Tampering cost = $(n - k) \times T_{\text{hash}} \times D$ — grows linearly with chain length.
- [x] **Finality:** More confirmations → exponentially smaller attack success probability.
- [x] **Public Ledger ≠ Anonymous:** It is *pseudonymous* (addresses, not names) but fully *transparent*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line exam-ready summary:** *"Blockchain is a decentralized, cryptographically-chained, append-only public ledger maintained by consensus across a peer-to-peer network, making it trustless, transparent, and practically immutable."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blockchain as Public Ledgers, Block in a Blockchain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Blockchain as Public Ledgers, Block in a Blockchain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Blockchain as a Public Ledger — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Public Ledger (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *public ledger* is an append-only, tamper-evident, globally replicated data structure that records transactions in a chronological and cryptographically verifiable manner. When implemented as a **blockchain**, it is distributed across a peer-to-peer (P2P) network where every participating node maintains an identical copy, ensuring *transparency*, *immutability*, and *decentralization* without requiring a trusted central authority.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, blockchain is treated as a specific **Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)** that bundles transactions into cryptographically linked structures called **blocks**, each referencing its predecessor via a cryptographic hash.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Village Ledger" Intuition

Imagine a village where every transaction (someone selling rice, lending money, buying land) is recorded in a notebook.

- **Old World:** One trusted village elder keeps the notebook. If he is dishonest or the book burns, the records are lost.
- **Blockchain World:** Every villager has a *photocopy* of the notebook. When a new transaction occurs, it is announced, and every villager updates their copy **simultaneously**. To add a new page (a *block*), villagers must solve a puzzle. Once added, the page is **sealed with a wax stamp** (cryptographic hash) and **glued to the previous page** (chain linkage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The defining property of a public ledger is that **no single entity controls it**, and **any participant can verify** the entire history independently. This is the philosophical foundation of cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin and Ethereum.

## 1.3 The Block — KTU Standard Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Block (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *block* is a fundamental data container in a blockchain that bundles a set of validated transactions along with a **block header** containing metadata — including the hash of the previous block, a timestamp, a nonce, and the Merkle root of the transactions. Each block, once validated and appended, becomes a permanent, cryptographically sealed unit of the ledger.

| Term | Quick Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Ledger** | A record of all transactions ever executed |
| **Public** | Open to read/write for anyone on the network |
| **Decentralized** | No single point of control |
| **Immutable** | Past records cannot be altered without enormous cost |
| **Append-Only** | New records can be added, old ones never deleted |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Block — The 6 Critical Components

Every block in a blockchain is composed of two logical parts: the **Block Header** and the **Transaction Body**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners frequently ask students to *list the contents of a block header*. Memorize the six components below — they appear in nearly every KTU Module 1 question paper.

### Block Header Components (Memorize This List)

1. **Previous Block Hash (Prev_Hash):** The SHA-256 hash of the previous block's header. This is the *cryptographic glue* that links blocks into a chain.
2. **Merkle Root Hash:** A single hash that cryptographically summarizes *all transactions* within the current block, organized as a Merkle Tree.
3. **Timestamp:** The Unix epoch time (seconds since January 1, 1970, UTC) when the block was mined/created.
4. **Nonce ("Number used once"):** An arbitrary 32-bit integer that miners vary to find a valid hash below the network's *difficulty target* (Proof-of-Work).
5. **Difficulty Target:** A 256-bit number that the block hash must be less than. Determines mining hardness.
6. **Version Number:** Indicates the block validation rules followed (e.g., Bitcoin v4, BIP 91, etc.).

### Transaction Body

Contains a list of validated transactions. The first transaction in a Bitcoin-style block is the **coinbase transaction**, which rewards the miner with newly minted cryptocurrency plus collected transaction fees.

## 2.2 How Blocks Form a Chain — The Cryptographic Linkage

The chaining mechanism is the heart of immutability. Each block stores the hash of the previous block's header. Any attempt to alter a historical block would change *its* hash, which would invalidate *every subsequent block* — making tampering computationally infeasible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Chain Equation:** Let $B_i$ be the $i^{th}$ block. The link is:
>
> $$H_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}(B_i))$$
>
> $$\text{Prev\_Hash}_{i+1} = H_i$$
>
> Where $\text{SHA-256}$ is the standard cryptographic hash function producing a 256-bit (32-byte) digest.

## 2.3 Public vs. Private vs. Consortium Ledgers

| Ledger Type | Read Access | Write Access | Consensus | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Public** (Permissionless) | Open to all | Open to all (via mining/validation) | PoW, PoS, PoH | Bitcoin, Ethereum |
| **Private** (Permissioned) | Restricted to org | Restricted to selected nodes | PBFT, Raft | Hyperledger Fabric |
| **Consortium** | Restricted to consortium | Pre-approved validators | Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance | R3 Corda, Quorum |
| **Hybrid** | Configurable | Configurable | Mixed | Dragonchain |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU questions often test the *differences* between public and private ledgers. Remember: *Public* = open and trustless; *Private* = permissioned and efficient.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula or Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Hash of block $i$ | $H_i = \text{SHA256}(\text{Header}_i)$ | 256 bits (64 hex chars) |
| Chain linkage | $\text{PrevHash}_{i+1} = H_i$ | Establishes immutability |
| Merkle root | $R = H(H(T_1 \Vert T_2) \Vert H(T_3 \Vert T_4))$ | Summarizes all $T$ transactions |
| Block size (Bitcoin) | $\leq 1{,}000{,}000$ bytes | Hard cap (SegWit: 4,000,000 weight units) |
| Block time (Bitcoin) | $\approx \mathbf{600}$ seconds | Target (10 minutes) |
| Block reward (Bitcoin 2024) | $\mathbf{3.125}$ BTC | Post-April 2024 halving |
| Difficulty adjustment | Every $\mathbf{2016}$ blocks | $\approx$ every 2 weeks |
| Total block header size | $\mathbf{80}$ bytes | Fixed in Bitcoin protocol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Blockchains as public ledgers power cross-border payments (Ripple, Stellar), supply chain provenance (IBM Food Trust), decentralized finance (DeFi), and digital identity systems (Sovrin). The block structure's elegance lies in balancing *data integrity* with *efficient verification* via Merkle trees.

## 2.5 Why This Matters — The Engineering Perspective

From a systems engineering perspective, the block-based public ledger solves three classical distributed systems problems simultaneously:

1. **The Byzantine Generals' Problem** — achieving consensus among untrusted nodes.
2. **The Double-Spend Problem** — preventing the same digital coin from being spent twice.
3. **The Single Point of Failure** — eliminating centralized trust authorities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Chain Linkage Integrity Property

**Goal:** Prove that altering a single historical block invalidates the entire subsequent chain.

### Step 1: Formal Setup

Let the blockchain be a sequence of $n$ blocks: $B_1, B_2, B_3, \dots, B_n$. Each block $B_i$ has:
- A header $H_i$ containing the Merkle root $M_i$, timestamp $t_i$, nonce $n_i$, and PrevHash field $P_i$.
- A set of transactions $\mathcal{T}_i = \{T_{i,1}, T_{i,2}, \dots, T_{i,k}\}$.

### Step 2: Define the Hash Function

We use the SHA-256 cryptographic hash function:

$$H_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_i)$$

where $\text{Header}_i$ is the 80-byte serialized header.

### Step 3: Define the Chain Linkage Rule

By protocol design, the PrevHash field of block $B_{i+1}$ must equal the hash of block $B_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{i+1} &= H_i \\
P_{i+1} &= \text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_i)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Hypothesize a Tampering Attempt

Suppose an attacker alters transaction $T_{j,1}$ in block $B_j$ (where $1 \le j < n$). The Merkle root of $B_j$ changes:

$$M_j^{\text{new}} \ne M_j$$

### Step 5: Propagate the Cascade

Since $M_j$ is part of $\text{Header}_j$, the hash of $B_j$ changes:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_j^{\text{new}} &= \text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_j^{\text{new}}) \\
H_j^{\text{new}} &\ne H_j
\end{aligned}$$

But $P_{j+1}$ is **stored** in $B_{j+1}$ as the literal value $H_j$. Now:

$$P_{j+1} = H_j \ne H_j^{\text{new}}$$

This means the linkage breaks: $B_{j+1}$ references a hash that no longer matches the *actual* hash of $B_j$.

### Step 6: Cascade Effect

The attacker must now recompute $H_{j+1}, H_{j+2}, \dots, H_n$ — i.e., **re-mine every subsequent block** — to restore chain validity. This requires:

$$\text{Computational cost} = \sum_{k=j}^{n} D \cdot 2^{32}$$

where $D$ is the current network difficulty. For Bitcoin in 2024, this is approximately $10^{20}$ hashes per block — computationally infeasible.

### Step 7: Conclusion

We have shown that **tampering with any single block invalidates all subsequent blocks**. Therefore, the chain is *tamper-evident* and *practically immutable*.

$$\boxed{\text{Block tamper in } B_j \implies \text{Full re-mining of } B_j, B_{j+1}, \dots, B_n}$$

## 3.2 Merkle Root Derivation (Binary Tree Construction)

Given $k = 2^m$ transactions $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_{2^m}$, the Merkle root is computed as follows:

### Base Level (Leaves):

$$L_i^{(0)} = \text{SHA-256}(T_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, 2^m$$

### Recursive Combination:

At each level $\ell$ for $\ell = 1, 2, \dots, m$:

$$L_i^{(\ell)} = \text{SHA-256}\left(L_{2i-1}^{(\ell-1)} \Vert L_{2i}^{(\ell-1)}\right)$$

where $\Vert$ denotes byte concatenation.

### Root:

$$\text{Merkle Root} = L_1^{(m)}$$

### Example: 4 Transactions

$$\begin{aligned}
L_1^{(0)} &= \text{SHA256}(T_1) \\
L_2^{(0)} &= \text{SHA256}(T_2) \\
L_3^{(0)} &= \text{SHA256}(T_3) \\
L_4^{(0)} &= \text{SHA256}(T_4) \\
L_1^{(1)} &= \text{SHA256}(L_1^{(0)} \Vert L_2^{(0)}) \\
L_2^{(1)} &= \text{SHA256}(L_3^{(0)} \Vert L_4^{(0)}) \\
\text{Merkle Root} &= L_1^{(2)} = \text{SHA256}(L_1^{(1)} \Vert L_2^{(1)})
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Block Properties Across Networks

| Property | Bitcoin (BTC) | Ethereum (ETH) | Hyperledger Fabric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block header size | **80 bytes** | ~500 bytes (variable) | Variable |
| Hash function | **SHA-256 (double)** | Keccak-256 (Ethash → PoS) | SHA-256 |
| Average block time | **~10 min** | ~12 sec (post-Merge) | Configurable (0.5–5 sec) |
| Consensus | Proof of Work | Proof of Stake (PoS) | PBFT / Raft |
| Merkle trees used | 1 (transactions) | 3 (state, txs, receipts) | Yes (configurable) |
| Permissionless | Yes | Yes | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a public ledger. List any four characteristics. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **public ledger** is a distributed, append-only data structure that records transactions in a cryptographically verifiable, chronological order and is replicated across all participants of a peer-to-peer network without requiring a central trusted authority.

**Four Characteristics:**

1. **Decentralized** — No single entity controls the ledger; all nodes maintain a copy.
2. **Immutable** — Once a transaction is recorded, it cannot be altered without invalidating the chain.
3. **Transparent** — Any participant can read the entire history of transactions.
4. **Append-Only** — New records can only be added; old records are never deleted or modified.

### Question 2: What is a block in a blockchain? List the components of a block header. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **block** is a data container in a blockchain that bundles a set of validated transactions along with a block header containing metadata for cryptographic linking and validation.

**Components of a Block Header (6 total):**

1. Version Number (4 bytes)
2. Previous Block Hash (32 bytes)
3. Merkle Root Hash (32 bytes)
4. Timestamp (4 bytes)
5. Difficulty Target / Bits (4 bytes)
6. Nonce (4 bytes)

Total header size = **80 bytes** in Bitcoin.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose marks on the following:
> - **Confusing the Merkle root with the block hash** — they are *different*. Merkle root summarizes *transactions only*; block hash summarizes the *entire header*.
> - **Forgetting to mention coinbase transaction** — the first transaction in a block is special; it mints new coins.
> - **Writing "SHA-256" without "double SHA-256"** for Bitcoin — Bitcoin actually applies SHA-256 twice.
> - **Not explaining the cascade effect** when describing immutability — examiners want to see that you understand *why* tampering fails.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a block in a blockchain. List all the fields of a block header. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the block anatomy.

**Structure of a Block:**

A block in a blockchain consists of two main parts:

1. **Block Header (80 bytes in Bitcoin)** — Contains metadata:
   - **Version Number** (4 bytes): Indicates the block validation rules.
   - **Previous Block Hash** (32 bytes): SHA-256 hash of the previous block's header.
   - **Merkle Root** (32 bytes): Root hash of the Merkle tree of all transactions in the block.
   - **Timestamp** (4 bytes): Unix time of block creation.
   - **Difficulty Target / Bits** (4 bytes): Encoded form of the mining difficulty.
   - **Nonce** (4 bytes): The 32-bit integer that miners vary to find a valid hash.

2. **Transaction Counter** — Number of transactions in the block.
3. **Transactions** — The list of all validated transactions. The first transaction is the **coinbase transaction**, which awards the miner the block reward + transaction fees.

**Purpose of the Header:** The header is the part that is hashed to produce the block's identity. The transactions are not directly hashed; instead, the Merkle root in the header summarizes them.

**(b) Explain how blocks are linked to form a blockchain. Why is it computationally infeasible to tamper with a historical block? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Linking Mechanism:**

Each block $B_i$ contains in its header the **Previous Block Hash** field, which is set equal to the SHA-256 hash of the previous block's header. Formally:

$$\text{PrevHash}_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_{i-1})$$

This single 32-byte field creates a **cryptographic chain**: every block points to its predecessor, all the way back to the **Genesis Block** ($B_0$), which has no predecessor.

**Why Tampering is Infeasible:**

Suppose an attacker wishes to alter transaction $T_{j,k}$ in block $B_j$:

1. The change alters the Merkle root of $B_j$: $M_j \to M_j'$.
2. This changes the hash of $B_j$: $H_j \to H_j'$.
3. But $B_{j+1}$ stores $P_{j+1} = H_j$ (the *old* hash). Now $P_{j+1} \ne H_j'$.
4. The attacker must therefore re-mine $B_{j+1}$ to update $P_{j+1}$.
5. This re-mining cascade propagates forward: $B_{j+1}, B_{j+2}, \dots, B_n$ all need re-mining.

The total computational work required is:

$$W = \sum_{k=j}^{n} D \cdot 2^{32}$$

where $D$ is the current network difficulty. In 2024, Bitcoin's network computes $\sim 5 \times 10^{19}$ hashes per second collectively, meaning re-mining even a few blocks would require resources vastly exceeding the attacker's capacity. Furthermore, the honest network will have already extended the chain further, making the attacker's tampered chain the *shorter* one — automatically rejected by the **longest-chain rule**.

Hence, tampering is **computationally infeasible** and **economically irrational**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Define a public ledger. Compare public ledgers with private ledgers in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Public Ledger Definition:**

A *public ledger* is a distributed, permissionless, append-only data structure replicated across an open peer-to-peer network, where any participant can read, submit, and validate transactions. Consensus is achieved through mechanisms like Proof of Work (PoW) or Proof of Stake (PoS). Examples: Bitcoin, Ethereum.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Public Ledger | Private Ledger |
|---|---|---|
| **Access** | Permissionless; anyone can join | Permissioned; pre-approved participants |
| **Read Permission** | Open to all | Restricted to authorized members |
| **Write Permission** | Anyone (via mining/validation) | Restricted to selected nodes |
| **Consensus** | PoW, PoS, PoH (slow, energy-intensive) | PBFT, Raft (fast, efficient) |
| **Throughput** | Low (~7 TPS for Bitcoin) | High (1000+ TPS) |
| **Immutability** | Strong (cryptoeconomic) | Weaker (admin can override) |
| **Centralization** | Fully decentralized | Partially centralized |
| **Trust Model** | Trustless | Trust in operator |
| **Examples** | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Hyperledger Fabric, Corda |
| **Use Cases** | Cryptocurrency, DeFi | Enterprise, supply chain, banking |

**Conclusion:** Public ledgers prioritize censorship resistance and decentralization; private ledgers prioritize throughput and governance control.

**(b) Explain the Merkle tree structure used in a blockchain block. How does it enable efficient transaction verification? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Merkle Tree Definition:**

A *Merkle tree* (binary hash tree) is a tree data structure where:
- **Leaves** are the hashes of individual transactions: $L_i = \text{SHA-256}(T_i)$.
- **Internal nodes** are the hash of the concatenation of their two children: $P = \text{SHA-256}(L_{\text{left}} \Vert L_{\text{right}})$.
- **Root** (Merkle root) is the single hash at the top, stored in the block header.

**Construction (for 4 transactions):**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= \text{SHA256}(T_1), \quad L_2 = \text{SHA256}(T_2) \\
L_3 &= \text{SHA256}(T_3), \quad L_4 = \text{SHA256}(T_4) \\
\text{Node}_{12} &= \text{SHA256}(L_1 \Vert L_2) \\
\text{Node}_{34} &= \text{SHA256}(L_3 \Vert L_4) \\
\text{Merkle Root} &= \text{SHA256}(\text{Node}_{12} \Vert \text{Node}_{34})
\end{aligned}$$

**Efficient Verification via Merkle Proof (SPV):**

A **Simplified Payment Verification (SPV)** client (light node) can verify that a transaction $T_i$ is included in a block without downloading all transactions. It only needs:

1. The Merkle root from the block header.
2. A **Merkle proof** — the sibling hashes along the path from $T_i$ to the root (logarithmic in number of transactions).

For $k$ transactions, the proof size is only $O(\log_2 k)$ hashes. This enables:

- **Scalability:** Light nodes (mobile wallets) can verify without storing the full blockchain (~500 GB for Bitcoin in 2024).
- **Integrity:** Any change to a transaction alters the Merkle root, invalidating the block.
- **Efficiency:** Verification time is logarithmic, not linear.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!**

- **Public Ledger:** Distributed, append-only, immutable, transparent, decentralized. *No central authority.*
- **Block = Header (80 bytes) + Transactions.**
- **Block Header Fields (6):** Version, PrevHash, MerkleRoot, Timestamp, Bits/Difficulty, Nonce.
- **Chain Linkage Equation:** $\text{PrevHash}_{i+1} = H_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_i)$.
- **Tamper Cascade:** One altered block $\implies$ re-mine all subsequent blocks $\implies$ computationally infeasible.
- **Merkle Root:** Root of binary hash tree of all transactions; enables SPV (light client) verification.
- **Genesis Block:** $B_0$, the first block, has no predecessor; hardcoded into the protocol.
- **Coinbase Transaction:** First transaction in a block; mints new coins for the miner.
- **Bitcoin Block Reward (2024):** 3.125 BTC (post-April 2024 halving).
- **Bitcoin Block Time:** ~10 minutes; difficulty adjusts every 2016 blocks (~2 weeks).
- **Hash Function:** SHA-256 (applied twice in Bitcoin = "double SHA-256"), produces 256-bit output.
- **Public vs Private:** Public = open, trustless, slow. Private = permissioned, efficient, governance-controlled.
- **Longest Chain Rule:** Honest nodes always extend the chain with the most accumulated proof-of-work.
- **Block Size Limit (Bitcoin):** ~1 MB (legacy); 4M weight units post-SegWit.
- **Consensus Mechanisms:** Public chains use PoW/PoS; private chains use PBFT/Raft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Advice:** When drawing the block structure in your exam, *always* label the byte sizes. When explaining immutability, *always* show the cascade effect numerically. These two habits alone can push your score from a 7 to a full 14 in Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Transactions

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
A **Transaction** in a blockchain is a cryptographically signed, atomic data record that authorizes the transfer of digital value (cryptocurrency, tokens, or state) from one or more inputs to one or more outputs, broadcast to the peer-to-peer network for validation and inclusion in a block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** A transaction is the fundamental unit of state change in any blockchain. It is NOT a "block" — a block is merely a batch of validated transactions.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Digital Cheque"
Think of a blockchain transaction like a **tamper-proof digital cheque** drawn on a public ledger:
- The **drawer** signs it with a private key (impossible to forge).
- The **amount**, **payee address**, and **drawer's signature** are all visible.
- The cheque is **broadcast** to a clearinghouse (the network) where thousands of inspectors (nodes) verify the signature and check the drawer actually has the funds.
- Once verified, it is filed (mined into a block) and **cannot be reversed**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** A Bitcoin transaction is approximately **250 bytes** on average, while an Ethereum transaction is typically **100–500 bytes** (depending on calldata).

---

## 1.2 The Chain

### Formal Definition
The **Chain** (Blockchain) is an **append-only, cryptographically linked, sequential sequence of blocks**, where each block header contains a cryptographic hash of the immediately preceding block's header, forming an unbroken chain of data dependencies from the genesis block to the current head.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Stone Tablets with Fingerprints"
Imagine an ancient monastery where monks maintain a sacred ledger:
- Each **page (block)** is glued to the previous page with a unique **fingerprint (hash)** of the previous page stamped on top.
- If a forger tries to alter an old page, the fingerprint **mismatches**, and every subsequent page is also invalidated.
- The pages can **only be added** at the end — never edited, never removed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The chain is **append-only** (immutable history) and **cryptographically ordered** (each block depends on its parent).

---

## 1.3 The Longest Chain

### Formal Definition
The **Longest Chain Rule** (also called the **Nakamoto Consensus Rule**) is a decentralized agreement protocol where honest nodes always choose to extend the valid chain branch that has accumulated the **greatest amount of computational work (Proof-of-Work)**, ensuring all honest nodes converge on a single, canonical history.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Democratic History Book"
Imagine two historians writing competing accounts of events simultaneously:
- Both write valid, but **conflicting**, chapters.
- Other historians (the network) only **continue copying the version that has the most chapters written** (most work invested).
- The shorter version is **abandoned as an "orphan"**.
- Eventually, everyone agrees on the **longest, most-validated history**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** In Bitcoin, a transaction is considered practically irreversible after **6 confirmations** (~1 hour), as the probability of a competing longer chain catching up becomes negligible (< 0.1%).

---

## 1.4 Permissioned Model of Blockchain

### Formal Definition
The **Permissioned Blockchain Model** (also called **Private** or **Consortium Blockchain**) is a distributed ledger architecture where participation — including reading, submitting transactions, and validating blocks — is **restricted to pre-authenticated, known identities** governed by an access control layer.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Corporate Intranet vs Public Internet"
- The **Permissionless Model** (Bitcoin, Ethereum) is like the **public internet** — anyone can join, read, and write anonymously.
- The **Permissioned Model** (Hyperledger Fabric, Corda, Quorum) is like a **company intranet** — only employees with valid credentials can log in, and all actions are auditable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** Permissioned ≠ Public/Private distinction in *read* access. A permissioned blockchain can still be **public-readable** (e.g., a consortium bank chain) but is **restricted in write/validate** access.

---

## Summary Table — The Four Pillars

| Concept | Type | Mutable? | Access | Key Property |
|---------|------|----------|--------|--------------|
| Transaction | Data record | Once confirmed: No | Broadcast to all | Atomic state change |
| The Chain | Data structure | Append-only | Read: all | Cryptographic linkage |
| Longest Chain | Consensus rule | Selects canonical | All nodes compute | Most-work-wins |
| Permissioned Model | Architecture | Append-only | Restricted write | Identity-based |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Bitcoin Transaction

A standard transaction contains the following fields:

| Field | Purpose | Size (Bytes) |
|-------|---------|--------------|
| `version` | Protocol version | 4 |
| `input_count` | Number of inputs (varint) | 1–9 |
| `txin` (per input) | Previous tx hash + index + scriptSig | ~148 |
| `output_count` | Number of outputs (varint) | 1–9 |
| `txout` (per output) | Value (8B) + scriptPubKey | ~34 |
| `locktime` | Earliest block/time when tx is valid | 4 |

### Transaction Lifecycle (5 Stages)

1. **Construction** — Wallet builds the transaction with inputs (UTXOs) and outputs.
2. **Signing** — Sender signs the transaction hash using their private key (ECDSA).
3. **Broadcasting** — Transaction is propagated via gossip protocol to all nodes.
4. **Validation & Mempool** — Nodes verify signatures, UTXO existence, and double-spend prevention. Valid tx enters the **mempool** (waiting area).
5. **Mining/Confirmation** — Miners select transactions, form a candidate block, solve Proof-of-Work, and broadcast the new block. Each subsequent block adds **1 confirmation**.

---

## 2.2 Block Structure and Chain Linking

A **Block** has two components: **Header** (80 bytes) and **Body** (variable).

### Block Header Fields

| Field | Size | Function |
|-------|------|----------|
| `version` | 4 B | Block version number |
| `previous_block_hash` | 32 B | SHA-256(SHA-256) of parent block header |
| `merkle_root` | 32 B | Root of Merkle tree of all transactions |
| `timestamp` | 4 B | Unix time of mining |
| `n_bits` (difficulty target) | 4 B | Encoded PoW difficulty |
| `nonce` | 4 B | Counter for PoW solution |

### Chain Linking Mathematics

Each block $B_n$ is linked to its parent $B_{n-1}$ via:

$$H_n = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}(B_n)))$$

The parent linkage is:

$$\text{previous\_block\_hash} = H_{n-1} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}(B_{n-1})))$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why double SHA-256?** This is called **HASH256** in Bitcoin. It defends against **length-extension attacks** on the SHA-2 family and was an early security belt-and-braces design choice by Satoshi.

---

## 2.3 The Longest Chain Rule — Operational Logic

When two miners find a valid block at nearly the same time, a **fork** occurs. The network resolves it as follows:

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Broadcast** — Both valid blocks propagate through the network.
2. **Node Decision** — Each node accepts the **first valid block** it hears about into its local chain.
3. **Parallel Mining** — Some miners extend Block A, others extend Block B.
4. **Work Accumulation** — One branch receives more hash power and grows faster.
5. **Convergence** — The branch with the **greatest cumulative difficulty** becomes the canonical chain.
6. **Orphan Rejection** — Blocks on the losing branch are called **stale/orphan blocks**; their transactions return to the mempool.

### Cumulative Work Formula

For two competing chains $C_A$ and $C_B$, the canonical chain $C^*$ is:

$$C^* = \arg\max_{C \in \{C_A, C_B\}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} D(B_i)$$

where $D(B_i)$ is the difficulty of block $B_i$ and $n$ is the chain length.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** "Longest" in modern blockchains means **heaviest by cumulative work**, not literally the most blocks. Two blocks can have identical length but different difficulty (e.g., due to difficulty retargeting every 2016 blocks in Bitcoin).

---

## 2.4 Permissioned Model — Characteristics & Consensus

### Core Properties

| Property | Permissionless (Public) | Permissioned (Private/Consortium) |
|----------|-------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Identity | Pseudonymous / Anonymous | Known & verified (KYC) |
| Validator Set | Open, anyone with hash power | Closed, pre-approved |
| Consensus | PoW / PoS | PBFT, Raft, Tendermint, HotStuff |
| Throughput | ~7 TPS (Bitcoin), ~30 TPS (Ethereum) | 1,000–100,000+ TPS |
| Finality | Probabilistic (6+ confirmations) | Deterministic (instant) |
| Energy Use | Very High | Low |
| Examples | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Hyperledger Fabric, Corda, Quorum |

### Permissioned Consensus Algorithms (KTU High-Yield)

- **PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance):** Tolerates $f$ faulty nodes out of $3f+1$ total. Used in Hyperledger.
- **Raft:** Crash-fault tolerant, leader-based. Used in Quorum.
- **Tendermint:** Byzantine fault tolerant with rotating validators. Used in Hyperledger Besu.
- **HotStuff:** Linear view-change, BFT. Used in Facebook's Libra/Diem.

### Engineering Utility

Permissioned blockchains are used in:
- **Supply Chain Management** (IBM Food Trust, Maersk TradeLens)
- **Inter-bank Settlement** (R3 Corda)
- **Healthcare Records** (MedRec, MIT)
- **Government Land Registries** (Georgia, Sweden)
- **Digital Identity** (Sovrin, Microsoft ION)

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | Block hash (Bitcoin) | $H_n = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{Header}(B_n)))$ | Double SHA-256 |
| 2 | Chain linkage | $\text{prev\_hash}_n = H_{n-1}$ | Parent dependency |
| 3 | Merkle root | $M = H(H(T_1 \vert\vert T_2) \vert\vert H(T_3 \vert\vert T_4))$ | Binary tree of tx hashes |
| 4 | Cumulative work (longest chain) | $W(C) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D(B_i)$ | Pick max $W$ |
| 5 | PoW inequality | $\text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{Header})) < T$ | $T$ = target |
| 6 | Difficulty | $D = \frac{D_1 \cdot T_1}{T}$ | $D_1, T_1$ = reference values |
| 7 | PBFT safety | $N \geq 3f + 1$ | Tolerate $f$ Byzantine |
| 8 | Block time (Bitcoin) | $\approx 10$ minutes | Difficulty retarget every 2016 blocks |
| 9 | Confirmation safety | Prob. of reversal $\approx (q/z)^n$ | $q$ = attacker hash rate, $z$ = honest, $n$ = confirmations |
| 10 | Merkle proof size | $O(\log_2 N)$ | For $N$ transactions |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hash Chain Linking and Tamper Evidence

### Objective
Prove that altering any transaction in block $B_k$ invalidates all subsequent blocks $B_{k+1}, B_{k+2}, \ldots, B_n$.

### Setup
Let:
- $H_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}(B_i)))$ — block hash of $B_i$.
- $\text{prev\_hash}_i = H_{i-1}$ — parent hash field in $B_i$.

The chain relation is:

$$H_i = \text{HASH}(\text{Header}(B_i))$$

where Header contains `prev_hash` $= H_{i-1}$.

### Step 1 — Define the Chain Dependency

The hash of any block $B_i$ is a deterministic function of its own header, which embeds $H_{i-1}$. Formally:

$$H_i = f(\text{version}, H_{i-1}, \text{merkle\_root}, \text{ts}, \text{difficulty}, \text{nonce})$$

### Step 2 — Assume a Tampering Event

Suppose an attacker modifies transaction $T_j$ in block $B_k$ (where $1 \leq k \leq n$). This changes the **Merkle root** $M_k$ of block $B_k$ because:

$$M_k = \text{MerkleRoot}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_m)$$

The Merkle root is cryptographically sensitive — changing a single bit in any transaction $T_j$ propagates up the binary hash tree and changes $M_k$ entirely (avalanche effect of SHA-256).

### Step 3 — Propagation to Block Hash

Since $M_k$ is part of $\text{Header}(B_k)$, and $H_k = \text{HASH}(\text{Header}(B_k))$:

$$M_k \to M_k' \implies \text{Header}(B_k) \to \text{Header}(B_k)' \implies H_k \to H_k'$$

The block hash $H_k$ changes to a completely different value $H_k'$ (with overwhelming probability).

### Step 4 — Cascade to All Subsequent Blocks

In block $B_{k+1}$, the field `prev_hash` still stores the **old** $H_k$ (the honest nodes' canonical value). The tampered chain has:

$$\text{prev\_hash}_{k+1}' = H_k' \neq H_k$$

This mismatch is immediately detected by any full node performing validation:

$$\text{VALIDATE}(B_{k+1}) = \text{False}$$

The attacker must now re-mine $B_{k+1}$ (re-do PoW), which changes $H_{k+1}$, propagating the cascade. By induction, **every subsequent block $B_i$ for $i \geq k$ must be re-mined**.

### Step 5 — Computational Impossibility

The total work required to redo blocks $k$ through $n$ is:

$$W_{\text{redo}} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} D(B_i) = W_{\text{total}} - W_{\text{blocks 1 to } k-1}$$

In Bitcoin, with $n \approx 800{,}000$ blocks (as of recent height) and the attacker controlling less than 50% of hash power, the probability of catching up decays **exponentially** with the number of confirmations $n - k$.

### Conclusion
The cryptographic chain linkage $H_i = f(\ldots, H_{i-1}, \ldots)$ makes **retroactive tampering computationally infeasible** without re-doing all subsequent Proof-of-Work.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Merkle Root for Transactions

### Objective
Show how $N$ transactions are condensed into a single 32-byte Merkle root embedded in the block header.

### Binary Tree Construction

Let transactions be $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_N$ (in pairs). Define:

$$\text{Leaf hash: } L_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(T_i))$$

For an even number of leaves, internal nodes:

$$P_{i,j} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256)}(L_i \vert\vert L_j)$$

For an odd number of leaves, the last leaf is duplicated:

$$P_{i,\text{last}} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256)}(L_{\text{last}} \vert\vert L_{\text{last}})$$

### Worked Example: $N = 4$ Transactions

$$M = P_{12,34} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256)}(P_{1,2} \vert\vert P_{3,4})$$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{1,2} &= \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256)}(L_1 \vert\vert L_2) \\
P_{3,4} &= \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256)}(L_3 \vert\vert L_4) \\
M &= \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256)}(P_{1,2} \vert\vert P_{3,4})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Tree Depth
For $N$ transactions, the Merkle tree has depth:

$$\text{depth} = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$$

So for $N = 1024$ transactions, depth $= 10$. A **Merkle proof** for one transaction requires only $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ hashes (~80 bytes per hash = 640 bytes), enabling **Simple Payment Verification (SPV)**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Longest Chain Probability (Bitcoin White Paper)

### Setup (Nakamoto, 2008)
- $q$ = attacker's hash power fraction.
- $z = 1 - q$ = honest nodes' hash power fraction.
- Attacker mined a block at confirmation depth $n$.

### Probability Attacker Catches Up

The attacker must "overtake" the honest chain by mining $k$ more blocks than the honest network mines $n$ blocks in the same time. Using the **binomial / random walk** model:

$$P(\text{catch up}) = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } q \geq z \\
\left(\frac{q}{z}\right)^n & \text{approximation for small } q
\end{cases}$$

### KTU Calculation Example

Suppose $q = 0.1$ (10% attacker), $n = 6$ confirmations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{catch up}) &= \left(\frac{0.1}{0.9}\right)^6 \\
&= \left(\frac{1}{9}\right)^6 \\
&= \frac{1}{9^6} \\
&= \frac{1}{531{,}441} \\
&\approx 1.88 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **6 confirmations** is the de-facto standard in Bitcoin: it reduces the double-spend attack probability to less than 1 in 500,000 for a 10% attacker.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison & Decision Logic

| Scenario | Permissionless Chain | Permissioned Chain |
|----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Participants | Anyone with internet | Pre-vetted, known |
| Sybil Resistance | Cost of PoW / PoS stake | Identity gating |
| Trust Model | Trustless (no central party) | Trusted consortium |
| Finality | Probabilistic (longest chain) | Deterministic (BFT) |
| Fork Resolution | Longest chain rule | Voting/leader protocol |
| Privacy | Pseudonymous | KYC-bound (know your customer) |
| Governance | Open-source, community | Corporate board, DAO |
| Use Case Fit | Digital cash, public DeFi | B2B, regulated industries |

### Decision Logic Flowchart (Textual)

1. **Need public, censorship-resistant value transfer?** → **Permissionless** (Bitcoin, Ethereum).
2. **Need high throughput, low latency, regulatory compliance?** → **Permissioned** (Hyperledger, Corda).
3. **Need trust between known enterprises (e.g., banks)?** → **Consortium Permissioned** (R3 Corda, B3i).
4. **Need single-organization control?** → **Fully Private Permissioned** (Quorum, Multichain).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Block Validation Cost

### Problem
A block at height 700,000 is found to contain an altered transaction. Estimate the cost for an attacker with 30% hash power to forge the chain up to the current tip at height 700,006 (6 blocks later).

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Work to redo} &= \sum_{i=0}^{5} D(B_{700000+i}) \\
&\approx 6 \times D_{\text{current}} \\
&\approx 6 \times (\text{expected hashes per block})
\end{aligned}
$$

Bitcoin's current difficulty $D \approx 8.4 \times 10^{22}$ hashes per block. With attacker fraction $q = 0.3$, expected time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{time}] &= \frac{6 \text{ blocks}}{0.3 \times \frac{1 \text{ block}}{10 \text{ min}}} \\
&= \frac{6}{0.03} \text{ hours} \\
&= 200 \text{ minutes} \\
&\approx 3.33 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

Honest network grows in parallel: $6 \text{ blocks} / 0.7 \text{ rate} \approx 85.7$ minutes. The attacker **never catches up** because the gap grows.

$$\text{Probability of success} = \left(\frac{0.3}{0.7}\right)^6 = (0.4286)^6 \approx 0.00616 \approx 0.62\%$$

The attacker would need to spend **millions of dollars in electricity** for a sub-1% success chance — economically irrational.

## 4.5 Transaction Lifecycle (Detailed State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
```
