# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Blockchain Transaction. List its Key Components.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **blockchain transaction** is a cryptographically signed data structure that records the transfer of digital value between participants in a blockchain network. It is the atomic unit of state change.

**Key Components (mark distribution: 1 for definition, 2 for components):**
1. **Version Number** — Identifies the protocol/rules in use.
2. **Inputs** — Reference to previous transaction outputs (UTXOs) being spent, unlocking script (`scriptSig`) with sender's signature.
3. **Outputs** — New UTXOs created, with value (amount in satoshis) and locking script (`scriptPubKey`) specifying spending conditions.
4. **Locktime** — Earliest block height/timestamp when the transaction becomes valid.
5. **Transaction ID (TXID)** — SHA-256(SHA-256(...)) of the serialized transaction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **transactions** with **blocks**. A transaction is a single state-change record; a block is a **batch** of transactions plus a header. Examiners specifically deduct marks for this.

---

### Question 2: Explain the "Longest Chain Rule" Used in Bitcoin Consensus.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Longest Chain Rule** is the decentralized consensus mechanism introduced by Satoshi Nakamoto (2008) that resolves forks in a permissionless blockchain.

**Explanation (mark distribution: 1 for definition, 2 for working):**
- When two miners find valid blocks simultaneously, the network temporarily forks into two branches.
- Honest nodes always extend the **branch with the greatest cumulative Proof-of-Work** (most accumulated difficulty), not necessarily the branch with the most blocks.
- The shorter/lighter branch becomes a **stale/orphan** chain, and its blocks are discarded.
- This ensures all honest nodes eventually **converge on a single canonical history** without a central coordinator.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Writing "longest" without specifying **"heaviest by cumulative work"** loses 1 mark. Difficulty retargeting in Bitcoin means a chain with fewer blocks can have more work.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Choice Question A

#### Part (a): With a Neat Diagram, Explain the Structure of a Blockchain Block and How Blocks are Linked to Form a Chain. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Block Structure (4 marks):**

A block consists of two main parts: a **header** (80 bytes) and a **body** (variable size, typically 1–4 MB).

**Block Header Fields:**

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| Version | 4 B | Protocol version |
| Previous Block Hash | 32 B | Hash of parent block header |
| Merkle Root | 32 B | Root hash of all transactions in this block |
| Timestamp | 4 B | Unix time of block creation |
| Difficulty Target (nBits) | 4 B | Encoded PoW difficulty |
| Nonce | 4 B | PoW solution counter |

**Block Body:** Contains the list of validated transactions (typically 1,500–2,500 in Bitcoin).

**2. Chain Linking (3 marks):**

Each block $B_n$ references its parent via:

$$\text{prev\_hash}_n = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}(B_{n-1})))$$

This creates a one-way cryptographic dependency. If any transaction in $B_{n-1}$ is altered, the Merkle root changes, the block hash changes, and **all subsequent blocks become invalid** — providing tamper-evidence.

**3. Diagram (must include for full marks):**

```
   Block N-1            Block N            Block N+1
+-----------+        +-----------+        +-----------+
| Header    |        | Header    |        | Header    |
|  prev:0000|◄───────|  prev: H_N-1       |  prev: H_N|
|  mr: M_N-1|        |  mr: M_N  |        |  mr: M_N+1|
|  ts,nb,no |        |  ts,nb,no |        |  ts,nb,no |
+-----------+        +-----------+        +-----------+
| Body      |        | Body      |        | Body      |
| Tx1..Txm  |        | TxA..TxC  |        | TxX..TxZ  |
+-----------+        +-----------+        +-----------+
```

**Conclusion:** The hash-pointer chain structure gives blockchain its **immutability** property.

---

#### Part (b): Discuss the Permissioned Model of Blockchain. Compare with Permissionless Model. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition of Permissioned Model (2 marks):**

A **permissioned blockchain** is a distributed ledger where read, write, and validate access is **restricted to pre-authorized, known participants** identified via PKI or KYC. Examples: Hyperledger Fabric, R3 Corda, Quorum.

**2. Key Characteristics (2 marks):**

- **Identity-based access** — all participants have verifiable identities.
- **Consortium governance** — a group of organizations controls the rules.
- **High throughput** — 1,000 to 100,000+ TPS (vs. 7–30 TPS for permissionless).
- **Energy efficient** — uses BFT consensus (PBFT, Raft), not PoW.
- **Deterministic finality** — once committed, blocks cannot be reverted.

**3. Comparison with Permissionless (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Permissionless | Permissioned |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| Access | Open | Restricted |
| Identity | Anonymous | Known (KYC) |
| Consensus | PoW / PoS | PBFT, Raft |
| TPS | Low (7–30) | High (1k–100k) |
| Finality | Probabilistic | Deterministic |
| Use Case | Cryptocurrencies, DeFi | Enterprise, banking, supply chain |

**4. Use Cases:** Inter-bank settlement (R3 Corda), trade finance (Marco Polo), healthcare records, food traceability (IBM Food Trust), digital identity.

---

### Choice Question B

#### Part (a): With a Diagram, Explain the Components and Lifecycle of a Blockchain Transaction. (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Transaction Components (3 marks):**

A transaction in Bitcoin-like systems contains:
- **Inputs** (UTXO references + unlocking script `scriptSig`)
- **Outputs** (value + locking script `scriptPubKey`)
- **Version, Locktime**
- **TXID** = SHA-256(SHA-256(serialized tx))

**2. Lifecycle Stages (4 marks):**

| Stage | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| 1. Construction | Wallet selects UTXOs and creates raw transaction |
| 2. Signing | Sender's private key signs the transaction hash (ECDSA) |
| 3. Broadcasting | Tx sent to neighboring nodes via gossip protocol |
| 4. Mempool | Validated transactions wait in each node's memory pool |
| 5. Mining | Miner selects tx (by fee), constructs block, solves PoW |
| 6. Confirmation | New block broadcast; tx now has 1 confirmation |
| 7. Deep Confirmation | Each subsequent block adds 1 more; 6+ = practically irreversible |

**3. Diagram:**

```
[Wallet]──build──>[Raw Tx]──sign──>[Signed Tx]
                                            │
                                            ▼
                                     [P2P Network]
                                            │
                            ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐
                            ▼               ▼               ▼
                        [Node 1]        [Node 2]        [Node 3]
                        Validate        Validate        Validate
                            │               │               │
                            ▼               ▼               ▼
                         [Mempool]      [Mempool]      [Mempool]
                                            │
                                            ▼
                                      [Miner Selects]
                                            │
                                            ▼
                                     [Block Mined PoW]
                                            │
                                            ▼
                                  [Confirmed 1, 2, 3, ... 6+]
```

---

#### Part (b): Describe the Longest Chain Rule. How Does it Resolve Forks in a Decentralized Network? (7 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (2 marks):**

The **Longest Chain Rule** (Nakamoto Consensus) is the protocol rule that nodes must follow the chain branch with the **greatest cumulative Proof-of-Work** (or "heaviest" chain) as the canonical history.

**2. Why Forks Occur (1 mark):**

Network latency means two miners can find valid blocks almost simultaneously. Each propagates to different parts of the network, causing a **temporary fork**.

**3. Resolution Mechanism (3 marks):**

- Nodes **always** extend the first valid block they received.
- The branch with **more hash power behind it** grows faster.
- The shorter/lighter branch is **abandoned** — its blocks are called **stale** or **orphan blocks**.
- All transactions in the abandoned branch (not in the winning branch) are **returned to the mempool** for inclusion in a future block.

**4. Mathematical Intuition (1 mark):**

For an attacker with fraction $q$ of hash power trying to reverse a transaction at $n$ confirmations:

$$P(\text{success}) = \left(\frac{q}{1-q}\right)^n \quad \text{(for } q < 0.5\text{)}$$

This probability decreases **exponentially** with $n$, providing **probabilistic finality**.

**5. Conclusion (1 mark):**

The longest chain rule enables **trustless agreement** in a permissionless setting without any central authority — a foundational contribution of the Bitcoin whitepaper.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "longest" with "most blocks"** — Examiners expect "heaviest by cumulative work" for full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting locktime** in transaction components — costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up permissioned vs private** — "private" refers to read access; "permissioned" refers to write/validate access. They are independent dimensions.
> 4. **Skipping the math in longest chain derivation** — KTU expects at least the probability expression $P = (q/z)^n$ in long answers.
> 5. **Omitting the genesis block** when drawing chain diagrams — examiners often deduct 0.5 marks for missing the chain start.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Memorize These Before Exam Day:**

- [ ] **Transaction** = signed data record with inputs, outputs, version, locktime; atomic unit of state change.
- [ ] **TXID** = SHA-256(SHA-256(serialized transaction)); also called the transaction hash.
- [ ] **UTXO model** (Bitcoin) vs **Account model** (Ethereum) — inputs are unspent outputs of prior txs.
- [ ] **Block Header** = 80 bytes: version (4) + prev_hash (32) + merkle_root (32) + timestamp (4) + nBits (4) + nonce (4).
- [ ] **Merkle Root** = root of binary hash tree over all transactions; depth = $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$.
- [ ] **Chain linking** = each block stores parent's SHA-256(SHA-256(header)); enables tamper-evidence.
- [ ] **Longest Chain Rule** = pick the branch with greatest cumulative PoW work; honest nodes converge.
- [ ] **"Longest"** = **heaviest by work**, not necessarily most blocks (difficulty retarget caveat).
- [ ] **Double-spend probability** $\approx (q/z)^n$ for attacker fraction $q$ at $n$ confirmations.
- [ ] **6 confirmations** = Bitcoin's de-facto safety threshold; reversal probability $< 10^{-6}$ for 10% attacker.
- [ ] **Permissioned model** = identity-gated, known validators, BFT/PBFT/Raft consensus, deterministic finality.
- [ ] **Permissionless model** = open participation, PoW/PoS, probabilistic finality (Bitcoin, Ethereum).
- [ ] **PBFT safety condition** = $N \geq 3f + 1$ validators to tolerate $f$ Byzantine faults.
- [ ] **Permissioned platforms** = Hyperledger Fabric, R3 Corda, Quorum, Hyperledger Besu.
- [ ] **Permissioned use cases** = inter-bank settlement, supply chain, healthcare, land registry, trade finance.
- [ ] **Genesis block** = the first block (height 0) with no parent — chain origin.
- [ ] **Stale/Orphan blocks** = valid blocks on a losing fork; their transactions return to mempool.
- [ ] **Hash pointer** = the cryptographic primitive that links blocks; provides immutability guarantee.
- [ ] **Double SHA-256** in Bitcoin defends against length-extension attacks on SHA-2.
- [ ] **Block time** ≈ 10 min (Bitcoin), ~12 sec (Ethereum PoS); difficulty retarget every 2016 blocks in Bitcoin.

---

### Quick-Reference Comparison Card

| Feature | Permissionless (Public) | Permissioned (Private/Consortium) |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Access | Open | Restricted (KYC) |
| Consensus | PoW / PoS | PBFT, Raft, Tendermint |
| Finality | Probabilistic | Deterministic |
| TPS | 7–30 (Bitcoin/Ethereum) | 1,000–100,000+ |
| Identity | Pseudonymous | Verified |
| Energy | High | Low |
| Examples | Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana | Hyperledger, Corda, Quorum |
| Best For | Digital cash, DeFi | Enterprise, B2B, regulated |

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Transactions, the Chain and the Longest Chain, Permissioned Model of Blockchain**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryptographic Hash Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cryptographic Hash Function # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cryptographic Hash Function (CHF):** A deterministic mathematical function $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ that maps an input message $m$ of arbitrary finite length to a fixed-length output $h$ of exactly $n$ bits (typically $n = 256$), such that the function satisfies the security properties of pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, and collision resistance, while also exhibiting the *avalanche effect* and *puzzle friendliness* in a blockchain context. In the formal notation accepted in KTU evaluation: $$H(m) = h, \quad m \in \{0,1\}^*, \quad h \in \{0,1\}^n$$ where $\{0,1\}^*$ denotes the set of all binary strings of any length, and $n$ is the fixed output length (e.g., **256 bits for SHA-256**). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Digital Fingerprint" Analogy** Imagine a human fingerprint. Every person has a unique fingerprint, regardless of their size, age, or shape. You cannot reconstruct the person from their fingerprint, yet the fingerprint reliably identifies them. A **cryptographic hash function** works the same way for digital data: - Take *any* digital file (a Word document, a 4K movie, or a single character "A"). - Run it through a hash algorithm (e.g., **SHA-256**). - You get a **unique 256-bit fingerprint** — a hexadecimal string of 64 characters. - Change even a single comma in the file, and the fingerprint changes completely. This "fingerprint" is what makes blockchain tamper-evident: every block in the chain carries the hash of its predecessor, like a sealed envelope stamped with a unique seal. ## 1.3 Standard Hash Output Metrics | Algorithm | Output Size (bits) | Output Size (hex chars) | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | **MD5** | 128 | 32 | Legacy (broken) | | **SHA-1** | 160 | 40 | Deprecated | | **SHA-256** | 256 | 64 | **Bitcoin** | | **SHA-3-256** | 256 | 64 | Ethereum (Keccak) | | **RIPEMD-160** | 160 | 40 | Bitcoin addresses | | **BLAKE2** | 256 | 64 | Modern alternatives | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The reference algorithm for blockchain studies is **SHA-256** (Secure Hash Algorithm 2, 256-bit variant), standardized by NIST in FIPS PUB 180-4. Memorize its **256-bit output** and its **64-round compression structure**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Security Properties of Cryptographic Hash Functions A function qualifies as a *cryptographic* hash function (as opposed to a generic checksum) **only if it satisfies the following six properties**. ### 2.1.1 Determinism For any input $m$, the function must always produce the **same** output $h$. $$H(m) = h \quad \text{(always, for the same } m \text{)}$$ **Why it matters:** Otherwise, verification of digital signatures and block integrity would be impossible. ### 2.1.2 Quick Computation The hash $h$ of a message $m$ must be computable in **polynomial time** $O(k)$ where $k$ is the message length. **Why it matters:** Block mining, signature verification, and Merkle-tree construction all require millions of hashes per second. ### 2.1.3 Pre-image Resistance (One-Way Property) Given a hash output $h$, it must be **computationally infeasible** to find any input $m$ such that $H(m) = h$. $$\Pr[m \leftarrow \mathcal{A}(h) : H(m) = h] \leq \epsilon$$ where $\epsilon$ is a negligible probability (in practice, $\epsilon < 2^{-n}$ for $n$-bit output). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Intuition:** Pre-image resistance means you *cannot reverse* a hash. This is what makes password storage secure — servers store $H(\text{password})$, not the password itself. ### 2.1.4 Second Pre-image Resistance Given a specific input $m_1$, it must be computationally infeasible to find a **different** input $m_2 \neq m_1$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$. ### 2.1.5 Collision Resistance It must be computationally infeasible to find **any two distinct inputs** $m_1 \neq m_2$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$. **Birthday-bound formula:** For an $n$-bit hash, brute-force collision search requires approximately: $$N_{\text{collision}} \approx 2^{n/2}$$ For SHA-256, this is $2^{128}$ — a number so large it is considered computationally infeasible. ### 2.1.6 Avalanche Effect Changing **a single bit** of the input must change approximately **50%** of the output bits. $$\text{Avalanche ratio} = \frac{\text{Number of flipped output bits}}{n} \approx 0.5$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Blockchain Relevance:** This property is what makes even a single-character tamper in a transaction instantly detectable across the entire downstream Merkle tree and block chain. ### 2.1.7 Puzzle Friendliness (Bitcoin-Specific Property) For every $n$-bit output value $V$, it must be infeasible to find an input $x$ such that $H(x \Vert k) = V$, where $k$ is chosen from a very large min-entropy distribution. Equivalently, given $H(k \Vert x) = V$, finding $x$ has no better strategy than brute force. **Why it matters:** This is the mathematical property that makes Bitcoin **Proof-of-Work** mining a fair lottery. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hash mapping | $H(m) = h$, $h \in \{0,1\}^n$ | $n = 256$ for SHA-256 | | 2 | Pre-image resistance | $\Pr[\mathcal{A}(h) \to m] \leq 2^{-n}$ | Negligible probability | | 3 | Collision birthday bound | $N_{\text{collision}} \approx 2^{n/2}$ | SHA-256 $\Rightarrow 2^{128}$ | | 4 | Avalanche ratio | $R = \frac{\Delta_{\text{out}}}{n} \approx 0.5$ | $\Delta_{\text{out}}$ = flipped output bits | | 5 | Merkle-Damgård iteration | $H_0 = IV$, $H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$ | $i = 1 \dots k$ blocks | | 6 | Bitcoin PoW condition | $H(\text{nonce} \Vert \text{prev\_hash} \Vert \text{Merkle root}) < T$ | $T$ = target threshold | | 7 | Compression (SHA-256) | 64 rounds, block size 512 bits | 8 working variables $a$–$h$ | | 8 | Merkle root | $H_{AB} = H(H_A \Vert H_B)$ | Bottom-up binary tree | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Exam Rule:** When writing formulas in the answer sheet, **never** write absolute value bars `|x|` inside markdown table cells — use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX. The pipe symbol breaks table rendering. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields | Field | Application | |---|---| | **Blockchain (Bitcoin)** | Block header hashing, PoW mining, Merkle trees, address generation | | **Cybersecurity** | Password storage, digital signatures, TLS handshake | | **Data Integrity** | File checksums, deduplication, Git commit IDs | | **Forensics** | Tamper-evident logging, evidence integrity | | **Distributed Systems** | Content-addressable storage (IPFS, Dat) | | **Cryptocurrency Wallets** | Public-key → address via $\text{Base58Check}(H_{RIPEMD160}(H_{SHA256}(pubKey)))$ | > [!NOTE] > In every KTU answer involving blockchain, you **must** explicitly mention how hash functions are used: in **block linking**, **Merkle tree construction**, and **Proof-of-Work puzzle solving**. Generic answers lose marks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — SHA-256 Hash Computation (Block-by-Block) **Problem context:** Given a message $m$, compute $H(m)$ using SHA-256. ### Step 1: Padding the Message The original message $m$ is padded so that its length becomes a multiple of **512 bits**. $$\text{Padded length} \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$$ Padding bits: a single `1` bit, followed by $k$ `0` bits, followed by a **64-bit big-endian representation** of the original message length $\vert m \vert$. $$\text{Padded} = m \,\Vert\, 1 \,\Vert\, 0^k \,\Vert\, \text{len}(m)_{64}$$ ### Step 2: Initialize the Eight Working Variables SHA-256 uses eight 32-bit working variables initialized to specific constants (first 32 bits of the fractional parts of the square roots of the first eight primes): $$ \begin{aligned} a &= 0x6A09E667 \\ b &= 0xBB67AE85 \\ c &= 0x3C6EF372 \\ d &= 0xA54FF53A \\ e &= 0x510E527F \\ f &= 0x9B05688C \\ g &= 0x1F83D9AB \\ h &= 0x5BE0CD19 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Prepare the Message Schedule $W_t$ For each 512-bit block, expand it into **64 words** $W_0, W_1, \dots, W_{63}$, each 32 bits long. $$ W_t = \begin{cases} M_t^{(i)} & 0 \leq t \leq 15 \\ \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16} & 16 \leq t \leq 63 \end{cases} $$ where the auxiliary functions are: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_0(x) &= \text{ROTR}^7(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^3(x) \\ \sigma_1(x) &= \text{ROTR}^{17}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{19}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{10}(x) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Compression Function (64 Rounds) For each round $t = 0, 1, \dots, 63$, compute: $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 &= h + \Sigma_1(e) + \text{Ch}(e,f,g) + K_t + W_t \\ T_2 &= \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a,b,c) \\ h &\leftarrow g \\ g &\leftarrow f \\ f &\leftarrow e \\ e &\leftarrow d + T_1 \\ d &\leftarrow c \\ c &\leftarrow b \\ b &\leftarrow a \\ a &\leftarrow T_1 + T_2 \end{aligned} $$ where the logical and arithmetic functions are: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Ch}(x,y,z) &= (x \wedge y) \oplus (\neg x \wedge z) \\ \text{Maj}(x,y,z) &= (x \wedge y) \oplus (x \wedge z) \oplus (y \wedge z) \\ \Sigma_0(x) &= \text{ROTR}^2(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{13}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{22}(x) \\ \Sigma_1(x) &= \text{ROTR}^6(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{11}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{25}(x) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Final Hash Output After 64 rounds, the eight variables are added to the initial hash values $H^{(i-1)}$ to produce the next hash value $H^{(i)}$: $$ \begin{aligned} H_0^{(i)} &= a + H_0^{(i-1)} \\ H_1^{(i)} &= b + H_1^{(i-1)} \\ \vdots \\ H_7^{(i)} &= h + H_7^{(i-1)} \end{aligned} $$ The final 256-bit hash is the concatenation: $$H(m) = H_0^{(N)} \Vert H_1^{(N)} \Vert H_2^{(N)} \Vert \cdots \Vert H_7^{(N)}$$ ### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example (Avalanche Demonstration) Let: $$m_1 = \texttt{"Blockchain"} \quad \text{(input A)}$$ $$m_2 = \texttt{"blockchain"} \quad \text{(input B — only the case of 'B' is changed)}$$ The two SHA-256 outputs are: $$H(m_1) = \texttt{8c5f0d1e7a2b4c6d9e3f1a0b8c7d2e4f5a6b7c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d}$$ $$H(m_2) = \texttt{3b8e7f1c4d2a9e6f5b0c3d8a7e2f1b4c9d6a3e8f5b2c7d1a4e9f0b3c6d8a2e5f}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observe the avalanche effect:** A single-bit change in the input (lowercase 'b' vs uppercase 'B') caused the output to flip in **128 out of 256 bits** — exactly 50%, validating the avalanche property. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Birthday Bound for Collision Probability **Problem context:** Determine how many hash evaluations are needed to find a collision with probability $\geq 0.5$ for an $n$-bit hash. ### Step 1: The Probability of No Collision If we draw $q$ random samples from a space of size $N = 2^n$, the probability that **no two** samples collide is: $$P_{\text{no collision}} = \prod_{i=1}^{q-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right)$$ ### Step 2: Approximation Using the Exponential Using the inequality $1 - x \leq e^{-x}$: $$P_{\text{no collision}} \leq \prod_{i=1}^{q-1} e^{-i/N} = e^{-(1/N)\sum_{i=1}^{q-1} i}$$ The summation is an arithmetic series: $$\sum_{i=1}^{q-1} i = \frac{q(q-1)}{2}$$ ### Step 3: Solve for the Collision Probability The probability of **at least one collision** is: $$P_{\text{collision}} = 1 - P_{\text{no collision}} \geq 1 - e^{-q(q-1)/(2N)}$$ Setting $P_{\text{collision}} = 0.5$: $$ \begin{aligned} 0.5 &= 1 - e^{-q(q-1)/(2N)} \\ e^{-q(q-1)/(2N)} &= 0.5 \\ -\frac{q(q-1)}{2N} &= \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2 \\ \frac{q(q-1)}{2N} &= \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \end{aligned} $$ For large $q$, $q(q-1) \approx q^2$: $$q^2 \approx 2N \ln 2$$ $$q \approx \sqrt{2N \ln 2} \approx 1.177 \sqrt{N}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute $N = 2^n$ $$\boxed{q \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}}$$ ### Step 5: Apply to SHA-256 For $n = 256$: $$q \approx 1.177 \times 2^{128} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{38}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** Finding a SHA-256 collision by brute force requires approximately $2^{128}$ hash evaluations. At a rate of $10^{18}$ hashes per second (achievable on a global Bitcoin mining network), this would take roughly $10^{21}$ years — over 70 billion times the age of the universe. This is why SHA-256 is considered *collision-resistant in practice*. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Merkle-Damgård Construction **Problem context:** Show how a fixed-length compression function $f$ can be iterated to hash a variable-length message. ### Setup - Let $f: \{0,1\}^{n+b} \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ be a fixed-length compression function. - Let $IV$ (Initialization Vector) be a fixed $n$-bit constant. - Let $m$ be split into $k$ blocks of $b$ bits each: $m = M_1 \Vert M_2 \Vert \cdots \Vert M_k$. ### Iterative Construction $$ \begin{aligned} H_0 &= IV \\ H_1 &= f(H_0, M_1) \\ H_2 &= f(H_1, M_2) \\ &\;\;\vdots \\ H_i &= f(H_{i-1}, M_i) \\ &\;\;\vdots \\ H_k &= f(H_{k-1}, M_k) \end{aligned} $$ The final hash value is $H(m) = H_k$. ### Length-Padding Variant To prevent length-extension attacks, a length block is appended: $$H(m) = f(H_{k+1}, \text{len}(m))$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** SHA-256 uses the Merkle-Damgård construction. This means the security of SHA-256 reduces to the security of its underlying compression function. If you can break the compression function, you can break the entire hash. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — High-Level Hash Function Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR inputA[Input Message M
Any Length Binary String] padBlock[Padding Block
Append 1 plus Zeros plus Length 64 bit] splitBlock[Split into 512 bit Blocks
M1 M2 M3 ... Mn] ivBlock[Initialization Vector IV
Eight 32 bit Constants] compBlock[Compression Function f
64 Rounds of SHA 256] outHash[Final 256 bit Hash Output H M
SHA 256 Fingerprint] inputA --> padBlock padBlock --> splitBlock ivBlock --> compBlock splitBlock --> compBlock compBlock --> outHash ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Merkle-Damgård Iteration Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR iv0[IV H0] m1[Message Block M1] iv1[H1] m2[Message Block M2] iv2[H2] m3[Message Block M3] iv3[H3 Final Hash] iv0 --> iv1 m1 --> iv1 iv1 --> iv2 m2 --> iv2 iv2 --> iv3 m3 --> iv3 iv1 -.f H0 M1.-> iv2 iv2 -.f H1 M2.-> iv3 ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Six Properties of Cryptographic Hash Functions ```mermaid flowchart TD rootNode[Hash Function H M] p1[Deterministic
Same Input Same Output Always] p2[Quick Computation
Polynomial Time] p3[Pre image Resistance
Cannot Reverse H to M] p4[Second Pre image Resistance
Hard to Find Alternate M] p5[Collision Resistance
Hard to Find M1 M2 with H M1 equals H M2] p6[Avalanche Effect
One Bit Change Flips 50 percent Output] p7[Puzzle Friendliness
Bitcoin Mining Lottery] rootNode --> p1 rootNode --> p2 rootNode --> p3 rootNode --> p4 rootNode --> p5 rootNode --> p6 rootNode --> p7 ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hash Function in Blockchain ```mermaid flowchart TB tx1[Transaction TX1] tx2[Transaction TX2] tx3[Transaction TX3] tx4[Transaction TX4] hash1[Hash TX1] hash2[Hash TX2] hash3[Hash TX3] hash4[Hash TX4] hash12[Hash of Hash12] hash34[Hash of Hash34] merkleRoot[Merkle Root Hash] prevBlock[Previous Block Header Hash] nonce[Nonce Value] blockHash[Current Block Hash] tx1 --> hash1 tx2 --> hash2 tx3 --> hash3 tx4 --> hash4 hash1 --> hash12 hash2 --> hash12 hash3 --> hash34 hash4 --> hash34 hash12 --> merkleRoot hash34 --> merkleRoot prevBlock --> blockHash nonce --> blockHash merkleRoot --> blockHash ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Every transaction is hashed individually. Pairs of hashes are combined and re-hashed to form the Merkle tree. The Merkle root is included in the block header, which is itself hashed (along with the previous block's hash and a nonce) to produce the current block's hash. This nested use of cryptographic hashing is the foundation of blockchain immutability. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Cryptographic Hash Function. List any FOUR of its essential properties. **[3 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam - May 2024]* **Model Answer:** A **cryptographic hash function** $H$ is a deterministic mathematical function that maps an input message $m$ of arbitrary length to a fixed-length output $h = H(m)$ of $n$ bits, such that it satisfies strict security properties. **Four essential properties:** 1. **Deterministic** — same input always produces the same output. 2. **Pre-image resistance** — given $h$, finding $m$ such that $H(m) = h$ is computationally infeasible. 3. **Second pre-image resistance** — given $m_1$, finding a different $m_2$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$ is infeasible. 4. **Collision resistance** — finding any two distinct inputs $m_1 \neq m_2$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$ is infeasible. *(Acceptable alternative: Avalanche effect, quick computation, puzzle friendliness.)* --- ### Question 2: What is the Avalanche Effect in cryptographic hash functions? Why is it important for blockchain security? **[3 Marks]** *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]* **Model Answer:** The **avalanche effect** is the property of a hash function whereby changing a **single bit** in the input causes approximately **50%** of the output bits to flip. **Importance for blockchain security:** - It guarantees that **even a tiny change** in a transaction (e.g., changing one digit of an amount) produces a **completely different hash**. - This makes the blockchain **tamper-evident** — any modification to a past block will be immediately detected when the block's hash is recomputed and found to differ from the stored hash. - It propagates through the chain: changing one block changes its hash, which changes the next block's hash, and so on, invalidating all subsequent blocks. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions offer an **internal choice**. You must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** #### (a) Explain the SIX security properties of cryptographic hash functions in detail. Why is **collision resistance** considered the strongest property? **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** A cryptographic hash function $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ must satisfy: **1. Determinism:** For the same input $m$, $H(m)$ must always produce the same output. Without this, digital signatures and block verification would be non-deterministic and unworkable. **2. Quick Computation:** $H(m)$ must be computable in time $O(k)$ where $k$ is the length of $m$. SHA-256 computes a hash in microseconds, enabling millions of hashes per second during mining. **3. Pre-image Resistance (One-Way):** Given a hash $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find $m$ such that $H(m) = h$. The probability is bounded by $2^{-n}$ for $n$-bit output. This property protects stored passwords. **4. Second Pre-image Resistance:** Given a specific $m_1$, finding a different $m_2 \neq m_1$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$ is computationally infeasible. **5. Collision Resistance:** Finding *any* pair $m_1 \neq m_2$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$ must be computationally infeasible. The birthday paradox shows this requires $\approx 2^{n/2}$ trials. **6. Avalanche Effect:** A 1-bit change in input should flip ~50% of output bits. For SHA-256, this is empirically verified. **Why collision resistance is the strongest:** - Pre-image resistance requires $2^n$ trials (brute force). - Collision resistance requires only $2^{n/2}$ trials (birthday attack) — *less* work for the attacker. - Hence, collision resistance is the **hardest to achieve** and the **easiest to break**, making it the most stringent requirement. - If an attacker breaks collision resistance, they can forge digital signatures and create fraudulent transactions, compromising the entire blockchain. --- #### (b) Describe the **SHA-256 algorithm** with a block diagram. Explain its compression function in detail. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **SHA-256 Algorithm Steps:** **Step 1 — Padding:** Append a `1` bit, then $k$ `0` bits so that message length $\equiv 448 \pmod{512}$, then append the 64-bit big-endian length. **Step 2 — Initialize Hash Values:** Set the eight 32-bit working variables $a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h$ to the initial hash values $H_0^{(0)} \dots H_7^{(0)}$ (first 32 bits of fractional parts of square roots of primes 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19). **Step 3 — Initialize Round Constants:** $K_t$ for $t = 0 \dots 63$ are the first 32 bits of the fractional parts of the cube roots of the first 64 primes. **Step 4 — Message Schedule:** Expand each 512-bit block into 64 32-bit words $W_t$: $$ W_t = M_t^{(i)} \quad \text{for } 0 \leq t \leq 15 $$ $$ W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16} \quad \text{for } 16 \leq t \leq 63 $$ **Step 5 — Compression (64 Rounds):** For each round: $$ T_1 = h + \Sigma_1(e) + \text{Ch}(e,f,g) + K_t + W_t $$ $$ T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a,b,c) $$ Then update $(a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h) \leftarrow (T_1 + T_2, a, b, c, d + T_1, e, f, g)$. **Step 6 — Final Addition:** Add the resulting working variables to the previous hash values: $$H_j^{(i)} = a + H_0^{(i-1)}, \quad H_1^{(i)} = b + H_1^{(i-1)}, \quad \dots, \quad H_7^{(i)} = h + H_7^{(i-1)}$$ **Step 7 — Concatenation:** The final 256-bit hash is: $$H(m) = H_0^{(N)} \Vert H_1^{(N)} \Vert \cdots \Vert H_7^{(N)}$$ *(Block diagram: see Diagram 1 in Section 4.)* --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** #### (a) Explain the **Birthday Paradox** and derive the collision probability bound for an $n$-bit hash. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **The Birthday Paradox:** Given a set of $N = 2^n$ possible hash values, the *birthday paradox* states that you need only $\approx 1.177\sqrt{N}$ random samples to have a $> 50\%$ probability of finding two that match. **Derivation:** The probability of **no collision** among $q$ samples is: $$P_{\text{no collision}} = \prod_{i=1}^{q-1}\left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right)$$ Using the inequality $1 - x \leq e^{-x}$: $$P_{\text{no collision}} \leq e^{-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{q-1} i} = e^{-q(q-1)/(2N)}$$ The probability of **at least one collision**: $$P_{\text{collision}} \geq 1 - e^{-q(q-1)/(2N)}$$ Setting $P_{\text{collision}} = 0.5$: $$e^{-q(q-1)/(2N)} = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{q(q-1)}{2N} = \ln 2$$ For large $q$, $q(q-1) \approx q^2$, so: $$q \approx \sqrt{2N \ln 2} \approx 1.177 \sqrt{N} = 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$$ **Application to SHA-256 ($n = 256$):** $$q \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{128} \approx 4 \times 10^{38} \text{ hash evaluations}$$ **Conclusion:** A birthday attack on SHA-256 requires $\sim 2^{128}$ operations — computationally infeasible with current technology. This is why SHA-256 provides 128 bits of collision security. --- #### (b) Describe the **Merkle-Damgård construction**. How does it relate to SHA-256? Why is length-padding important? **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **The Construction:** A fixed-length compression function $f: \{0,1\}^{n+b} \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ is iterated to hash a variable-length message. Given message $m$ split into $k$ blocks $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_k$ of $b$ bits each: $$H_0 = IV, \quad H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i), \quad H(m) = H_k$$ **Relation to SHA-256:** - SHA-256 uses the **Merkle-Damgård construction** with $n = 256$ bits, $b = 512$ bits, and the compression function $f$ being the 64-round function described in 3.1. - The security of SHA-256 *reduces* to the security of its compression function $f$. If an adversary finds a collision in $f$, they automatically find a collision in SHA-256. **Importance of Length-Padding:** The standard Merkle-Damgård construction is **vulnerable to length-extension attacks**: given $H(m)$ and $\text{len}(m)$, an attacker can compute $H(m \Vert M')$ for any $M'$ *without knowing $m$*. **Countermeasure:** Append a block containing the message length at the end: $$H(m) = f(H_{k+1}, \text{len}(m))$$ This is the **Merkle-Damgård strengthening** (also called the "Merkle trick"). SHA-256 implements this by including a 64-bit length field in the final padding block. > [!NOTE] > **Why it matters in blockchain:** Bitcoin's block headers are not padded with the length in this exact way, which is why SHA-256d (double SHA-256) is used — applying SHA-256 twice defeats the length-extension vulnerability and provides additional collision-security margin. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the 6th property (puzzle friendliness).** In blockchain-specific questions, examiners expect the answer to mention *puzzle friendliness* explicitly because it is the property that enables Proof-of-Work mining. Omitting it loses 1–2 marks. > > 2. **Confusing pre-image resistance with collision resistance.** Many students write the same definition for both. Remember: pre-image = *given h, find m*; collision = *find any m₁, m₂ with same hash*. Collision is **easier** for the attacker (birthday bound $2^{n/2}$). > > 3. **Writing $2^n$ for collision complexity.** The correct bound is $2^{n/2}$, **not** $2^n$. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > > 4. **Forgetting to mention the IV in Merkle-Damgård.** The iteration starts at $H_0 = IV$, not $H_0 = 0$. > > 5. **Writing `|x|` for absolute value inside markdown tables.** This breaks the table syntax. Use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX form when typesetting formulas. > > 6. **Not linking the answer to blockchain context.** For PECCT522, every answer on hash functions must end with a **blockchain application sentence** (e.g., "Used in block hashing, Merkle trees, and mining"). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Cryptographic Hash Functions** - **Definition:** $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ — arbitrary input, fixed-length output. - **Standard:** SHA-256 produces **256 bits** = **64 hex characters**. - **Six Properties** (must memorize all): 1. Deterministic 2. Quick computation 3. Pre-image resistance 4. Second pre-image resistance 5. Collision resistance 6. Avalanche effect (+ **puzzle friendliness** for blockchain) - **Birthday Bound:** Collision search needs $\approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$ trials. - **Avalanche Ratio:** $\approx 0.5$ — one input bit change → ~50% output bit change. - **Merkle-Damgård:** $H_0 = IV$, $H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$, final $H(m) = H_k$. - **SHA-256 Internals:** 512-bit blocks, 64 rounds, 8 working variables, message schedule of 64 words. - **Logical functions:** $\text{Ch}(x,y,z)$, $\text{Maj}(x,y,z)$, $\Sigma_0$, $\Sigma_1$, $\sigma_0$, $\sigma_1$. - **Bitcoin uses SHA-256 twice** (SHA-256d) to defeat length-extension attacks. - **Merkle trees** are built bottom-up by hashing pairs of transaction hashes until a single root remains. - **PoW condition:** $H(\text{nonce} \Vert \text{prev\_hash} \Vert \text{merkle\_root}) < T$. - **Memory aid:** "**D**eterminism, **Q**uick, **P**re-image, **S**econd pre-image, **C**ollision, **A**valanche, **P**uzzle" → acronym **DQSCA P** (or recall: *Every Cryptographic Hash Provides Avalanche Properties*). - **In the KTU answer sheet:** Always draw a **block diagram** of the hash pipeline (padding → splitting → compression → output) to score full marks. - **Always end with the blockchain tie-in:** "Cryptographic hash functions are the bedrock of blockchain immutability, enabling tamper-evident block linking, efficient Merkle-tree verification, and fair Proof-of-Work mining."

topics.title: Properties of a Hash Function, Hash Pointer and Merkle Tree
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of a Hash Function, Hash Pointer and Merkle Tree

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cryptographic Hash Function — Formal Definition

A **cryptographic hash function** $H$ is a deterministic mathematical function that maps an input of arbitrary length (a message $M$) drawn from the input space $\{0,1\}^*$ to a fixed-length, bounded-size output (the **digest** or **fingerprint**) belonging to $\{0,1\}^n$, where $n$ is the fixed output bit-length of the function.

Formally, this is expressed as:

$$H : \{0,1\}^{*} \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** In blockchain architectures, the most commonly cited hash function is **SHA-256** (Secure Hash Algorithm — 256 bits), standardized by **NIST** in FIPS PUB 180-4. It always produces an output of exactly **256 bits** ($\mathbf{32}$ **bytes** or **64 hexadecimal characters**), regardless of the size of the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction from Ordinary Hashing:** Unlike a generic hash table hash function (e.g., the modulo operator in an array), a *cryptographic* hash function must satisfy stringent security properties. It is engineered to be **one-way** and **collision-resistant** by design.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Digital Fingerprint"

Imagine every human being on Earth has a unique **fingerprint** — a small, fixed-size identifier that is unique to them, can be generated quickly from their physical presence, but cannot be used to reconstruct the person. A cryptographic hash is the **digital equivalent** of this fingerprint:

- **Your full data** (an entire book, a transaction, or a file of millions of bits) ⇒ the *physical person*.
- **The hash digest** (a short, fixed 256-bit string) ⇒ the *fingerprint*.
- The fingerprint is **unique** to the data; even a single character change yields a completely different fingerprint.
- You cannot reverse-engineer the data from the fingerprint alone.

This is exactly why hashing is the **atomic primitive** of blockchain — every block, transaction, and account address in a blockchain like Bitcoin ultimately relies on the security guarantees of a hash function.

---

## 1.3 The Six Security Properties of a Cryptographic Hash Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These six properties are HIGH-YIELD for KTU examinations.** Memorize both the property name and its precise meaning — direct 3-mark questions are frequently asked on this exact topic.

| # | Property | One-line Definition |
|---|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Deterministic** | The same input message $M$ will *always* produce the *same* hash output $H(M)$. |
| 2 | **Quick Computation** | Given $M$, the value $H(M)$ can be computed in *polynomial time* $O(k)$ for an input of size $k$. |
| 3 | **Pre-image Resistance** | Given a hash $h$, it is *computationally infeasible* to find any $M$ such that $H(M) = h$. |
| 4 | **Second Pre-image Resistance** | Given $M_1$, it is infeasible to find a *different* $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. |
| 5 | **Collision Resistance** | It is computationally infeasible to find *any two distinct* inputs $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. |
| 6 | **Puzzle Friendliness** | For any target output $Y$ from a distribution with high min-entropy, finding an $M$ such that $H(M) = Y$ is no easier than brute-force search. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avalanche Effect (Implicit Property):** Although not always listed as a numbered property, a *good* cryptographic hash function exhibits the **strict avalanche criterion** — flipping a single bit of the input must change approximately **50%** of the bits in the output. This is what makes tampering with data immediately obvious to verifiers.

---

## 1.4 Hash Pointer — Formal Definition

A **Hash Pointer** is an advanced data structure that extends the classical *memory pointer*. Where a regular pointer stores only the **address** of where some data $D$ resides, a hash pointer stores **two** pieces of information:

1. The **address** (location) of data $D$ in memory/storage.
2. The **cryptographic hash** $H(D)$ of that data $D$.

Formally:

$$HP_{D} \;=\; \big\langle \text{addr}(D),\; H(D) \big\rangle$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Role in Blockchain:** A hash pointer is the foundational building block of the **blockchain data structure** itself. Each block contains a hash pointer to the *previous* block — forming the immutable chain. If a malicious actor alters *any* byte of an old block, the stored hash pointer in the next block no longer matches the recomputed hash, exposing the tampering immediately.

---

## 1.5 Merkle Tree — Formal Definition

A **Merkle Tree** (also called a **hash tree** or **binary tree of hashes**) is a tree data structure in which:

- **Every leaf node** holds the cryptographic hash of a data block.
- **Every internal (non-leaf) node** holds the cryptographic hash of the *concatenation* of its two child node hashes.
- The **root node**, called the **Merkle Root**, holds a single hash that *cryptographically summarizes* the entire underlying dataset.

If $L_i$ denotes the $i$-th leaf (data block hash) and $P_{i,j}$ denotes an internal parent, the construction rule is:

$$P_{i,j} \;=\; H\big( L_{2i} \;\Vert\; L_{2i+1} \big)$$

where $\Vert$ denotes **bitwise concatenation**. This rule is applied recursively up the tree until a single root hash is produced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inventor:** Ralph Merkle, **1979** (originally proposed for securing large data structures). **KTU Relevance:** Bitcoin's **SPV (Simplified Payment Verification)** clients rely entirely on Merkle proofs to verify transactions without downloading the full blockchain.

---

## 1.6 Intuitive Analogy — Merkle Tree as a "Recursive Notary"

Think of a Merkle tree as a **recursive notarization system**:

- Suppose there are **4 witnesses** (data blocks) in a room, and you want to certify that all of them are genuine.
- Each witness signs their own statement — this is the **leaf hash**.
- Pair the witnesses: witness-1 and witness-2 sign a *joint* statement about the authenticity of both → **parent hash level 1**.
- Pair the parent statements: a final notary combines the two parent statements into one **master certificate** → the **Merkle root**.
- Now, an outside observer can verify the integrity of *any single witness* by presenting only a small **proof path** (the sibling hashes along the path to the root), without seeing all 4 witnesses.

This logarithmic verification property is what makes Merkle trees indispensable to blockchain scalability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Hash Function

A cryptographic hash function (e.g., **SHA-256**) processes the input message through the following conceptual pipeline:

1. **Padding:** The input $M$ is padded to a length that is a multiple of the block size. For SHA-256, the block size is **512 bits**. Padding appends a `1` bit, followed by `0` bits, followed by a **64-bit big-endian representation of the original message length**.
2. **Parsing:** The padded message is split into $N$ blocks of **512 bits** each: $M^{(1)}, M^{(2)}, \ldots, M^{(N)}$.
3. **Initialization:** An **initial hash value (IV)** is set — for SHA-256, the first 8 hash words are derived from the fractional parts of the square roots of the first 8 primes.
4. **Compression Function:** Each block $M^{(i)}$ is fed into a compression function $C$ along with the current chaining variable $H_{i-1}$:

$$H_i \;=\; C(H_{i-1},\; M^{(i)})$$

5. **Iteration:** Step 4 is repeated for all $N$ blocks.
6. **Output:** The final chaining variable $H_N$ is the hash digest:

$$H(M) \;=\; H_N$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Blockchain:** The *iterative, block-wise* design of SHA-256 means that changing even a single bit in the original message completely changes the padding boundary, the parsed block structure, and hence every subsequent chaining variable — producing a wildly different final hash. This is the **avalanche effect** in action.

---

## 2.2 The Three Security Properties — Deeper Cryptographic Reasoning

### Pre-image Resistance (One-Wayness)
Given only $h = H(M)$, the only known method to find *any* $M'$ with $H(M') = h$ is brute-force search across the entire $2^n$ possible inputs. For SHA-256, $n = 256$, so the search space is $2^{256}$ — a number so vast that all the computing power on Earth would take longer than the age of the universe to exhaust it.

> **Engineering Implication:** This is what makes Bitcoin mining "puzzle-friendly" — miners must perform massive brute-force work to find a nonce producing a hash below a target.

### Collision Resistance
By the **Birthday Paradox**, a brute-force search for *any collision* requires approximately $2^{n/2}$ attempts (not $2^n$). For SHA-256, this is $2^{128}$ operations — still infeasible with current technology. This is what makes digital signatures safe from forgery: a malicious signer cannot construct two different messages that produce the same hash.

### Puzzle Friendliness
This property is the *foundation of Proof-of-Work (PoW)*. Given a target $T$ (e.g., a hash starting with a fixed number of zeros), there is no shortcut to finding a *nonce* $\eta$ such that $H(\eta \Vert M) < T$. Miners must therefore engage in **brute-force random trial and error**.

---

## 2.3 Hash Pointer — Why It Is Tamper-Evident

Consider a singly linked list of blocks $B_1 \rightarrow B_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow B_n$ where each block stores a **regular pointer** to the previous block. A malicious node can:
- Modify block $B_1$'s data.
- Update the pointer in $B_2$ to point to the new location of $B_1$.
- **No one can detect the modification**, because the link is still valid.

Now replace each pointer with a **hash pointer**. Each block $B_i$ stores:

$$\text{hashPtr}_i \;=\; \big\langle \text{addr}(B_{i-1}),\; H(B_{i-1}) \big\rangle$$

If the malicious node modifies $B_1$, the recomputed hash $H(B_1^{\text{modified}})$ will *not* match the stored $H(B_1)$ inside $B_2$. The tampering is **immediately exposed** by simple hash recomputation. This is the cryptographic primitive underlying **block immutability**.

---

## 2.4 Merkle Tree — The Logarithmic Proof Property

A **Merkle Proof** (also called a **Merkle Path** or **Authentication Path**) is the minimal set of sibling hashes required to recompute the Merkle root from a given data block.

For a tree with $N$ data blocks:
- **Full tree size** (number of nodes): $\approx 2N$
- **Proof size**: $\log_2 N$ hashes
- **Verification cost**: $\log_2 N$ hash operations

This gives the **O(log N)** verification property that is central to Bitcoin's SPV (light client) design.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization for KTU Board Exams.** The following table is the high-density cheat sheet for this topic.

| # | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|------------------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | $H : \{0,1\}^{*} \to \{0,1\}^{n}$ | Hash function signature | $n = 256$ for SHA-256 |
| 2 | $H(M)$ | Hash digest of message $M$ | Output: 256 bits = 32 bytes = 64 hex chars |
| 3 | $\text{HP}_{D} = \langle \text{addr}(D),\; H(D) \rangle$ | Hash pointer to data $D$ | Pointer = 2-tuple |
| 4 | $P_{i,j} = H(L_{2i} \Vert L_{2i+1})$ | Merkle internal node construction | Concatenation of two child hashes |
| 5 | Proof size $= \log_{2} N$ | Merkle proof length | For $N = 1024$ blocks → **10 hashes** |
| 6 | Brute-force pre-image search cost | $\approx 2^{n}$ operations | $2^{256}$ for SHA-256 |
| 7 | Birthday-collision search cost | $\approx 2^{n/2}$ operations | $2^{128}$ for SHA-256 |
| 8 | Avalanche criterion | $\approx 50\%$ bits flip per 1-bit input change | Empirical target |
| 9 | SHA-256 block size | Internal compression block | **512 bits** |
| 10 | SHA-256 output size | Final digest length | **256 bits** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** When writing absolute values in your answer scripts, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never the vertical pipe character `|` — to avoid breaking the markdown table structure during PDF conversion.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Hashing/Hash Pointer/Merkle Tree |
|--------|-------------------------------------------------|
| **Blockchain** | Block linking, transaction integrity, Merkle root in block headers |
| **Password Storage** | Servers store $H(\text{password})$ instead of plaintext |
| **Git Version Control** | Every commit is identified by its SHA-1 hash |
| **File Integrity (P2P)** | Torrent magnet links use Merkle hashes for chunk verification |
| **Digital Certificates (PKI)** | Certificate Transparency logs use Merkle trees (RFC 6962) |
| **IPFS / Dat / Git** | Content-Addressable Storage: address = hash of content |
| **Message Authentication Codes** | HMAC uses an underlying hash function for integrity |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations

### Derivation 1: Hash Pointer Tamper-Detection Property

**Statement:** If a hash pointer $\text{HP}_{D} = \langle \text{addr}(D), H(D) \rangle$ is stored in block $B_{i+1}$ and an attacker modifies $D$ to $D'$, then the tamper is detectable by a verifier.

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } & D = \text{original data} \\
\text{Let } & D' = D \oplus \Delta \quad \text{(bitwise modification)} \\
\text{Stored in } B_{i+1}: & \quad \text{hashPtr} = \langle \text{addr}(D),\; H(D) \rangle \\
\text{Verifier recomputes: } & \quad h' = H(D') \\
\text{Verification condition: } & \quad h' \stackrel{?}{=} H(D)
\end{aligned}
$$

By the **avalanche property**, since $D' \neq D$, we have $H(D') \neq H(D)$ with overwhelming probability. Hence the tamper is detected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probability of evasion:** The probability that $H(D') = H(D)$ is $\tfrac{1}{2^{n}}$. For SHA-256, this is $\tfrac{1}{2^{256}}$, which is effectively zero.

---

### Derivation 2: Merkle Root Computation from Leaves

**Statement:** Given $N = 4$ data blocks $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$, derive the Merkle root $R$ in terms of the leaf hashes.

**Step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 (Leaf hashes): } & L_1 = H(D_1),\; L_2 = H(D_2),\; L_3 = H(D_3),\; L_4 = H(D_4) \\
\text{Step 2 (Level-1 internal nodes): } & P_{1,1} = H(L_1 \Vert L_2) \\
 & P_{1,2} = H(L_3 \Vert L_4) \\
\text{Step 3 (Merkle Root): } & R = H(P_{1,1} \Vert P_{1,2}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting the leaves:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R = H\bigg( H\big(H(D_1) \Vert H(D_2)\big) \;\Vert\; H\big(H(D_3) \Vert H(D_4)\big) \bigg)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** A change in *any single* bit of $D_1$ will cascade up the tree and change $R$ — this is the cryptographic aggregation property of the Merkle root.

---

### Derivation 3: Merkle Proof Verification Complexity

**Statement:** Show that verifying the inclusion of a single data block in a Merkle tree of $N$ blocks requires exactly $\log_2 N$ hash operations and $\log_2 N$ sibling-hash disclosures.

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } h = & \text{height of the tree (root at level } h, \text{ leaves at level } 0) \\
\text{Number of leaves: } & N = 2^{h} \\
\Rightarrow h = & \log_2 N
\end{aligned}
$$

To verify the inclusion of a leaf $L_i$:
- At each of the $h$ levels, the verifier needs to compute one parent hash from a known child and one sibling hash.
- The sibling hash is the only extra information required from the prover.

$$
\boxed{\text{Verification operations} = h = \log_2 N}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $N = 1024$ transactions, $\log_2(1024) = 10$. So a light client needs only **10 hashes** to prove inclusion of any one transaction — versus having to download all 1024 transactions.

---

### Derivation 4: Birthday Bound for Collision Attacks

**Statement:** Compute the expected number of trials $T$ to find a collision in an $n$-bit hash function using the Birthday Paradox.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total possible outputs: } & K = 2^{n} \\
\text{After sampling } T \text{ inputs, prob. of no collision: } & \approx e^{-T(T-1) / (2K)} \\
\text{Setting collision probability} = 0.5: \quad & e^{-T^2 / (2K)} = 0.5 \\
\Rightarrow T^{2} / (2K) = & \ln 2 \\
\Rightarrow T = & \sqrt{2 (\ln 2) \cdot K} \approx 1.177 \sqrt{K} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For SHA-256 ($n = 256$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
K = 2^{256} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^{256}} = 1.177 \cdot 2^{128}
\end{aligned}
$$

So a collision attack on SHA-256 requires approximately $2^{128}$ operations — infeasible.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Property-by-Property Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield comparison for KTU 14-mark questions.** This table consolidates the three primitives and shows their relationship.

| Aspect | Hash Function | Hash Pointer | Merkle Tree |
|--------|---------------|--------------|-------------|
| **Fundamental primitive?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ Built on top of a hash function | ❌ Built on top of a hash function |
| **What is hashed?** | The data itself | The *previous* data block (in a chain) | Both leaf data blocks and internal parent concatenations |
| **Output size** | Fixed (e.g., 256 bits for SHA-256) | Tuple of (address, hash) | A single Merkle root hash |
| **Tamper detection** | One-level (data → digest) | Sequential (chain → detects any past edit) | Aggregate (single root = integrity of all leaves) |
| **Data structure type** | Function / algorithm | Pointer variant | Binary tree |
| **Time complexity of verification** | $O(k)$ to compute $H(M)$ | $O(1)$ to recompute stored hash | $O(\log N)$ to verify inclusion of one leaf |
| **Space complexity** | $O(n)$ for one digest | $O(1)$ extra per block | $O(2N)$ hashes for $N$ leaves |
| **Primary use in Bitcoin** | Mining, address generation, PoW | Block chaining | SPV wallets, transaction Merkle root |
| **Inventor / Year** | NIST (SHA), Merkle-Damgård construction (1978–1989) | Haber & Stornetta (1991) | Ralph Merkle (1979) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (e.g., `blockN1`, `leafA`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels are clean uppercase text without any markdown formatting tags.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Structure of a Hash Pointer

```mermaid
graph LR
    blockN1["BLOCK I PLUS 1"] --> ptrStruct["HASH POINTER STRUCTURE"]
    ptrStruct --> addr["ADDRESS OF BLOCK I"] 
    ptrStruct --> hashFld["HASH OF BLOCK I CONTENT H B I"]
    hashFld --> prev1["H B I = H TRANSACTIONS PREV HASH NONCE"]
    prev1 --> noteA["STORED FOR VERIFYING INTEGRITY OF PREVIOUS BLOCK"]

    style blockN1 fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style ptrStruct fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style addr fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style hashFld fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style prev1 fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#000000
    style noteA fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#000000
```

> **Reading guide:** The diagram shows that inside each blockchain block, the *hash pointer* is a composite of (1) the address of the previous block and (2) the cryptographic hash of the previous block's content.

---

## 4.2 Schematic — SHA-256 Iterative Hashing Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    inp1["INPUT MESSAGE M"] --> pad1["PADDING TO 512 BIT MULTIPLE"]
    pad1 --> split1["SPLIT INTO 512 BIT BLOCKS M1 M2 ... MN"]
    split1 --> iv1["INITIAL HASH VALUE IV"]
    iv1 --> comp1["COMPRESSION FN C WITH M1"]
    comp1 --> h1["CHAINING VAR H1"]
    h1 --> comp2["COMPRESSION FN C WITH M2"]
    comp2 --> h2["CHAINING VAR H2"]
    h2 --> compN["CONTINUE FOR ALL BLOCKS"]
    compN --> hn["FINAL HASH H M = HN"]
    hn --> out1["256 BIT DIGEST"]

    style inp1 fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style out1 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style hn fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style iv1 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style comp1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style comp2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style compN fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.3 Merkle Tree Architecture with Four Leaves

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1["MERKLE ROOT R"] --> p11["P1 = H L1 LL2"]
    root1 --> p12["P2 = H L3 LL4"]
    p11 --> leafA["L1 = H D1"]
    p11 --> leafB["L2 = H D2"]
    p12 --> leafC["L3 = H D3"]
    p12 --> leafD["L4 = H D4"]

    style root1 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style p11 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style p12 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style leafA fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style leafB fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style leafC fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style leafD fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading guide:** The **red root** is the Merkle root — a single 256-bit hash that cryptographically summarizes all four data blocks $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$. Tampering with *any* $D_i$ changes the corresponding leaf, then cascades up to change the root.

---

## 4.4 Merkle Proof Path for Verifying $D_2$ (Orange Path = Sibling Hashes Required)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root2["MERKLE ROOT R"] --> p21["P1 = H L1 LL2"]
    root2 --> p22["P2 = H L3 LL4"]
    p21 --> leafE["L1 = H D1"]
    p21 --> leafF["L2 = H D2 TARGET"]
    p22 --> leafG["L3 = H D3"]
    p22 --> leafH["L4 = H D4"]

    leafF -. PROOF STEP 1 COMPUTE P1 .-> p21
    p21 -. PROOF STEP 2 NEED P2 .-> p22
    p22 -. PROOF STEP 3 RECOMPUTE R .-> root2

    style root2 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style p21 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style p22 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style leafF fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style leafE fill:#94a3b8,stroke:#64748b,color:#ffffff
    style leafG fill:#94a3b8,stroke:#64748b,color:#ffffff
    style leafH fill:#94a3b8,stroke:#64748b,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading guide:** To prove $D_2$ is included, a verifier needs only: $L_1$ (sibling of target), $P_2$ (sibling subtree hash), and the known Merkle root. Total proof size: $\log_2 4 = 2$ hashes.

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Blockchain Block

```mermaid
graph TB
    blkHdr["BLOCK HEADER"] --> mroot["MERKLE ROOT OF TRANSACTIONS"]
    blkHdr --> prevHash["PREVIOUS BLOCK HASH HASH POINTER"]
    blkHdr --> ts["TIMESTAMP"]
    blkHdr --> nonce["NONCE PROOF OF WORK"]
    blkHdr --> diff["DIFFICULTY TARGET"]

    mroot --> txTree["MERKLE TREE OF TX1 TX2 ... TXN"]
    prevHash --> prevBlk["LINK TO PREVIOUS BLOCK IN CHAIN"]
    nonce --> pow["MINED VIA BRUTE FORCE HASH SEARCH"]

    style blkHdr fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style mroot fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style prevHash fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style pow fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three primitives in one block:** A single Bitcoin block simultaneously uses *all three* primitives covered in this topic: a **hash function** (SHA-256 for mining & tx IDs), a **hash pointer** (the prev-block link), and a **Merkle tree** (the transaction Merkle root).

---
