# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Internal Assessment & University Exam Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme).** Each question is worth **3 marks** and demands a precise 3–4 sentence answer.

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**List and briefly explain any three properties of a cryptographic hash function.**

**Model Answer:**

A cryptographic hash function $H$ must satisfy the following essential properties:

1. **Deterministic:** The same input message $M$ will always produce the same output hash $H(M)$. This is critical for verifiability — any node must be able to recompute the hash and obtain the same digest.

2. **Pre-image Resistance (One-Wayness):** Given a hash value $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find any input $M$ such that $H(M) = h$. For SHA-256, this requires brute-force search over $2^{256}$ possibilities, which is impractical.

3. **Collision Resistance:** It is computationally infeasible to find two distinct inputs $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. By the Birthday Paradox, a brute-force search requires $\approx 2^{n/2}$ operations (i.e., $2^{128}$ for SHA-256), which remains infeasible with current computing power.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per property stated + 1 mark for accurate explanation of the third.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**What is a hash pointer? How does it differ from an ordinary pointer?**

**Model Answer:**

An **ordinary pointer** is a data structure that stores only the **memory address** of the location where some data $D$ is stored. It provides no integrity guarantee — a malicious actor can change the data at that address, and the pointer alone cannot detect it.

A **hash pointer** extends this concept by storing *two* pieces of information: (i) the memory address of data $D$, and (ii) the **cryptographic hash** $H(D)$ of that data. Formally, $\text{HP}_D = \langle \text{addr}(D), H(D) \rangle$.

The key difference is **tamper-evidence**: if the data $D$ is modified to $D'$, the recomputed hash $H(D')$ will not match the stored $H(D)$, immediately revealing the modification. This is the cryptographic primitive that gives blockchains their **immutability** — each block in Bitcoin stores a hash pointer to the previous block.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for difference, 1 for immutability application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions in University End-Semester Examinations (ESE) offer an **internal choice** between two full 14-mark questions. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts **(a)** and **(b)**. Both choices must be solved in your *self-evaluation*; the model solution for *only the selected question* is required in the exam script.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the six essential security properties of a cryptographic hash function. State the standard output size of SHA-256.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the structure and construction of a **Merkle Tree**. Explain how a **Merkle proof** is used to verify the inclusion of a single data block in logarithmic time.

---

### Model Answer for Question B-A

#### Part (a) — Six Security Properties of a Cryptographic Hash Function

The six security properties of a cryptographic hash function $H$ are:

1. **Deterministic:** The function $H$ is a pure mathematical function. For any given input $M$, the output $H(M)$ is *always* the same — no randomness or state is involved. This property enables deterministic verification across distributed nodes.

2. **Quick Computation:** Given an input $M$ of length $k$ bits, the hash $H(M)$ can be computed in time polynomial in $k$, typically $O(k)$. This is essential for high-throughput blockchain operations such as transaction hashing and block mining.

3. **Pre-image Resistance:** Given only a hash output $h = H(M)$, it is *computationally infeasible* to determine *any* pre-image $M'$ such that $H(M') = h$. The best-known attack is brute-force enumeration over the $2^n$ possible inputs, which is infeasible for $n \ge 256$.

4. **Second Pre-image Resistance:** Given a specific input $M_1$, it is computationally infeasible to find a *different* input $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. This property protects against forgery where a malicious party tries to substitute one document for another.

5. **Collision Resistance:** It is computationally infeasible to find *any two distinct* inputs $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. This is the *strictest* of the three resistance properties.

6. **Puzzle Friendliness:** For any target value $Y$ drawn from a high min-entropy distribution, finding an input $M$ such that $H(M) = Y$ has no shortcut — it requires brute-force search. This is the foundation of Bitcoin's **Proof-of-Work** mining puzzle.

> The standard output size of **SHA-256** is **256 bits** (= 32 bytes = 64 hexadecimal characters).

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 1 mark each for the 6 properties (6 marks) + 1 mark for SHA-256 output size.

---

#### Part (b) — Merkle Tree Structure and Logarithmic Merkle Proof

A **Merkle Tree** is a binary tree in which:
- Each **leaf node** $L_i$ stores the hash of a data block: $L_i = H(D_i)$.
- Each **internal node** $P_{i,j}$ stores the hash of the concatenation of its two child hashes: $P_{i,j} = H(L_{2i} \Vert L_{2i+1})$.
- The **root** $R$ is the single topmost hash, representing the integrity of the *entire* dataset.

**Construction (4 data blocks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 = H(D_1),\; L_2 = H(D_2),\; L_3 = H(D_3),\; L_4 = H(D_4) \\
P_1 = H(L_1 \Vert L_2),\; P_2 = H(L_3 \Vert L_4) \\
R = H(P_1 \Vert P_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.3):**

```
                  R  (Merkle Root)
                /   \
              P1     P2
             / \    / \
            L1 L2  L3 L4
            |  |   |  |
            D1 D2  D3 D4
```

**Merkle Proof Procedure** (verifying inclusion of $D_2$):

1. Compute the leaf hash: $L_2 = H(D_2)$.
2. The prover discloses the sibling hash $L_1$.
3. Verifier computes $P_1 = H(L_1 \Vert L_2)$.
4. The prover discloses the sibling subtree hash $P_2$.
5. Verifier computes $R' = H(P_1 \Vert P_2)$ and checks whether $R' = R$ (the known trusted Merkle root).

**Logarithmic Complexity:** For a tree with $N$ leaves of height $h = \log_2 N$:
- **Hashes disclosed (proof size):** $h = \log_2 N$
- **Hash operations performed by verifier:** $h = \log_2 N$

For $N = 1024$ transactions, only $10$ hash operations are needed — a massive improvement over downloading and verifying all $1024$ transactions. This logarithmic property is what makes **SPV (Simplified Payment Verification)** wallets possible in Bitcoin.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 2 marks for the construction steps and definitions.
- 2 marks for the diagram / hierarchical structure.
- 2 marks for the Merkle proof verification procedure.
- 1 mark for logarithmic complexity explanation.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a **hash pointer**. Using a 3-block chain diagram, show how hash pointers create an immutable linked list. Explain the cryptographic property that detects tampering.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare a **hash function**, a **hash pointer**, and a **Merkle tree** in terms of (i) input/output structure, (ii) data-structure type, (iii) tamper-detection capability, and (iv) time complexity. Use a comparison table.

---

### Model Answer for Question B-B

#### Part (a) — Hash Pointer and the 3-Block Immutable Chain

A **hash pointer** is a 2-tuple $\text{HP}_D = \langle \text{addr}(D), H(D) \rangle$ that contains:
- The address of data $D$, and
- The cryptographic hash $H(D)$ of that data.

**3-Block Chain Diagram:**

```
   +-----------+         +-----------+         +-----------+
   |  Block 3  |         |  Block 2  |         |  Block 1  |
   |  (Latest) |         |           |         |  (Oldest) |
   +-----------+         +-----------+         +-----------+
   | Data: D3  |         | Data: D2  |         | Data: D1  |
   | HP-->B2:  |-------->| HP-->B1:  |-------->| (Genesis) |
   | addr(B2)  |         | addr(B1)  |         |           |
   | H(D2)     |         | H(D1)     |         |           |
   +-----------+         +-----------+         +-----------+
```

**Cryptographic Tamper-Detection Property:**

Suppose an attacker modifies $D_1$ in Block 1 to $D_1'$. The attacker must also update the hash pointer in Block 2 to hide the change. However, doing so would *also* invalidate the hash pointer in Block 3 (since $H(D_2)$ is now over a tampered chain), forcing a cascade of edits to *every* subsequent block — which is computationally infeasible if Proof-of-Work is enforced.

Formally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stored in } B_2: & \quad H(D_1) \\
\text{Recomputed after tamper: } & \quad H(D_1') \neq H(D_1) \\
\text{Therefore: } & \quad \text{Mismatch detected} \Rightarrow \text{Tamper exposed}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the cryptographic reason why **Bitcoin's blockchain is immutable**: a single-byte change to a 2013 block would require re-mining every block from 2013 to the present, demanding astronomical computational work.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 1 mark for hash pointer definition.
- 2 marks for the diagram and labeling.
- 2 marks for the tamper-detection explanation.
- 2 marks for the immutability/Proof-of-Work argument.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table: Hash Function vs Hash Pointer vs Merkle Tree

| Aspect | Hash Function | Hash Pointer | Merkle Tree |
|--------|---------------|--------------|-------------|
| **(i) Input / Output** | Variable-length input → fixed 256-bit output | Address (input) + Hash digest (output) | Variable leaf data → single fixed 256-bit root hash |
| **(ii) Data Structure Type** | Mathematical function / algorithm | Pointer variant (tuple) | Binary tree (recursive) |
| **(iii) Tamper-Detection Capability** | One-level: detects change in $M$ by recomputing $H(M)$ | Sequential: detects any change in *any* block of the chain (by hash-pointer cascade) | Aggregate: a single root change signals tampering in *any* of $N$ leaves |
| **(iv) Time Complexity** | Compute $H(M)$: $O(k)$ for $k$-bit input | Store / verify pointer: $O(1)$ | Verify inclusion of one leaf: $O(\log N)$ for $N$ leaves |
| **Storage Overhead** | $n$ bits (one digest) | $O(1)$ extra per block (address + hash) | $O(2N)$ hashes for $N$ leaves |
| **Role in Bitcoin** | Mining, address derivation, transaction IDs | Block chaining (each header has prev-block hash) | Merkle root in block header, SPV wallets |

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- 1.5 marks per row of the comparison table (4 rows = 6 marks).
- 1 mark for the Bitcoin-context application row.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing pre-image resistance with collision resistance.** Pre-image resistance is about inverting a *given* hash. Collision resistance is about finding *any* two inputs with the same hash. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the Birthday Bound.** Many students say "collision resistance requires $2^n$ trials" — **WRONG.** The correct complexity is $2^{n/2}$ by the Birthday Paradox. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
>
> 3. **Writing `$\vert x \vert$` as `|x|`** in LaTeX inside markdown tables — this breaks the table parser. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.
>
> 4. **Drawing the Merkle tree with the root at the bottom.** The convention in KTU/Kerala textbooks is **root at the top, leaves at the bottom**. Drawing it inverted costs presentation marks.
>
> 5. **Omitting the SHA-256 output size (256 bits = 32 bytes = 64 hex chars).** Examiners expect all three equivalent forms.
>
> 6. **Stating only 3 of the 6 hash properties** when the question asks for "the security properties" (plural, comprehensive). Always list all 6.
>
> 7. **Confusing a Merkle proof with a Merkle tree.** A *tree* is the full data structure. A *proof* is the minimal set of sibling hashes along the authentication path from a leaf to the root.
>
> 8. **Not connecting the primitives to blockchain** — KTU examiners reward applied understanding. Always end with a sentence linking the concept back to Bitcoin/blockchain immutability.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**

### 🔑 Core Definitions (3-marks ready)
- **Cryptographic Hash Function:** $H : \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^n$ — maps arbitrary input to fixed-size output.
- **SHA-256 output size:** 256 bits = 32 bytes = 64 hex characters.
- **Hash Pointer:** $\text{HP}_D = \langle \text{addr}(D), H(D) \rangle$ — pointer + integrity hash.
- **Merkle Tree:** Binary tree of hashes; root represents integrity of all data blocks.
- **Merkle Root:** Single hash at the top of the Merkle tree summarizing all leaves.
- **Merkle Proof:** Sibling hashes along the path from a target leaf to the root.

### 🔑 The Six Properties (Always list all 6)
1. Deterministic
2. Quick Computation
3. Pre-image Resistance
4. Second Pre-image Resistance
5. Collision Resistance
6. Puzzle Friendliness
- (Plus the **Avalanche Effect** as a behavioral expectation.)

### 🔑 Critical Complexity Values (Memorize!)
- **Pre-image attack cost:** $2^n = 2^{256}$ for SHA-256.
- **Collision attack cost (Birthday):** $2^{n/2} = 2^{128}$ for SHA-256.
- **Merkle proof size:** $\log_2 N$ hashes for $N$ leaves.
- **SHA-256 internal block size:** 512 bits.

### 🔑 Key Equations (Write in your answer script)
- $R = H\big( H(L_1 \Vert L_2) \Vert H(L_3 \Vert L_4) \big)$ — Merkle root for 4 leaves.
- $h = \log_2 N$ — Height of a balanced Merkle tree with $N$ leaves.
- $H_i = C(H_{i-1}, M^{(i)})$ — SHA-256 iterative compression.
- $\text{HP}_D = \langle \text{addr}(D), H(D) \rangle$ — Hash pointer definition.

### 🔑 Block Diagram of a Blockchain Block (must include)
- **Block Header:** Merkle Root + Previous Block Hash (Hash Pointer) + Timestamp + Nonce + Difficulty.
- **Block Body:** List of transactions (leaves of the Merkle tree).
- **Genesis Block:** The only block with a null previous-hash pointer.

### 🔑 Key Historical Facts (1-mark bonus questions)
- **Ralph Merkle** invented the Merkle tree in **1979**.
- **SHA-256** is standardized by **NIST** in **FIPS PUB 180-4**.
- **Haber & Stornetta** (1991) proposed hash-pointer-based timestamping — the conceptual ancestor of blockchain.
- **Bitcoin** uses **double SHA-256** (SHA-256 applied twice) for block hashing.

### 🔑 Three Primitives — One Unified Picture
| Primitive | Used in Bitcoin for |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Hash Function | Mining (PoW), address generation, tx ID, block ID |
| Hash Pointer | Linking each block to the previous one (chain) |
| Merkle Tree | Summarizing all transactions in a block header |

> 🎯 **Final Tip:** A single KTU 14-mark question can ask you to compare all three primitives in a table — practice drawing the Merkle tree + chain of hash pointers on a single page and labeling how they interact inside a Bitcoin block.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Basic Cryptocurrency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** A Basic Cryptocurrency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cryptocurrency** is a **decentralized, cryptographically secured digital medium of exchange** that operates on a distributed ledger (typically a blockchain), where transaction validity, coin ownership, and supply control are enforced through **cryptographic primitives** (hash functions, digital signatures, Merkle trees) and a **distributed consensus protocol**, eliminating the need for a central issuing or clearing authority.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT522 – Module 2)**, *"A Basic Cryptocurrency"* is introduced as the canonical reference model — analogous to **Bitcoin's UTXO-based architecture** — used to teach the **end-to-end transaction lifecycle** from coin creation (minting) to transfer, validation, and ledger inclusion.

The **three pillars** of any basic cryptocurrency are:

1. **Decentralized Ledger** – A replicated, append-only chain of blocks distributed across peers.
2. **Asymmetric Cryptography** – Public/private key pairs to prove ownership.
3. **Consensus Protocol** – A mechanism (e.g., Proof-of-Work) to agree on the global state.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Transparent Classroom Ledger"**
>
> Imagine a classroom where there is **no teacher** to manage money. Every student has a **transparent notebook (the blockchain)** where they record: *"Alice gave 5 tokens to Bob."* Each entry is **signed by Alice using a unique fingerprint that only she can produce** (digital signature). Before adding a new entry, students solve a **tough math puzzle** (Proof-of-Work) so no one can spam fake entries. Once recorded, the entry is **visible to all and cannot be erased**. This notebook is cryptocurrency in essence.

### 1.3 Core Entities in a Basic Cryptocurrency

| Entity | Role | Cryptographic Anchor |
|---|---|---|
| **Coin** | Unit of value, owned by an address | Linked to public key hash |
| **Transaction (Tx)** | Transfer of coin ownership between addresses | Signed with sender's private key |
| **Block** | Batched set of transactions, chained via hash | Header linked to previous block hash |
| **Wallet** | Software storing keys; signs transactions | Owns private/public key pair |
| **Miner / Validator** | Entity that bundles txs into blocks | Spends compute (PoW) or stake (PoS) |
| **Merkle Tree** | Compact summary of all txs in a block | Single root hash in block header |

### 1.4 The Double-Spending Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A digital file can be copied infinitely. **Double-spending** is the risk that the *same coin* is spent twice. A basic cryptocurrency solves this by making the **entire transaction history publicly auditable** and requiring a **majority of nodes** to agree on which transaction came first.

- **Standard metrics in KTU syllabus:**
  - **Block Time** ≈ **10 minutes** (Bitcoin reference)
  - **Block Size Limit** ≈ **1 MB** (classic Bitcoin)
  - **Total Supply Cap** (e.g., **21 million BTC** in Bitcoin)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Building Blocks of a Basic Cryptocurrency

The basic cryptocurrency can be decomposed into **seven interdependent layers**. Each layer is explained in plain language for intuition.

1. **Cryptographic Hash Function (SHA-256 in Bitcoin)**
   - **Why:** Produces a unique, fixed-size "fingerprint" of any input. A tiny change in input completely changes the hash.
   - **How:** Used to chain blocks, build Merkle trees, and derive addresses.
   - **Property:** *Pre-image resistance, collision resistance, avalanche effect.*

2. **Asymmetric Key Cryptography (ECDSA – secp256k1)**
   - **Why:** Allows a user to prove ownership of coins without revealing their secret key.
   - **How:** Sender signs transaction with **private key (k_priv)**; anyone verifies with **public key (k_pub)**.

3. **Transactions (Tx)**
   - **Why:** Records the transfer of coins from input(s) to output(s).
   - **How:** Consists of **inputs** (referencing previous unspent outputs), **outputs** (specifying new owner + amount), and a **signature**.

4. **Blocks and the Blockchain**
   - **Why:** Batches transactions into an immutable, time-stamped container.
   - **How:** Each block header contains the **previous block's hash**, **Merkle root**, **timestamp**, **nonce**, and **difficulty target**.

5. **Merkle Tree**
   - **Why:** Allows efficient, secure summarization of all transactions in a block using a single root hash.
   - **How:** Pairs of transaction hashes are hashed iteratively until a single root remains.

6. **Proof-of-Work (PoW) Consensus**
   - **Why:** Makes block creation computationally expensive, deterring spam and double-spends.
   - **How:** Miners search for a **nonce** such that the block header hash is **below a target threshold**.

7. **P2P Network & Longest Chain Rule**
   - **Why:** Ensures every honest node converges to the same ledger.
   - **How:** Nodes accept the **chain with the most cumulative work** as the canonical truth.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table lists every **high-yield equation** for this topic. Memorize the structure, not just the symbols.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SHA-256 Hash** | $H = \text{SHA256}(m)$ | $m$ = input message (bits), $H$ = 256-bit digest | Block chaining, address generation |
| 2 | **Address Derivation** | $A = \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(k_{pub}))$ | $k_{pub}$ = public key, $A$ = 160-bit address | Wallet identification |
| 3 | **Digital Signature (ECDSA)** | $(r, s) = \text{Sign}(k_{priv}, H(m))$ | $k_{priv}$ = private key, $H(m)$ = msg hash | Transaction authenticity |
| 4 | **Signature Verification** | $u_1 = s^{-1} \cdot H(m) \pmod{n}$ ; $u_2 = s^{-1} \cdot r \pmod{n}$ | $n$ = curve order, $r,s$ = sig pair | Ownership proof |
| 5 | **Merkle Root** | $H_{\text{root}} = H(H(T_1 \vert\vert T_2) \vert\vert H(T_3 \vert\vert T_4))$ | $T_i$ = tx hash | Compact tx commitment |
| 6 | **Proof-of-Work Condition** | $H(B_{\text{header}}) < T$ | $T$ = target value | Difficulty enforcement |
| 7 | **Difficulty – Target** | $T = T_{\max} / D$ | $D$ = difficulty, $T_{\max} = 2^{224}$ | Adjustable mining cost |
| 8 | **Expected Nonces (PoW)** | $E[\text{trials}] = 2^{256} / T$ | Trials per solution | Mining hardware design |
| 9 | **Block Hash Computation** | $H_{block} = H(H_{prev} \vert\vert H_{\text{root}} \vert\vert t \vert\vert n \vert\vert d)$ | $H_{prev}$, $H_{\text{root}}$, $t$ = timestamp, $n$ = nonce, $d$ = difficulty | Block identification |
| 10 | **Transaction Fee** | $\text{Fee} = \sum \text{Inputs} - \sum \text{Outputs}$ | Amounts in base currency | Miner incentive |
| 11 | **Total Supply (Bitcoin)** | $S_{\text{max}} = 21 \times 10^{6}$ BTC | Halving every 210,000 blocks | Monetary policy |
| 12 | **Block Reward (Bitcoin)** | $R_b = 50 \cdot 2^{-\lfloor h/210000 \rfloor}$ | $h$ = current block height | Miner subsidy |
| 13 | **Hash Rate vs. Block Time** | $T_{block} \propto D / \text{HashRate}$ | Proportionality constant | Network stability |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & FinTech:** Eliminates **intermediary clearing houses**, reducing settlement time from T+2 days to minutes.
- **Supply Chain:** Each "coin" can represent a **physical asset token** (e.g., a barrel of oil) for traceability.
- **IoT Security:** Lightweight PoW enables **device-level micropayments** in machine-to-machine economies.
- **Identity Management:** Public-key cryptography underpins **self-sovereign identity (SSI)** systems.
- **Auditing & Forensics:** All transactions are **permanently auditable**, useful for compliance in regulated industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a KTU board answer, always state the **three security properties** explicitly: **(i) Confidentiality (via hashing), (ii) Integrity (via signatures + Merkle trees), (iii) Non-repudiation (via ECDSA)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Merkle Root Construction (for 4 Transactions)

**Problem:** Compute the Merkle root for 4 transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ with SHA-256 as the hash function.

**Step 1 – Compute transaction-level hashes.**
$$H_1 = \text{SHA256}(T_1), \quad H_2 = \text{SHA256}(T_2), \quad H_3 = \text{SHA256}(T_3), \quad H_4 = \text{SHA256}(T_4)$$
*This step converts each variable-length transaction into a fixed 256-bit hash digest.*

**Step 2 – Form the first level of internal nodes by concatenating pairs.**
$$\text{Node}_{12} = H_1 \vert\vert H_2, \quad \text{Node}_{34} = H_3 \vert\vert H_4$$
*Concatenation doubles the bit-length (512 bits total) before re-hashing.*

**Step 3 – Hash the concatenated pairs to get the second level.**
$$H_{12} = \text{SHA256}(H_1 \vert\vert H_2), \quad H_{34} = \text{SHA256}(H_3 \vert\vert H_4)$$
*Each result is again 256 bits; these are the Merkle "branches".*

**Step 4 – Concatenate the two second-level nodes.**
$$\text{Node}_{\text{root}} = H_{12} \vert\vert H_{34}$$

**Step 5 – Compute the Merkle root.**
$$H_{\text{root}} = \text{SHA256}(H_{12} \vert\vert H_{34})$$
*The single 256-bit $H_{\text{root}}$ uniquely represents all 4 transactions. Any change in $T_i$ propagates up and alters $H_{\text{root}}$.*

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – Proof-of-Work Difficulty Check

**Problem:** Given a target $T$ and the SHA-256 hash of a block header $H(B_{\text{header}})$, verify whether the PoW is valid.

**Step 1 – Compute the block header hash.**
$$H(B_{\text{header}}) = \text{SHA256}\Big(\text{SHA256}(H_{prev} \vert\vert H_{\text{root}} \vert\vert t \vert\vert n \vert\vert d)\Big)$$
*The double-hashing (Bitcoin convention) mitigates length-extension attacks on SHA-256.*

**Step 2 – Interpret the hash as a 256-bit unsigned integer.**
$$h_{\text{int}} = \text{int}\big(H(B_{\text{header}})\big) \in [0, 2^{256} - 1]$$
*This step converts the byte string into a comparable numeric value.*

**Step 3 – Apply the PoW condition.**
$$h_{\text{int}} < T$$
*If satisfied, the block is valid. Otherwise, the miner must change the nonce $n$ (or other fields like extra-nonce, tx order) and recompute.*

**Step 4 – Compute expected number of trials.**
$$E[\text{trials}] = \frac{2^{256}}{T}$$
*For Bitcoin's initial target $T \approx 2^{224}$, this gives $E[\text{trials}] \approx 2^{32} \approx 4.3$ billion hashes per block solution.*

**Step 5 – Adjust difficulty dynamically.**
Every 2016 blocks (≈ 2 weeks), the network recalibrates:
$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{expected}}}$$
*This keeps the average block time at ~10 minutes regardless of total network hash rate.*

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – ECDSA Signature Verification (Simplified)

**Problem:** Given message hash $z$, signature $(r, s)$, public key $Q$, and curve order $n$, verify the signature.

**Step 1 – Compute the modular inverse of $s$.**
$$w = s^{-1} \pmod{n}$$
*This $w$ value is needed to "undo" the signing operation.*

**Step 2 – Compute two scalars.**
$$u_1 = z \cdot w \pmod{n}, \quad u_2 = r \cdot w \pmod{n}$$

**Step 3 – Compute the verification point on the elliptic curve.**
$$(x_1, y_1) = u_1 \cdot G + u_2 \cdot Q$$
*Here $G$ is the generator point and $Q$ is the public key.*

**Step 4 – Reduce modulo the curve order.**
$$v = x_1 \pmod{n}$$

**Step 5 – Final verification condition.**
$$\text{Valid} \iff v = r$$
*If true, the signature is authentic; otherwise, reject.*

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Layer-to-Equation Mapping

| Layer | Key Equation | KTU Board Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **Hashing Layer** | $H = \text{SHA256}(m)$ | High (definitions) |
| **Address Layer** | $A = \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(k_{pub}))$ | Medium |
| **Signature Layer** | $(r,s) = \text{Sign}(k_{priv}, z)$ | Very High (14-mark qs) |
| **Merkle Layer** | $H_{\text{root}} = H(H_{12} \vert\vert H_{34})$ | High |
| **Consensus Layer** | $H(B_{\text{header}}) < T$ | Very High |
| **Incentive Layer** | $R_b = 50 \cdot 2^{-\lfloor h/210000 \rfloor}$ | Medium |
| **Network Layer** | Longest chain = canonical | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Transaction Flow in a Basic Cryptocurrency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Initiates Tx in Wallet] --> B[Wallet Constructs Raw Tx]
    B --> C[Tx Inputs: previous unspent outputs]
    B --> D[Tx Outputs: recipient address + amount + change]
    C --> E[Sign Tx with Sender Private Key]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Broadcast Tx to P2P Network]
    F --> G[Nodes Validate: signatures, format, double-spend]
    G --> H[Valid Txs enter Mempool]
    H --> I[Miner Selects Txs from Mempool]
    I --> J[Build Block Header + Merkle Root]
    J --> K[Search Nonce: SHA256 lt Target]
    K --> L[Block Found: Broadcast to Network]
    L --> M[Other Nodes Verify PoW and Txs]
    M --> N[Append Block to Local Chain]
    N --> O[Tx is Now Confirmed]
```

### 4.2 Block Structure Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BH[Block Header 80 bytes]
        V1[Previous Block Hash 32 bytes]
        V2[Merkle Root 32 bytes]
        V3[Timestamp 4 bytes]
        V4[Difficulty Target 4 bytes]
        V5[Nonce 4 bytes]
    end
    subgraph BC[Block Body Variable Size]
        W1[Transaction Count]
        W2[Tx 1]
        W3[Tx 2]
        W4[Tx N]
    end
    V1 --> BC
    V2 --> BC
    BC --> X[Block Hash SHA256x2 of Header]
    BH --> X
```

### 4.3 Merkle Tree Construction (4 Transactions)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[T1] --> H1[H1 SHA256]
    A2[T2] --> H2[H2 SHA256]
    A3[T3] --> H3[H3 SHA256]
    A4[T4] --> H4[H4 SHA256]
    H1 --> C1[H12 SHA256 of H1 concatenated H2]
    H2 --> C1
    H3 --> C2[H34 SHA256 of H3 concatenated H4]
    H4 --> C2
    C1 --> R1[Hroot Merkle Root]
    C2 --> R1
```

### 4.4 Mining Loop (Proof-of-Work)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Assemble Block Header] --> S2[Set Nonce to 0]
    S2 --> S3[Compute H SHA256 of Header]
    S3 --> S4{H less than Target}
    S4 -->|No| S5[Increment Nonce]
    S5 --> S3
    S4 -->|Yes| S6[Block Valid: Broadcast]
    S6 --> S7[Other Nodes Accept and Extend Chain]
    S7 --> S1
```

### 4.5 Address and Key Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Private Key k 256 bit random] --> K2[Public Key k_pub ECDSA point on secp256k1]
    K2 --> K3[SHA256 of k_pub 256 bit]
    K3 --> K4[RIPEMD160 of above 160 bit]
    K4 --> K5[Add Version Byte and Checksum]
    K5 --> K6[Base58Check Encode]
    K6 --> A1[Final Address e.g. 1A1zP1...]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Define a *cryptocurrency* and list its three defining properties.

**Model Answer:**
A cryptocurrency is a **decentralized digital currency** secured by cryptography and maintained on a distributed ledger.
**Three defining properties:**
1. **Decentralization** – No central authority controls issuance or validation.
2. **Cryptographic Security** – Uses hash functions and digital signatures.
3. **Consensus-driven Ledger** – State agreed upon by majority of network nodes.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** What is the *double-spending problem*? How does a basic cryptocurrency prevent it?

**Model Answer:**
**Double-spending** is the risk that the same digital coin is spent in two different transactions.
**Prevention mechanism:**
- All transactions are publicly broadcast and timestamped in blocks.
- The **longest-chain rule** ensures only one history is accepted.
- Proof-of-Work makes rewriting history computationally infeasible.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**
> ### Question A
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the role of **cryptographic hash functions** and **digital signatures** in a basic cryptocurrency. Use **SHA-256** and **ECDSA** as examples.
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **structure of a block** in a basic cryptocurrency. Show how **Merkle trees** are used to summarize transactions and why this design is **efficient for Simplified Payment Verification (SPV)**.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Cryptographic Hash Function (SHA-256):**
- A hash function $H$ maps arbitrary input $m$ to a **fixed 256-bit output** $H(m)$.
- **Properties:** Deterministic, pre-image resistant, collision resistant, avalanche effect.
- **Role:** Links blocks via $H_{prev}$, forms Merkle root, derives addresses.
- **Equation:** $H_{\text{block}} = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(H_{prev} \vert\vert H_{\text{root}} \vert\vert t \vert\vert n \vert\vert d))$.

**Digital Signature (ECDSA on secp256k1):**
- Sender signs the transaction hash with private key $k_{priv}$:
$$(r, s) = \text{Sign}(k_{priv}, H(Tx))$$
- Receiver verifies using public key $k_{pub}$:
$$\text{Valid} \iff v = x_1 \pmod{n} \text{ equals } r$$
- **Role:** Provides **authenticity, integrity, and non-repudiation**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Block Structure:**
- **Block Header (80 bytes):** Previous Hash, Merkle Root, Timestamp, Difficulty, Nonce.
- **Block Body:** Transaction counter + list of transactions.

**Merkle Tree Role:**
- All transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ are pairwise hashed iteratively:
$$H_{\text{root}} = H(\ldots H(H(T_1 \vert\vert T_2)) \vert\vert \ldots)$$
- A single 256-bit $H_{\text{root}}$ in the header commits to **all transactions**.

**Efficiency for SPV:**
- Light clients (SPV) download only the **block headers**, not full transactions.
- To verify a single transaction, they request a **Merkle path** of $\log_2(n)$ hashes.
- Complexity: $O(\log n)$ instead of $O(n)$ — extremely bandwidth-efficient.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks – Alternative Choice]**
> ### Question B
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Proof-of-Work (PoW)** consensus mechanism used in a basic cryptocurrency. Derive the **expected number of hash trials** required to find a valid block.
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **transaction lifecycle** from initiation to confirmation. Include the role of **mempool, miners, and block confirmation depth**.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Proof-of-Work Concept:**
- Mining is the process of finding a **nonce** $n$ such that:
$$H(B_{\text{header}}) < T$$
- where $T$ is the **target** derived from the current difficulty $D$:
$$T = \frac{T_{\max}}{D}, \quad T_{\max} = 2^{224}$$

**Derivation of Expected Trials:**
- Treat each hash as a uniform random sample in $[0, 2^{256})$.
- Probability of success per trial: $p = T / 2^{256}$.
- Expected number of trials (geometric distribution):
$$E[\text{trials}] = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{2^{256}}{T}$$

**Numerical Example (Bitcoin Genesis):** $T \approx 2^{224} \Rightarrow E[\text{trials}] \approx 2^{32} \approx 4.3 \times 10^9$.

**Difficulty Adjustment:**
$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{20160 \text{ min}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Transaction Lifecycle Stages:**

1. **Initiation:** Wallet constructs raw transaction with inputs (UTXO references) and outputs (recipient + change address).
2. **Signing:** Wallet signs the transaction hash using the sender's private key.
3. **Broadcasting:** Transaction is sent to connected P2P nodes.
4. **Validation & Mempool:** Nodes independently validate format, signatures, and double-spend; valid txs enter the **mempool**.
5. **Selection by Miner:** A miner picks high-fee txs from the mempool and assembles a candidate block.
6. **Mining (PoW):** Miner iterates nonces until $H(B_{\text{header}}) < T$.
7. **Block Propagation:** Successful block is broadcast to all peers.
8. **Confirmation:** When other nodes accept the block, the included transactions gain **1 confirmation**. After **6 confirmations** (≈ 60 minutes), transactions are considered practically irreversible.

**Block Confirmation Depth:** Deeper blocks are exponentially more secure because an attacker would need to redo PoW for that block *and all subsequent blocks*.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic (lose 2–4 marks easily):**
> 1. **Confusing SHA-256 with SHA-1** — Bitcoin uses **SHA-256**, not SHA-1.
> 2. **Writing $H$ vs $H_{\text{int}}$ interchangeably** — Always convert hash to integer before comparing with target.
> 3. **Forgetting the double-hash in Bitcoin block headers** — Always use $\text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(...))$.
> 4. **Omitting the change output** in transactions — unspent difference goes back to sender.
> 5. **Stating "miners validate transactions" without the consensus context** — full nodes validate; miners also validate before including txs.
> 6. **Missing the role of mempool** — many students skip this stage, which costs a full mark in 14-mark answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – "A Basic Cryptocurrency"**

- **Cryptocurrency** = decentralized + cryptographic + consensus-driven digital currency.
- **Three pillars:** Decentralized ledger, Asymmetric cryptography, Consensus protocol.
- **Double-spending** is prevented by public ledger + PoW + longest chain rule.
- **SHA-256** is the primary hash; produces **256-bit** digests; used in block headers and Merkle trees.
- **Block header contains:** Previous hash, Merkle root, timestamp, difficulty, nonce.
- **Merkle tree** gives $O(\log n)$ verification; root in header commits to all txs.
- **PoW condition:** $H(B_{\text{header}}) < T$, where $T = T_{\max}/D$.
- **Expected trials:** $E = 2^{256}/T$.
- **ECDSA on secp256k1** provides digital signatures using private/public key pair.
- **Address = Base58Check(RIPEMD160(SHA256(public key)) + version + checksum).**
- **Block reward halves** every **210,000 blocks** in Bitcoin.
- **6 confirmations** ≈ practical finality in Bitcoin (~60 min).
- **Full nodes validate**; **SPV clients** use Merkle paths for lightweight verification.
- **Longest chain with most cumulative work** is canonical.
- **Mining hardware** (ASICs) optimized for SHA-256 throughput in GH/s or TH/s.
- **Network metrics** to remember: **10 min block time**, **21 M supply cap**, **50 → 25 → 12.5 → ... BTC reward**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creation of Coins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Creation of Coins # 🪙 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Creation of Coins (Coin Generation / Coin Minting)** is the cryptographically governed process by which new monetary units (coins or tokens) are introduced into a blockchain network's circulating supply in a predetermined, verifiable, and tamper-resistant manner. In Bitcoin and similar Proof-of-Work (PoW) systems, coin creation is the side-effect of successful block validation, where the protocol rewards the winning miner with a **Coinbase Transaction**. In Proof-of-Stake (PoS) systems, coin creation occurs through protocol-defined issuance to validators who successfully propose or attest to new blocks. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, coin creation is the **monetary policy layer** of a blockchain protocol, governed by mathematical rules embedded in the consensus algorithm rather than by a central monetary authority. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Digital Gold Mine** > Imagine a village where gold nuggets are hidden inside a giant cryptographic lockbox. Every 10 minutes, a new lockbox appears somewhere in the village. Only the villager who solves a complex mathematical puzzle (guess a specific number) gets to open that lockbox and claim the gold inside. The rules of the game are written in stone at the village square (the protocol), and every villager can verify that the gold was honestly extracted. > - The **lockbox** = a candidate block > - The **mathematical puzzle** = the Proof-of-Work hash target > - The **gold inside** = the newly minted coins (block subsidy + transaction fees) > - The **stone rules** = the consensus protocol's monetary policy > - The **goldsmith who checks honesty** = every full node that validates the new block In a **Proof-of-Stake** system, replace the puzzle-solving villager with a **lottery system** — villagers who lock up (stake) more gold have a higher chance of being chosen to forge the new lockbox and earn freshly minted coins. ## 1.3 Key Entities and Their Roles | Entity | Role in Coin Creation | |---|---| | **Miner / Validator** | The network participant who expends resources (compute or stake) to earn the right to create a block. | | **Coinbase Transaction** | The first transaction in a block; it has no inputs and creates new coins out of thin air. | | **Block Reward (Subsidy)** | The newly created coins awarded to the miner/validator per block. | | **Halving** | The scheduled periodic reduction of the block subsidy by 50%. | | **Total Supply Cap** | The maximum number of coins that will ever exist (e.g., **21 million BTC**). | | **Genesis Block** | Block #0, the very first block, whose coinbase famously contains the message referencing the Times headline. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric:** The Bitcoin protocol's block reward schedule is precisely **50 BTC → 25 BTC → 12.5 BTC → 6.25 BTC → 3.125 BTC** (post-2024 halving), halving every **210,000 blocks** (approximately every **4 years**). ## 1.4 Why "Creation of Coins" Matters in KTU Examinations This topic is a **high-yield Module 2 topic** because it bridges the cryptographic (hashing, digital signatures) and economic (monetary policy, incentives) layers of a blockchain. KTU 2024 Scheme questions on this topic typically evaluate: - The student's ability to describe **how** new coins enter circulation - The student's grasp of **why** coin creation is necessary (incentivization + supply control) - The student's ability to **compute** the total supply and remaining minable coins - Comparative analysis of coin creation across **PoW, PoS, and hybrid consensus** # 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Coin Creation (PoW Context) The lifecycle of a newly minted coin follows a precise, deterministic sequence: 1. **Transaction Aggregation:** Miners collect pending transactions from the mempool into a candidate block. 2. **Coinbase Construction:** A special transaction (the *coinbase tx*) is created as the first transaction. It has: - **No UTXO inputs** (it is the source of the new coins). - Outputs that send the new coins to the miner's address. 3. **Block Header Assembly:** The miner assembles a block header containing: - Previous block hash - Merkle root of transactions - Timestamp - Difficulty target - Nonce (a 32-bit counter) 4. **Proof-of-Work Execution:** The miner iteratively changes the nonce and re-hashes the block header until the resulting hash is numerically less than the target. 5. **Block Broadcast & Validation:** The winning miner broadcasts the block. Other nodes verify: - The PoW is valid. - The coinbase reward ≤ protocol-allowed subsidy + sum of transaction fees. 6. **Block Reward Credit:** Once accepted, the miner can spend the newly created coins only after **100 confirmations** (Bitcoin standard, approximately ~16.6 hours). 7. **Supply Increment:** The total circulating supply is incremented by the new coinbase amount. > [!NOTE] > **The 'Why' Behind Each Step:** Coin creation must be **expensive to forge** (so attackers cannot flood the supply) but **cheap to verify** (so honest nodes can quickly check the work). This asymmetry is the cornerstone of PoW monetary security. ## 2.2 Detailed Mechanics of the Coinbase Transaction A coinbase transaction is fundamentally different from a regular transaction: | Property | Regular Transaction | Coinbase Transaction | |---|---|---| | Number of inputs | ≥ 1 UTXO reference | Exactly 1 (with arbitrary data, no UTXO ref) | | Source of value | Existing UTXOs | New coins (protocol issuance) | | Validity constraint | Must reference unspent outputs | Must be the **first tx** in the block | | Reward rules | Sum of inputs = sum of outputs (usually) | Output ≤ current_subsidy + Σ(fees) | | Maturity | Spendable after 0 confirmations | Spendable after 100 confirmations | ## 2.3 Block Subsidy & Halving Schedule > [!IMPORTANT] > The Bitcoin subsidy follows the formula: > > $$\text{Subsidy}_n = \frac{50 \text{ BTC}}{2^n}$$ > > where $n$ is the halving epoch number (starting at $n=0$). - **Epoch 0** (Genesis to Block 209,999): Subsidy = 50 BTC - **Epoch 1** (Block 210,000 to 419,999): Subsidy = 25 BTC - **Epoch 2** (Block 420,000 to 629,999): Subsidy = 12.5 BTC - **Epoch 3** (Block 630,000 to 839,999): Subsidy = 6.25 BTC - **Epoch 4** (Block 840,000 onwards, from April 2024): Subsidy = **3.125 BTC** ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is your **exam-ready reference** for any numerical question on coin creation. | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Block Subsidy (current epoch) | $S_n = 50 \cdot 2^{-n}$ BTC | $n$ = halving epoch | | Halving interval | $H = 210{,}000$ blocks | Bitcoin protocol constant | | Average time per halving | $T \approx 4 \text{ years}$ | Assuming 10-min block time | | Total block reward (actual) | $R = S_n + \sum_{i} f_i$ | $f_i$ = tx fee of $i$-th transaction | | Total BTC supply (theoretical) | $C_{\infty} = 50 \cdot 210{,}000 \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 2^{-k}$ BTC | Geometric series | | Total BTC supply (converged) | $C_{\infty} = 21{,}000{,}000$ BTC | After $\approx 32$ halvings (~2140) | | Remaining supply at epoch $n$ | $C_{rem} = 50 \cdot 210{,}000 \cdot \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} 2^{-k}$ BTC | $= 50 \cdot 210{,}000 \cdot 2^{-(n-1)}$ | | Coins per day (avg) | $D = 144 \cdot S_n$ | 144 blocks/day in Bitcoin | | Coins per year (avg) | $Y = 525{,}600 \cdot S_n$ BTC | 365.0 days × 1440 min ÷ 10 min | | Mining reward with fees | $M = S_n + \text{Total Fees in Block}$ | Miner receives both | | Block number from date | $B(t) \approx B_0 + \frac{(t - t_0) \cdot 144}{1}$ | Approximation, $t$ in days | > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Notice the use of `\cdot` and `\sum` instead of literal symbols to prevent markdown table corruption. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding coin creation is essential for: - **Cryptocurrency Exchange Engineers:** They must track circulating supply and inflation rate. - **Smart Contract Developers (Solidity/Rust):** They implement custom minting logic in ERC-20 / ERC-721 contracts. - **Blockchain Auditors:** They verify that a protocol's actual issuance matches its declared monetary policy. - **Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) Architects:** They contrast decentralized issuance with centralized digital money creation. ## 2.6 Coin Creation in Other Consensus Mechanisms ### Proof of Stake (PoS) — Ethereum 2.0 Model - Validators lock up **32 ETH** as collateral. - A pseudo-random selection algorithm (RANDAO + VDF) chooses a proposer per slot. - The proposer mints a new block and receives the **issuance reward**. - Issuance is dynamic: it adjusts based on the **total staked ETH** to keep the staked ratio near a target (e.g., 0.5%–1% annual yield). ### Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) — EOS / Tron Model - Token holders vote for a fixed number of **block producers** (e.g., 21 in EOS). - Selected producers rotate turns to produce blocks and receive newly minted tokens. ### Proof of Authority (PoA) — VeChain, Kovan - Pre-approved validator nodes create blocks. Reward is typically **zero or fixed** (low/no inflation). ### Proof of Burn (PoB) — Slimcoin, Counterparty - Miners **destroy** (send to an unspendable address) existing coins to earn the right to mine future blocks. # 🔢 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The 21 Million Bitcoin Cap This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU exams. We will derive it **completely and exhaustively**. > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Prove that the total number of bitcoins ever created converges to exactly **21,000,000 BTC**. **Step 1 — Define the constants of the Bitcoin protocol.** The protocol fixes the following two constants at launch: - Initial block subsidy: $S_0 = 50$ BTC - Halving interval: $H = 210{,}000$ blocks **Step 2 — Express the subsidy in epoch $n$.** At the start of epoch $n$ (i.e., after $n$ halvings have occurred), the subsidy is halved $n$ times. Therefore: $$ S_n = S_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n = 50 \cdot 2^{-n} \;\;\text{BTC} $$ **Step 3 — Compute total coins minted in epoch $n$.** In epoch $n$, there are exactly $H = 210{,}000$ blocks. Each block mints $S_n$ new BTC (ignoring fees for the cap calculation). Therefore, the total coins minted in epoch $n$ are: $$ C_n = H \cdot S_n = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-n} $$ **Step 4 — Sum over all epochs to infinity.** The total supply is the infinite geometric series: $$ C_{\infty} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} C_n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-n} $$ **Step 5 — Factor out the constants.** $$ C_{\infty} = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 2^{-n} $$ **Step 6 — Evaluate the geometric series.** The infinite geometric series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x^n$ converges to $\frac{1}{1-x}$ when $\vert x \vert < 1$. Here, $x = \frac{1}{2}$: $$ \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 2^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{2}} = 2 $$ **Step 7 — Substitute back to obtain the total supply.** $$ C_{\infty} = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2 = 210{,}000 \cdot 100 $$ $$ C_{\infty} = 21{,}000{,}000 \;\;\text{BTC} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The total supply of Bitcoin converges asymptotically to exactly **21,000,000 BTC**. The last BTC will be mined around the year **2140**, after which miners will rely solely on **transaction fees** for revenue. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Coins Minted Per Day in Epoch $n$ > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Derive a general formula for the average number of new coins created per day, given the current epoch $n$. **Step 1 — Recall the average block time.** Bitcoin targets a block time of $T_b = 600$ seconds = 10 minutes. **Step 2 — Compute blocks per day.** $$ B_{day} = \frac{86{,}400 \text{ sec/day}}{600 \text{ sec/block}} = 144 \text{ blocks/day} $$ **Step 3 — Multiply by the subsidy per block.** $$ D_n = B_{day} \cdot S_n = 144 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-n} = 7200 \cdot 2^{-n} \;\;\text{BTC/day} $$ **Numerical verification for $n = 3$ (pre-2024):** $$ D_3 = 7200 \cdot 2^{-3} = 7200 \cdot 0.125 = 900 \;\;\text{BTC/day} $$ **Numerical verification for $n = 4$ (post-2024 halving):** $$ D_4 = 7200 \cdot 2^{-4} = 7200 \cdot 0.0625 = 450 \;\;\text{BTC/day} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Years Until the Next Halving > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Show how to estimate the time until the next halving given the current block height. **Step 1 — Compute remaining blocks to the next halving.** Let $B_{now}$ be the current block height. The next halving occurs at block $B_{next} = (n+1) \cdot 210{,}000$. Then: $$ \Delta B = (n+1) \cdot 210{,}000 - B_{now} $$ **Step 2 — Convert blocks to days.** $$ \Delta T_{days} = \frac{\Delta B}{144} $$ **Step 3 — Convert days to years.** $$ \Delta T_{years} = \frac{\Delta B}{144 \cdot 365.0} = \frac{\Delta B}{52{,}560} $$ **Worked example:** Suppose we are at block $B_{now} = 840{,}000$ (the most recent halving in April 2024). Then: $$ \Delta B = 1{,}050{,}000 - 840{,}000 = 210{,}000 $$ $$ \Delta T_{years} = \frac{210{,}000}{52{,}560} \approx 3.995 \approx 4 \text{ years} $$ This confirms the canonical **~4 year halving cadence**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Coin Creation Across Consensus Mechanisms | Mechanism | How Coins Are Created | Energy Model | Inflation Behavior | Example Networks | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Proof of Work (PoW)** | Miner solves hash puzzle → claims block subsidy + fees | High energy (computational) | Disinflationary (halvings) | Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin (aux) | | **Proof of Stake (PoS)** | Validator selected by stake-weighted lottery mints block | Low energy (capital lockup) | Often dynamic; may be deflationary if fees burned | Ethereum (post-Merge), Cardano | | **Delegated PoS (DPoS)** | Elected block producers rotate turns to mint blocks | Very low energy | Fixed schedule by token holders' vote | EOS, Tron, BNB Chain | | **Proof of Authority (PoA)** | Pre-approved authorities mint blocks; often zero reward | Negligible | Zero or fixed | VeChain, Kovan testnet | | **Proof of Burn (PoB)** | Miners send coins to verifiably unspendable address | Medium energy (one-time burn) | Deflationary (coins destroyed) | Slimcoin, Counterparty | | **Proof of Space & Time (PoST)** | Farmers allocate disk space + VDF wait time | Moderate (storage) | Fixed schedule | Chia | | **Proof of History (PoH)** | Verifiable delay function timestamps events | Low | Fixed issuance | Solana (combined with PoS) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked to *compare* coin creation mechanisms, always structure your answer around four axes: **(i) trigger, (ii) resource consumed, (iii) inflation model, (iv) security guarantee**. --- ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Problem: "How many BTC remain to be mined at the start of Epoch 4?" **Given:** We are at the start of Epoch 4 (subsidy = 3.125 BTC, block height = 840,000). **Step 1 — Use the remaining supply formula.** $$ C_{rem} = 50 \cdot 210{,}000 \cdot \sum_{n=4}^{\infty} 2^{-n} $$ **Step 2 — Factor the infinite sum from the start of a geometric series.** $$ \sum_{n=4}^{\infty} 2^{-n} = \frac{2^{-4}}{1 - \frac{1}{2}} = 2 \cdot 2^{-4} = 2^{-3} = 0.125 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the remaining supply.** $$ C_{rem} = 50 \cdot 210{,}000 \cdot 0.125 = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 0.125 = 1{,}312{,}500 \;\;\text{BTC} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the already-mined supply.** $$ C_{mined} = 21{,}000{,}000 - 1{,}312{,}500 = 19{,}687{,}500 \;\;\text{BTC} $$ > **Interpretation:** Roughly **93.75%** of all BTC had been mined by the start of Epoch 4, and only **~6.25%** (1.3125 million BTC) remains to be issued over the next ~116 years. --- ## 3.6 Coin Creation in Custom Tokens (ERC-20 Minting) For exam context on tokens (not just BTC), note that smart-contract-based tokens (ERC-20, ERC-721) use a different minting primitive: - The contract has a function `function mint(address to, uint256 amount) public onlyOwner` (or with role-based access). - This function **increments** the total supply and the recipient's balance. - The Ethereum base layer does not "know" about the mint; it only sees a regular contract-call transaction whose gas fees are paid in **ETH**. > [!NOTE] > **Key distinction:** Bitcoin-style coin creation is **protocol-level** and enforced by every full node. ERC-20 minting is **application-level** and enforced only by the contract code; a bug or owner key compromise can mint unlimited tokens. # 🧩 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Structure Showing the Coinbase Transaction The diagram below illustrates where the coinbase transaction sits in a block and how it injects new coins into the UTXO set. ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef coinbase fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef block fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000 classDef tx fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 classDef utxo fill:#FFF0F5,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 PrevHash["PrevBlockHash
000000000000000000043a..."]:::block Coinbase["COINBASE TX
NO INPUTS
Output: 3.125 BTC + Fees
To: Miner's Address"]:::coinbase Tx1["Tx 1
Alice -> Bob 0.5 BTC"]:::tx Tx2["Tx 2
Carol -> Dave 0.2 BTC"]:::tx TxN["Tx N
... other transactions ..."]:::tx MerkleRoot["Merkle Root
a3f2c8..."]:::block Nonce["Nonce
1928374"]:::block Target["Difficulty Target
0x1d00ffff"]:::block Hash["Block Hash
00000000000000000001a8..."]:::block PrevHash --> MerkleRoot Coinbase --> MerkleRoot Tx1 --> MerkleRoot Tx2 --> MerkleRoot TxN --> MerkleRoot Nonce --> Hash Target --> Hash MerkleRoot --> Hash Coinbase -->|new coins enter| UTXOSet["UTXO Set
Miner's address: +3.125 BTC"]:::utxo Tx1 --> UTXOSet Tx2 --> UTXOSet TxN --> UTXOSet Hash -->|linked to| NextBlock["Next Block
PrevHash field"]:::block ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading guide:** The Coinbase transaction is the *only* transaction in the block that can introduce new coins into the UTXO set. All other transactions merely reshuffle existing UTXOs. --- ## 4.2 Lifecycle Flow of a Newly Minted Coin This block-level functional architecture shows the full path of a coin from its creation in the coinbase transaction to its eventual spendability. ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef stage1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef stage2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 classDef stage3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 classDef stage4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000 classDef stage5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 A["Step 1: Miner Solves PoW
Hash < Target"]:::stage1 B["Step 2: Construct Coinbase Tx
No inputs, miner output"]:::stage2 C["Step 3: Broadcast Block
to Network"]:::stage3 D["Step 4: Full Nodes Validate
PoW + Subsidy + Signatures"]:::stage4 E["Step 5: Block Appended
to Main Chain"]:::stage5 F["Step 6: Coin Maturity Period
100 confirmations ~16.6 hrs"]:::stage5 G["Step 7: Coin Spendable
UTXO usable in any tx"]:::stage5 A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G ``` --- ## 4.3 Halving Schedule: Subsidy Over Time ```mermaid graph LR classDef epoch fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000,stroke-width:2px E0["EPOCH 0
2009-2012
Subsidy = 50 BTC"]:::epoch E1["EPOCH 1
2012-2016
Subsidy = 25 BTC"]:::epoch E2["EPOCH 2
2016-2020
Subsidy = 12.5 BTC"]:::epoch E3["EPOCH 3
2020-2024
Subsidy = 6.25 BTC"]:::epoch E4["EPOCH 4
2024-2028
Subsidy = 3.125 BTC"]:::epoch E5["EPOCH 5
2028-2032
Subsidy = 1.5625 BTC"]:::epoch EN["... EPOCH 32
~2140
Subsidy = 0"]:::epoch E0 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4 --> E5 --> EN ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** The subsidy halves every ~4 years and asymptotically approaches zero. The dashed epochs after Epoch 5 represent the long tail of coin creation, which still mints non-zero (but tiny) rewards until around the year 2140. --- ## 4.4 Comparative Functional Architecture: PoW vs. PoS Coin Creation ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef pow fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#B22222,color:#000 classDef pos fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 subgraph POW["PROOF OF WORK COIN CREATION PATH"] P1["Miner Gathers TXs"]:::pow P2["Iterate Nonce + Hash Header"]:::pow P3["Hash less than Target?"]:::pow P4["YES - Mint Coinbase TX
Reward = Subsidy + Fees"]:::pow P5["NO - Increment Nonce, Retry"]:::pow P3 -->|Yes| P4 P3 -->|No| P5 P5 --> P2 end subgraph POS["PROOF OF STAKE COIN CREATION PATH"] Q1["Validator Staked 32+ ETH"]:::pos Q2["RANDAO + VDF Selects Proposer"]:::pos Q3["Proposer Assembles Block"]:::pos Q4["Mint Block
Reward = Issuance + Tips"]:::pos Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4 end POW --> Common["Coins Enter
Circulating Supply"] POS --> Common ``` # 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: What is a coinbase transaction in a blockchain? How does it differ from a regular transaction?** **Model Answer:** A **coinbase transaction** is the first transaction in a blockchain block, used to credit the miner (or validator) with newly minted coins and the collected transaction fees. Unlike a regular transaction: 1. It has **no UTXO inputs** — the coins are created *ex novo* by the protocol. 2. Its output amount must be ≤ **(current block subsidy) + (sum of transaction fees in the block)**. 3. It is **mandatory** and must occupy index 0 in the transaction list. 4. Its reward is **locked** (un-spendable) until the block has received 100 confirmations (maturity rule). > **Word count target:** 80–100 words for 3 marks. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Why is coin creation necessary in a blockchain network? List two mechanisms by which new coins are introduced.** **Model Answer:** Coin creation is necessary to: 1. **Incentivize** honest participation (mining/staking) and secure the network against Sybil attacks. 2. **Distribute** the currency initially in a decentralized manner (no central bank needed). 3. **Control supply** programmatically to achieve predictable monetary policy. Two mechanisms: - **Block Subsidy (Coinbase Reward)** — new coins minted in each block (Bitcoin). - **Staking Rewards / Validation Rewards** — new coins minted to validators per epoch (Ethereum PoS). --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the process of coin creation in a Bitcoin-like Proof-of-Work blockchain. Include the role of the coinbase transaction, block reward structure, and the concept of halving. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive mathematically that the total supply of Bitcoin converges to 21 million BTC. Also compute the number of bitcoins that will be mined per day at the start of the current halving epoch (post-April 2024). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a): 1. **Step 1 — The Need for Coin Creation:** Bitcoin requires miners to expend real-world resources (electricity, hardware). Without a reward, no rational actor would mine. The protocol therefore issues new coins as a **subsidy** to compensate honest miners and to bootstrap the currency's distribution. 2. **Step 2 — The Coinbase Transaction:** Each candidate block begins with a special transaction called the *coinbase transaction*. It has: - Exactly **one input** (the *coinbase input*) with no UTXO reference, containing arbitrary 2–100 byte data (commonly the block height and an extra-nonce). - **One or more outputs** paying the miner: `subsidy + Σ(transaction fees)`. 3. **Step 3 — Block Construction & Proof of Work:** The miner assembles the block header (previous hash, Merkle root, timestamp, difficulty bits, nonce) and iterates the nonce until the double-SHA256 hash of the header falls below the network's **target threshold**. This proves computational work was expended. 4. **Step 4 — Block Propagation & Validation:** On broadcast, full nodes verify (i) the PoW is valid, (ii) the coinbase subsidy matches the protocol-allowed value, (iii) all transactions are valid, and (iv) the Merkle root is consistent. On success, the block is appended to the longest chain and the miner receives the reward. 5. **Step 5 — Halving Mechanism:** The block subsidy halves every **210,000 blocks** (~4 years). This creates a **disinflationary** supply schedule. The series is: 50 → 25 → 12.5 → 6.25 → 3.125 → ... → approaching zero around the year 2140. 6. **Step 6 — Long-Term Sustainability:** After all bitcoins are mined, miners will be incentivized solely by **transaction fees**, creating a "security budget" question for the network. --- #### Model Solution to (b): **Part 1: Derivation of the 21 million cap.** **Step 1.** At launch, the protocol fixes $S_0 = 50$ BTC and halving interval $H = 210{,}000$ blocks. **Step 2.** Subsidy in epoch $n$ is: $$ S_n = 50 \cdot 2^{-n} \;\;\text{BTC} $$ **Step 3.** Total coins in epoch $n$ is $C_n = H \cdot S_n = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-n}$. **Step 4.** Sum over all epochs: $$ C_{\infty} = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 2^{-n} $$ **Step 5.** Evaluate the geometric series: $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 2^{-n} = \frac{1}{1-0.5} = 2$. **Step 6.** Therefore: $$ C_{\infty} = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \;\;\text{BTC} $$ **Part 2: Coins per day at start of current epoch (Epoch 4, $n=4$).** **Step 7.** Subsidy at epoch 4: $S_4 = 50 \cdot 2^{-4} = 50 / 16 = 3.125$ BTC. **Step 8.** Blocks per day: $B_{day} = 86{,}400 / 600 = 144$. **Step 9.** Coins per day: $$ D_4 = 144 \cdot 3.125 = 450 \;\;\text{BTC/day} $$ **Step 10.** Coins per year: $450 \cdot 365.0 = 164{,}250$ BTC/year. > **Final answer for (b):** Total supply = **21,000,000 BTC**; daily issuance at start of Epoch 4 = **450 BTC/day**. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]` **Q: (a)** Compare and contrast coin creation in **Proof of Work (PoW)** and **Proof of Stake (PoS)** systems, with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A blockchain starts with an initial block reward of 100 coins and halves the reward every 50,000 blocks. Assuming a block time of 5 minutes, compute: (i) the block reward at the 4th halving, (ii) the total coins minted per day, and (iii) the asymptotic total supply. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a): | Aspect | Proof of Work (PoW) | Proof of Stake (PoS) | |---|---|---| | **Trigger for coin creation** | Solving a hash puzzle (computational race) | Validator selected by stake-weighted pseudo-random lottery | | **Resource consumed** | Electricity + specialized hardware (ASICs) | Locked capital (staked tokens) | | **Coinbase example** | Bitcoin block reward (50 → 25 → 12.5 → 6.25 → 3.125 BTC) | Ethereum per-epoch issuance (~0.5–1% annual) | | **Inflation model** | Disinflationary (halving) | Often dynamic, can be deflationary (EIP-1559 burns fees) | | **Security basis** | Cost of energy → economic disincentive to attack | Cost of staked capital → slashable if misbehave | | **Energy footprint** | Very high | Very low | | **Examples** | Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin (aux) | Ethereum (post-Merge), Cardano, Polkadot | **Conclusion:** PoW ties coin creation to *physical work*; PoS ties it to *locked economic value*. PoS is more energy-efficient and flexible, but PoW has a longer battle-tested security history. --- #### Model Solution to (b): **Given:** $S_0 = 100$ coins, $H = 50{,}000$ blocks, $T_b = 5$ min. **(i) Block reward at 4th halving:** The 4th halving means $n = 4$. Using $S_n = S_0 \cdot 2^{-n}$: $$ S_4 = 100 \cdot 2^{-4} = 100 / 16 = 6.25 \text{ coins} $$ **(ii) Total coins minted per day at 4th halving:** Blocks per day: $$ B_{day} = \frac{1440 \text{ min/day}}{5 \text{ min/block}} = 288 \text{ blocks/day} $$ Daily issuance: $$ D_4 = 288 \cdot 6.25 = 1800 \text{ coins/day} $$ **(iii) Asymptotic total supply:** $$ C_{\infty} = H \cdot S_0 \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 2^{-n} = 50{,}000 \cdot 100 \cdot 2 = 10{,}000{,}000 \text{ coins} $$ > **Final answers:** (i) **6.25 coins**, (ii) **1,800 coins/day**, (iii) **10,000,000 coins**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:** > 1. **Forgetting the geometric series convergence step.** Many students write $50 \times 210{,}000 \times \infty$ and stop. Always explicitly evaluate $\sum 2^{-n} = 2$. > 2. **Confusing block subsidy with total reward.** Total reward = subsidy + fees. The cap derivation ignores fees (which actually push the supply *slightly above* 21M, since lost coins make up the difference). > 3. **Using the wrong halving interval.** It is **210,000 blocks for Bitcoin**, not 100,000 or 1,000,000. > 4. **Treating PoS minting as identical to PoW.** They are structurally different: PoS issuance is often **dynamic** (based on total staked), not a fixed halving schedule. > 5. **Missing the maturity rule.** The newly mined coins are unspendable for **100 blocks** in Bitcoin; many students forget this constraint. > 6. **Unit confusion in custom-token minting.** ERC-20 minting is a contract-level event, not a protocol-level coinbase. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — memorize these bullets before entering the exam hall.** - ✅ **Coinbase Tx:** First transaction in a block, no UTXO input, mints new coins + collects fees. - ✅ **Block Reward = Subsidy + Σ(fees).** Only the miner of a valid block gets this. - ✅ **Halving formula:** $S_n = S_0 \cdot 2^{-n}$, with $H = 210{,}000$ blocks for Bitcoin. - ✅ **Total supply cap:** $C_\infty = H \cdot S_0 \cdot 2 = 21{,}000{,}000$ BTC (Bitcoin). - ✅ **Maturity rule:** Coinbase outputs in Bitcoin require **100 confirmations** before spending. - ✅ **Daily issuance:** $D_n = 144 \cdot S_n$ BTC (for Bitcoin, 10-min blocks). - ✅ **PoW vs. PoS:** PoW mints via computational race (energy-backed); PoS mints via stake-weighted lottery (capital-backed). - ✅ **Disinflation:** Bitcoin's supply is disinflationary; issuance rate halves, approaching zero. - ✅ **Genesis Block:** Block #0; mints unspendable 50 BTC (hardcoded into the protocol). - ✅ **Coinbase maturity exists to prevent** a miner from spending the reward and then re-organizing the chain away. - ✅ **ERC-20 vs. native coins:** Custom token minting is application-level (contract code); native coin minting is protocol-level (consensus-enforced). - ✅ **Last BTC mined:** Approximately the year **2140**, after which security comes entirely from transaction fees. - ✅ **Post-2024 epoch:** We are currently in **Epoch 4**, with subsidy = **3.125 BTC** and daily issuance = **450 BTC**. - ✅ **PoS issuance in Ethereum:** Approximately **0.5%–1% annual yield** on staked ETH, dynamically adjusted. - ✅ **Total BTC remaining at start of Epoch 4:** Approximately **1,312,500 BTC** (~6.25% of the cap).

topics.title: Payments and Double Spending
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Payments and Double Spending

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Online Payments — Formal Definition

In the context of blockchain and cryptocurrencies, **payments** refer to the transfer of monetary value between two parties over a digital network. Under the KTU 2024 syllabus, online payment systems are classified into two broad paradigms:

- **Centralized Payment Systems** — Operated through trusted intermediaries such as banks, payment processors (Visa, Mastercard, PayPal), and clearinghouses. These parties maintain the authoritative ledger of all transactions.
- **Decentralized Payment Systems** — Peer-to-peer (P2P) networks such as Bitcoin, where no single trusted authority exists, and consensus among distributed nodes maintains the global state of the ledger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):**
> A digital currency must satisfy four properties to function as a credible medium of exchange: **(1) Permanence, (2) Security, (3) Atomicity, and (4) Verifiability**. Any payment system that fails these properties collapses as a trusted value-transfer mechanism.

## 1.2 Double-Spending — The Fundamental Problem

**Double Spending** is a critical failure mode unique to digital cash systems, defined formally as:

> **Double Spending** is the act of successfully spending the same digital token (coin/unit of value) more than once by exploiting the ease of duplicating digital information.

In physical cash, this problem is naturally impossible — a ₹500 note handed to a merchant cannot simultaneously be handed to another merchant. In the digital world, a file (representing a coin) can be perfectly duplicated, and without a central authority, the network has no immediate way to detect such duplication.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Photocopy Problem"

Imagine a small town where everyone trades using **handwritten IOUs (promissory notes)**. The town has no bank. If Alice gives Bob an IOU for ₹100, Bob has no way to confirm:
1. That Alice hasn't already given the *same* IOU to Charlie.
2. That the IOU hasn't been "photocopied" and the duplicate used elsewhere.
3. That Alice still has the underlying ₹100 to back the IOU.

This is the **double-spending problem** in its purest form. Bitcoin's innovation was to invent a **global, shared, append-only ledger** (the blockchain) that the entire town maintains together — and once a transaction is recorded, it is computationally and economically infeasible to rewrite.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Double-spending is to digital cash what counterfeiting is to physical cash — except the counterfeiter doesn't need special equipment; they only need to copy a string of bits. Blockchain solves this by making the *ledger* tamper-evident rather than trying to make the *coin* uncopyable.

## 1.4 Why Double-Spending is Harder than Counterfeiting

| Property | Physical Counterfeiting | Digital Double-Spending |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Effort required | High (specialized equipment, paper, ink) | Trivial (copy a file) |
| Detection | Visual/serial-number checks | Cryptographic ledger check |
| Prevention | Holograms, watermarks | Distributed consensus |
| Scale of attack | Limited by physical resources | Potentially infinite at zero marginal cost |

This asymmetric cost structure is precisely what makes double-spending the **central design challenge** of any digital currency — and the reason Module 2 of your KTU syllabus dedicates an entire topic to it.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Properties of a Digital Currency

Any viable digital currency (fiat-backed or crypto-native) must satisfy the following four properties, as enumerated in the foundational literature cited under KTU Module 2:

### 2.1.1 Permanence
Once a transaction is committed, it must be **irreversible** and **unalterable**. A successful payment cannot be rolled back, even by the system operator. This is achieved in Bitcoin through:
- Cryptographic hash chains (each block references the previous block's hash)
- Massive replicated computation (Proof-of-Work) so that rewriting history is economically prohibitive.

### 2.1.2 Security
Unauthorized parties must not be able to **create, alter, or forge** transactions. In Bitcoin, this is enforced by:
- **ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)** for transaction authorization
- **SHA-256** for block hashing
- A user controls funds by possessing the **private key** corresponding to the public key (address).

### 2.1.3 Atomicity
A transaction must be **all-or-nothing** — either the full value is transferred, or nothing is transferred. There can be no partial state. This is enforced in Bitcoin by the **UTXO (Unspent Transaction Output) model**, where each transaction references specific unspent outputs and consumes them entirely.

### 2.1.4 Verifiability
Any third party (auditor, merchant, regulator) must be able to **independently verify** the validity of a transaction without trusting the transacting parties. Bitcoin achieves this through:
- Public blockchain transparency
- Deterministic validation rules (every node runs the same checks)

## 2.2 The Double-Spending Attack Taxonomy

Double-spending attacks in cryptocurrency networks are generally classified into the following categories:

### 2.2.1 Race Attack
An attacker sends **two conflicting transactions** to the network almost simultaneously — one to the victim merchant, and one back to the attacker's own address. If the merchant accepts the payment with **zero confirmations**, the attacker's second transaction may be the one that gets confirmed first.

- **Defense:** Merchants should wait for at least **6 confirmations** (≈ 60 minutes in Bitcoin).

### 2.2.2 Finney Attack
Named after Hal Finney. The attacker **pre-mines a block** containing a conflicting transaction (e.g., sending coins back to themselves), then makes a payment to a merchant who accepts zero-confirmation. If the merchant broadcasts the transaction before the attacker releases their pre-mined block, the attacker's block overrides it.

- **Defense:** Same as race attack — wait for confirmations.

### 2.2.3 Vector76 / One-Confirmation Attack
A combination of the Race and Finney attacks. The attacker creates a pre-mined block with a single confirmation that double-spends to themselves, then races to extend that branch.

### 2.2.4 51% Attack (Majority Hash Power Attack)
The most severe form. If an attacker controls **>50%** of the network's total hashing power, they can:
- Privately mine an alternate chain longer than the public one
- Include a double-spend transaction on their private chain
- Broadcast it once it's longer, causing honest nodes to accept it via the **longest-chain rule**

The probability of success decreases **exponentially** with the number of confirmations the merchant waits for.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The famous Bitcoin whitepaper (Nakamoto, 2008)** explicitly addresses the double-spending problem in Section 3 and proves via probabilistic modeling that an attacker with less than 50% hash power has an exponentially vanishing probability of catching up to the honest chain.

## 2.3 How Bitcoin Solves Double-Spending

Bitcoin's solution is a **distributed timestamp server** that issues a chronological ordering of all transactions known to the network. The mechanism works as follows:

1. **Transactions are broadcast** to all nodes in the P2P network.
2. **Each node collects new transactions into a block.**
3. **Each node works on finding a difficult Proof-of-Work** for its block.
4. **When a node finds a proof**, it broadcasts the block to all other nodes.
5. **Nodes accept the block only if all transactions in it are valid** and not already spent.
6. **Nodes express their acceptance** by working on creating the next block in the chain, using the accepted block's hash as the previous hash.

The **longest chain** is collectively agreed to be the authoritative history. A double-spend is impossible in this model because once a transaction is buried under several blocks, producing an alternative chain would require enormous computational work.

## 2.4 The Confirmation Mechanism

A **confirmation** is each subsequent block added to the chain after the block containing a given transaction. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following table (very high-yield for exams):

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variable Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Hash target (Bitcoin) | $T = 2^{224} \cdot D^{-1}$ | $D$ = difficulty target | Adjusted every **2016 blocks** |
| 2 | Block time (Bitcoin) | $\Delta t = 10 \text{ min}$ | Mean inter-block interval | $600$ seconds |
| 3 | Difficulty adjustment | $D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \dfrac{t_{actual}}{t_{expected}}$ | Re-targeting every 2016 blocks | $t_{expected} = 20160$ min |
| 4 | 51% attack feasibility | $P(\text{attack success}) = \sum_{k=0}^{z} \binom{z+k-1}{k} \cdot p^{z} \cdot (1-p)^{k}$ | $z$ = confirmations waited, $p$ = attacker hash fraction | $p < 0.5$ gives negligible probability for $z \geq 6$ |
| 5 | UTXO consumption rule | $\sum_{inputs} = \sum_{outputs} + \text{fees}$ | Conservation of value | Enforced by every full node |
| 6 | ECDSA signature size | $S_{size} = 512$ bits | Signature pair $(r, s)$ | Compact: ~71 bytes DER-encoded |
| 7 | Merkle tree depth | $D_{merkle} = \lceil \log_{2}(n) \right\rceil$ | $n$ = number of transactions in block | Bitcoin: 10–14 levels for ~3000 txs |
| 8 | Block reward (2024) | $R = 3.125 \text{ BTC}$ | Current Bitcoin subsidy post-2024 halving | Halves every **210,000 blocks** |
| 9 | Total BTC supply cap | $S_{max} = 21 \text{ million BTC}$ | Sum of geometric halving series | Reached ~year 2140 |
