> The most exam-relevant formulas are the **difficulty adjustment (Row 3)** and the **51% attack probability (Row 4)**. Be prepared to derive or at least interpret Row 4 in a 14-mark question.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

The double-spending solution underpins real applications beyond cryptocurrency:
- **Cross-border remittances** — Eliminates intermediary banking fees.
- **Smart contract platforms** — Ethereum uses the same UTXO/account-balance paradigm to prevent asset duplication.
- **Supply chain provenance** — Each product token cannot be "double-claimed" as belonging to two supply chains.
- **Digital identity credentials** — Verifiable credentials cannot be presented to multiple verifiers simultaneously.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Probability of a 51% Attack Success

This is the most mathematically demanding concept in the topic, and is a regular KTU 14-mark question. We derive the **Gambler's Ruin probability** for a double-spend attacker.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Let:
- $p$ = fraction of total hash power controlled by the **attacker**.
- $q = 1 - p$ = fraction controlled by the **honest network**.
- $z$ = number of confirmations the merchant waits before accepting payment.
- $k$ = the lead (number of blocks) the attacker has on the honest chain at the moment of attack.

### 3.1.2 Assumptions
1. Attacker privately mines an alternative chain starting $k$ blocks behind the public chain.
2. Honest miners continue to extend the public chain.
3. The attacker must close the gap from $k$ to $0$ (i.e., catch up by $k$ blocks).

### 3.1.3 The Random Walk Model
The difference in chain lengths evolves as a **biased random walk**. Each time the attacker finds a block, the gap decreases by 1. Each time an honest miner finds a block, the gap increases by 1.

The probability that the attacker catches up by exactly $z$ blocks when starting $z$ blocks behind is given by the **negative binomial / Poisson-like formula**:

$$
P(\text{success}) = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } p > q \\[8pt]
\left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^{z} & \text{if } p < q
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.1.4 Full Derivation (Exhaustive Steps)

**Step 1 — Define the random walk state.**
Let $X_t$ = the number of blocks the attacker is *behind* the honest chain at time $t$. Initially, $X_0 = z$.

**Step 2 — Compute transition probabilities.**
In each discrete step (a block found by *someone*):
$$
P(X_{t+1} = X_t + 1) = q \quad \text{(honest block found)}
$$
$$
P(X_{t+1} = X_t - 1) = p \quad \text{(attacker block found)}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the goal.**
The attacker succeeds if and only if $X_t$ ever reaches $0$ before growing without bound. This is a classic **first-passage problem** for a random walk with absorbing barrier at $0$.

**Step 4 — Solve via the martingale method.**
For a random walk with drift, define:
$$
\alpha = \dfrac{p}{q}
$$
The generating function for the walk is $G(s) = qs + ps^{-1}$, with the smaller root:
$$
s_0 = \dfrac{p}{q} = \alpha
$$

**Step 5 — Use the boundary condition.**
The probability of ever reaching 0 starting from $z$ is proportional to $s_0^{z}$, normalized by the boundary value. For the case $p < q$ (honest majority), this yields:
$$
P_{\text{success}}(z) = \left(\dfrac{p}{q}\right)^{z} = \left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right)^{z}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
This is the famous **exponential decay** result. Even with $p = 0.3$ (a strong attacker), the success probability drops as:
- $z = 1$: $(0.3/0.7)^1 \approx 0.4286$
- $z = 2$: $\approx 0.1837$
- $z = 6$: $\approx 0.0210$
- $z = 10$: $\approx 0.0024$

For $p = 0.1$:
- $z = 6$: $(0.1/0.9)^6 \approx 1.71 \times 10^{-6}$ — effectively zero.

**Step 7 — Generalization for the attacker who starts with lead $k$ (or zero lead but waits).**
The full Nakamoto formula (also commonly tested) is:
$$
P = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{z} \binom{z+k-1}{k} \cdot p^{z} \cdot q^{k} \cdot \left(1 - \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^{z-k}\right)
$$

For the case $p \le q$ and $z$ confirmations waited, this simplifies to the form already derived.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Typical KTU Numerical

**Problem:** An attacker controls $p = 0.2$ of the network hash rate. A merchant waits for $z = 5$ confirmations. Compute the probability that a double-spend attack succeeds.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**
$$
q = 1 - p = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8
$$
$$
P_{\text{success}} = \left(\dfrac{p}{q}\right)^{z} = \left(\dfrac{0.2}{0.8}\right)^{5}
$$
$$
P_{\text{success}} = (0.25)^{5}
$$
$$
P_{\text{success}} = 0.25 \times 0.25 \times 0.25 \times 0.25 \times 0.25
$$
$$
P_{\text{success}} = 9.76 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.0976\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **1 in 1024** chance of success — low, but not negligible. This is why KTU answers typically conclude: *"Waiting for 6+ confirmations gives an attacker probability of success so low that it is considered computationally and economically infeasible."*

## 3.3 The UTXO Conservation Equation — Step-by-Step

For every transaction, full nodes verify:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} O_j + f
$$
where:
- $I_i$ = value of the $i$-th input (UTXO being consumed)
- $O_j$ = value of the $j$-th output (new UTXO being created)
- $f$ = transaction fee paid to the miner
- $n, m$ = number of inputs, outputs respectively

**Derivation Logic:**
1. Each input $I_i$ references a previous transaction's output that has *not yet been spent* (i.e., is in the UTXO set).
2. When a transaction is broadcast, every full node removes the consumed UTXOs from its in-memory UTXO set.
3. The new outputs are added to the UTXO set.
4. The fee $f$ is implicit — it is the difference between input sum and output sum. The miner who includes the block collects $f$.

**Example:** Alice sends 0.5 BTC to Bob, referencing a previous UTXO of exactly 0.5 BTC. She sets output = 0.5 BTC to Bob, fees = 0. The transaction is valid because $0.5 = 0.5 + 0$.

If Alice had referenced a 0.5 BTC UTXO but created outputs summing to 0.6 BTC, the transaction would be **rejected by every honest node** because $0.5 \neq 0.6 + f$ for any $f \ge 0$.

## 3.4 Merkle Tree Verification — Analytic Walkthrough

To verify that a specific transaction $T_x$ is included in block $B$, a Simplified Payment Verification (SPV) client only needs:
- The block header (containing the Merkle root $M_{root}$)
- A **Merkle path** consisting of $\log_2(n)$ sibling hashes

**Step-by-step verification:**
1. SPV client requests block header from a full node.
2. SPV client requests the Merkle path for $T_x$ from a full node.
3. Client computes $h_{T_x} = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(T_x))$.
4. Client combines $h_{T_x}$ with the first sibling hash $s_1$ to get $h_1 = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(h_{T_x} \, \vert\, s_1))$.
5. Client combines $h_1$ with $s_2$ to get $h_2 = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(h_1 \, \vert\, s_2))$.
6. This continues for $\log_2(n)$ levels.
7. Final result $h_{top}$ is compared with $M_{root}$ stored in the block header.

If $h_{top} = M_{root}$, the transaction's inclusion in that block is **cryptographically proven** with probability $1 - 2^{-256}$ of forgery.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Payment Flow — Online vs. Blockchain Comparison

The following diagram contrasts a **traditional centralized online payment** with a **Bitcoin blockchain payment**, highlighting where the double-spending prevention actually occurs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD["Traditional Centralized Payment"]
        A1[Alice] -->|1. Initiate payment| B1[Bank Server]
        B1 -->|2. Verify balance| DB1[(Central Ledger)]
        DB1 -->|3. Debit Alice| B1
        B1 -->|4. Credit Merchant| M1[Merchant]
        M1 -->|5. Ship goods| A1
    end

    subgraph BCH["Bitcoin Blockchain Payment"]
        A2[Alice] -->|1. Sign transaction| NET[Bitcoin P2P Network]
        NET -->|2. Broadcast to nodes| N1[Full Node 1]
        NET -->|2. Broadcast to nodes| N2[Full Node 2]
        NET -->|2. Broadcast to nodes| N3[Full Node 3]
        N1 -->|3. Validate UTXO and signature| POOL[Memory Pool]
        N2 -->|3. Validate UTXO and signature| POOL
        N3 -->|3. Validate UTXO and signature| POOL
        POOL -->|4. Miner selects tx into block| MINER[Miner Solves PoW]
        MINER -->|5. Block broadcast| CHAIN[(Global Blockchain)]
        CHAIN -->|6. Confirmations accumulate| M2[Merchant accepts]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** In the traditional model, the bank is the **single point of trust and authority**. In the blockchain model, **every full node independently performs the same validation**, and the *longest valid chain* is the consensus truth. Double-spending is impossible because as soon as Alice's UTXO is consumed in the confirmed chain, it disappears from the UTXO set of every other node.

## 4.2 Double-Spend Attack — Race Attack Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Att as Attacker
    participant Net as Bitcoin Network
    participant Mer as Merchant
    participant Hon as Honest Miners

    Note over Att: Owns 1 BTC in address X
    Att->>Net: Tx1: Send 1 BTC to Merchant (signed)
    Att->>Net: Tx2: Send 1 BTC back to Attacker (conflicting)
    Net-->>Mer: Tx1 received, broadcast
    Mer-->>Mer: Accepts with 0 confirmations
    Mer->>Mer: Ships goods
    Hon->>Net: Confirm Tx1 in Block N
    Note over Att,Hon: Attacker hopes Tx2 confirms first
    alt Honest chain wins
        Hon->>Net: Extend chain with Tx1
        Net->>Mer: Tx1 confirmed permanently
        Note over Mer: SAFE - Attacker paid, goods shipped
    else Attacker chain wins (rare)
        Att->>Net: Release pre-mined chain with Tx2
        Net->>Mer: Tx1 invalidated (orphan)
        Note over Mer: ATTACK SUCCEEDED - Attacker has goods AND coins
    end
```

**Key insight from the sequence diagram:** The *only* mitigation is **time** — the merchant must wait for confirmations. Each confirmation is a block that an attacker must out-race.

## 4.3 UTXO Lifecycle — How Coins Are Spent Once

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unspent: Coinbase / previous tx output
    Unspent --> Unspent: Available in UTXO set
    Unspent --> Spent: Referenced as input in new tx
    Spent --> Unspent: Becomes output of new tx in other address
    note right of Unspent
        Every full node maintains
        the live UTXO set in memory
    end note
    note right of Spent
        Removal from UTXO set
        is atomic across the network
        once block is confirmed
    end note
```

**Why this matters for double-spending:** The moment a UTXO is referenced in a confirmed transaction, it is **deleted from the global UTXO set** of every honest node. Any second transaction trying to spend the *same* UTXO is rejected with a `double-spend` error by every validating node.

## 4.4 Merkle Tree — Transaction Inclusion Proof

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TX1["Tx A"]:::leaf --> H1["H_AB"]:::mid
    TX2["Tx B"]:::leaf --> H1
    TX3["Tx C"]:::leaf --> H2["H_CD"]:::mid
    TX4["Tx D"]:::leaf --> H2
    H1 --> H3["H_ABCD"]:::root
    H2 --> H3
    H3 -.->|Stored in| HDR[Block Header]:::header

    classDef leaf fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#aa9,color:#000
    classDef root fill:#cde8ff,stroke:#369,color:#000
    classDef header fill:#e2f0d9,stroke:#693,color:#000
```

This compact representation is what allows **light clients (SPV)** to verify a transaction's inclusion with only $\log_2(n)$ hashes — making mobile wallets feasible without downloading the entire blockchain.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`
**Define the double-spending problem in digital currencies. Why is it not a concern in physical cash transactions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Double-spending** refers to the act of spending the same digital token (or unit of value) more than once. In physical cash, a coin or note handed to one merchant cannot simultaneously be held by another, because physical objects cannot be perfectly duplicated without specialized effort. In the digital realm, however, information (such as a string of bits representing a coin) can be **trivially copied** with zero marginal cost. Without a centralized authority to track ownership, a digital cash system faces the challenge of ensuring that each unit of value is consumed exactly once. Bitcoin solves this through a **distributed, append-only ledger (blockchain)** maintained by consensus among all nodes, where every spent coin is removed from the global Unspent Transaction Output (UTXO) set and cannot be re-referenced in a future valid transaction.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**List and briefly explain the four essential properties that a digital currency must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A viable digital currency must satisfy the following four properties, as enumerated in foundational literature on electronic cash:

1. **Permanence** — Once a transaction is committed, it cannot be reversed or altered by any party, including the system operator. *(1 mark)*
2. **Security** — Only the rightful owner of funds (holder of the private key) can authorize a transaction; unauthorized creation, forgery, or modification must be infeasible. *(1 mark)*
3. **Atomicity** — A transaction must be all-or-nothing: either the full value is transferred, or no transfer occurs. Partial states are not permitted. *(0.5 marks)*
4. **Verifiability** — Any third party (merchant, auditor, regulator) can independently verify the validity of a transaction without trusting the transacting parties. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **double-spending problem** in digital cash systems. Describe the two broad paradigms (centralized vs. decentralized) for online payments and illustrate how a centralized system inherently prevents double-spending. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a clear block diagram, describe the **structure of a Bitcoin transaction** and the role of the **UTXO model** in preventing double-spending. Explain what happens when two conflicting transactions are broadcast simultaneously. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Double-spending is the unique failure mode of digital cash where a single unit of value is spent more than once by exploiting the ease of digital duplication.

**Centralized Online Payments (3 marks):**
In a centralized model, a trusted third party (e.g., a bank, Visa, PayPal) maintains a **central ledger** of all account balances. When Alice wishes to pay Bob:
- Alice's bank debits her account by the payment amount.
- Bob's bank credits his account.
- The bank's server is the **single point of truth**.

Double-spending is prevented because the central server:
- Atomically checks Alice's balance before approving the payment.
- Maintains transactional ACID properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability).
- Rejects any payment that would result in a negative balance.

**Decentralized Online Payments (2 marks):**
In a P2P model (e.g., Bitcoin), there is **no central server**. Instead, every node maintains a copy of the ledger, and consensus rules determine the canonical state. This is harder because:
- There is no single authority to enforce balance checks.
- Network latency means different nodes may see transactions in different orders.
- Malicious actors can broadcast conflicting transactions.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Centralized systems solve double-spending through trusted authority, while decentralized systems must solve it through cryptographic and consensus mechanisms — the central innovation of blockchain technology.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Bitcoin Transaction Structure (3 marks):**
A Bitcoin transaction consists of:
- **Inputs** — References to previous transaction outputs (UTXOs) being consumed, along with unlocking scripts (signatures) proving ownership.
- **Outputs** — New UTXOs being created, with locking scripts specifying the conditions (public key hash) under which they can be spent in the future.
- **Metadata** — Version number, locktime, and other fields.

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
A typical block diagram shows: `TxID → Inputs (with ScriptSig) → Outputs (with ScriptPubKey + Value) → Fee = Σinputs − Σoutputs`.

**UTXO Model and Double-Spend Prevention (2 marks):**
Each full node maintains a **UTXO set** — the set of all transaction outputs that have been created but not yet spent. When a transaction is received:
1. The node checks that every input references a UTXO in the current set.
2. If valid, the referenced UTXOs are removed and new outputs are added.
3. The sum of inputs must equal the sum of outputs plus the transaction fee.

This atomic removal from the UTXO set means a coin can be spent **only once** across the network.

**Conflicting Transactions Scenario (1 mark):**
If Alice broadcasts two transactions that both reference the same UTXO, only the **first one to be confirmed in a block** is valid. The second is rejected by every honest node with a `double-spend` error. The probability of a successful double-spend attack depends on confirmation count and attacker hash power.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** What is a **51% attack** in the context of blockchain? Derive the **probability formula** for the success of a double-spend attack by an attacker controlling fraction $p$ of the network hash power when the merchant waits for $z$ confirmations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An attacker controls $p = 0.30$ of the total hash power. A merchant waits for $z = 6$ confirmations before accepting payment. Calculate the probability of a successful double-spend attack. Compare this with the probability if the merchant waits for $z = 12$ confirmations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition of 51% Attack (1 mark):**
A 51% attack occurs when a single entity (or colluding group) controls more than 50% of a blockchain network's total hashing power. This majority allows the attacker to:
- Privately mine an alternative chain.
- Censor or reverse transactions.
- Double-spend their own coins.

**Setup of the Probability Model (2 marks):**
Let:
- $p$ = attacker's hash power fraction
- $q = 1 - p$ = honest network's hash power
- $z$ = number of confirmations the merchant waits
- $X_t$ = the gap (in blocks) between the honest chain and the attacker's private chain

The attack succeeds if $X_t$ ever reaches 0 (attacker catches up).

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Each block found is independent. The probability that the *next* block extends the attacker's chain is $p$; the honest chain with probability $q$. This is a biased random walk.

Using the **martingale stopping theorem** for a random walk with absorbing barrier at 0 and starting point $z$, the probability of ever reaching 0 is:
$$
P_{\text{success}} = \left(\dfrac{p}{q}\right)^{z} = \left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right)^{z}
$$

This is valid when $p < q$ (honest majority). For $p > q$, the probability approaches 1.

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
The probability decreases **exponentially** with $z$. This is the mathematical basis for the rule-of-thumb that **6 confirmations** is sufficient for high-value Bitcoin transactions.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $p = 0.30$, $q = 0.70$, $z = 6$ and $z = 12$.

**Case 1: $z = 6$ (1 mark for setup, 2 marks for calculation):**
$$
P_6 = \left(\dfrac{0.30}{0.70}\right)^{6} = \left(\dfrac{3}{7}\right)^{6}
$$
$$
P_6 = (0.4286)^{6}
$$
$$
P_6 = 0.4286^2 \times 0.4286^2 \times 0.4286^2
$$
$$
P_6 = 0.1837 \times 0.1837 \times 0.1837
$$
$$
P_6 = 0.1837^2 \times 0.1837 = 0.03375 \times 0.1837
$$
$$
P_6 \approx 6.20 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.620\%
$$

**Case 2: $z = 12$ (1 mark for setup, 2 marks for calculation):**
$$
P_{12} = \left(\dfrac{0.30}{0.70}\right)^{12} = (P_6)^2
$$
$$
P_{12} = (0.00620)^{2}
$$
$$
P_{12} \approx 3.84 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.00384\%
$$

**Comparison and Conclusion (1 mark):**
Doubling the confirmation count from 6 to 12 reduces the attack success probability by a factor of approximately **160×** (from ~0.62% to ~0.0038%). This exponential decay is the fundamental security property of Proof-of-Work consensus.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "transaction" with "coin"** — Bitcoin doesn't have "coins" floating around. It has UTXOs. Examiners specifically look for UTXO understanding. Using "coin" loosely costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $q = 1 - p$ in the 51% formula** — Students often write $\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)^{z}$ but forget to substitute $q = 1 - p$. This is a guaranteed half-mark loss.
> 3. **Not stating the precondition $p < q$** — The formula is only valid for honest majority. Writing it without this assumption is incomplete.
> 4. **Skipping the 4 properties of digital currency** — A favorite 3-mark question. Memorize: **Permanence, Security, Atomicity, Verifiability** in that order.
> 5. **Confusing block confirmation with network confirmation** — A block is mined; a *transaction* receives confirmations as subsequent blocks are added. Use the correct terminology.
> 6. **Writing "Bitcoin prevents double-spending"** without explaining *how* — Always say: **UTXO deletion + longest-chain consensus + exponential cost of rewriting history**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Payments and Double Spending**

- 🔑 **Double-spending** = spending the same digital token more than once. Unique to digital cash; physical cash is immune.
- 🔑 **Four properties of digital currency:** **Permanence, Security, Atomicity, Verifiability**.
- 🔑 **Two payment paradigms:** Centralized (trust a bank) vs. Decentralized (P2P, consensus-based).
- 🔑 **UTXO model** is the core double-spend prevention mechanism. Every full node maintains the UTXO set; a spent output is atomically removed.
- 🔑 **Transaction validity equation:** $\sum I = \sum O + \text{fees}$ (must hold exactly).
- 🔑 **51% attack probability formula (for $p < q$):** $P_{\text{success}} = \left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right)^{z}$
- 🔑 **6 confirmations rule-of-thumb** for high-value Bitcoin payments (≈ 60 minutes).
- 🔑 **Attack taxonomy:** Race attack, Finney attack, Vector76 attack, 51% attack — each mitigated by waiting for confirmations.
- 🔑 **Difficulty adjustment** happens every **2016 blocks** to maintain ~10-minute block time.
- 🔑 **Merkle trees** enable SPV (Simplified Payment Verification) with only $\log_2(n)$ hashes.
- 🔑 **Bitcoin's block reward (post-2024 halving)** = **3.125 BTC**, with total supply capped at **21 million BTC**.
- 🔑 **Coinbase maturity** = **100 blocks** before newly mined coins can be spent.
- 🔑 **Key insight:** Double-spending is not prevented by making coins uncopyable; it is prevented by making the *ledger* tamper-evident through Proof-of-Work.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FORTH - the Precursor for Bitcoin Scripting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** FORTH - the Precursor for Bitcoin Scripting # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of FORTH ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **FORTH** is a procedural, **stack-based**, **concatenative programming language** designed by **Charles H. Moore** in the late **1960s** (circa **1968–1970**). It combines an interpreter, a compiler, and an interactive shell into a single unified environment, where programs are constructed by composing small reusable procedures called **"words"**. FORTH's most distinctive feature is its use of **Reverse Polish Notation (RPN)** and a **virtual operand stack** for parameter passing — a paradigm that directly influenced the design of **Bitcoin Script**, the Forth-like non-Turing-complete scripting language used in the Bitcoin protocol by **Satoshi Nakamoto** (Whitepaper, 2008). In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT522)**, FORTH is studied specifically because it serves as the **architectural and conceptual precursor** to **Bitcoin Script** — the language in which Bitcoin transaction validation conditions (locking/unlocking scripts) are written. --- ## 1.2 Key Terminology Mapped to KTU Syllabus | KTU Term | Meaning in FORTH Context | Relevance to Bitcoin Script | |----------|--------------------------|------------------------------| | **Stack** | LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structure holding operands | Bitcoin Script uses an identical **execution stack** | | **Word** | A named routine (the unit of program composition in FORTH) | Bitcoin Script has **opcodes** (OP_DUP, OP_HASH160, OP_CHECKSIG) | | **Dictionary** | Linked-list of word definitions (for execution lookup) | Bitcoin Script's opcode lookup table | | **RPN** | Postfix notation: `2 3 +` instead of `2 + 3` | Bitcoin Script evaluates expressions in **postfix** order | | **Concatenative** | Programs are formed by **stringing words together** | Bitcoin Scripts are concatenated opcode sequences | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The Stack of Plates in a Cafeteria:** > > Imagine a cafeteria plate dispenser. You can only: > 1. **PUSH** a new plate onto the top of the stack. > 2. **POP** the top plate off to use it. > 3. **PEEK** at the top plate without removing it. > > You **cannot** access the plate at the bottom directly. This is exactly how FORTH (and Bitcoin Script) handles data. When you write `2 3 +` in FORTH: > - You **push 2** onto the stack. > - You **push 3** onto the stack. > - The `+` operator **pops both**, adds them, and **pushes the result (5)** back. > > The closest real-world example most students recognize is the **Hewlett-Packard RPN scientific calculator** (HP-12C, HP-48) — it is literally a hardware implementation of FORTH-like thinking. --- ## 1.4 Why FORTH Matters for Blockchain / Bitcoin > [!NOTE] > **Satoshi Nakamoto's design choice for Bitcoin Script** was deliberate. A scripting language for a decentralized monetary network **must**: > - Be **deterministic** (every node must reach the same result). > - Be **non-Turing complete** (no infinite loops → bounded execution → **no DoS attacks**). > - Be **simple and auditable** (humans must be able to read it). > - Run on a **stack-based virtual machine** (minimal hardware abstraction, easy to sandbox). > > FORTH already embodied **all four** of these properties decades earlier. Thus, Bitcoin Script is essentially a **stripped-down, security-hardened descendant of FORTH**. --- ## 1.5 Historical Timeline of FORTH (Box-Exam Relevant) - **1968** — Charles H. Moore begins FORTH at Mohasco Industries for controlling telescopes. - **1970** — First complete implementation on an **IBM 1130** minicomputer. - **1971** — **FORTH Inc.** founded (the world's oldest surviving software company). - **1977** — **FORTH-79** standard published (first ANSI-affiliated standard). - **1983** — **FORTH-83** standard. - **1994** — **ANSI X3.215-1994** standard (official ANSI FORTH). - **2008** — Bitcoin whitepaper released; Bitcoin Script uses FORTH-like stack model. - **2010+** — **Bitcoin's `script` interpreter** in `bitcoin/src/script/` is openly a FORTH descendant. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The FORTH Computational Model (Operational Breakdown) FORTH's architecture is built on **four cooperating components**, each of which has a direct analogue in Bitcoin Script. ### Component 1 — The Parameter (Data) Stack - A simple integer-addressed memory region. - **Operations allowed**: only PUSH, POP, PEEK, SWAP, DUP, OVER, ROT, DROP, NIP, TUCK. - **Direct analogue**: Bitcoin Script's `script.h` `stack` (a `std::vector>`). ### Component 2 — The Return (Control) Stack - Tracks subroutine (word) calls. Every time a new word is invoked, a **return frame** is pushed. - **Bitcoin Script analogue**: Bitcoin Script is **flat** (no subroutine calls), so this stack is mostly absent — a security simplification. ### Component 3 — The Dictionary - A **linked list of word headers**, each containing: - A **name** (for human-readable lookup), - A **code field** (pointer to executable code or another word), - A **parameter field** (for data words). - **Bitcoin Script analogue**: the **opcode dispatch table** indexed by opcode byte value (0x01–0xFF, with many deliberately disabled for safety). ### Component 4 — The Input Buffer / Text Interpreter - Reads source text, tokenizes by whitespace, and either: 1. **Executes** the token if found in the dictionary, or 2. Treats it as a **literal number** and pushes it onto the stack. - **Bitcoin Script analogue**: the `EvalScript()` function in Bitcoin Core — it iterates opcodes byte-by-byte, pushing numeric literals (when prefixed by `OP_PUSHBYTES_N`) and executing opcodes. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Operation Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **must-memorize cheat sheet** for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions on FORTH. The "Stack Notation" column uses the convention `top-of-stack → rightmost element`, e.g., `( a b -- c )` means *"input: a on top, b below; output: c on top"*. This is the **standard FORTH stack-effect comment** (used in every FORTH textbook and in the Bitcoin source code comments). | FORTH Word | Bitcoin Script Opcode | Stack Effect `( before -- after )` | Purpose | |------------|-----------------------|-----------------------------------|---------| | `+` | `OP_ADD` | `( a b -- a+b )` | Pops two, pushes sum | | `-` | `OP_SUB` | `( a b -- a-b )` | Pops two, pushes difference | | `*` | `OP_MUL` (disabled) | `( a b -- a*b )` | Multiplication (Bitcoin disabled to limit DoS) | | `DUP` | `OP_DUP` | `( a -- a a )` | Duplicates top element | | `DROP` | `OP_DROP` | `( a -- )` | Removes top element | | `SWAP` | `OP_SWAP` | `( a b -- b a )` | Swaps top two | | `OVER` | `OP_OVER` | `( a b -- a b a )` | Copies 2nd to top | | `ROT` | `OP_ROT` | `( a b c -- b c a )` | Rotates 3 elements | | `2DUP` | `OP_2DUP` | `( a b -- a b a b )` | Duplicates top pair | | `@` (fetch) | *(none — security)* | `( addr -- value )` | Memory read (forbidden in Bitcoin) | | `!` (store) | *(none — security)* | `( value addr -- )` | Memory write (forbidden in Bitcoin) | | `IF / THEN` | `OP_IF / OP_ENDIF` | `( flag -- )` | Conditional execution (one branch only) | | `:` ... `;` | *(no equivalent)* | n/a | **Word definition** (no user-defined words in Bitcoin) | > [!NOTE] > **Critical observation for KTU exams:** Bitcoin Script **deliberately removed** looping (`BEGIN ... UNTIL`), user-defined words, and arbitrary memory access. It is a **deliberately crippled** FORTH — a design choice Satoshi called "intentionally not Turing-complete" to guarantee script termination. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of the FORTH Model | Application Domain | Use of FORTH-Like Stack Model | |--------------------|-------------------------------| | **Embedded / Real-Time Systems** | FORTH was the OS of choice for NASA's **Deep Space 1** ion-propulsion probe (1998) and many on-board satellite control systems — extreme **memory efficiency** and **determinism** | | **Boot Loaders** | Open Firmware (`OpenBoot`) on SPARC/Sun workstations was FORTH-based | | **Cryptocurrency** | **Bitcoin Script** (and **Ethereum's EVM** is also stack-based, though different) | | **Calculator firmware** | HP RPN calculators, SwissMicros DM42, etc. | | **Industrial Control** | Valve & motor controllers in 1980s–90s factory automation | --- ## 2.4 Worked Mini-Example (FOUNDATION for Part B questions) Evaluate the FORTH phrase: **`2 3 4 + *`** **Step-by-step using the stack discipline:** | Step | Token Read | Stack (top → right) | Explanation | |------|------------|---------------------|-------------| | 0 | (start) | `( )` | Empty stack | | 1 | `2` | `( 2 )` | Literal push | | 2 | `3` | `( 2 3 )` | Literal push | | 3 | `4` | `( 2 3 4 )` | Literal push | | 4 | `+` | `( 2 7 )` | `+` pops 4 and 3, pushes 7 | | 5 | `*` | `( 14 )` | `*` pops 7 and 2, pushes 14 | **Result = 14**. The same evaluation order — **strict left-to-right, postfix** — is exactly how Bitcoin Script's `EvalScript()` walks opcodes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Definitions & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why FORTH's Postfix Model Maps Cleanly to Bitcoin Script ### Step 1 — Establish the universal evaluation model Both FORTH and Bitcoin Script execute using a **state machine** defined as the tuple: $$ S_t \;=\; \langle \mathbf{Stack}_t,\ \mathbf{State}_t,\ \mathbf{PC}_t \rangle $$ Where: - $\mathbf{Stack}_t$ is the contents of the operand stack at step $t$, - $\mathbf{State}_t$ is auxiliary state (e.g., alt-stack, condition flag in Bitcoin), - $\mathbf{PC}_t$ is the **program counter** (the index of the next opcode to execute). ### Step 2 — State transition function Each opcode (or FORTH word) implements a transition function: $$ \delta : \mathbf{Stack}_t \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{Stack}_{t+1} $$ with the constraint that for **deterministic execution**: $$ \forall \ t,\qquad \delta(\mathbf{Stack}_t) \;=\; \mathbf{Stack}_{t+1} $$ A consensus network (Bitcoin) requires that **every node** computes the same final stack, so this determinism is **non-negotiable**. ### Step 3 — Derive the postfix evaluation rule Given a sequence of tokens $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$, define the working stack $\mathbf{Stack}^{(k)}$ after processing the first $k$ tokens: $$ \mathbf{Stack}^{(k)} \;=\; \begin{cases} \mathbf{Stack}^{(k-1)} \,\Vert\, n & \text{if } T_k = n \text{ (numeric literal, push)} \\ \delta_{\text{op}}(\mathbf{Stack}^{(k-1)}) & \text{if } T_k = \text{op (operator word)} \\ \end{cases} $$ Here $\Vert$ denotes **stack-push** and $\delta_{\text{op}}$ is the operator-specific transition. This is the **same recurrence** used in classic **Dijkstra two-stack shunting-yard** postfix evaluation — a direct theoretical ancestor. ### Step 4 — Apply the rule to the Bitcoin P2PKH script The standard **Pay-to-PubKey-Hash** Bitcoin script verification is: $$ \textbf{scriptSig} \;\Vert\; \textbf{scriptPubKey} \;=\; \langle Sig,\ Pk \rangle \;\Vert\; \langle OP\_DUP,\ OP\_HASH160,\ addr,\ OP\_EQUALVERIFY,\ OP\_CHECKSIG \rangle $$ Let us trace the execution step-by-step. **Initial stack (after scriptSig pushes):** $$ \mathbf{Stack}_0 \;=\; \langle \texttt{Sig},\ \texttt{Pk} \rangle $$ **Step 1 — `OP_DUP`** (pops top, pushes two copies of it): $$ \delta_{\text{DUP}}(\langle \texttt{Sig},\ \texttt{Pk} \rangle) \;=\; \langle \texttt{Sig},\ \texttt{Pk},\ \texttt{Pk} \rangle $$ **Step 2 — `OP_HASH160`** (pops top, pushes 160-bit hash using SHA-256 then RIPEMD-160): $$ \mathbf{Stack}_2 \;=\; \langle \texttt{Sig},\ \texttt{Pk},\ H_{160}(\texttt{Pk}) \rangle $$ **Step 3 — Push ``** (the 20-byte address literal embedded in scriptPubKey): $$ \mathbf{Stack}_3 \;=\; \langle \texttt{Sig},\ \texttt{Pk},\ H_{160}(\texttt{Pk}),\ \texttt{addr} \rangle $$ **Step 4 — `OP_EQUALVERIFY`** (pops two, aborts if unequal; otherwise pushes nothing): $$ \text{Require: } H_{160}(\texttt{Pk}) \;\equiv\; \texttt{addr} $$ Upon success: $$ \mathbf{Stack}_4 \;=\; \langle \texttt{Sig},\ \texttt{Pk} \rangle $$ **Step 5 — `OP_CHECKSIG`** (pops top two, verifies signature, pushes `TRUE`/`FALSE`): $$ \delta_{\text{CHECKSIG}}(\langle \texttt{Sig},\ \texttt{Pk} \rangle) \;=\; \langle \mathbf{1} \rangle \;\; \text{ iff } \text{Verify}(\texttt{Sig},\ \texttt{Pk}) = \text{TRUE} $$ **Final requirement for transaction validity:** $$ \mathbf{Stack}_{\text{final}} \;=\; \langle \mathbf{1} \rangle \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Transaction is VALID} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** This 5-step trace is the **gold-standard answer template** for any 7-mark "Explain Bitcoin Script execution using the FORTH stack model" question. Memorize the table form. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Why Bitcoin Script is *Intentionally* Non-Turing-Complete By Rice's theorem, any non-trivial semantic property of a Turing-complete language is **undecidable**. In particular, halting is undecidable. Therefore: $$ \text{If Bitcoin Script were Turing-complete,} \quad \exists \text{ script that does not halt in finite time.} $$ A non-halting script would be executed by **every full node forever**, enabling a **Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack** against the network. **Design fix (Satoshi's choice):** $$ \text{Bitcoin Script} \;=\; \text{FORTH} \;\setminus\; \{\text{loops, user-defined jumps, unbounded memory}\} $$ Therefore every script provably halts in $O(n)$ where $n$ = script length. This is a **provable upper bound on computation cost** — the same property that makes FORTH suitable for **hard real-time embedded systems** in aerospace. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (FORTH vs. Bitcoin Script) | Property | FORTH | Bitcoin Script | KTU Exam Weight | |----------|-------|----------------|------------------| | **Stack-based execution** | Yes | Yes | ★★★★★ | | **Postfix (RPN) syntax** | Yes | Yes | ★★★★★ | | **Turing complete** | Yes (with `DO...LOOP`, `BEGIN...UNTIL`) | **No** (intentionally) | ★★★★★ | | **User-defined words / functions** | Yes (via `:` ... `;`) | **No** | ★★★★ | | **Memory access (`@`/`!`)** | Yes | **No** (no RAM access) | ★★★★ | | **Loops** | Yes | **No** (only conditional `OP_IF`) | ★★★★★ | | **Determinism** | Yes (no implicit I/O in core) | Yes (mandatory for consensus) | ★★★★ | | **Standardized** | ANSI X3.215-1994 | Bitcoin Improvement Proposals (BIPs) | ★★★ | | **Used in production crypto** | No (legacy) | **Yes (Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin)** | ★★★★★ | | **Interpreter style** | Direct-threaded / indirect-threaded | Big-step opcode loop in Bitcoin Core | ★★ | --- ## 3.4 Stack Trace Practice (Essential for 14-mark answers) ### Example 1 — Evaluate `5 DUP * 1 -` | Step | Token | Stack (top → right) | Note | |------|-------|---------------------|------| | 0 | — | `( )` | Empty | | 1 | `5` | `( 5 )` | Literal push | | 2 | `DUP` | `( 5 5 )` | Duplicates top | | 3 | `*` | `( 25 )` | Pops two, pushes 25 | | 4 | `1` | `( 25 1 )` | Literal push | | 5 | `-` | `( 24 )` | Pops two, pushes 24 | **Result = 24** (i.e., $5^2 - 1 = 24$). ### Example 2 — Bitcoin multisig trace (M-of-2) A 2-of-2 multisig redeem script is: $$ \langle 2,\ Pk_1,\ Pk_2,\ 2,\ OP\_CHECKMULTISIG \rangle $$ With scriptSig providing signatures $\langle Sig_1, Sig_2 \rangle$: **Step 1 — Initial state:** $$ \mathbf{Stack}_0 \;=\; \langle Sig_2,\ Sig_1 \rangle $$ **Step 2 — Push literals `2`, `Pk1`, `Pk2`, `2`:** $$ \mathbf{Stack}_4 \;=\; \langle Sig_2,\ Sig_1,\ 2,\ Pk_1,\ Pk_2,\ 2 \rangle $$ **Step 3 — `OP_CHECKMULTISIG`** pops one dummy `OP_0` (or reads it from the alt-stack requirement), then the `n` (here 2), then `n` pubkeys, then `m` (here 2), then expects `m` signatures: $$ \text{Result: } \mathbf{Stack}_{\text{final}} \;=\; \langle \mathbf{1} \rangle \;\;\text{iff each } Sig_i \text{ is valid for } Pk_i $$ The `OP_CHECKMULTISIG` is famously **off-by-one** (pops one extra stack element due to a historical bug) — a classic **KTU viva / Part A question**. --- ## 3.5 The FORTH Inner Interpreter in C — Conceptual Walk-Through > [!NOTE] > FORTH's **inner interpreter** (the tight inner loop that executes threaded code) is conceptually the same as Bitcoin's `EvalScript()`. Pseudocode: $$ \text{do forever:} \quad \begin{cases} \text{nextInstruction} = \text{readCodeWord}(W) \\ W = \text{execute}(W) \\ \text{if } W = 0 \text{ (e.g., OP\_RETURN, FORTH BYE): break} \end{cases} $$ Where `W` is the instruction pointer threaded through a linked list of code-field addresses. The two interpreters are structurally **isomorphic**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — FORTH System Architecture vs. Bitcoin Script Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **four-component FORTH architecture** alongside its **Bitcoin Script counterpart**, illustrating the one-to-one conceptual mapping. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FORTH_SYS["FORTH Virtual Machine (1970s)"] direction TB TEXTIN["Text Input Buffer
Source code lines"] DIC["Dictionary
Linked list of word headers"] CODEF["Code Field
Threaded word pointers"] PSTK["Parameter Stack
Integer / cell arithmetic"] RSTK["Return Stack
Word call frames"] TIB["Text Interpreter
NEXT routine"] OUT1["Console / I/O"] TEXTIN --> TIB DIC --> TIB TIB --> PSTK TIB --> RSTK TIB --> CODEF CODEF --> PSTK TIB --> OUT1 end subgraph BTC_SYS["Bitcoin Script Engine (2008-present)"] direction TB SCR["Transaction Scripts
scriptSig + scriptPubKey"] OPD["Opcode Dispatch Table
0x51 OP_1, 0x76 OP_DUP, ..."] EVM["EvalScript Function
step-and-evaluate loop"] BSTK["Execution Stack
vector of byte vectors"] ASTK["Alt-Stack OP_TOALTSTACK
Disabled in most scripts"] VER["Result Validator
final element must be TRUE"] SCR --> EVM OPD --> EVM EVM --> BSTK EVM --> ASTK EVM --> VER end TIB -. "conceptual mirror" .-> EVM PSTK -. "one-to-one" .-> BSTK DIC -. "dispatch table" .-> OPD RSTK -. "removed for security" .-> ASTK ``` **Interpretation:** Notice the deliberate simplifications — Bitcoin Script has **no return stack** (no subroutines), **no general I/O**, and a **flat opcode table** instead of a mutable dictionary. These are **security-driven deletions**, not oversights. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Stack Trace of `2 3 4 + *` (Visualized) ```mermaid flowchart LR S0["Stack: empty"] S1["Stack: 2"] S2["Stack: 2 3"] S3["Stack: 2 3 4"] S4["Stack: 2 7"] S5["Stack: 14"] T0["Input: 2
(literal push)"] T1["Input: 3
(literal push)"] T2["Input: 4
(literal push)"] T3["Input: +
OP_ADD"] T4["Input: *
OP_MUL"] S0 -->|T0| S1 S1 -->|T1| S2 S2 -->|T2| S3 S3 -->|T3| S4 S4 -->|T4| S5 ``` **Final stack top = 14**, matching our analytical derivation in §3.4. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Bitcoin P2PKH Execution Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User / Spender participant S as scriptSig participant P as scriptPubKey participant E as EvalScript Engine participant V as Validator U->>S: Provide Sig + PubKey S->>E: Push Sig, then Pk E->>E: OP_DUP (Stack: Sig Pk Pk) E->>E: OP_HASH160 (Stack: Sig Pk hash(Pk)) E->>P: Read embedded pubKeyHash P->>E: push pubKeyHash E->>E: OP_EQUALVERIFY (Stack: Sig Pk) [aborts if mismatch] E->>E: OP_CHECKSIG (verify Sig on tx using Pk) E->>V: Final stack top V-->>U: Accept if TRUE, Reject if FALSE ``` --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — FORTH Word Definition Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Source: : SQUARE DUP * ;"] B["Tokenizer
Splits on whitespace"] C["Colon Compiler
: enters compile mode"] D["Dictionary Header
Allocate new node"] E["Code Field
Points to DUP"] F["Parameter Field
Stores literal (for literals)"] G["; exits compile mode
back to interpret"] H["SQUARE registered
in dictionary"] I["User: 5 SQUARE"] J["Lookup SQUARE in dictionary"] K["Execute threaded code:
DUP then *"] L["Result: 25 on stack"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H H --> I --> J --> K --> L ``` **Bitcoin Script equivalent:** **none**. The colon-semicolon word-definition mechanism is *exactly* the part Satoshi **removed** from FORTH to produce Bitcoin Script. This is a **frequently asked viva question**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define FORTH and explain why it is considered the precursor of Bitcoin Script.** **Model Answer (3 marks — expected ~60–80 words):** > **FORTH** is a stack-based, concatenative programming language developed by **Charles H. Moore** in the late 1960s. It uses **Reverse Polish Notation** and a **parameter stack** for all computation. It is considered the precursor of Bitcoin Script because Bitcoin's scripting language is essentially a **deliberately restricted subset of FORTH**: both share the **stack-based execution model**, **postfix (RPN) syntax**, and **deterministic single-pass interpreter**. Satoshi Nakamoto, when designing Bitcoin's transaction validation logic in 2008, used the same **concatenative, non-Turing-complete-by-design** architecture that FORTH had pioneered four decades earlier. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Q: List any three differences between FORTH and Bitcoin Script.** **Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per difference):** | # | FORTH | Bitcoin Script | |---|-------|----------------| | 1 | **Turing-complete** (supports `DO...LOOP`, `BEGIN...UNTIL`, recursion) | **Intentionally non-Turing-complete** (no loops) | | 2 | Allows **user-defined words** via `:` ... `;` | **No user-defined words** — only fixed opcodes | | 3 | Provides **direct memory access** via `@` (fetch) and `!` (store) | **No memory access** — operates only on the execution stack | **(3 marks — 1 for each pair)** --- ## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice Format) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Exam) format: each module question has an **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions carry **14 marks** with two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]` **Q: (a)** Explain the FORTH computational model in detail. Describe the role of the **parameter stack**, **return stack**, **dictionary**, and **text interpreter**, with diagrams. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast FORTH with Bitcoin Script. Why did Satoshi Nakamoto choose a FORTH-like model for Bitcoin transaction validation? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** The **FORTH computational model** is built around **four cooperating components**: 1. **Parameter (Data) Stack** [2 marks] - A **LIFO stack** holding operands for arithmetic and logic. - All FORTH words **pop their inputs from the top** and **push their results back**. - Example: in `2 3 +`, the `+` pops `3`, pops `2`, computes `2+3=5`, and pushes `5`. - The stack is the **only** means of inter-word data communication — no registers, no global variables (by convention). 2. **Return (Control) Stack** [1.5 marks] - Tracks **subroutine return addresses** when one word calls another. - Pushed on each `CALL`, popped on each `;` (semi-colon, word terminator). - Enables **recursion** and **nested word execution** (which Bitcoin Script has removed). 3. **Dictionary** [2 marks] - A **linked list of word headers**, each containing: a **name field** (a counted string for lookup), a **code field** (pointer to executable code or to a list of sub-words), and a **parameter field** (for data words). - Words are searched **most-recent-first**, allowing the programmer to **redefine** standard words locally (a powerful but dangerous feature — a famous "footgun" of FORTH). 4. **Text Interpreter** [1.5 marks] - Reads source text from the **input buffer** one whitespace-delimited token at a time. - For each token, the interpreter either **(i)** looks it up in the dictionary and executes it, or **(ii)** if not found, treats it as a **numeric literal** and pushes it onto the parameter stack. - This is the **inner loop** — identical in spirit to Bitcoin Script's `EvalScript()`. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Comparison Table:** [3 marks] | Aspect | FORTH | Bitcoin Script | |--------|-------|----------------| | Stack-based | Yes | Yes | | Postfix syntax | Yes | Yes | | Turing complete | Yes (with loops) | **No** (intentionally) | | User-defined words | Yes (`:` ... `;`) | **No** | | Memory access | Yes (`@`/`!`) | **No** | | Standard I/O | Yes | **No** | | Designed for | Embedded / real-time systems | Decentralized consensus | **Why Satoshi chose a FORTH-like model** [4 marks]: 1. **Determinism:** Every full node must reach the **same result** for the same transaction. A stack-based, side-effect-free interpreter guarantees this. 2. **Provable termination:** By **removing loops** and **unbounded memory**, every Bitcoin Script is guaranteed to halt in time proportional to its length. This prevents a class of **Denial-of-Service attacks** (an attacker embedding an infinite loop in a transaction). 3. **Minimal attack surface:** Without `@`/`!` (memory), without I/O, without user-defined words, the language has a **very small** set of opcodes to formally verify and audit. The total opcode space is just **256 byte values**, with many deliberately unused or disabled. 4. **Embedded-systems heritage:** FORTH had a 40-year track record in **mission-critical embedded systems** (NASA Deep Space 1, Open Firmware). Satoshi leveraged this proven, real-time-safe execution model. 5. **Stack-based simplicity:** The Bitcoin Core reference implementation's `EvalScript()` function is a clean, ~100-line loop — auditable, testable, and ported to every platform. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Module 2]` **Q: (a)** What is **Reverse Polish Notation (RPN)**? Evaluate the FORTH expression `3 4 2 * + 5 -` showing the stack at every step. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the step-by-step execution of the **Bitcoin P2PKH (Pay-to-PubKey-Hash) script** using the FORTH stack model. Show the contents of the execution stack at each opcode. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Reverse Polish Notation (RPN) [2 marks]:** RPN (also called **postfix notation**) is a mathematical notation in which **every operator follows all of its operands**. It eliminates the need for parentheses and follows a strict left-to-right evaluation. Named after the Polish logician **Jan Łukasiewicz** (1924), the *reverse* form was popularized by FORTH and by **Hewlett-Packard RPN calculators** (HP-12C, HP-48). The FORTH parameter stack is the **natural evaluation engine** for RPN. **Evaluation of `3 4 2 * + 5 -`** [5 marks — 1 per row]: | Step | Token | Stack (top → right) | Explanation | |------|-------|---------------------|-------------| | 0 | (start) | `( )` | Empty stack | | 1 | `3` | `( 3 )` | Literal push | | 2 | `4` | `( 3 4 )` | Literal push | | 3 | `2` | `( 3 4 2 )` | Literal push | | 4 | `*` | `( 3 8 )` | Pops 2 and 4, pushes 4×2 = 8 | | 5 | `+` | `( 11 )` | Pops 8 and 3, pushes 3+8 = 11 | | 6 | `5` | `( 11 5 )` | Literal push | | 7 | `-` | `( 6 )` | Pops 5 and 11, pushes 11−5 = 6 | **Result = 6**, which corresponds to the infix expression $(3 + 4 \times 2) - 5 = (3 + 8) - 5 = 6$. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** The **P2PKH script** is the most common Bitcoin transaction type. It consists of: - **scriptSig** (unlocking script): ` ` - **scriptPubKey** (locking script): `OP_DUP OP_HASH160 OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG` The Bitcoin engine **concatenates** scriptSig + scriptPubKey and evaluates the combined sequence on the stack. **Step-by-step execution trace** [6 marks — diagram + table]: | Step | Opcode / Data | Stack (top → right) | Effect | |------|---------------|---------------------|--------| | 0 | (initial) | `( )` | Empty | | 1 | `` | `( sig )` | scriptSig pushes signature | | 2 | `` | `( sig pk )` | scriptSig pushes public key | | 3 | `OP_DUP` | `( sig pk pk )` | Duplicates top → two copies of pk | | 4 | `OP_HASH160` | `( sig pk h(pk) )` | Hashes top pk: SHA-256 → RIPEMD-160 (20 bytes) | | 5 | `` | `( sig pk h(pk) addr )` | scriptPubKey pushes the embedded address | | 6 | `OP_EQUALVERIFY` | `( sig pk )` | Checks `h(pk) == addr`; aborts (marks tx invalid) if not | | 7 | `OP_CHECKSIG` | `( 1 )` | Verifies that `sig` is a valid ECDSA signature on the transaction using `pk`. Pushes `1` (TRUE) on success | | 8 | Final check | top = `1`? | If yes → **transaction is VALID**; if `0` (FALSE) or empty → **invalid** | **Diagram** [1 mark]: ``` scriptSig scriptPubKey ----------- ------------------------------------------------- OP_DUP OP_HASH160 OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG │ │ │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ push sig,pk DUP pk HASH160 pk push addr verify h==addr verify sig │ │ │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ [ sig pk ] [ sig pk pk ] [sig pk hpk] [sig pk hpk addr] [ sig pk ] [ 1 ] ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (where students lose 1–2 marks):** > 1. **Do NOT forget the `OP_EQUALVERIFY` step** — many students skip it or write it as a non-consuming opcode. It **consumes** two stack elements (or aborts). > 2. **Do NOT confuse `OP_HASH160` with `OP_HASH256`.** P2PKH uses **160-bit** (RIPEMD-160(SHA-256(x))). 256-bit is used for P2WSH and segwit witness programs. > 3. **Always state the final-stack-top-must-be-TRUE rule** at the end — this is the universal Bitcoin Script validity criterion. > 4. **In RPN evaluation questions, show the stack at every step** — the table form gets full marks; a single final answer with no intermediate steps gets only 2 of 5. > 5. **Do not write "FORTH is an obsolete language".** It is **still used in aerospace and embedded firmware** and is **alive** in the form of Bitcoin Script. Use the term "precursor" or "conceptual ancestor", not "obsolete ancestor". --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Most Important Things to Remember — FORTH as Precursor to Bitcoin Script** - 🔑 **FORTH = Stack-based + Postfix (RPN) + Concatenative** — these three properties are inherited **verbatim** by Bitcoin Script. - 🔑 **Charles H. Moore (1968–1970)** is the inventor. **Satoshi Nakamoto (2008)** is the inheritor (Bitcoin Script). - 🔑 **FORTH has a parameter stack and a return stack; Bitcoin Script has only an execution stack** (and an optional alt-stack for `OP_TOALTSTACK`/`OP_FROMALTSTACK`). - 🔑 **FORTH is Turing-complete; Bitcoin Script is NOT** — this is **by design**, to guarantee script termination and prevent DoS. - 🔑 **FORTH allows `:` ... `;` user-defined words; Bitcoin Script does not** — only fixed opcodes (0x01–0xFE; many disabled). - 🔑 **FORTH has loops (`BEGIN...UNTIL`, `DO...LOOP`); Bitcoin Script has only one-level conditionals** (`OP_IF`/`OP_NOTIF`/`OP_ELSE`/`OP_ENDIF`). - 🔑 **FORTH has memory access (`@`/`!`); Bitcoin Script has no general memory** — purely stack + alt-stack + script data. - 🔑 **RPN evaluation = strict left-to-right, postfix, using a LIFO stack** — both languages use the **same Dijkstra-style two-stack algorithm**. - 🔑 **Stack-effect comment convention `( before -- after )`** is universal — used in FORTH textbooks AND in Bitcoin Core source comments. - 🔑 **The `OP_CHECKMULTISIG` historical off-by-one bug** (pops one extra element) is a classic viva question. - 🔑 **Bitcoin validity rule**: after `EvalScript()`, the **top of the stack must be a non-zero "TRUE"** value (`OP_TRUE`, `0x01`, or any non-zero byte string post-`OP_VERIFY`). - 🔑 **Saturation integer arithmetic** is used in Bitcoin (no overflow; values wrap modulo $2^{256}$ for newer opcodes, modulo $2^{32}$ for legacy opcodes). - 🔑 **P2PKH is the canonical example** — `OP_DUP OP_HASH160 OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG` — and is the most frequently asked P2PKH execution trace in KTU exams. > **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Notes — Module 2 — FORTH: The Precursor for Bitcoin Scripting**

topics.title: Bitcoin Scripts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bitcoin Scripts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bitcoin Script** is a simple, stack-based, deliberately non-Turing-complete **Forth-like programming language** used in the Bitcoin protocol to lock and unlock transaction outputs. Each Bitcoin transaction contains two scripts: the **ScriptPubKey** (locking script, embedded in the transaction output) and the **ScriptSig** (unlocking script, provided in the input). When a node validates a spend, it concatenates the unlocking and locking scripts and executes them left-to-right on a stack. Successful execution requires that the **top of the stack evaluates to TRUE** (a non-zero value).

**Key Terminology in Exact KTU 2024 Form:**

- **Script (Bitcoin Script / `script`)**: A sequence of instructions (opcodes + data) executed by every full node during transaction validation.
- **ScriptPubKey (Locking Script)**: The script placed on an output, defining the conditions under which it can be spent.
- **ScriptSig (Unlocking Script)**: The script placed on an input, satisfying the conditions of the previous output's ScriptPubKey.
- **Stack**: A LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) data structure on which all script operations are performed.
- **Opcode (Operation Code)**: A single-byte instruction (e.g., `OP_DUP`, `OP_HASH160`, `OP_CHECKSIG`).
- **Witness Data**: In SegWit (BIP-141), signature/script data is segregated into a separate *witness* field, not counted toward the legacy block-size limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Bitcoin Script is **not Turing-complete** — it deliberately lacks loops (no `OP_WHILE`/`OP_FOR` in standard execution), making on-chain execution **deterministic and bounded**, which prevents infinite-loop denial-of-service attacks on the network.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Safe with a Puzzle Box" Model

Imagine you place a **gold coin** inside a **transparent safe** and lock it with a mechanical puzzle:

- The **Safe's lock mechanism** = `ScriptPubKey` (the conditions for spending).
- The **Key you produce to open it** = `ScriptSig` (the unlocking data, such as a signature).
- The **combination of puzzle + key** = the full script execution.
- The **mechanic checking if the safe opens cleanly** = every full node on the Bitcoin network.

If the puzzle requires *"show a signature proving ownership of address `1A1zP1eP5QGefi2DMPTfTL5SLmv7DivfNa`"*, then your key (ScriptSig) must contain exactly that cryptographic proof. If the mechanic's gears turn and end with a *click* (top of stack = TRUE), the spend is valid. Otherwise, the transaction is rejected by every honest node.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this elegant?** The "puzzle" itself is **publicly visible on the blockchain** (anyone can read ScriptPubKey), but it is **mathematically infeasible to forge the key** (ScriptSig) without knowing the private key. This is the foundation of trustless value transfer.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bitcoin Script

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum script size (legacy)** | **10,000 bytes** | Hard cap on combined unlocking + locking script for non-SegWit |
| **Maximum opcode count** | **201 opcodes** | Per script execution (consensus rule) |
| **Maximum number value (bytes)** | **4 bytes** | Integer values in scripts are limited to 4-byte signed (`int32_t`) |
| **Maximum stack size** | **1000 elements** | Each element ≤ 520 bytes (`MAX_SCRIPT_ELEMENT_SIZE`) |
| **OP_RETURN data carrier size** | **80 bytes** (post-taproot, witness-different) | Standard size for embedding arbitrary data |
| **Block weight (SegWit)** | **4,000,000 weight units** | Equivalent to ~1 MB legacy + ~3 MB witness |
| **Hash functions used** | **SHA-256, RIPEMD-160, HASH160 = SHA256 ∘ RIPEMD160** | Defined in BIP-141 / consensus rules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All values above are part of Bitcoin **consensus rules** — deviating from them makes a transaction/blocks invalid across the entire network. KTU questions frequently test these numerical limits.

---

## 1.4 The Two Halves of Every Bitcoin Transaction Script

### A. ScriptPubKey — The Locking Script

Embedded in the **transaction output (vout)**. Defines the *spending conditions*. It is **frozen at creation time** and can never be modified.

### B. ScriptSig — The Unlocking Script

Embedded in the **transaction input (vin)**. Provides the *proof* (signature, public key, hash preimage, etc.) required by the referenced output's ScriptPubKey.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation Flow (Concatenated Execution)**: A node performs
> `<ScriptSig> <ScriptPubKey>` → execute together. If the top stack element is **TRUE**, the input is valid. Historically, the bug **CVE-2010-5145** (where nodes mistakenly ran them in reverse order) was patched; the correct, current order is **unlocking first, locking second**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bitcoin Execution Environment — A Stack Machine

Bitcoin Script is a **reverse-Polish notation (postfix)** stack machine. There is **no register file, no memory model, no function-call stack** — only one linear stack.

### Step-by-Step Execution Logic

1. **Initialize** an empty stack (and an altstack available via `OP_TOALTSTACK` / `OP_FROMALTSTACK`).
2. **Concatenate** the ScriptSig (unlocking) and the ScriptPubKey (locking) into a single byte stream.
3. **Parse & Execute** each opcode from **left to right**:
   - **Data pushes** (e.g., a signature, public key, or hash) → pushed onto the stack as raw bytes.
   - **Opcodes** → pop arguments, perform an action, push a result (or multiple results).
4. **Final check**: After the last opcode, if the stack is **non-empty** AND the **top item** is a non-zero byte sequence (interpreting as little-endian signed integer, `true` ≡ ≠ 0), the script **succeeds**; otherwise, it **fails** and the input is rejected.
5. **All side effects** (e.g., signature verification using `OP_CHECKSIG`) are **deterministic** and depend only on the transaction, the script, and the spent output.

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Opcode Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table — it appears in 90% of KTU University Exam Part A (3-mark) questions on Bitcoin Scripts.

| Opcode | Hex | Stack Effect (before → after) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `OP_DUP` | `0x76` | `x` → `x, x` | Duplicate the top stack item |
| `OP_HASH160` | `0xA9` | `x` → `HASH160(x)` | Apply SHA-256 ∘ RIPEMD-160 |
| `OP_HASH256` | `0xAA` | `x` → `SHA256(SHA256(x))` | Double SHA-256 (used for txids) |
| `OP_EQUAL` | `0x87` | `x, y` → `1` / `0` | Test equality |
| `OP_EQUALVERIFY` | `0x88` | `x, y` → *(empty)* or fail | Equal *and* remove both |
| `OP_CHECKSIG` | `0xAC` | `sig, pubkey` → `1` / `0` | Verify ECDSA signature against tx |
| `OP_CHECKMULTISIG` | `0xAE` | `n, pubkey1..pubkeyn, m, sig1..sigm, n` → `1` / `0` | Verify m-of-n multisig |
| `OP_RETURN` | `0x6A` | `0` → fail | Marks output as **provably unspendable**; data carrier |
| `OP_VERIFY` | `0x69` | `x` → fail if x = 0 | Assert top is TRUE |
| `OP_IF` / `OP_ELSE` / `OP_ENDIF` | `0x63/67/68` | Conditional execution | (Limited use; pre-Taproot) |
| `OP_PUSHBYTES_N` | `0x01–0x4B` | Push next N bytes | Variable-length data push |
| `OP_CHECKLOCKTIMEVERIFY` | `0xB1` (CLTV) | Asserts tx locktime | Enables time-locked spends |
| `OP_CHECKSEQUENCEVERIFY` | `0xB2` (CSV) | Asserts relative locktime | Enables relative time locks |
| `OP_PUSHNUM_N` | `0x51–0x60` | Push small integer | Numeric constants |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable from Bitcoin consensus rules. They are not approximations.

### 2.3.1 Cryptographic Primitives Used in Scripts

| Formula | Definition | Used By |
|---|---|---|
| $H_{160}(x) = \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(x))$ | Bitcoin's 160-bit hash | P2PKH, P2WPKH, P2SH |
| $H_{256}(x) = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(x))$ | Double SHA-256 | TXID, block hashes, Merkle trees |
| $\text{address}_{P2PKH} = \text{Base58Check}(0x00 \,\vert\, H_{160}(\text{pubkey}))$ | Legacy address | Pay-to-PubKey-Hash |
| $\text{address}_{P2SH} = \text{Base58Check}(0x05 \,\vert\, H_{160}(\text{redeemScript}))$ | Script-hash address | Pay-to-Script-Hash |
| $\text{address}_{Bech32} = \text{bech32\_encode}(\text{hrp}=\text{"bc"},\,\text{witness\_program})$ | Native SegWit | P2WPKH, P2WSH |
| $w_{\text{tx}} = 4 \cdot \text{size}_{\text{base}} + \text{size}_{\text{witness}}$ | SegWit tx weight | Block size limit |
| $v_{\text{vbyte}} = \dfrac{w_{\text{tx}}}{4}$ | Virtual byte (vbyte) size | Fee calculation |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**: Per the engine rule, the absolute-value/mash operator is rendered as `$\vert$` to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe. For example, $H_{160}(x) = \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(x))$ is the HASH160 used in legacy addresses.

### 2.3.2 Standard Script Templates

| Script Type | ScriptPubKey Template | ScriptSig / Witness |
|---|---|---|
| **P2PK (Pay-to-PubKey)** | `<pubKey> OP_CHECKSIG` | `<signature>` |
| **P2PKH (Pay-to-PubKey-Hash)** | `OP_DUP OP_HASH160 <pubKeyHash> OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG` | `<signature> <pubKey>` |
| **P2SH (Pay-to-Script-Hash)** | `OP_HASH160 <redeemScriptHash> OP_EQUAL` | `<signatures...> <serialized redeemScript>` |
| **P2WPKH (SegWit v0)** | `OP_0 <20-byte pubKeyHash>` | Witness: `<signature> <pubKey>` |
| **P2WSH (SegWit v0)** | `OP_0 <32-byte SHA256(redeemScript)>` | Witness: `<signatures...> <redeemScript>` |
| **P2TR (Taproot, v1)** | `OP_1 <32-byte tweaked pubkey>` | Schnorr signature (Key path) or script tree (Script path) |
| **OP_RETURN (data carrier)** | `OP_RETURN <data>` | (None — provably unspendable) |

### 2.3.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Smart Contracts Engineering**: P2SH and P2WSH enable **escrow, atomic swaps, and Lightning Network** HTLCs.
- **Cybersecurity**: Bitcoin Script underpins most **multi-signature custodial wallets** used by exchanges (Cold Storage).
- **Fintech / Auditing**: `OP_RETURN` is used for **timestamped proof-of-existence** (e.g., Verisart, academic certifications).
- **Supply Chain**: Companies like **IBM Food Trust** use Bitcoin-anchored OP_RETURN data to immutably timestamp supply-chain events.
- **Legal-tech & DAOs**: Multi-sig P2SH enables **board-controlled treasury management**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 P2PKH — Full Execution Walkthrough (The Most Tested Script in KTU Exams)

> [!NOTE]
> P2PKH is the single most important script type for KTU 2024 Module 2. You **must** be able to trace every opcode on the stack.

### 3.1.1 The Two Scripts in Raw Form

Let:
- $S$ = signature bytes (from ScriptSig)
- $K$ = public-key bytes (from ScriptSig)
- $H_K = \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(K))$ = the pubkey hash (embedded in ScriptPubKey)

**ScriptSig (unlocking):** `<sig> <pubKey>` → i.e., bytes for $S$ and $K$ pushed onto stack.

**ScriptPubKey (locking):**
`OP_DUP OP_HASH160 <pubKeyHash> OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG`

### 3.1.2 Concatenated Execution Stream

When concatenated, the validator executes:

`<sig> <pubKey> OP_DUP OP_HASH160 <pubKeyHash> OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG`

### 3.1.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Stack Trace

We walk through **every** opcode, showing stack state at each step.

| Step | Opcode / Data | Stack (bottom → top) | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | *(initial)* | `[ ]` | Empty stack |
| 1 | `<sig>` (data push) | `[ S ]` | ScriptSig pushes the **signature** $S$ |
| 2 | `<pubKey>` (data push) | `[ S, K ]$ | ScriptSig pushes the **public key** $K$ |
| 3 | `OP_DUP` | `[ S, K, K ]$ | Duplicates top: $K$ appears twice |
| 4 | `OP_HASH160` | `[ S, K, H_{160}(K) ]$ | Hash160 of top $K$ → compute pubkey hash |
| 5 | `<pubKeyHash>` (data push) | `[ S, K, H_{160}(K), H_K ]$ | ScriptPubKey pushes the **expected** pubkey hash |
| 6 | `OP_EQUALVERIFY` | `[ S, K ]$ | Pops top two; **fails** if $H_{160}(K) \neq H_K$; otherwise pops both. **Cryptographically binds $K$ to the address.** |
| 7 | `OP_CHECKSIG` | `[ 1 ]$ (TRUE) or `[ 0 ]$ (FALSE) | Pops $S$ and $K$; verifies ECDSA signature on the modified transaction sighash. Pushes 1 on success, 0 on failure. |
| 8 | *(end)* | `[ 1 ]$ | Top of stack is non-zero (TRUE) → **transaction input is valid** |

### 3.1.4 The Math of Step 6 (Equality Binding)

The crucial cryptographic binding is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OP\_EQUALVERIFY checks: } &\quad H_{160}(K) \stackrel{?}{=} H_K \\
\text{where } &\quad H_K = \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(K)) \\
\text{So the check is: } &\quad \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(K)) = \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(K_{real}))
\end{aligned}
$$

> Because RIPEMD160 and SHA256 are both **collision-resistant hash functions**, finding a fake $K' \neq K$ such that $H_{160}(K') = H_{160}(K)$ is **computationally infeasible** (best attack: ~$\mathcal{O}(2^{80})$ work via birthday paradox on a 160-bit hash). Thus the script is **secure under standard cryptographic assumptions**.

### 3.1.5 The Math of Step 7 (Signature Verification)

Let the modified transaction sighash be denoted $\tilde{T}$ (a 32-byte hash of the transaction with the input scripts replaced by a specific sighash flag version of themselves — exact construction defined in BIP-143 for SegWit, or via the legacy double-SHA256 for P2PKH).

`OP_CHECKSIG` succeeds iff the following ECDSA verification holds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Given: } (r, s) \text{ parsed from } S, \text{ and pubkey } K = (x, y) \\
&\text{Step 1: } u_1 = s^{-1} \cdot H(\tilde{T}) \pmod{n} \\
&\text{Step 2: } u_2 = s^{-1} \cdot r \pmod{n} \\
&\text{Step 3: } R = u_1 \cdot G + u_2 \cdot K \pmod{p} \\
&\text{Step 4: } \text{Verify: } r \equiv R_x \pmod{n} \\
&\text{where } G \text{ is the secp256k1 generator point, } n \text{ is its prime order}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The SIGHASH flag byte (last byte of $S$) determines what parts of the transaction the signature commits to (e.g., `SIGHASH_ALL = 0x01`, `SIGHASH_NONE`, `SIGHASH_SINGLE`).

---

## 3.2 P2SH — Pay-to-Script-Hash: 3-Mark & 14-Mark Exam Favorite

### 3.2.1 Motivation (Why P2SH Exists)

P2PKH forces the **sender** to know the recipient's full script (e.g., for a multisig with 15 public keys, the sender must pay 15×33 bytes of data into the ScriptPubKey, and the wallet must track a heavy UTXO). P2SH **inverts** the burden: the sender pays a 20-byte hash of *any* redeem script; the recipient reveals the script only at spend time.

### 3.2.2 Two-Phase Validation

#### **Phase 1 — Hash Match** (script → script hash)

ScriptPubKey: `OP_HASH160 <20-byte redeemScriptHash> OP_EQUAL`
ScriptSig: `<... signatures ...> <serialized redeemScript>`

Stack trace:

| Step | Opcode / Data | Stack | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `<serialized redeemScript>` | `[ RS ]$ | Push redeem script |
| 2 | `OP_HASH160` | `[ H_{160}(RS) ]$ | Hash the redeem script |
| 3 | `<redeemScriptHash>` | `[ H_{160}(RS), H_R ]$ | Push expected hash |
| 4 | `OP_EQUAL` | `[ 1 ]$ or `[ 0 ]$ | TRUE iff hashes match |
| 5 | *(then)* | *(continue)* | If TRUE, the script proceeds to **deserialize RS** and execute it as a new script with the remaining stack items as initial inputs. |

#### **Phase 2 — Redeem Script Execution**

If the hash matched, the network **deserializes the redeem script** and runs it with the **preceding ScriptSig items** (signatures, etc.) as the initial stack. For example, if the redeem script is a 2-of-3 multisig:

```
2 <pubKey1> <pubKey2> <pubKey3> 3 OP_CHECKMULTISIG
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake**: Forgetting that `OP_CHECKMULTISIG` has a **known off-by-one bug** — it pops **one extra item** off the stack. Implementations work around it by prepending a dummy `OP_0` (or "fake stack element") in the ScriptSig.

### 3.2.3 The Address Derivation Math

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Redeem script: } & RS \\
\text{Redeem script hash: } & H_R = \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(RS)) \\
\text{P2SH address: } & A_{P2SH} = \text{Base58Check}(\text{version}=0x05 \,\vert\, H_R)
\end{aligned}
$$

The leading `0x05` is the version byte; the Base58Check adds a 4-byte checksum (first 4 bytes of `SHA256(SHA256(payload))`).

---

## 3.3 SegWit (BIP-141) — Why It Was Introduced

### 3.3.1 The Problem: Transaction Malleability

In pre-SegWit Bitcoin, the **signatures in ScriptSig could be modified by a third party** (e.g., flipping the `S` value to `n-S` per ECDSA's symmetry) **without invalidating the signature**. This changed the **TXID** (which double-SHA256s the entire transaction including scripts) and broke dependent protocols (e.g., Lightning Network channel construction).

### 3.3.2 The Solution: Move Signatures to the Witness

SegWit introduced a **witness** field *outside* the legacy transaction serialization. The new transaction weight is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{tx}} &= 4 \cdot \text{size}_{\text{base}} + \text{size}_{\text{witness}} \\
\text{with } \quad \text{size}_{\text{base}} &\leq 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ virtual bytes} \\
w_{\text{tx}} &\leq 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ weight units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Implication**: Witness data is **discounted 4×** in the block size calculation, but is **not included in the TXID hash**, eliminating third-party malleability.

### 3.3.3 P2WPKH ScriptPubKey

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ScriptPubKey: } & \texttt{OP\_0} \,\,\langle 20 \text{-byte } H_{160}(K) \rangle \\
\text{Witness: } & \langle \text{signature} \rangle \,\langle \text{pubKey} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

The `OP_0` (`0x00`) is the **witness version**; the 20-byte push encodes the witness program (= pubkey hash). When validating, the node:
1. Recognizes the version 0 + 20-byte program.
2. Constructs the **equivalent P2PKH script**:
   `OP_DUP OP_HASH160 <H_{160}(K)> OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG`
3. Runs the witness items on that script.

> [!TIP]
> This is why P2WPKH is sometimes called a "**witness program**" — the scriptPubKey no longer contains opcodes; it just **commits to a hash** and the actual spending logic is reconstructed by the consensus rules.

---

## 3.4 OP_RETURN — Data Carrier & Provably Unspendable

```
OP_RETURN <up to 80 bytes of arbitrary data>
```

`OP_RETURN` immediately marks the script as **failed/unspendable**. No node will ever keep this output in its UTXO set, so it does not bloat the global state. It is used to **embed commitment hashes, document digests, or asset issuance metadata**.

Key properties:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Output value: } & \geq 0 \text{ (often exactly 0)} \\
\text{Data size: } & \leq 80 \text{ bytes (standard policy)} \\
\text{Spendability: } & \text{None (provably unspendable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 80-byte limit is **policy** (`IsStandard()`), not consensus. Miners *can* include larger OP_RETURNs, but most refuse (as of 2024, many pools accept up to ~$83$ bytes or larger by policy update). KTU 2024 syllabus states **80 bytes** — use this in exam answers.

---

## 3.5 Taproot (BIP-341) — Modern Script Evolution

P2TR (Pay-to-Taproot, witness v1) replaces ECDSA with **Schnorr signatures** and introduces **MAST (Merkelized Alternative Script Tree)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Output key: } & Q = P + H(\text{tagged\_hash}(P, m)) \cdot G \\
\text{where } & P = \text{internal key}, \,\, m = \text{merkle root of script tree}
\end{aligned}
$$

Two spending paths:
- **Key path**: Single Schnorr signature against $Q$. Looks like a normal single-sig payment (privacy + efficiency).
- **Script path**: Reveal only the *executed* script and its Merkle path, hiding all unexecuted alternative scripts (privacy + scaling).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this in KTU 2024?** Module 2 expects students to know the **evolution of script types**: P2PK → P2PKH → P2SH → P2WSH → P2TR. The examiner often asks for a **comparison table** in Part B (14 marks).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Reminder

> All node IDs in the Mermaid diagrams below are **purely alphanumeric** (e.g., `node1`, `node2`, `stepA`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`) are used as node names. All labels are **plain uppercase text** (no bold, italics, or markdown inside quotes) per the engine's anti-collision rule.

---

## 4.2 Diagram A — Full Bitcoin Transaction Script Lifecycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[USER CREATES TRANSACTION] --> A1[REFERENCES PREVIOUS UTXO]
    A1 --> A2[FETCHES PREVIOUS SCRIPTPUBKEY]
    A2 --> A3[BUILDS SCRIPTSIG WITH SIG AND PUBKEY]
    A3 --> A4[BROADCASTS RAW TRANSACTION]
    A4 --> A5[FULL NODE RECEIVES TX]
    A5 --> A6[STAGE 1 CONCATENATE SCRIPTSIG AND SCRIPTPUBKEY]
    A6 --> A7[STAGE 2 PARSE OPCODES LEFT TO RIGHT]
    A7 --> A8[STAGE 3 EXECUTE ON STACK]
    A8 --> A9{STACK TOP IS TRUE}
    A9 -->|YES| A10[INPUT MARKED VALID]
    A9 -->|NO| A11[TX REJECTED BY NODE]
    A10 --> A12[UTXO SET UPDATED]
    A12 --> A13[TX ENTERS MEMPOOL]
    A13 --> A14[MINER INCLUDES IN BLOCK]
    A14 --> A15[BLOCK PROPAGATED TO NETWORK]
    A15 --> A16[FULL CONSENSUS ACHIEVED]

    classDef process fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#f8fafc
    classDef decision fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fed7aa,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff7ed
    classDef final fill:#064e3b,stroke:#86efac,stroke-width:2px,color:#f0fdf4

    class A0,A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7,A8,A12,A13,A14,A15 process
    class A9,A11 decision
    class A10,A16 final
```

> **Reading Guide**: This diagram shows the *complete, end-to-end* lifecycle of a script from the user's wallet through consensus finality. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Trace the journey of a Bitcoin transaction from creation to confirmation."* — this diagram is your answer.

---

## 4.3 Diagram B — P2PKH Stack Trace (Section 3.1 Visualized)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[EMPTY STACK] --> B1[PUSH SIG]
    B1 --> B2[PUSH PUBKEY]
    B2 --> B3[OP_DUP DUPLICATE TOP]
    B3 --> B4[OP_HASH160 HASH TOP TWICE]
    B4 --> B5[PUSH EXPECTED HASH]
    B5 --> B6[OP_EQUALVERIFY COMPARE]
    B6 --> B7{PUBKEY MATCHES ADDRESS}
    B7 -->|NO| B8[SCRIPT FAILS]
    B7 -->|YES| B9[OP_CHECKSIG VERIFY ECDSA]
    B9 --> B10{SIGNATURE VALID}
    B10 -->|NO| B8
    B10 -->|YES| B11[PUSH TRUE]
    B11 --> B12[SCRIPT SUCCEEDS]

    classDef stackOp fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#7dd3fc,color:#f0f9ff
    classDef cryptoOp fill:#581c87,stroke:#d8b4fe,color:#faf5ff
    classDef success fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,color:#f0fdf4
    classDef fail fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,color:#fef2f2

    class B1,B2,B3,B5 stackOp
    class B4,B6,B9 cryptoOp
    class B11,B12 success
    class B8,B7,B10 fail
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram C — P2SH Two-Phase Validation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[SCRIPTSIG AND SCRIPTPUBKEY LOADED] --> C1[PHASE 1 HASH MATCH]
    C1 --> C2[OP_HASH160 ON REDEEM SCRIPT]
    C2 --> C3[PUSH EXPECTED HASH]
    C3 --> C4[OP_EQUAL]
    C4 --> C5{HASHES EQUAL}
    C5 -->|NO| C6[REJECT]
    C5 -->|YES| C7[PHASE 2 REDEEM SCRIPT EXECUTION]
    C7 --> C8[DESERIALIZE REDEEM SCRIPT]
    C8 --> C9[EXECUTE REDEEM SCRIPT]
    C9 --> C10{REDEEM SCRIPT TOP TRUE}
    C10 -->|NO| C6
    C10 -->|YES| C11[INPUT VALID]

    classDef phase1 fill:#312e81,stroke:#a5b4fc,color:#eef2ff
    classDef phase2 fill:#831843,stroke:#f9a8d4,color:#fdf2f8
    classDef final fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,color:#f0fdf4
    classDef fail fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,color:#fef2f2

    class C0,C1,C2,C3,C4 phase1
    class C7,C8,C9 phase2
    class C11 final
    class C5,C6,C10 fail
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram D — Script Type Evolution & Block-Weight Composition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0[P2PK 2009] --> D1[P2PKH 2009]
    D1 --> D2[P2SH 2012]
    D2 --> D3[P2WPKH P2WSH SEG WIT 2017]
    D3 --> D4[P2TR TAPROOT 2021]

    D5[BLOCK WEIGHT 4000000 WU] --> D6[BASE 1000000 VBYTES WEIGHT 4]
    D5 --> D7[WITNESS DATA WEIGHT 1]
    D6 --> D8[TOTAL WEIGHT 4 BASE 1 WITNESS]
    D7 --> D8

    classDef history fill:#1e293b,stroke:#94a3b8,color:#f1f5f9
    classDef weight fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fdba74,color:#fff7ed

    class D0,D1,D2,D3,D4 history
    class D5,D6,D7,D8 weight
```
