# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Direct Recall)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part A questions are 3 marks each and require **concise, definition-style** answers in 30–50 words. **Two questions per topic are expected**; below are two representative questions with model answers.

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP] — 3 Marks**
**Define Bitcoin Script. Why is it designed as a non-Turing-complete language?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 3 key points × 1 mark each):**

1. **Definition (1 mark)**: Bitcoin Script is a simple, stack-based, Forth-like scripting language embedded in every Bitcoin transaction. It consists of opcodes and data pushes executed on a stack to validate spend conditions.

2. **Non-Turing-Complete Property (1 mark)**: It deliberately lacks loops (`while`/`for`) and unbounded memory, making the language **deterministic and bounded** in execution.

3. **Why this design choice (1 mark)**: This prevents infinite-loop and denial-of-service attacks on the network, ensures every node reaches the same result for the same input, and keeps validation cost predictable for block-size economics.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP] — 3 Marks**
**List any three commonly used Bitcoin opcodes and explain their stack-level behavior.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 3 opcodes × 1 mark each):**

1. **`OP_DUP`** (1 mark): Duplicates the top stack element. Stack effect: `x` → `x, x`. Used in P2PKH to duplicate the public key for hashing and signing.

2. **`OP_HASH160`** (1 mark): Hashes the top stack element with `RIPEMD160(SHA256(x))` and pushes the 20-byte result. Used to derive an address from a public key.

3. **`OP_CHECKSIG`** (1 mark): Pops a signature and a public key from the stack, verifies the ECDSA signature against the transaction sighash, and pushes `1` (true) or `0` (false). Stack effect: `sig, pubkey` → `1` or `0`.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme: Module 2 contributes 14 marks (typically a 14-mark question or 7+7 subparts). Below are **TWO complete alternative questions** providing internal choice.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2, Q2]**

> **(a)** Explain the structure and execution model of Bitcoin Script with a neat diagram. Differentiate between **ScriptPubKey** and **ScriptSig** with examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a detailed stack trace, explain how a **Pay-to-PubKey-Hash (P2PKH)** transaction is validated. Include the relevant opcodes and cryptographic operations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q2(a) — (7 marks)

**1. Definition and Architecture (2 marks):**
Bitcoin Script is a stack-based, reverse-Polish, intentionally non-Turing-complete language. It is executed by every full node on the Bitcoin network during transaction validation. The execution model uses a single LIFO stack; opcodes pop arguments, perform computation, and push results.

**2. ScriptPubKey vs ScriptSig — Tabular Comparison (3 marks):**

| Property | ScriptPubKey | ScriptSig |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Embedded in transaction **output** (vout) | Embedded in transaction **input** (vin) |
| **Role** | **Locking** script — defines spending conditions | **Unlocking** script — provides proof |
| **Who sets it** | The **receiver** (when the address is generated) | The **spender** (at spend time) |
| **Example (P2PKH)** | `OP_DUP OP_HASH160 <hash> OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG` | `<signature> <publicKey>` |
| **Visibility** | Stored on-chain in the UTXO | Revealed only at spend time (then becomes part of history) |

**3. Execution Flow (2 marks):**
The validator concatenates ScriptSig + ScriptPubKey and executes them left-to-right. Data bytes are pushed onto the stack; opcodes manipulate it. After the final opcode, the **top stack element must be a non-zero value (TRUE)** for the input to be considered valid. If the script ends with an empty stack or `0` on top, validation fails and the input is rejected.

---

#### Model Solution for Q2(b) — (7 marks)

**Full P2PKH Stack Trace (with marks distribution):**

Given:
- $S$ = signature (provided in ScriptSig)
- $K$ = public key (provided in ScriptSig)
- $H_K$ = expected pubkey hash = `RIPEMD160(SHA256(K))` (embedded in ScriptPubKey)

**Step-by-step execution** (`<sig> <pubKey> OP_DUP OP_HASH160 <pubKeyHash> OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG`):

| # | Opcode | Stack | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `<sig>` (push) | `[ S ]$ | 1 mark |
| 2 | `<pubKey>` (push) | `[ S, K ]$ | 1 mark |
| 3 | `OP_DUP` | `[ S, K, K ]$ | 0.5 mark |
| 4 | `OP_HASH160` | `[ S, K, H_{160}(K) ]$ | 1 mark |
| 5 | `<pubKeyHash>` (push) | `[ S, K, H_{160}(K), H_K ]$ | 0.5 mark |
| 6 | `OP_EQUALVERIFY` | `[ S, K ]$ (or FAIL if hashes differ) | 1 mark |
| 7 | `OP_CHECKSIG` | `[ 1 ]$ (TRUE) or `[ 0 ]$ (FAIL) | 1 mark |
| **End** | — | Top = TRUE → valid | 1 mark (for conclusion) |

**Cryptographic Binding (1 mark):**
`OP_EQUALVERIFY` ensures that the public key $K$ revealed by the spender matches the hash $H_K$ committed in the ScriptPubKey. This is secure because finding $K' \neq K$ with $H_{160}(K') = H_{160}(K)$ requires breaking RIPEMD160's collision resistance (~$\mathcal{O}(2^{80})$ work).

**Signature Verification (1 mark):**
`OP_CHECKSIG` performs ECDSA verification on the secp256k1 curve. It computes the sighash of the transaction (with script codes replaced as per SIGHASH flag), then verifies the signature against the public key. If both conditions hold, the script ends with `[ 1 ]$` on the stack and the input is valid.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **(a)** Explain the **Pay-to-Script-Hash (P2SH)** mechanism. How does it differ from P2PKH? Derive the address format and explain the two-phase validation with a diagram. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss **Segregated Witness (SegWit)** as introduced in BIP-141. Explain how it addresses **transaction malleability**, and derive the new **block weight** formula. How does it change the script structure of P2WPKH? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q2B(a) — (7 marks)

**1. Motivation and Definition (1.5 marks):**
P2SH (BIP-16, 2012) inverts the burden of script complexity: instead of the **sender** embedding the full redeem script in the ScriptPubKey, only a **20-byte hash of the redeem script** is committed. The recipient reveals the full script at spend time.

**2. Address Format (1.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Redeem script: } & RS \\
H_R &= \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(RS)) \\
A_{P2SH} &= \text{Base58Check}(\text{version} = 0x05 \,\vert\, H_R)
\end{aligned}
$$

The leading byte `0x05` causes the Base58 address to start with a `'3'`.

**3. Two-Phase Validation (3 marks):**
- **Phase 1 — Hash Match (1.5 marks)**: ScriptPubKey `OP_HASH160 <H_R> OP_EQUAL` runs over the scriptSig (which ends with the serialized redeem script). If `HASH160(RS) = H_R`, Phase 1 succeeds.
- **Phase 2 — Redeem Script Execution (1.5 marks)**: The network deserializes `RS` and runs it as a new script, using the **remaining items** of the ScriptSig (e.g., signatures, public keys) as the initial stack.

**4. P2PKH vs P2SH — Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | P2PKH | P2SH |
|---|---|---|
| ScriptPubKey size | ~25 bytes | 23 bytes (fixed) |
| Who bears script complexity | Sender | Receiver |
| Address prefix | `'1'` | `'3'` |
| Use case | Single-sig | Multisig, escrows, atomic swaps |

---

#### Model Solution for Q2B(b) — (7 marks)

**1. The Malleability Problem (1.5 marks):**
In pre-SegWit Bitcoin, signatures in the ScriptSig could be modified by a third party (e.g., flipping the `S` value in ECDSA to `n - S`) without invalidating the signature. This **changed the TXID** (which double-SHA256s the entire transaction, scripts included) and broke dependent protocols such as the Lightning Network.

**2. The SegWit Fix (1.5 marks):**
BIP-141 moved all signature and witness data into a **separate `witness` field** *outside* the legacy transaction serialization. Because the TXID hash no longer covers the witness, third-party malleability becomes impossible.

**3. Block Weight Formula (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{tx}} &= 4 \cdot \text{size}_{\text{base}} + 1 \cdot \text{size}_{\text{witness}} \\
\text{Limits: } & w_{\text{tx}} \leq 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ weight units} \\
& \text{size}_{\text{base}} \leq 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ virtual bytes} \\
& \text{vbyte} = w_{\text{tx}} / 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Witness data is **discounted 4×** in block-size accounting, incentivizing migration from legacy to SegWit.

**4. P2WPKH Script Change (2 marks):**
The new ScriptPubKey is just two items:
`OP_0 <20-byte pubKeyHash>`

Validation:
- Node sees `OP_0` (witness version 0) + 20-byte push → recognizes as a P2WPKH program.
- Reconstructs the **equivalent P2PKH script** internally:
  `OP_DUP OP_HASH160 <hash> OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG`
- Runs the witness items (`<signature> <pubKey>`) on this reconstructed script.

This is called a **witness program**: the locking script no longer contains opcodes; it merely commits to a hash and the actual logic is reconstructed by consensus.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Bitcoin Script questions** (based on KTU 2024 valuation key patterns):
>
> 1. **Confusing ScriptSig and ScriptPubKey roles** (-2 marks typical). Always state: ScriptPubKey is the **lock** on the **output**; ScriptSig is the **key** in the **input**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the final stack check** (-1 mark). Many students stop at `OP_CHECKSIG` and forget to write *"the script ends with `1` on top of the stack → input is valid."*
>
> 3. **Wrong concatenation order** (-1 mark). The correct order is `<ScriptSig> <ScriptPubKey>`, NOT reversed. The 2010 `OP_CHECKSIG` bug was due to executing them in the wrong order; this is a common trick question.
>
> 4. **Missing the `OP_CHECKMULTISIG` off-by-one bug** (-1 mark). When asked about multisig, always mention the dummy stack element (extra `OP_0` pop) that implementations compensate for.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Base58Check version byte** (-0.5 to -1 mark). P2PKH addresses use `0x00`, P2SH uses `0x05`. P2WPKH uses Bech32 (`bc1q...`), not Base58Check.
>
> 6. **In stack-trace questions, omitting the cryptographic justification** (-1 mark). Just writing "stack ends with 1" is not enough — say *why* it's secure (collision resistance, ECDSA security).
>
> 7. **Treating SegWit as "free block space"** (-0.5 mark). The total weight (4 M WU) is equivalent to ~1 MB legacy + 3 MB witness — it's a re-weighting, not a pure expansion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and keep it beside you during the last 24 hours before the exam.**

- [x] **Bitcoin Script is a stack-based, non-Turing-complete, Forth-like language.**
- [x] **Two scripts per transaction**: ScriptPubKey (lock, in output) and ScriptSig (unlock, in input).
- [x] **Execution order**: Concatenate `<ScriptSig><ScriptPubKey>` → execute left-to-right → top of stack must be TRUE.
- [x] **P2PKH template**:
  `OP_DUP OP_HASH160 <pubKeyHash> OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG`
  with `<sig><pubKey>` as the ScriptSig.
- [x] **P2SH template**:
  ScriptPubKey: `OP_HASH160 <redeemScriptHash> OP_EQUAL`
  ScriptSig: `<signatures> <serialized redeemScript>` (then run RS).
- [x] **Address formats**: P2PKH → `1...` (Base58Check, version 0x00); P2SH → `3...` (version 0x05); P2WPKH → `bc1q...` (Bech32).
- [x] **Cryptographic primitives**: HASH160 = RIPEMD160(SHA256(x)); HASH256 = SHA256(SHA256(x)).
- [x] **SegWit (BIP-141)** moves signatures to a separate **witness** field → fixes malleability.
- [x] **Block weight formula**:
  $w_{\text{tx}} = 4 \cdot \text{size}_{\text{base}} + \text{size}_{\text{witness}} \le 4{,}000{,}000$ WU.
- [x] **P2WPKH ScriptPubKey**: `OP_0 <20-byte pubKeyHash>` (a "witness program").
- [x] **OP_RETURN** = provably unspendable data carrier (≤ 80 bytes per KTU syllabus).
- [x] **OP_CHECKMULTISIG bug** = pops one extra stack item → pre-pend a dummy `OP_0`.
- [x] **Taproot (BIP-341)** = Schnorr + MAST, witness v1, address prefix `bc1p...`.
- [x] **Limits to remember**: 10,000-byte script cap; 520-byte max element; 1000-item max stack; 201-opcode cap.
- [x] **Why non-Turing-complete?** Prevents infinite loops → predictable validation cost → network security.
- [x] **Common KTU trick**: "What is the difference between ScriptSig and ScriptPubKey?" — always give location, role, who sets it, and a concrete example.
- [x] **Memorize the stack trace** for P2PKH; examiners regularly award 5–6 of 7 marks just for an accurate trace.
- [x] **Always end your answer** with: "the script ends with TRUE on top of the stack → input is valid" — this is the closing line expected by the KTU valuation key.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bitcoin P2P Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bitcoin P2P Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bitcoin Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Network** is a decentralized, flat-topology overlay network of independently operating nodes that collectively maintain and synchronize the Bitcoin blockchain without relying on any central server, trusted authority, or hierarchical infrastructure. It operates primarily over **TCP port 8333** (mainnet) and uses a custom, unstructured gossip-based protocol to propagate transactions, blocks, and peer addresses across a global mesh of participants.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is defined as:

> *"A self-organizing, fault-tolerant application-layer overlay network where each participating node acts simultaneously as a client and a server, employing randomized flooding to achieve eventual consistency of the distributed ledger."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Bitcoin P2P network is fundamentally a **broadcast network** built on the principle of *eventual consistency*. There is no single source of truth — every honest full node independently verifies the entire chain, making the network **Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT)** as long as honest nodes control more than **51%** of the total computational power.

### 1.2 Key Characteristics (Exam-Relevant Bullets)

- **Flat Topology:** No hierarchy; every node is equal in capability.
- **Randomized Peer Discovery:** New nodes connect via DNS seeds, then exchange `addr` messages.
- **Gossip Protocol:** Information spreads by each node forwarding new data to a subset of its peers.
- **TCP-Based:** Reliable, ordered, connection-oriented transport (port 8333 for mainnet).
- **Best-Effort Delivery:** The network does not guarantee delivery to all nodes; it aims for *propagation* to most honest nodes quickly.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Town Crier Ledger"

Imagine a medieval town where there is no king, no government office, and no official scribe. Yet every resident carries an identical copy of a giant ledger that records every transaction in town.

- When Alice pays Bob 5 coins, she doesn't tell one official — she **shouts the news** to her 8 neighbors at the market.
- Each neighbor verifies the news against their own ledger, then **shouts it to their 8 neighbors** (and so on).
- Within minutes, the entire town knows. Anyone holding a fake announcement is dismissed because the ledgers don't match.
- The "town crier" here is **every citizen simultaneously** — this is the essence of P2P.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:**
> Bitcoin's P2P network is a global "town square" of computers. Instead of trusting one bank, you trust the *crowd* — because the crowd independently verifies every transaction and block. Trust is replaced by **redundant verification**.

### 1.4 Standard Network Metrics (Bolded for Visibility)

| Metric | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Mainnet Port** | **TCP 8333** |
| **Testnet Port** | **TCP 18333** |
| **Message Header Size** | **24 bytes** (magic + command + length + checksum) |
| **Magic Bytes (Mainnet)** | **0xF9BEB4D9** |
| **Target Block Propagation** | **\< 12 seconds** globally |
| **Average Active Peers per Node** | **8 to 125** (default outbound: 8) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Stack of the Bitcoin P2P Network

The Bitcoin P2P network functions in a layered, deterministic manner. Below is the structured logic flow that every node follows when connecting to and participating in the network.

#### Step 1 — Peer Discovery (Bootstrap Phase)
- A new node boots up with a hardcoded list of **DNS seeds** (e.g., `seed.bitcoin.sipa.be`, `dnsseed.bluematt.me`).
- The node sends a DNS query to obtain IP addresses of long-lived, well-connected Bitcoin nodes.
- Alternatively, the node may use **hardcoded seed IPs** as a fallback.

#### Step 2 — Handshake (Version Exchange)
- The new node initiates a TCP connection to a discovered peer.
- It sends a `version` message containing:
  - Protocol version (currently `70015`)
  - Services bitfield (e.g., `NODE_NETWORK = 1`)
  - Best block height
  - Timestamp
- The peer responds with `version` + `verack` to complete the handshake.

#### Step 3 — Address Exchange (Network Learning)
- Nodes send `getaddr` to ask for known peers.
- They respond with `addr` messages containing up to **1000 peer addresses** (older protocol) or **up to 10% of `addrv2` capacity** (newer protocol, BIP-155).
- A new node gradually builds its **peer table** (address manager database).

#### Step 4 — Transaction & Block Propagation
- When a node receives a new transaction via `tx` message, it:
  1. Validates it against consensus rules.
  2. Adds it to its **memory pool (mempool)**.
  3. Announces it via `inv` (inventory) messages to all connected peers.
- Peers that haven't seen it respond with `getdata`; the originator sends the full `tx` payload.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use `inv` instead of sending the full transaction?**
> Inventory messages are **tiny (36 bytes)**. Sending `inv` first prevents bandwidth waste — a node only requests the full data if it actually needs it (BIP-37 / Bloom filters can further filter this).

#### Step 5 — Block Synchronization
- A mining node that finds a valid block broadcasts a `block` message directly.
- Other nodes validate, then propagate it via the same gossip mechanism.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Peer Discovery | Prevents the network from being a closed system; enables organic growth. |
| Handshake | Establishes protocol compatibility and mutual awareness of node capabilities. |
| Address Exchange | Each node becomes a *mini directory*, distributing knowledge across the mesh. |
| Inventory-First | Drastically reduces redundant data transfer. |
| Validation Before Forwarding | Prevents flooding attacks and DoS via invalid data. |

### 2.3 Node Types — A Critical Distinction for KTU Exams

Bitcoin has a **heterogeneous** participant set. Examiners frequently test this.

| Node Type | Stores Full Blockchain? | Validates? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Node** | Yes (every block) | Fully validates all rules | Wallets, exchanges, miners (non-pool) |
| **Archival Full Node** | Yes (full UTXO set history) | Fully validates | Block explorers, researchers |
| **Pruned Full Node** | Yes (up to a cap, e.g., 550 MB) | Fully validates | Resource-constrained devices |
| **SPV (Simplified Payment Verification) Node** | Only block headers | Validates headers + Merkle proofs | Mobile wallets (lightweight) |
| **Mining Node** | Yes (full) | Fully validates + mines | Block producers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**
> **SPV nodes do NOT store the full blockchain.** They trust that the longest chain with most PoW is valid. They verify transactions using **Merkle proofs** linking the transaction to a block header.

### 2.4 Network Topology Properties

Bitcoin's P2P network is mathematically modeled as a **random regular graph** with properties close to a small-world network.

- **Degree of a node (k):** Number of outbound connections (typically **8**) + inbound (variable, often 100+).
- **Network diameter:** Estimated at **3 to 5 hops** for global message propagation.
- **Diameter bound (KTU formula):**

$$
D \approx \frac{\log N}{\log k}
$$

where $N$ is total number of reachable nodes and $k$ is the average node degree.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the symbols and units. KTU often asks to "explain the network parameters" for 7 marks.

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Value / Unit | Equation Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D$ | Network diameter (hops) | dimensionless | $D = \log N / \log k$ |
| $N$ | Number of reachable nodes | count (nodes) | varies (~15,000+ public) |
| $k$ | Average node degree | count (peers) | $\approx 8$ outbound |
| $T_p$ | Block propagation time | seconds | target $\le 12$ s |
| $L$ | Block size limit | megabytes | $4 \text{ MB}$ (post-SegWit weight) |
| $f_{\text{honest}}$ | Fraction of honest hash power | ratio | $> 0.5$ for security |
| $P_{\text{reach}}(t)$ | Probability node reached by time $t$ | $[0,1]$ | exponential decay model |
| $\lambda$ | Mean inter-arrival time of messages | seconds | protocol-defined |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Bitcoin P2P protocol design directly influenced:
- **Ethereum's devp2p** (uses Kademlia DHT, more structured)
- **Libp2p** (used by IPFS, Filecoin, Polkadot)
- **Lightning Network** (uses a custom P2P layer with gossip for channel updates)
- **Resilient mesh networks** in disaster-recovery communication systems

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The randomized gossip design trades off **deterministic delivery guarantees** for **resilience and decentralization**. In critical infrastructure (e.g., military comms, IoT), this trade-off is studied under *epidemic routing protocols*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Block Propagation Reach Over Time

In an unstructured gossip network like Bitcoin's, the spread of a new block behaves like an **epidemic diffusion process**. We model the probability that a node is informed at time $t$.

#### Setup
Let:
- $N$ = total number of honest reachable nodes.
- $k$ = number of outbound peers per node.
- At $t = 0$, only **1 node** (the miner) has the block.
- Each informed node independently contacts its $k$ peers.

#### Step 1: Fraction of Informed Nodes at Time $t$

The number of newly informed nodes in an infinitesimal time interval $dt$ is proportional to the number of (informed) $\times$ (uninformed) pairs:

$$
dI = \beta \cdot I \cdot (N - I) \, dt
$$

where $\beta$ is the *contact rate* (messages per second per connection), $I$ is the number of informed nodes, and $N - I$ is the number of uninformed.

#### Step 2: Convert to Fraction $p = I/N$

Let $p = I/N$. Then $I = pN$ and $dI = N \, dp$. Substituting:

$$
N \, dp = \beta \cdot (pN) \cdot (N - pN) \, dt
$$

$$
N \, dp = \beta \cdot p \cdot (1 - p) \cdot N^2 \, dt
$$

Dividing both sides by $N$:

$$
dp = \beta N \cdot p(1-p) \, dt
$$

This is the classic **logistic growth equation**.

#### Step 3: Solve the Differential Equation

Separate variables:

$$
\frac{dp}{p(1-p)} = \beta N \, dt
$$

Use partial fraction decomposition: $\dfrac{1}{p(1-p)} = \dfrac{1}{p} + \dfrac{1}{1-p}$.

$$
\int \left( \frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{1-p} \right) dp = \int \beta N \, dt
$$

$$
\ln p - \ln(1-p) = \beta N \cdot t + C
$$

$$
\ln \left( \frac{p}{1-p} \right) = \beta N t + C
$$

#### Step 4: Apply Initial Condition

At $t = 0$, $p = 1/N$ (only one node informed).

$$
\ln \left( \frac{1/N}{1 - 1/N} \right) = C
$$

$$
C = \ln \left( \frac{1}{N - 1} \right)
$$

#### Step 5: Final Closed-Form Solution

$$
\ln \left( \frac{p}{1-p} \right) = \beta N t + \ln \left( \frac{1}{N - 1} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{p}{1-p} = \frac{1}{N-1} \cdot e^{\beta N t}
$$

Solving for $p$:

$$
p(t) = \frac{e^{\beta N t}}{N - 1 + e^{\beta N t}}
$$

#### Step 6: Interpretation

- As $t \to \infty$, $p \to 1$ (full propagation).
- **Doubling time** of informed population: $t_2 = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\beta N}$ (early phase).
- The product $\beta N$ is the effective *epidemic rate*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Bitcoin:** If $N$ is very large or $\beta$ is small (e.g., due to bandwidth throttling), propagation slows. This creates the *block propagation bottleneck*, which is why miners prefer **compact block relay (BIP-152)** to reduce $\beta$ requirement.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Information Propagation Methods

| Method | Mechanism | Bandwidth Cost | Latency | Use Case in Bitcoin |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unsolicited Push** | Send full block/tx to all peers | High | Lowest | Initial block broadcast |
| **Inventory-First (Inv)** | Send hashes; on demand send full data | Low | Slightly higher | Transaction relay |
| **Compact Blocks (BIP-152)** | Send 80-byte header + short transaction IDs | Very low | Low | Block relay (post-2016) |
| **Graphene / Xthin** | Set reconciliation via Bloom filters | Minimal | Higher complexity | Experimental |
| **SPV Merkle Proofs** | Send path from tx to Merkle root | Logarithmic | High for SPV clients | Light client verification |

### 3.3 Derivation: Network Diameter Bound

Assume $N$ nodes each maintain $k$ outbound connections. Information spreads one hop per round. We want the minimum number of rounds $D$ such that $k^D \ge N$ (a tree of depth $D$ from the source covers at most $k^D$ distinct nodes).

$$
k^D \ge N
$$

Take $\log_k$ on both sides:

$$
D \ge \log_k N = \frac{\log N}{\log k}
$$

For Bitcoin with $k = 8$ and $N = 15{,}000$ reachable nodes:

$$
D \ge \frac{\log 15000}{\log 8} = \frac{9.62}{2.08} \approx 4.62
$$

So information reaches the entire network in approximately **5 hops**. This is a *lower bound*; real-world propagation has redundancy and latency overhead.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A: Bitcoin P2P Network — Node Discovery & Handshake Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Node Bootstraps] --> B[Query DNS Seeds]
    B --> C[Receive Seed IP List]
    C --> D[Initiate TCP Connection Port 8333]
    D --> E[Send version Message]
    E --> F[Receive version from Peer]
    F --> G[Send verack]
    G --> H[Connection Established]
    H --> I[Send getaddr]
    I --> J[Receive addr Messages]
    J --> K[Populate Peer Table]
    K --> L[Maintain 8 Outbound Connections]
    L --> M[Begin Listening for tx block inv]
    M --> N[Active P2P Participant]
```

### 4.2 Diagram B: Block Propagation via Inventory-Then-Push (BIP-152 Hybrid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Miner as Mining Node
    participant P1 as Peer 1
    participant P2 as Peer 2
    participant P3 as Peer 3

    Miner->>P1: inv block_hash
    P1->>Miner: getdata block_hash
    Miner->>P1: block full payload
    P1->>P1: Validate block
    P1->>P2: inv block_hash
    P2->>P1: getdata
    P1->>P2: block
    P2->>P3: inv block_hash
    P3->>P2: getdata
    P2->>P3: block
    Note over Miner,P3: Block reaches majority of network in under 12 seconds
```

### 4.3 Diagram C: Node Type Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Bitcoin Network Participant] --> B[Full Node]
    A --> C[SPV Light Node]
    A --> D[Mining Node]

    D --> D1[Pool Miner]
    D --> D2[Solo Miner]
    C --> C1[Mobile Wallet SPV]
    C --> C2[Bloom Filter Client]

```

### 4.4 Diagram D: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bitcoin Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NW[NETWORK LAYER]
        N1[TCP Socket Manager Port 8333]
        N2[Peer Address Manager]
        N3[Message Serializer Deserializer]
    end
    subgraph AL[APPLICATION LAYER]
        A1[Message Dispatcher]
        A2[Inventory Tracker]
        A3[Mempool Manager]
    end
    subgraph CL[CONSENSUS LAYER]
        C1[Script Verifier]
        C2[UTXO Set Engine]

        S2[UTXO Cache]

    end
```
