# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Define block propagation delay. Why is it considered the most critical bottleneck in blockchain scalability?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Block propagation delay $T_p$ is the wall-clock time elapsed between the moment a miner successfully mines a new block and the moment that block is received and validated by the farthest (in network terms) full node in the system.

> **Criticality — Point 1 (1 Mark):** $T_p$ directly determines the *stale block rate* $f_{orphan} \approx (\Delta/T_b)^2$. A higher $T_p$ increases the probability of competing blocks being mined simultaneously, which leads to chain forks and wasted hash power.

> **Criticality — Point 2 (1 Mark):** $T_p$ imposes a *hard upper bound* on the maximum achievable throughput $\lambda_{max} \leq 1/T_p$. Without reducing $T_p$ via compact blocks, relay networks, or sharding, the blockchain cannot safely process more than $\sim 1/T_p$ transactions per second.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series 1]`

**What is Compact Block Relay (BIP 152)? Mention two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Compact Block Relay, defined in **Bitcoin Improvement Proposal 152 (BIP 152)**, is a protocol optimization that reduces block propagation bandwidth by transmitting only the **80-byte block header** and a list of **6-byte short transaction identifiers (short IDs)**, rather than the full transaction data. Recipients reconstruct full transactions from their local mempool using the short IDs as lookup keys.

> **Advantage 1 (1 Mark):** **Bandwidth reduction of 96-99%** — a typical 1.4 MB block requires only ~20 KB to relay, dramatically reducing backbone traffic and ISP costs for full node operators.

> **Advantage 2 (1 Mark):** **Single round-trip (0-RTT) propagation** — the entire compact message is sent in one UDP-like burst, eliminating the legacy INV/GETDATA two-trip handshake and reducing $T_p$ by a factor of 2-3×.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has a sub-part split of **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must attempt either **Question A OR Question B** (internal choice).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the block propagation mechanism in a Bitcoin-like blockchain P2P network. Draw a neat diagram showing the gossip-based dissemination.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Block propagation in Bitcoin uses an *unsolicited block push* model combined with a *gossip protocol* overlay. When a miner successfully produces a block, it does not wait to be asked — it actively pushes the block to its outbound peers.

**Step-by-Step Mechanism (5 Marks):**

1. **Block Creation & Local Verification:** The miner assembles a block with $n$ transactions, computes the Merkle root, and embeds the previous block hash. The miner verifies each transaction locally (signature, format, double-spend check).

2. **Initial Push (Unsolicited Broadcast):** The miner transmits the block to all of its outbound peers (typically 8-125 connections, default 8 in Bitcoin Core). In modern implementations, the miner sends a **CMPCTBLOCK** message (BIP 152) containing the header and short IDs.

3. **Peer Reception & Reconstruction:** Each recipient node receives the compact message and attempts to reconstruct each transaction by looking up the 6-byte short IDs in its local mempool. If all transactions are reconstructed, the node proceeds to full validation; if some are missing, the node sends a **GETBLOCKTXN** request for only the missing indices.

4. **Full Validation:** Each transaction is verified:
   - ECDSA signature against the previous UTXO.
   - Input UTXO exists in the UTXO set (or has been confirmed in the same block).
   - Script execution succeeds (e.g., P2PKH, P2SH).
   - Coinbase reward does not exceed block subsidy + fees.

5. **Re-Gossip (Cascading Broadcast):** After validation, each peer re-broadcasts the block to *its* outbound peers. This creates an exponential wave governed by the gossip protocol. With peer degree $d$, the time to inform all $N$ nodes is $T_{gossip} = \log_{d-1}(N) \cdot RTT$.

6. **Chain Extension:** Each node appends the new block to its local copy of the blockchain, extending the chain by one. If a competing block at the same height is received, the node waits for the next block to decide via the *longest chain rule* (or, in Ethereum, the *GHOST heuristic*).

**Diagram (1 Mark):**
(Refer to Section 4.1 Block Propagation Flow Diagram above.)

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the Compact Block Relay protocol (BIP 152) in detail. How does it achieve bandwidth savings while preserving security?**

**Model Solution:**

**Background (1 Mark):**
The traditional block relay protocol required ~1.4 MB of data per block to be transmitted to each peer, leading to a global bandwidth cost of ~$1.4 \times 10^4 \times 8 / 600 \approx 186$ KB/s backbone traffic per node, which is unsustainable for low-bandwidth peers and centralized in terms of node operation.

**Compact Block Construction (2 Marks):**
BIP 152 specifies that the sender transmits:
- **Block header** (80 bytes)
- **Nonce** (8 bytes) — random salt to prevent short-ID collisions across multiple blocks
- **shortids** (6 bytes each) — first 48 bits of the SipHash-2-4(transaction_id, nonce)
- **Prefilled transactions** (optional) — for transactions the sender knows the recipient may not have (e.g., the coinbase, or very recent transactions)

Total size: $S_{compact} = 80 + 8 + 6n \approx 14$ KB for a 2000-transaction block.

**Reconstruction on the Receiver (2 Marks):**
The recipient:
1. Computes $SipHash-2-4(TxID_i, \text{nonce})$ for each transaction in its mempool.
2. Performs a hash-table lookup for each received shortid in the computed set.
3. If all shortids match (probability $> 99.9\%$ for typical mempools), the block is fully reconstructed and validated without further communication.
4. If 1-2 shortids miss, the recipient sends a **GETBLOCKTXN** message requesting only the missing transactions by index. This is a *fallback* path — it adds one extra RTT but only in the rare case of mempool divergence.

**Security Preservation (2 Marks):**
- **No trust assumption is weakened** — the receiver still fully validates *every* transaction's signature, UTXO, and script. The compact representation is purely a *transport-layer* optimization.
- **Collision resistance via SipHash** — a 48-bit short ID has $2^{48}$ possible values. For a mempool of 5000 transactions, the birthday-bound collision probability is $\approx 1 - e^{-n^2/2^{49}} \approx 1 - e^{-5000^2/2^{49}} \approx 0$, ensuring negligible false matches.
- **Adversarial resistance** — a malicious sender cannot trick a receiver into accepting an invalid block because the receiver must still execute the full transaction scripts. The compact format is not a security shortcut.

**Conclusion:** Compact Block Relay reduces $T_p$ by ~2-3× and bandwidth by ~98% without compromising the trustless verification model — a canonical example of *layered engineering optimization* in blockchain systems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2 - Alternative]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the role of dedicated block relay networks such as FIBRE, Falcon, and BloXRoute. Why are they necessary even after Compact Block Relay?**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Block relay networks are *infrastructure-level optimizations* that operate *above* the P2P layer, providing ultra-low-latency paths for block distribution. They sit between miners and full nodes, functioning as a *content distribution network (CDN)* for blocks.

**FIBRE — Fast Internet Bitcoin Relay Engine (2 Marks):**
- Developed by **Coinbase** in 2016.
- Uses **UDP-based transmission** to avoid TCP's congestion control overhead, which can stall block propagation during the *slow-start* phase.
- Operates dedicated nodes at internet exchange points (IXPs) to minimize cross-ISP hops.
- Reduces intercontinental block propagation from ~10 s to ~1-2 s.

**Falcon — Fast Asynchronous Consensus via Lazy propagation (2 Marks):**
- Uses a *cut-through routing* approach where a node forwards a block header as soon as it is received, even before full validation.
- Employs *graph-based relay paths* to compute optimal propagation trees.
- Particularly optimized for *high-bandwidth-delay product* (BDP) networks (e.g., trans-Pacific links).

**BloXRoute — Blockchain Distribution Network (BDN) (1 Mark):**
- A *commercial* relay network using a proprietary *Gateway* and *Relay* node architecture.
- Achieves propagation times of ~200-500 ms globally.
- Uses *topological awareness* and *parallel multi-path* dissemination.

**Why Still Necessary After Compact Blocks? (1 Mark):**
- Compact blocks reduce *data size* but cannot reduce the *physical distance* or *internet routing delays* between nodes.
- Relay networks add a *fast backbone* layer that operates parallel to the slow P2P gossip.
- They also reduce the *verification bottleneck* by offloading initial dissemination to a trusted (but verifiable) relay path.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the stale block rate formula $f_{orphan} \approx (\Delta/T_b)^2$ and explain its significance for blockchain security.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model Setup (1 Mark):**
Assume blocks are mined according to a Poisson process with mean rate $\lambda = 1/T_b$. Two miners, A and B, find blocks at times $t_A$ and $t_B$ that may be in either order. The network propagation delay between A and B is symmetric, equal to $\Delta$.

**Step 2 — Fork Condition (1 Mark):**
A fork occurs if and only if A and B find blocks within a window of size $\Delta$ of each other, i.e., $|t_A - t_B| < \Delta$. If $|t_A - t_B| > \Delta$, the first block fully propagates before the second is mined, and no fork occurs.

**Step 3 — Probability of Tight Race (2 Marks):**
For a Poisson process, the inter-arrival time is exponentially distributed with rate $\lambda$. The probability that the next block arrives within time $\Delta$ is:

$$
P(|t_A - t_B| < \Delta) = 1 - e^{-\lambda \Delta}
$$

For small $\lambda \Delta$, this linearizes to $\lambda \Delta$.

**Step 4 — Probability of Propagation Failure (2 Marks):**
Given that a tight race exists, the *second* block has a probability proportional to the remaining propagation time of competing. The expected fraction of such forks that *fail to resolve* in time is again proportional to $\lambda \Delta$.

**Step 5 — Combined Rate (1 Mark):**
Multiplying the two independent probabilities:

$$
f_{orphan} \approx (\lambda \Delta)^2 = \left(\frac{\Delta}{T_b}\right)^2
$$

**Significance for Security (Bonus - integrated above):**
- **Hash power waste:** A stale block represents Wasted computational effort equal to one block's worth of PoW. With $f_{orphan} = 0.001$, the network wastes 0.1% of total hash power — a direct economic loss.
- **51% attack threshold reduction:** Effective hash power securing the chain is $(1 - f_{orphan}) \cdot H_{total}$. A higher $f_{orphan}$ makes 51% attacks easier to execute.
- **Selfish mining amplification:** Eyal and Sirer (2014) showed that selfish mining profitability increases non-linearly with $f_{orphan}$ (and thus with $T_p$), making propagation a *security-critical* parameter.
- **Decentralization pressure:** To reduce $T_p$, miners are incentivized to use centralized relay networks and large block-only connections, pushing out small miners.

**Conclusion:** The formula $f_{orphan} \approx (\Delta/T_b)^2$ reveals that *halving the block interval* without addressing propagation *quadruples* the stale rate — a core argument against reducing $T_b$ in Bitcoin without deploying sharding or Layer-2 solutions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Block Propagation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *Block Propagation Delay* with *Block Interval*** — Students often interchange $T_p$ and $T_b$. Remember: $T_p$ is the time for one block to reach all nodes; $T_b$ is the time between two consecutive blocks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the verification step** — The compact block is *not* a security shortcut. The receiver MUST still validate every transaction. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for omitting this.
>
> 3. **Not citing BIP numbers** — KTU expects standard protocol identifiers: *BIP 152 for Compact Blocks*, *BIP 37 for Bloom Filters* (mempool sync), etc. Vague references cost marks.
>
> 4. **Skipping the numerical example** — In derivation questions, the model answer *always* includes a numerical sanity check (e.g., plugging in $\Delta = 10$, $T_b = 600$ to get $f_{orphan} \approx 0.028\%$). Omitting this loses 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Stating "more bandwidth = faster propagation" without justification** — Bandwidth is only one factor; *latency*, *RTT*, and *verification CPU* also contribute. A complete answer must mention the multi-factor delay model $T_p = T_{serialization} + T_{network} + T_{verification}$.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the Scalability Trilemma** — A discussion of block propagation in 2024 scheme papers *must* reference the *trilemma* (decentralization vs. security vs. scalability) for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Block Propagation and Block Relay**

- **Block Propagation Delay $T_p$** is the time for a newly mined block to reach all full nodes in the P2P network. It is composed of *serialization*, *transmission*, *verification*, and *bandwidth-delay* components.
- The propagation mechanism in Bitcoin uses **unsolicited block push** combined with a **gossip protocol** overlay, achieving exponential dissemination at rate $\log_{d-1}(N)$ RTTs.
- **Stale/orphan block rate** is governed by the **Decker-Wattenhofer formula** $f_{orphan} = 1 - e^{-\Delta/T_b}(1 + \Delta/T_b)$, with the simplified approximation $f_{orphan} \approx (\Delta/T_b)^2$.
- **Compact Block Relay (BIP 152)** reduces block transmission size by 96-99% by sending only the 80-byte header plus **6-byte short transaction IDs (shortids)**, with recipients reconstructing transactions from their local mempool via **SipHash-2-4** keyed lookups.
- **Block Relay Networks** (FIBRE, Falcon, BloXRoute) operate as a *content distribution layer* above the P2P gossip, reducing global propagation to 200-500 ms.
- **Key trade-off:** Increasing block size raises throughput but proportionally increases $T_p$, which quadratically raises $f_{orphan}$, threatening security and decentralization.
- **Ethereum's GHOST protocol** mitigates propagation forks by rewarding *uncle* blocks, accepting a higher $f_{orphan}$ without security loss.
- **Maximum throughput** is bounded by $\lambda_{max} \leq 1/T_p$ — a fundamental scalability constraint rooted in the physics of internet packet routing.
- **Security implications:** Higher $T_p$ → higher $f_{orphan}$ → wasted hash power, easier 51% attacks, and increased *selfish mining* profitability (Eyal-Sirer 2014).
- **Mempool synchronization** is a prerequisite for compact block efficiency — peers with divergent mempools incur an extra GETBLOCKTXN round-trip, falling back to legacy relay only for missing transactions.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Block Propagation and Block Relay**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bitcoin Consensus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — BITCOIN CONSENSUS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bitcoin Consensus** is the decentralized protocol that enables all honest nodes in a peer-to-peer network to **agree on a single, canonical history of transactions** without relying on a central trusted authority. It is implemented as a **Nakamoto Consensus**, which combines **Proof of Work (PoW)**, **the longest-chain rule**, and **incentive compatibility** to probabilistically solve the Byzantine Generals Problem in an open, permissionless setting.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the consensus layer in Bitcoin is responsible for three engineering properties simultaneously:
1. **Agreement** — All honest nodes converge to the same ledger.
2. **Validity** — Only valid transactions (verified signatures, sufficient balances, correct syntax) are accepted.
3. **Termination / Liveness** — New transactions are eventually included in blocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Technical Constant:** The Bitcoin network targets a new block every **10 minutes**, with the protocol retargeting the mining difficulty every **2016 blocks** (≈ **14 days**). Maximum block size is **1 MB** (SegWit effective ~**4 MB**), and the block reward halves approximately every **210,000 blocks** (~**4 years**) in an event called the **Bitcoin Halving**.

### 1.2 Intuition: The Town Hall Voting Analogy

Imagine **5,000 villages** spread across a country trying to keep one **shared ledger of who owns what land**. There is no king, no post office, no central bank. Every village has a scribe (a *node*). Each scribe can propose new land transfers (transactions), but the villages must collectively agree on the *order* of these transfers.

The problem? Some scribes may be honest, others may be traitors (a **Byzantine** condition). How can honest scribes out-vote traitors when they cannot even be sure *who* the other scribes are?

**Bitcoin's answer is elegant:** Instead of voting on every transaction, villages elect a "lottery winner" every 10 minutes who publishes a *page* (a **block**) of transactions. The lottery is solved by brute computational force — a **Proof of Work** — which is verifiable by every village in milliseconds but expensive to fake. The longest chain of valid pages is treated as truth. This is **Nakamoto Consensus** in plain English.

### 1.3 The Byzantine Generals Problem — Formal Framing

The Byzantine Generals Problem (formulated by Lamport, Shostak, Pease in **1982**) is a classical distributed-systems dilemma: several divisions of an army surround a city and must coordinate an attack. Generals can communicate only by messenger, and **some generals may be traitors** who send conflicting messages. The question: *Can the loyal generals reach agreement on a common plan of action?*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Impossibility under pure voting):** In a synchronous network with $n$ generals, consensus is achievable **iff** the number of traitors $t$ satisfies $n \ge 3t + 1$. For $n=5$, only $t=1$ traitor is tolerable. Classical Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) protocols require this quorum — making them unsuitable for open systems with millions of unknown participants.

**Bitcoin's innovation:** Replace identity-based voting with **anonymous, expensive-to-produce Proof of Work tokens**, then use **the longest valid chain** as the agreement rule. This works without a fixed quorum and tolerates up to **< 50%** adversarial hash power (the famous **51% threshold**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bitcoin Consensus Loop (Operational Breakdown)

Bitcoin consensus runs in continuous, overlapping rounds. Each round produces exactly **one** globally accepted block. The protocol logic is:

1. **Transaction Broadcast** — Users sign transactions with **ECDSA (secp256k1)** and broadcast them across the gossip network; nodes keep unconfirmed transactions in a **mempool**.
2. **Block Construction** — Miners (technically *validators* who construct candidate blocks) assemble a candidate block: a **coinbase transaction** rewarding themselves, plus a fee-aggregated set of mempool transactions.
3. **Proof-of-Work Search** — The miner increments a **nonce** and computes $\text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{block header}))$ until the digest is numerically less than a **target threshold** $T$.
4. **Block Propagation** — Upon success, the miner broadcasts the block. Neighbors verify PoW (in O(1)) and propagate further.
5. **Chain Extension / Fork Resolution** — Honest nodes extend the chain with the longest valid PoW, breaking ties by the block received first.
6. **Difficulty Retarget** — Every 2016 blocks, all nodes independently recompute the difficulty so that the *next* 2016 blocks will again take ≈ 14 days.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Proof of Work?** A valid PoW is *asymmetrically costly to produce* (O(2²⁵⁶/T) hashes on average) but *trivially cheap to verify* (one SHA-256 evaluation). This asymmetry is the cryptographic anchor of Bitcoin consensus — it converts electricity into unforgeable digital voting weight.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Notation | Typical / Limit Value |
|----------|---------|----------|----------------------|
| Block reward schedule | $R_n = 50 \cdot 2^{-\lfloor n / 210000 \rfloor}$ BTC | $n$ = halving epoch index (0,1,2,…) | $R_0 = 50$, $R_1 = 25$, $R_2 = 12.5$, $R_3 = 6.25$, $R_4 = 3.125$ BTC |
| Total BTC supply cap | $S_{\max} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-n} = 210000 \cdot 100 = 21{,}000{,}000$ | Geometric series | **21 million BTC** (cap) |
| Mining success probability per hash | $p = T \,/\, 2^{256}$ | $T$ = target value | $p \approx 1 \,/\, (D \cdot 2^{32})$ |
| Expected hashes to find a block | $E[H] = 1/p = 2^{256}/T$ | | Currently $\approx 7.5 \times 10^{22}$ |
| Expected time to find one block (network) | $E[t] = 2^{256} \,/\, (T \cdot H_s)$ | $H_s$ = total network hash rate (hashes/sec) | **600 s** (10 min target) |
| Difficulty metric | $D = D_{\max} / T$ where $D_{\max} = 2^{224}$ | | $D \approx 7.5 \times 10^{13}$ (2024) |
| Difficulty retarget (every 2016 blocks) | $D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot \dfrac{t_{\text{actual}}}{20160 \text{ min}}$ | $t_{\text{actual}}$ = time elapsed for last 2016 blocks | Clamped to $0.25 \le D_{\text{new}}/D_{\text{old}} \le 4$ |
| Block subsidy halving interval | $210{,}000$ blocks | | $\approx 4$ years |
| Hash function | $h = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{header}))$ | 256-bit digest, expressed as 64-hex | |
| Nakamoto threshold | Byzantine hash power $f < 0.5$ | $f$ = fraction of adversary hash | **51% attack** threshold |
| Cumulative work of a chain | $W = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{2^{256}}{T_i}$ | $T_i$ = target of block $i$ | Longest chain rule uses $W$, not block count |
| Mean time between blocks (Poisson) | $P(\text{time} \le t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$, with $\lambda = H_s T / 2^{256}$ | | Exponential distribution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU 2024 Note:** The "longest chain" rule is actually more accurately phrased as the **"heaviest / most-work chain"** rule. A chain with more cumulative difficulty (sum of $2^{256}/T_i$) wins — *not* the chain with the most blocks, because difficulty can differ across forks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The consensus mechanism underpins every Proof-of-Work cryptocurrency (Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin, Bitcoin Cash, prior Ethereum). Variants of the same formula structure appear in:
- **PoS systems** (replace $2^{256}/T$ with stake-weighted voting).
- **Permissioned blockchains** (Hyperledger Fabric, R3 Corda) — adapt the formula to BFT-style voting with $n \ge 3f+1$.
- **Inter-bank settlement systems** explore the longest-chain rule for auditability.

Understanding Bitcoin consensus is therefore the **canonical entry point** for all distributed-ledger engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** All steps below are explicitly written; no logical step is skipped. Read each transition row carefully.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Bitcoin Supply Caps at 21 Million

**Step 1 — Define the reward function.**
The block reward at halving epoch $n$ (where $n = 0,1,2,3,\dots$) is:
$$R(n) = 50 \cdot 2^{-n} \ \text{BTC}$$

**Step 2 — Each epoch mines exactly 210,000 blocks.**
Per the Bitcoin protocol, the reward halves every 210,000 blocks:
$$\text{blocks per epoch} = 210{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Total BTC mined in epoch $n$.**
$$S(n) = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-n} = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2^{-n} \ \text{BTC}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the geometric series over all epochs.**
Total supply $S_{\max}$ is the infinite sum:
$$S_{\max} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2^{-n}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the geometric series identity $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x^n = 1/(1-x)$ with $x = 1/2$.**
$$S_{\max} = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}} = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2$$

**Step 6 — Final value.**
$$\boxed{S_{\max} = 21{,}000{,}000 \ \text{BTC}}$$

The convergence is *exponential*: by epoch $n=32$, the per-block reward drops below **1 satoshi** ($10^{-8}$ BTC), effectively halting further emission. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Difficulty Retarget Formula (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Establish the constants.**
- Target block interval: $\tau = 600$ seconds = 10 minutes.
- Retarget period: $N = 2016$ blocks.
- Ideal time for $N$ blocks: $T_{\text{ideal}} = N \cdot \tau = 2016 \cdot 600 = 1{,}209{,}600$ seconds = **20,160 minutes** = 14 days.

**Step 2 — Measure the actual elapsed time.**
Let $T_{\text{actual}}$ be the wall-clock seconds elapsed between the timestamps of block $2016(k-1)+1$ and block $2016k$, observed at the time the $(2016k+1)$-th block is being mined.

**Step 3 — Scale the old target proportionally.**
The new target $T_{\text{new}}$ is scaled so that, at the *current* network hashrate $H_s$, blocks will again arrive at 10-minute intervals:
$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{ideal}}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to difficulty form.**
Since $D = D_{\max} / T$ and $D_{\max}$ is constant, the difficulty version is:
$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{ideal}}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the safety clamp.**
The protocol prevents wild swings: any change is bounded by a factor of 4 in either direction:
$$0.25 \le \frac{D_{\text{new}}}{D_{\text{old}}} \le 4$$

This bounds the recovery from a 50% hash-rate drop (or 4× surge) within a single retarget window.

**Step 6 — Worked numerical example.**
Suppose $T_{\text{actual}} = 10{,}080$ minutes (7 days, blocks came in twice as fast because hashrate doubled). Then:
$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{10{,}080}{20{,}160} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot 0.50$$

Difficulty halves — restoring 10-minute cadence. ✅

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Expected Time to Mine a Block

**Step 1 — Define a single hash attempt's success probability.**
A block is valid iff the double-SHA-256 of the header is numerically less than target $T$. The 256-bit hash space is uniform over $[0, 2^{256}-1]$, so:
$$p = \Pr[h < T] = \frac{T}{2^{256}}$$

**Step 2 — Each hash attempt is an independent Bernoulli trial.**
The number of trials $H$ until the first success is geometrically distributed:
$$\Pr[H = h] = (1-p)^{h-1} \cdot p, \quad h = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

**Step 3 — Expected number of hashes.**
$$E[H] = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{2^{256}}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to wall-clock time using hashrate $H_s$.**
$$E[t] = \frac{E[H]}{H_s} = \frac{2^{256}}{T \cdot H_s}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the design hashrate at target $T$.**
Setting $E[t] = 600$ s:
$$H_s = \frac{2^{256}}{600 \cdot T} = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{600}$$

So network hashrate is *directly* observable from current $D$ and $E[t]$. ✅

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Probability of a Successful 51% Attack (Cumulative)

**Step 1 — Attacker controls fraction $q$ of total hashrate; honest miners control $p = 1 - q$.**

**Step 2 — Define a "catch-up" problem.**
If the attacker is $\Delta$ blocks behind, the probability of ever catching up (from the attacker's perspective, solving the **Gambler's Ruin** problem in random walks) is:
$$P_{\text{catch}}(\Delta) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } q \ge p \\ \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^{\Delta} & \text{if } q < p \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
- If $q \ge 0.5$ (attacker has majority), $P_{\text{catch}} = 1$ → guaranteed double-spend.
- If $q < 0.5$, $P_{\text{catch}}$ decays **exponentially** with $\Delta$.
  - Example: $q = 0.10$, $\Delta = 6$:
$$P_{\text{catch}} = (0.10/0.90)^6 \approx 1.69 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
This exponential decay is the **security backbone** of Bitcoin: an attacker would need not only majority hash power but also a *deep* lead to win, making the attack economically irrational given hardware and electricity costs.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Consensus Approaches)

| Property | Bitcoin PoW | Practical BFT (PBFT) | Proof of Stake (PoS) |
|----------|-------------|-----------------------|----------------------|
| Open / permissionless | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (fixed validator set) | ✅ Yes |
| Energy cost | ⚠️ Very high | ✅ Low | ✅ Low |
| Throughput | ~7 TPS | ~1,000 TPS | ~1,000–100,000 TPS |
| Finality | Probabilistic (~6 blocks ≈ 1 h) | Deterministic (immediate) | Probabilistic / deterministic variants |
| Adversary tolerance | $f < 0.5$ | $f < (n-1)/3$ | $f < 0.5$ (stake-weighted) |
| Sybil resistance | Cost of computation | Permissioned identity | Cost of stake |
| KTU exam weight | **High** (Module 3 core) | Medium | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Bitcoin Mining & Consensus Flow (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[User signs transaction with private key ECDSA secp256k1] --> A1[Broadcast to peer to peer gossip network]
    A1 --> A2[Node validates: signature balance syntax]
    A2 --> A3{Valid?}
    A3 -- no --> A4[Drop and ban misbehaving peer]
    A3 -- yes --> A5[Add to mempool unconfirmed set]
    A5 --> B0[Miner assembles candidate block: coinbase plus mempool txs]
    B0 --> B1[Build merkle tree: pairwise SHA256 hashing]
    B1 --> B2[Construct 80 byte block header: version prevhash merkleroot timestamp nbits nonce]
    B2 --> B3[Compute double SHA256 of header]
    B3 --> B4{Hash less than target T?}
    B4 -- no --> B5[Increment nonce or update coinbase extra nonce]
    B5 --> B3
    B4 -- yes --> B6[Broadcast new block to peers]
    B6 --> C1[Receiving node validates: PoW merkle signatures]
    C1 --> C2{Valid and extends longest chain?}
    C2 -- yes --> C3[Append to local copy of blockchain]
    C2 -- no --> C4[Reject or store as stale block]
    C3 --> D1[Update UTXO set: remove spent outputs add new outputs]
    D1 --> D2[Forward block to other peers]
    D2 --> E1[Every 2016 blocks: recompute difficulty retarget]
    E1 --> E2[Network converges to canonical chain]
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Fork Resolution & Longest Chain Rule

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Genesis Block] --> B1[Block 1]
    B1 --> B2[Block 2]
    B1 --> B3[Block 2 prime competing]
    B2 --> B4[Block 3 chain A]
    B3 --> B5[Block 3 prime chain B]
    B4 --> B6[Block 4 chain A extended]
    B5 --> B7[Block 4 prime chain B]
    B6 --> B8[Block 5 chain A wins]
    B7 -.-> X[Orphaned chain B blocks become stale]
    B8 --> C[Canonical longest chain with most cumulative work]
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Block Structure Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Block Header 80 bytes] --> H1[Version 4 bytes]
    H --> H2[Previous Block Hash 32 bytes SHA256 of prior header]
    H --> H3[Merkle Root 32 bytes root of tx merkle tree]
    H --> H4[Timestamp 4 bytes unix epoch seconds]
    H --> H5[nbits 4 bytes compact encoding of target T]
    H --> H6[Nonce 4 bytes incremented by miner]
    B[Block Body variable size up to 4 MB SegWit] --> B1[Coinbase transaction miner reward plus fees]
    B --> B2[Transaction 1]
    B --> B3[Transaction 2]
    B --> B4[Transaction N]
    B2 --> M[Merkle Tree built over all transactions]
    B3 --> M
    B4 --> M
    B1 --> M
    M --> H3
```

## 4.4 Diagram D — 51% Attack Double-Spend Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User sends BTC to merchant] --> M[Merchant waits k confirmations]
    U --> A[Attacker secretly mines parallel chain]
    A --> A1[Attacker re includes own payment back to self]
    A1 --> A2[Attacker extends private chain with hash power q]
    A2 --> Q{Attacker chain longer than public chain?}
    Q -- no --> Q1[Attack fails cost of wasted electricity]
    Q -- yes --> A3[Attacker broadcasts longer chain to network]
    A3 --> N[Honest nodes accept longest valid chain]
    N --> N1[Original payment to merchant is reverted]
    N1 --> N2[Merchant loses goods and BTC]
    N1 --> N3[Attacker keeps goods and recovers BTC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** The exponential decay in $P_{\text{catch}} = (q/p)^{\Delta}$ is precisely why merchants should wait for **k ≥ 6 confirmations** for high-value transactions. Each additional confirmation makes the attack cost grow by a factor of $p/q$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Bitcoin Consensus. Explain the role of Proof of Work in achieving decentralized agreement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **1 mark — Definition:** Bitcoin Consensus is a protocol enabling all honest nodes in a permissionless peer-to-peer network to agree on a single canonical transaction history without a central authority, by combining Proof of Work, the longest-chain rule, and economic incentives.
- **1 mark — PoW Role:** Proof of Work requires miners to find a nonce such that $\text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{header})) < T$, making block production expensive and random. This converts electricity into unforgeable voting weight, solving the Sybil problem.
- **1 mark — Outcome:** PoW is asymmetric — costly to produce but cheap to verify — so all nodes can validate consensus decisions in O(1) time, enabling global agreement on a shared ledger.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Byzantine Generals Problem? How does Bitcoin's longest-chain rule address it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **1 mark — BGP Definition:** The Byzantine Generals Problem is a distributed-systems dilemma where a group of generals (some loyal, some traitors) must agree on a coordinated action via unreliable messengers, with traitors potentially sending conflicting messages.
- **1 mark — Classical limit:** In synchronous systems with $n$ generals, classical BFT protocols require $n \ge 3t + 1$ to reach agreement, where $t$ is the number of traitors.
- **1 mark — Bitcoin's solution:** Bitcoin replaces identity-based voting with anonymous, computationally-expensive Proof-of-Work tokens. The longest valid chain (most cumulative work) is treated as truth, tolerating up to $f < 0.5$ adversarial hash power without requiring a fixed quorum.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *Attempt any one of the following:*

> **Q(A). (a)** Explain the Bitcoin Proof-of-Work consensus algorithm in detail, including the block header structure, mining process, and validation procedure. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q(A). (b)** Derive the difficulty retarget formula. If the previous 2016 blocks were mined in 10,080 minutes instead of the ideal 20,160 minutes, compute the new difficulty if the old difficulty was $D_{\text{old}} = 8.0 \times 10^{13}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q(A)(a) [7 marks]

1. **Block Header (2 marks):** The 80-byte Bitcoin block header contains — version (4 B), previous block hash (32 B), merkle root (32 B), timestamp (4 B), nbits (4 B, compact target), and nonce (4 B). The block body contains the coinbase transaction and ordinary transactions, summarized by the merkle root.
2. **Mining Process (3 marks):** A miner assembles a candidate block, constructs the merkle root, then iteratively increments the nonce and computes $h = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{header}))$ until $h < T$. The expected number of attempts is $2^{256}/T$. When found, the block is broadcast to peers.
3. **Validation (2 marks):** Each receiving node re-evaluates the double-SHA-256 against target $T$ (O(1)), verifies all transaction signatures, confirms the merkle root matches the listed transactions, checks that the coinbase reward is correctly computed, and ensures block size is within limits. Honest nodes extend the chain with the most cumulative work.

#### Model Solution — Q(A)(b) [7 marks]

1. **Recall the retarget formula (2 marks):**
$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{ideal}}}$$
where $T_{\text{ideal}} = 2016 \times 10 = 20{,}160$ minutes.

2. **Substitute the given value (1 mark):**
$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{10{,}080}{20{,}160} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot 0.5$$

3. **Numerical evaluation (2 marks):**
$$D_{\text{new}} = 8.0 \times 10^{13} \times 0.5 = 4.0 \times 10^{13}$$

4. **Interpretation (2 marks):** Since blocks came twice as fast (in 7 days instead of 14), the network hashrate effectively doubled during that window. The protocol halves the difficulty to restore the 10-minute block interval. This passes the safety clamp $0.25 \le D_{\text{new}}/D_{\text{old}} \le 4$, so it is applied directly. ✅

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *Alternative choice:*

> **Q(B). (a)** Discuss the 51% attack on Bitcoin. An attacker controls 30% of network hashrate. Compute the probability of catching up from a 6-block deficit. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q(B). (b)** Explain the block reward halving schedule. Derive mathematically why the total BTC supply converges to 21 million. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q(B)(a) [7 marks]

1. **Definition of 51% attack (2 marks):** A 51% attack occurs when a single entity controls a majority (>50%) of the network hashrate, allowing them to rewrite recent transaction history, perform double-spends, and censor transactions. With $q < 0.5$, attacks remain economically irrational.
2. **Gambler's Ruin formula (2 marks):** With attacker fraction $q$ and honest fraction $p = 1 - q$, the probability of catching up from a $\Delta$-block deficit (when $q < p$) is:
$$P_{\text{catch}}(\Delta) = \left(\frac{q}{p}\right)^{\Delta}$$
3. **Substitute $q = 0.30$, $p = 0.70$, $\Delta = 6$ (1 mark):**
$$P_{\text{catch}}(6) = \left(\frac{0.30}{0.70}\right)^{6} = (0.4286)^{6}$$
4. **Numerical evaluation (1 mark):**
$$P_{\text{catch}}(6) = (0.4286)^{6} \approx 6.19 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.62\%$$
5. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Even with 30% hashrate, catching up from 6 blocks is improbable ($\sim 0.6\%$). This demonstrates why the "6 confirmation" rule is widely adopted. The probability decays exponentially with $\Delta$.

#### Model Solution — Q(B)(b) [7 marks]

1. **Halving schedule (2 marks):** Every 210,000 blocks (~4 years), the block reward halves. The sequence is 50 → 25 → 12.5 → 6.25 → 3.125 → … BTC. This creates a **disinflationary** monetary policy.
2. **Formula (1 mark):** The reward at halving epoch $n$ is $R(n) = 50 \cdot 2^{-n}$ BTC.
3. **Set up the supply sum (1 mark):**
$$S_{\max} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-n} = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 2^{-n}$$
4. **Apply geometric series (1 mark):** Since $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (1/2)^n = 2$:
$$S_{\max} = 10{,}500{,}000 \times 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \ \text{BTC}$$
5. **Convergence justification (1 mark):** After 32 halvings, the per-block reward falls below 1 satoshi ($10^{-8}$ BTC), and rounding effectively halts emission, even though the geometric series technically never reaches zero.
6. **Engineering significance (1 mark):** This hard cap, mathematically enforced, distinguishes Bitcoin from fiat currencies and is a foundational property for its store-of-value narrative.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse** *longest chain* with *most blocks* — the rule is **most cumulative work**, weighted by $2^{256}/T_i$.
> - **Do not state** that Bitcoin is "instantly final" — finality is **probabilistic**, conventionally accepted after **6 confirmations** (~1 hour).
> - **Do not misquote** the cap: it is **21,000,000 BTC** (not 21 million coins, which technically exist as 100 million × 21 million = 2.1 quadrillion **satoshis**).
> - **Do not forget the safety clamp** $[0.25, 4]$ in the retarget formula — it is frequently tested.
> - **Do not present PoW energy cost as a flaw without context** — the security model is *defined* by the electricity expenditure, so the cost is a feature, not a bug, in Bitcoin's design philosophy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Bitcoin Consensus** = **Nakamoto Consensus** = PoW + longest-chain (most-work) rule + economic incentives.
- **Target interval:** 10 minutes per block; **retarget interval:** 2016 blocks ≈ 14 days.
- **Difficulty metric:** $D = D_{\max} / T$ with $D_{\max} = 2^{224}$.
- **Hash function:** double SHA-256 of the 80-byte block header.
- **Per-hash success probability:** $p = T / 2^{256}$.
- **Expected hashes per block:** $2^{256} / T$.
- **Retarget formula:** $D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot (T_{\text{actual}} / 20{,}160\ \text{min})$, clamped to $[0.25, 4]$.
- **Halving:** reward halves every 210,000 blocks; the **21 million BTC supply cap** is derived from the geometric series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 10.5M \cdot 2^{-n} = 21M$.
- **Byzantine tolerance:** $f < 0.5$ of total hash power (the **51% attack** threshold).
- **Catch-up probability (Gambler's Ruin):** $P_{\text{catch}}(\Delta) = (q/p)^{\Delta}$ when $q < p$.
- **6 confirmations** ≈ 1 hour of cumulative work — practical finality for high-value transactions.
- **Block size:** 1 MB legacy / ~4 MB SegWit; **block header:** exactly 80 bytes.
- **Consensus vs. validity:** the network must agree on *one* chain *and* every block must be *valid* (signatures, supply, scripts).
- **Probabilistic finality** is the hallmark of Nakamoto Consensus — distinguish from deterministic BFT finality.
- **Most-work ≠ most-blocks** — this is a KTU exam favourite trick question.
- **Forks:** accidental (resolved by most-work rule) vs. intentional (hard fork = protocol change, soft fork = tightening of rules).
- **Selfish mining** is a real attack vector where a miner withholds blocks to gain disproportionate revenue, related to the 51% analysis.
- **Energy → security:** PoW's electricity expenditure is the *physical anchor* of digital scarcity in Bitcoin.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proof of Work (PoW), Hashcash PoW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Proof of Work (PoW), Hashcash PoW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Proof of Work (PoW)

**Proof of Work (PoW)** is a **cryptographic consensus protocol** originally formalized by Cynthia Dwork and Moni Naor in 1993, and later refined for decentralized digital cash systems. In the context of blockchain, PoW is a **Sybil-resistance mechanism** that requires a participant (called a *miner*) to expend a measurable amount of real-world computational effort — typically in the form of repeated hash computations — before being allowed to append a new block of transactions to the distributed ledger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> *Proof of Work is a consensus algorithm in which network participants must solve a computationally intensive, but easily verifiable, mathematical puzzle based on cryptographic hash functions. The first participant to find a valid solution earns the right to propose the next block and is rewarded with newly minted cryptocurrency plus transaction fees.*

The defining property of PoW is its **asymmetric cost structure**:

$$
\text{Cost to Find} \gg \text{Cost to Verify}
$$

That is, finding the solution is *hard* (brute-force search), but once a solution is broadcast, every other node can verify it almost instantly with a single hash evaluation.

## 1.2 Formal Academic Definition of Hashcash PoW

**Hashcash** is a specific **Proof-of-Work scheme** proposed by **Adam Back in 1997** in the Cypherpunk mailing list, originally intended as an anti-spam mechanism for email systems. The Hashcash PoW algorithm requires the sender of a message to compute a *hash stamp* — a token whose cryptographic hash (e.g., SHA-1 or SHA-256) starts with a stipulated number of **leading zero bits**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hashcash Stamp (General Form):**
> A Hashcash stamp is a tuple *(version, date, message, counter)* such that a partially-colliding hash of the stamp yields a value less than a target. In Bitcoin, the message is replaced by the block header and the counter is the *nonce*.

In 2008, **Satoshi Nakamoto** adopted the Hashcash design as the consensus backbone of Bitcoin, replacing SHA-1 with double **SHA-256** and the email recipient string with the **Merkle root of transactions** plus the **previous block hash**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "National Lottery" Model

Imagine a **massive national lottery** held every 10 minutes:

- The government (protocol) publishes a *target number* (e.g., "ticket numbers below 1,000 win").
- Every player (miner) buys a *unique numbered ticket* every microsecond by repeatedly changing a *secret serial number* (the **nonce**) printed on it.
- Each ticket has a fixed, transparent *check digit* (the **cryptographic hash**) that anyone can compute instantly from the serial number.
- The first player whose ticket has a check digit below the target shouts "Bingo!" and broadcasts the winning ticket to all other players.
- Other players verify the winning ticket in milliseconds (one hash computation), then jointly update the official record book (the *blockchain*).

The "work" is the **electricity, hardware depreciation, and time** spent generating trillions of tickets. The "proof" is the **single winning ticket** that is mathematically tied to all the failed attempts via the deterministic hash function.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy works for KTU viva:**
> It captures the three pillars of PoW: (1) *expensive trial*, (2) *trivial verification*, and (3) *probabilistic, lottery-like winner selection*. Examiners love answers that frame PoW in terms of these three properties.

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Nonce** | *Number used ONCE*; a 32-bit counter incremented by miners until a valid hash is found. |
| **Target** | A 256-bit threshold; a block hash must be numerically less than this value. |
| **Difficulty** | The reciprocal measure of the target; higher difficulty = smaller target = more leading zero bits. |
| **Block Header** | 80-byte structure hashed in mining; contains version, prev-hash, Merkle root, timestamp, bits (encoded target), and nonce. |
| **Hash Rate** | Network-wide measure of hashes computed per second, expressed in **H/s, kH/s, MH/s, GH/s, TH/s, PH/s, EH/s, ZH/s**. |
| **Block Reward** | Newly minted coins awarded to the miner who finds a valid PoW solution (e.g., **3.125 BTC** post-2024 halving). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Network Metric (Bitcoin, 2024):** Average network hash rate ≈ **600–700 EH/s** (Exa-hashes per second), i.e., approximately $6 \times 10^{20}$ SHA-256 evaluations per second globally.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Hashcash PoW — Six Structured Steps

The Hashcash PoW algorithm decomposes into the following logical pipeline (board-exam favourite):

1. **Construct the candidate message block.** The miner assembles the 80-byte block header fields: *version*, *previous block hash*, *Merkle root of transactions*, *timestamp*, *encoded target (bits)*.
2. **Inject a variable field — the nonce.** A 32-bit unsigned integer (range $0$ to $2^{32}-1$) is appended to the header. This is the *only* field the miner freely mutates.
3. **Compute the cryptographic digest.** Apply the **double SHA-256** hash function to the entire header:
$$H = \text{SHA-256}\bigl(\text{SHA-256}(\text{header})\bigr)$$
4. **Compare against the target.** Check whether the resulting 256-bit digest $H$, interpreted as an unsigned integer, is strictly less than the **target** $T$:
$$H \overset{?}{<} T$$
5. **Branch on outcome.** If the inequality holds, the PoW is *valid* and the miner broadcasts the block. If not, the miner increments the nonce (or modifies the *extra-nonce* in the coinbase transaction) and returns to Step 3.
6. **Network verification.** Every full node independently re-computes the hash and confirms $H < T$ in a single iteration, instantly accepting or rejecting the block.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a hash function?** It is **deterministic** (anyone gets the same output), **one-way** (cannot reverse-engineer the nonce from $H$), and **avalanche-effect** (a 1-bit change in input flips ~50% of output bits, making brute-force search the only viable strategy).
- **Why double SHA-256?** Defends against **length-extension attacks** on the underlying SHA-256 compression function (a known weakness of naïve single-hash Merkle–Damgård constructions).
- **Why a 256-bit target?** Provides an astronomically large search space ($2^{256} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{77}$), ensuring that no adversary can feasibly enumerate the entire space.
- **Why 10 minutes (Bitcoin)?** A trade-off between **transaction confirmation latency** and **probability of forks** — longer intervals reduce simultaneous-block collisions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \text{SHA-256}\bigl(\text{SHA-256}(\text{header})\bigr)$ | Double-hash digest of block header | 256-bit output |
| 2 | $H < T$ | Validity condition for mined block | Both 256-bit integers |
| 3 | $P(\text{success per trial}) = \dfrac{T}{2^{256}}$ | Probability that a random hash falls below target | Dimensionless; $0 < P \ll 1$ |
| 4 | $P = 2^{-d}$ | Equivalent form when $d$ = required leading zero bits | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\mathbb{E}[N] = \dfrac{1}{P} = \dfrac{2^{256}}{T} = 2^{d}$ | Expected number of trials (PoW trials to find one block) | Number of hashes |
| 6 | $t_{\text{block}} = \dfrac{\mathbb{E}[N]}{R}$ | Expected time to mine one block | Seconds |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{\mathbb{E}[N]}{t_{\text{block}}}$ | Required network hash rate | Hashes per second |
| 8 | $D_{n+1} = D_n \times \dfrac{t_{\text{actual}}}{t_{\text{expected}}}$ | Bitcoin difficulty adjustment (every 2016 blocks) | Dimensionless ratio |
| 9 | $\text{Energy per hash} \approx \mathbf{30\text{–}50\ J/TH}$ | Modern ASIC efficiency benchmark (e.g., Antminer S21) | Joules per tera-hash |
| 10 | $\text{Bitcoin supply cap} = 21{,}000{,}000$ | Total coins that will ever exist | BTC |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table-Syntax Pitfall:** In KTU answer sheets, the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ is written with the LaTeX command `\vert`, **not** the vertical pipe `|`, which would break markdown table rendering. Examiners may deduct marks for illegible formulas.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of PoW Systems

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Cryptocurrency** | Block validation in Bitcoin, Litecoin (Scrypt), Dogecoin (Scrypt), Bitcoin Cash. |
| **Anti-Spam Email** | Hashcash stamps force spammers to spend CPU cycles per email, rendering bulk spam uneconomical. |
| **DDoS Mitigation** | Services require clients to submit a PoW before processing requests, raising the cost of attack. |
| **Decentralized Time-Stamping** | Proof-of-existence services (e.g., OriginStamp) anchor document hashes on PoW chains. |
| **Sidechains & Merged Mining** | Namecoin, Elastos historically piggybacked on Bitcoin's PoW for security. |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU viva:** When asked "Why not just use Proof of Stake?", reply: "PoW provides a **physical, thermodynamic anchor** to digital scarcity — the security budget is paid in real-world electricity, whereas PoS security is endogenous to the same token being secured, creating a circularity of incentives." This is a high-scoring 3-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Number of Hash Trials (Binomial → Geometric Distribution)

Each hash attempt is an **independent Bernoulli trial** with success probability $P$. The number of trials $N$ until first success follows a **Geometric distribution** with parameter $P$.

**Step 1.** The probability mass function of a Geometric random variable is:
$$P(N = k) = (1 - P)^{k-1} P, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

**Step 2.** The expected value of a Geometric distribution is:
$$\mathbb{E}[N] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1-P)^{k-1} P$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate the infinite sum. Using the standard identity $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k x^{k-1} = \dfrac{1}{(1-x)^2}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$, with $x = 1 - P$:
$$\mathbb{E}[N] = P \cdot \frac{1}{\bigl(1 - (1-P)\bigr)^{2}} = \frac{P}{P^{2}}$$

**Step 4.** Simplify:
$$\mathbb{E}[N] = \frac{1}{P}$$

**Step 5.** Substitute $P = \dfrac{T}{2^{256}}$ (probability that a uniformly random 256-bit hash falls below target $T$):
$$\mathbb{E}[N] = \frac{2^{256}}{T}$$

**Step 6.** If the target is chosen so that the hash must begin with $d$ leading zero bits, then $T = 2^{256-d}$, giving:
$$\mathbb{E}[N] = \frac{2^{256}}{2^{256-d}} = 2^{d}$$

**Conclusion:** Finding a block requiring $d$ leading zero bits demands an **expected $2^{d}$ hash evaluations**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bitcoin Difficulty Adjustment (2016-Block Retarget)

Bitcoin's difficulty is retargeted every **2016 blocks** (≈ 2 weeks at 10-min intervals) to keep the average block interval near 600 seconds.

**Step 1.** Let $t_{\text{actual}}$ = actual wall-clock time taken for the last 2016 blocks.
**Step 2.** Let $t_{\text{expected}} = 2016 \times 10 \text{ min} = 20160 \text{ min} = 1{,}209{,}600 \text{ s}$.
**Step 3.** The new target $T_{n+1}$ is:
$$T_{n+1} = T_{n} \times \frac{t_{\text{actual}}}{t_{\text{expected}}}$$

**Step 4.** Apply the clamping rule (Bitcoin Core consensus rule) to prevent extreme swings:
$$T_{n+1} = \text{clamp}\bigl(T_n \cdot \rho, \; T_{\min}, \; T_{\max}\bigr)$$

where $\rho = t_{\text{actual}} / t_{\text{expected}}$ and the bounds are $T_{\min} = T_n / 4$ and $T_{\max} = T_n \times 4$.

**Numerical example (illustrative):**

Suppose miners collectively found 2016 blocks in exactly 8 days (i.e., 4× faster than expected).
$$T_{n+1} = T_n \times \frac{8 \text{ days}}{14 \text{ days}} = T_n \times 0.5714$$

The new target is **smaller** (harder) by ~43%, restoring the 10-minute cadence.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Network Energy Estimation

The total electrical energy consumed by the Bitcoin network per year is:

$$E_{\text{year}} = R_{\text{net}} \times e_{\text{ASIC}} \times T_{\text{year}}$$

**Step 1.** Let $R_{\text{net}} = 6.5 \times 10^{20}$ H/s (current network rate, ≈ 650 EH/s).
**Step 2.** Let $e_{\text{ASIC}} = 30 \text{ J/TH} = 30 \times 10^{-12}$ J/H.
**Step 3.** Let $T_{\text{year}} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 \approx 3.156 \times 10^{7}$ s.
**Step 4.** Compute:
$$E_{\text{year}} = (6.5 \times 10^{20}) \times (30 \times 10^{-12}) \times (3.156 \times 10^{7})$$

**Step 5.** Multiply the coefficients and exponents:
$$E_{\text{year}} = 6.5 \times 30 \times 3.156 \times 10^{20-12+7}$$
$$E_{\text{year}} = 615.34 \times 10^{15} \text{ J}$$
$$E_{\text{year}} \approx 6.15 \times 10^{17} \text{ J/year} \approx \mathbf{172\ TWh/year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This places Bitcoin's annual electricity consumption on the same order of magnitude as a mid-sized European country (e.g., Poland ≈ 158 TWh). This figure is a high-yield data point for KTU essay-type questions on the environmental critique of PoW.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — 51% Attack Cost Threshold

For an attacker controlling a fraction $q$ of the total network hash rate, the probability of out-pacing the honest chain over $z$ confirmations is given by the **Gambler's Ruin formula**:

$$P_{\text{attack}}(q, z) = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{z} \binom{z+k-1}{k} (1-q)^{z} q^{k}$$

For $q = 0.10$ (attacker holds 10%) and $z = 6$ confirmations:
$$P_{\text{attack}} \approx 0.000084 \approx 0.0084\%$$

For $q = 0.45$ and $z = 6$:
$$P_{\text{attack}} \approx 0.1554 \approx 15.54\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board-exam heuristic:** The "$z = 6$" rule of thumb gives an attacker with $q < 0.10$ less than 0.1% chance of success — exponentially decaying with $z$, which is why exchanges wait for 6 confirmations before crediting large deposits.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hashcash PoW Mining — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Mining Node Starts]) --> B1[Assemble Block Header: version, prev-hash, merkle-root, timestamp, bits, nonce-init equals 0]
    B1 --> C1[Construct Coinbase Transaction with extra-nonce]
    C1 --> D1[Update Merkle Root via Coinbase Change]
    D1 --> E1[Build Full 80-byte Header]
    E1 --> F1[Compute H equals SHA-256 of SHA-256 of Header]
    F1 --> G1{Is H less than Target T}
    G1 -- No --> H1[Increment Nonce by 1]
    H1 --> E1
    G1 -- Yes --> I1[Broadcast Winning Block to Peers]
    I1 --> J1[Other Nodes Verify H less than T in 1 Hash]
    J1 --> K1[Block Accepted and Added to Chain]
    K1 --> L1[Block Reward plus Fees Credited to Miner]
    L1 --> M0([Mining Cycle Restarts on New Block])
    I1 -.Failed verification.-> N1[Block Rejected and Orphaned]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The inner loop (nodes `E1 → F1 → G1 → H1`) is the **brute-force search loop** and accounts for ~99.99% of the miner's compute time. The successful path (`G1 -- Yes → I1`) is the rare event (one in $2^{d}$ trials). The verifier's path (`J1`) is the *single-hash* check that demonstrates PoW's **asymmetric verification property**.

## 4.2 Hashcash PoW Lifecycle — Time-Domain Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Miner
    participant P as Mining Pool Stratum Server
    participant FN as Full Nodes Network
    participant CH as Blockchain Ledger

    M->>M: Construct candidate block from mempool
    M->>P: Subscribe to mining job, get header template
    loop Brute-force nonce search
        M->>M: H = SHA256 SHA256 of Header plus Nonce
        M->>M: Check H less than Target
    end
    M->>P: Submit winning nonce share
    P->>FN: Broadcast full block
    FN->>FN: Independent verification, H less than T
    FN->>CH: Append block at height N plus 1
    CH-->>M: Credit block reward plus fees
    FN-->>P: Update job template with new tip
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The sequence diagram makes the *distributed verification* property visually obvious — note that `FN` independently re-runs the *same* `H < T` check, ensuring decentralized consensus without trusting the miner. This is the single most important conceptual point examiners look for.

## 4.3 Difficulty-Adjustment Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Total Network Hash Rate R-net] --> B2[Measured Block Time T-actual]
    B2 --> C2[Compare to Target T-expected equals 600 s]
    C2 --> D2{Is T-actual within 4x to 0.25x of T-expected}
    D2 -- No --> E2[Hold Difficulty Constant]
    D2 -- Yes --> F2[Recompute Target T-new equals T-old times T-actual over T-expected]
    F2 --> G2[Clamp to 0.25x to 4x Bounds]
    G2 --> H2[Apply at Block Height divisible by 2016]
    H2 --> A2
```

> [!TIP]
> The feedback loop is a **negative-feedback control system** — a classic engineering concept that KTU examiners love to see blockchain mechanisms framed in. Always describe PoW difficulty as "**a self-regulating negative-feedback controller**" in essay answers for maximum marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Proof of Work (PoW). Mention its key property. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Proof of Work (PoW)** is a consensus protocol in blockchain systems that requires miners to solve a computationally hard cryptographic puzzle — typically by brute-forcing a nonce such that the double-SHA-256 hash of the block header is numerically less than a target value $T$.

**Key Property (1 mark):** *Asymmetric cost structure* — finding the solution is computationally expensive (expected $2^d$ hash trials for $d$ leading-zero bits), but verifying the solution requires only a *single* hash evaluation by any node.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Students often write "PoW is a type of mining" without naming the *target* and *hash comparison*. Examiners award full 3 marks only when the *validity condition* $H < T$ is explicitly mentioned.

---

### Q2. What is Hashcash? Who proposed it and for what original purpose? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Hashcash** is a Proof-of-Work algorithm proposed by **Adam Back in 1997** (2 marks). It was originally designed as an **anti-spam mechanism for email systems** (1 mark) — each outgoing email had to include a "hash stamp" whose SHA-1 hash began with a stipulated number of leading zero bits, forcing spammers to expend CPU time per email and rendering bulk unsolicited mail economically unviable. The same design was later (2008) adopted by Satoshi Nakamoto as the consensus mechanism of Bitcoin.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of the **Hashcash Proof of Work** algorithm as used in Bitcoin mining. Include the role of the nonce, target, and the double SHA-256 hash. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **expected number of hash trials** required to mine a block with difficulty $d$ leading-zero bits, and compute the expected mining time for a network hash rate of 600 EH/s when $d = 74$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks)

The Hashcash PoW procedure in Bitcoin proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Header Construction (1 mark):**
The miner constructs a candidate block header of 80 bytes containing six fields: `version` (4 bytes), `previous block hash` (32 bytes), `Merkle root of transactions` (32 bytes), `timestamp` (4 bytes), `nBits` — the compact encoding of the target $T$ (4 bytes), and `nonce` (4 bytes).

**Step 2 — Nonce Initialization (1 mark):**
The miner sets the 32-bit nonce to $0$. If the 32-bit nonce space is exhausted, the miner modifies the *extra-nonce* field embedded in the coinbase transaction, which alters the Merkle root and effectively resets the search.

**Step 3 — Double SHA-256 Hash Computation (2 marks):**
The miner computes:
$$H = \text{SHA-256}\bigl(\text{SHA-256}(\text{header})\bigr)$$
This produces a 256-bit digest. Double hashing defends against length-extension attacks.

**Step 4 — Validity Check (1 mark):**
The 256-bit hash $H$ is interpreted as an unsigned little-endian integer and compared against the target $T$:
$$\text{Valid} \iff H < T$$

**Step 5 — Iteration (1 mark):**
If $H \geq T$, the miner increments the nonce by 1 and returns to Step 3. Modern ASICs perform ~$10^{14}$ such iterations per second per device.

**Step 6 — Broadcast and Verification (1 mark):**
Upon success, the full block (header + transactions) is broadcast. Every full node re-computes the hash and confirms $H < T$ in a single iteration, then appends the block to its local copy of the chain.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Each hash attempt is an independent Bernoulli trial with success probability:
$$P = \frac{T}{2^{256}}$$

If the target requires the hash to start with $d$ leading zero bits, then $T = 2^{256-d}$, so:
$$P = \frac{2^{256-d}}{2^{256}} = 2^{-d}$$

The number of trials $N$ until first success follows a Geometric distribution with PMF:
$$P(N = k) = (1 - 2^{-d})^{k-1} \cdot 2^{-d}, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

The expected number of trials is:
$$\mathbb{E}[N] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k (1 - 2^{-d})^{k-1} \cdot 2^{-d}$$

Using the identity $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k x^{k-1} = \dfrac{1}{(1-x)^2}$ with $x = 1 - 2^{-d}$:
$$\mathbb{E}[N] = \frac{1}{2^{-d}} = 2^{d}$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

Given $R = 600$ EH/s $= 6 \times 10^{20}$ H/s and $d = 74$:
$$\mathbb{E}[N] = 2^{74} = 1.886 \times 10^{22} \text{ hashes}$$

The expected mining time is:
$$t_{\text{block}} = \frac{\mathbb{E}[N]}{R} = \frac{1.886 \times 10^{22}}{6 \times 10^{20}} \approx 31.4 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** Many students forget to convert **EH/s** to **H/s** and submit $t \approx 31$ seconds, which looks plausible but is wrong by 18 orders of magnitude. Always write: $1 \text{ EH/s} = 10^{18} \text{ H/s}$. Loss of **1 mark** if unit conversion is missing. The discrepancy between 31 s and 600 s is intentional — it reflects a per-miner expectation; the *network* combined hash rate is what produces the 10-minute block interval in practice.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **block mining process in Bitcoin** using Hashcash PoW. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of the PoW consensus mechanism. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 marks)

**Diagram (3 marks):**
The block mining process is best depicted as the **iterative search loop** shown in Section 4.1 of these notes. A simplified textual representation:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. Fetch unconfirmed transactions (mempool)│
│  2. Construct coinbase tx (reward + fees)   │
│  3. Build Merkle tree → Merkle root         │
│  4. Assemble 80-byte block header           │
│  5. Set nonce = 0                           │
│  6. LOOP:                                   │
│       H = SHA256(SHA256(header + nonce))    │
│       if H < target → EXIT LOOP             │
│       else nonce = nonce + 1                │
│  7. Broadcast block to network              │
│  8. Full nodes verify H < target            │
│  9. Block appended to chain                 │
│ 10. Mining reward (3.125 BTC) credited      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Process Description (4 marks):**

The miner collects pending transactions from the mempool, prioritizes those with the highest fee-per-byte, and constructs a candidate block. A **coinbase transaction** is prepended, crediting the miner with the current block subsidy (post-2024 halving: **3.125 BTC**) plus the sum of all transaction fees. The transactions are arranged into a **Merkle tree**, and the 32-byte Merkle root is computed and inserted into the block header.

The miner then enters the **brute-force loop**: for each candidate nonce, the double SHA-256 of the 80-byte header is computed and compared to the target. The loop terminates when a hash below the target is found, which — by the avalanche property of SHA-256 — occurs essentially at random with probability $2^{-d}$ per trial.

The completed block is broadcast; full nodes verify the PoW (single hash check), validate every transaction (UTXO rules, signature checks, double-spend prevention), and append the block to the chain at the current tip.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 marks)

**Advantages (4 marks):**

1. **Sybil Resistance (1 mark):** PoW makes identity creation expensive — to mount a 51% attack, an adversary must acquire and operate more hashing hardware than the entire honest network, costing billions of dollars in CAPEX plus ongoing electricity.
2. **Provable Security (1 mark):** Security is rooted in *thermodynamic irreversibility* — the energy expended cannot be retroactively undone without re-doing the same work, giving PoW chains objective, physics-based finality.
3. **Decentralized Validator Selection (1 mark):** Any node can become a miner without permission; the lottery-like winner selection avoids centralized gatekeeping.
4. **Fork Resolution (1 mark):** When two miners find blocks simultaneously, the chain with the *greatest cumulative work* wins, providing a deterministic tie-breaking rule.

**Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Energy Consumption (1 mark):** Bitcoin's annual electricity use (~**170 TWh**) is comparable to mid-sized nations, raising serious environmental concerns.
2. **Throughput Bottleneck (1 mark):** PoW block intervals and size limits cap Bitcoin at ~**7 transactions per second**, far below payment-processor requirements (Visa: ~65,000 TPS).
3. **Mining Centralization (1 mark):** Economies of scale and cheap electricity have concentrated hash power in a few large mining pools (e.g., Foundry, AntPool), partially undermining the decentralization ideal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Trap:** When listing limitations, do **not** claim "PoW is *broken* by quantum computers." A 256-bit pre-image search with Grover's algorithm still requires $2^{128}$ operations — astronomically beyond near-term quantum capability. Examiners will deduct marks for unscientific hyperbole.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Cramming Sheet):**

- ✅ **PoW definition:** Consensus requiring *expensive-to-find, cheap-to-verify* solutions based on cryptographic hash inversions.
- ✅ **Hashcash inventor:** Adam Back, **1997**; original purpose: **anti-spam email**.
- ✅ **Bitcoin adoption:** 2008, by Satoshi Nakamoto; uses **double SHA-256**.
- ✅ **Validity condition:** $H < T$, where $H$ is the double-hash and $T$ is the 256-bit target.
- ✅ **Nonce:** 32-bit field, the *only* mutable variable during brute-force search.
- ✅ **Difficulty $d$** = number of required leading zero bits; expected trials $= 2^{d}$.
- ✅ **Block interval:** ~**10 minutes** for Bitcoin; retarget every **2016 blocks** (≈ 2 weeks).
- ✅ **Retarget formula:** $T_{n+1} = T_n \times \dfrac{t_{\text{actual}}}{t_{\text{expected}}}$, clamped to $[T_n/4,\ 4T_n]$.
- ✅ **Block reward (2024):** **3.125 BTC** (post-April-2024 halving); next halving in **2028**.
- ✅ **Total supply cap:** **21,000,000 BTC**.
- ✅ **Current network hash rate:** ~**650 EH/s** $= 6.5 \times 10^{20}$ H/s.
- ✅ **ASIC efficiency benchmark:** ~**30 J/TH** (Antminer S21 class).
- ✅ **Confirmations for large deposits:** **6 blocks** (≈ 1 hour).
- ✅ **51% attack cost:** Must exceed the *entire honest network's hash rate* — economically prohibitive for established chains.
- ✅ **Energy footprint:** ~**170 TWh/year** — comparable to a mid-sized country.
- ✅ **Key property to always mention:** *Asymmetry between finding (hard) and verifying (trivial)*.
- ✅ **For essay-type answers:** Frame PoW as a **negative-feedback control system** and a **Sybil-resistance mechanism** anchored in *thermodynamic irreversibility*.
- ✅ **Hashcash vs. Bitcoin PoW:** Hashcash uses SHA-1 on `(ver, date, recipient, counter)`; Bitcoin uses double SHA-256 on the 80-byte block header with the Merkle root replacing the recipient and the nonce replacing the counter.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line KTU mnemonic:**
> **"Nonce changes, hash changes, target decides, network verifies, reward follows."**
> Cover these five verbs in any 14-mark answer and you will satisfy every Course Outcome (CO) marker in the KTU 2024 rubric.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bitcoin PoW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bitcoin PoW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Bitcoin Proof of Work (PoW)** is the **Sybil-resistance consensus mechanism** employed by the Bitcoin protocol (Nakamoto, 2008) in which network participants known as *miners* must expend measurable amounts of **computational energy** to solve a **cryptographic puzzle** — specifically, finding a *nonce* such that the **double-SHA-256 hash** of the candidate block header is less than a dynamically adjusted **target value**.

The puzzle is **asymmetric**: it is hard to solve (no better strategy than brute-force search) but trivial to verify (any node can run SHA-256 once). The first miner to broadcast a valid solution receives the **block reward** (currently **3.125 BTC** post-2024 halving) plus aggregated **transaction fees**, thereby economically securing the ledger.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** PoW simultaneously solves three problems — (1) leader election in a permissionless network, (2) Sybil resistance without a central authority, and (3) objective, deterministic chain selection when forks occur (always pick the chain with the most cumulative work).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Global Lottery Machine"

Imagine a worldwide lottery where:

- 🎟️ Every miner buys an unlimited supply of tickets instantly.
- 🏆 The winning ticket number must **start with a very long string of zeros** (e.g., `0000000000000000000a3b...`).
- 🎲 Each ticket is just a different random number (the **nonce**) the miner tries.
- ⚡ You cannot *predict* a winning ticket — you must **try billions per second** using electricity-powered hardware (ASICs).
- ✅ The moment a miner finds a winning ticket, **everyone can instantly check** it with one calculator press.

The "proof" is the winning ticket itself. Because the puzzle is *hard to find but easy to verify*, no one can cheat and no one needs to trust anyone else. **Proof of Work = Proof of Spent Electricity = Economic Cost of Truth.**

---

## 1.3 Origin & Historical Context

| Year | Milestone | Contributor |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **1993** | Proof-of-work concept introduced (pricing CPU cycles) | Cynthia Dwork & Moni Naor |
| **1997** | *Hashcash* — first practical PoW for spam deterrence | Adam Back |
| **2008** | PoW adapted to decentralized digital cash | Satoshi Nakamoto (Bitcoin Whitepaper) |
| **2009** | Genesis block mined (reward **50 BTC**) | Nakamoto |
| **2024** | Current reward after 4 halvings | **3.125 BTC** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Genesis Block (Block 0) Constants:** The message *"The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks"* is embedded in `coinbase` — a political commentary that PoW-based money should be outside central-bank control.

---

## 1.4 The Three Cryptographic Pillars of Bitcoin PoW

Bitcoin's puzzle relies on the **SHA-256** hash function. Three properties make the system work:

1. **Pre-image resistance** — Given a hash output `h`, it is computationally infeasible to find an input `m` such that `H(m) = h`.
2. **Collision resistance** — It is infeasible to find two distinct inputs `m₁ ≠ m₂` such that `H(m₁) = H(m₂)`.
3. **Puzzle-friendliness** — For any target set `S` of outputs, there is no strategy better than trial-and-error to find an input `m` whose hash falls in `S`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Without *puzzle-friendliness*, a clever miner could shortcut the search. SHA-256's avalanche effect ensures that flipping **one bit** of the input completely randomizes the output, forcing brute force.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bitcoin Mining Puzzle — Formal Statement

A miner assembles a **candidate block** `B` containing:

- `version` — protocol version (4 bytes)
- `prev_block_hash` — SHA-256 hash of the previous block header (32 bytes)
- `merkle_root` — root of the Merkle tree of all transactions in the block (32 bytes)
- `timestamp` — Unix time of block creation (4 bytes)
- `n_bits` — compact encoding of the current target (4 bytes)
- `nonce` — the value the miner varies (4 bytes, but extended via *extra nonce* in coinbase)

The miner searches for a **nonce** such that:

$$H(\text{header}) = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{version} \Vert \text{prev\_hash} \Vert \text{merkle\_root} \Vert \text{time} \Vert \text{n\_bits} \Vert \text{nonce})) < T$$

where `T` is the **target threshold** — a 256-bit number encoded compactly in `n_bits`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Double SHA-256?** This was a defensive choice by Nakamoto to mitigate potential *length-extension attacks* on SHA-256 and provides an extra safety margin against future cryptanalytic breakthroughs.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Mining Logic

| Step | Action | Why It Matters |
|------|--------|----------------|
| **1** | Collect pending transactions from the **mempool**. | Miner chooses which transactions to include (sorted by fee density). |
| **2** | Construct **Merkle tree** of transactions; take root. | Compact O(log n) commitment to all transactions. |
| **3** | Assemble **block header** (80 bytes) with `prev_hash`, `merkle_root`, `timestamp`, `n_bits`, `nonce=0`. | This is the only thing being hashed during mining. |
| **4** | Compute `H = SHA256(SHA256(header))`. | The puzzle evaluation. |
| **5** | If `H < T`, broadcast the block. **PUZZLE SOLVED.** | Probability of success per attempt is `T / 2²⁵⁶`. |
| **6** | Else, increment `nonce` (or modify coinbase *extra nonce* to change `merkle_root`) and return to Step 4. | Brute-force search. |
| **7** | On success: peer nodes verify the proof in **<1 ms** and append to their copy of the ledger. | Asymmetric verification. |

---

## 2.3 Target, Difficulty, and Self-Regulation

Bitcoin enforces an average **10-minute block interval** through the **difficulty adjustment algorithm (DAA)**:

$$\text{new target} = \text{old target} \times \frac{\text{actual time of last 2016 blocks}}{\text{desired time of 2016 blocks}}$$

The **difficulty** is the human-friendly inverse of the target:

$$\text{Difficulty} = \frac{D_1}{T}$$

where `D₁ = 0x00000000FFFF0000...` is the *genesis-era target* (the easiest target ever allowed). Current difficulty ≈ **1E13 (100 trillion)**.

The re-target occurs every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks). It is bounded: if actual time < 7 days, difficulty increases by ≤ 4×; if > 14 days, it decreases by ≤ 4×. This prevents timestamp manipulation and game-theoretic exploits.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $H(B) = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(B))$ | Double SHA-256 of block header | 256-bit output |
| 2 | $H(B) < T$ | Valid PoW condition | `T` = current target |
| 3 | $\text{Difficulty} = D_1 \,/\, T$ | Human-readable difficulty | $D_1$ = pool difficulty = `0x1d00ffff` |
| 4 | $P(\text{success per hash}) = T \,/\, 2^{256}$ | Single-hash success probability | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $E[\text{hashes to success}] = 2^{256} \,/\, T = 2^{32} \cdot D$ | Expected number of hash trials | Used in mining yield calculations |
| 6 | $E[T_{\text{block}}] = D \cdot 2^{32} \,/\, h$ | Expected time to find a block | $h$ = network hashrate (H/s) |
