| 7 | $\text{Block reward} = 50 \cdot 2^{-\lfloor h/210000 \rfloor}$ | Halving schedule (every 210k blocks) | Currently **3.125 BTC** (2024+) |
| 8 | $\text{Max supply} = \sum_{i=0}^{32} 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-i} = 21{,}000{,}000$ | Total BTC ever created | Hard cap |
| 9 | $E[\text{reward/hash}] = \text{block reward} \,/\, (D \cdot 2^{32})$ | Expected BTC per hash attempt | Determines miner profitability |
| 10 | $\text{Energy per hash} \approx 1 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{kWh}$ (modern ASIC) | ~100 J/TH | Hardware metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bars in tables:** Notice the use of $2^{256}$ instead of $\vert 2^{256} \vert$ to maintain markdown table integrity. KTU examiners frequently test formula 5 and 6 — memorize the relationship $E = D \cdot 2^{32} \cdot h$ between difficulty, hashrate, and time.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Beyond cryptocurrency, PoW has practical applications:

- **Anti-spam / Anti-DoS:** Hashcash forces email senders to spend CPU per message.
- **DDoS mitigation:** TLS client puzzles use PoW to rate-limit attackers.
- **Decentralized timestamping** (e.g., *Trusted Timestamp* services).
- **Commitment schemes** for sealed-bid auctions.
- **Cybersecurity:** A class of mitigation for botnets — making malware command channels expensive to operate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PoW over PoS for Bitcoin?** PoW ties security to **physical energy expenditure** (a non-replayable, real-world resource). PoS ties it to **inside-the-system capital** which can be replayed or loaned. This is the foundational trade-off Nakamoto made.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expected Number of Hashes to Find a Valid Block

**Given:**
- Target `T` (a 256-bit integer).
- Output space of SHA-256 is uniformly distributed over $\lbrace 0, 1, \dots, 2^{256}-1\rbrace$.
- Success condition: $H < T$.

**Find:** Expected number of trials $E[N]$.

**Step 1 — Probability of a single success.**

Because SHA-256 is a uniform pseudo-random function, the probability that one random hash falls in the interval $[0, T)$ is:

$$p = \frac{T}{2^{256}}$$

**Step 2 — Model as a geometric distribution.**

The number of trials $N$ until the first success follows a **Geometric distribution** with parameter $p$. By definition of the geometric expectation:

$$E[N] = \frac{1}{p}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $p$.**

$$E[N] = \frac{1}{T/2^{256}} = \frac{2^{256}}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of difficulty.**

Using the definition $\text{Difficulty} = D = D_1 / T$, we get $T = D_1 / D$. But the *pool difficulty* $D_1 = 2^{224}$ corresponds to the easiest target. Substituting:

$$E[N] = \frac{2^{256}}{D_1 / D} = \frac{2^{256} \cdot D}{D_1} = 2^{256-224} \cdot D = 2^{32} \cdot D$$

**Final Result (boxed for exam):**

$$\boxed{E[N] = 2^{32} \cdot D}$$

This is the **fundamental mining equation** that connects the abstract target $T$ to the practical difficulty $D$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expected Time-to-Block from Network Hashrate

**Given:**
- Network hashrate $h$ in hashes per second.
- Expected hashes per block $= 2^{32} \cdot D$.

**Find:** Expected wall-clock time $E[T_{\text{block}}]$.

**Step 1.** The total number of hashes performed by the network in $t$ seconds is $h \cdot t$.

**Step 2.** Set this equal to the expected hashes needed for one block:

$$h \cdot E[T_{\text{block}}] = 2^{32} \cdot D$$

**Step 3.** Solve for $E[T_{\text{block}}]$:

$$E[T_{\text{block}}] = \frac{2^{32} \cdot D}{h}$$

**Step 4.** Bitcoin is engineered so that when $D = D_{\text{current}}$ and $h = h_{\text{current}}$:

$$E[T_{\text{block}}] = 600\ \text{seconds} = 10\ \text{minutes}$$

This is achieved by retargeting $D$ every 2016 blocks.

**Final Result (boxed for exam):**

$$\boxed{E[T_{\text{block}}] = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{h}\ \text{seconds}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Probability of Successful 51% Attack

**Given:** Attacker controls fraction $\alpha$ of total network hashrate.

**Find:** Probability the attacker catches up from a deficit of $z$ blocks.

**Step 1 — Random walk model.** The attacker's progress follows a **binomial distribution** modeled as a 1D biased random walk. The attacker's catch-up process has a *success probability per block* of:

$$q = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

because the attacker wins one block with probability $\alpha$ and the honest chain wins with $1 - \alpha$. The attacker needs a net lead of 1 to gain 1, so we model per-block transition.

**Step 2 — Gambler's ruin probability.** For an attacker starting at deficit $z$, the probability of ever catching up is given by Nakamoto's formula (Gambler's Ruin):

$$P(\text{attack succeeds}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } q \leq 1 \ (\alpha \leq 0.5) \\ (q^{-z}) & \text{if } q > 1 \ (\alpha > 0.5) \end{cases}$$

Wait — re-derive carefully. If $q > 1$ (attacker has majority), the probability of ever catching up from deficit $z$ is:

$$P_{\text{success}} = 1 - \left(\frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}\right)^{z}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical intuition.** If $\alpha = 0.10$ (attacker has 10% of hashrate):

$$P_{\text{success}}(z=6) = 1 - \left(\frac{0.9}{0.1}\right)^{6} = 1 - 9^{6} = 1 - 531{,}441 = -531{,}440 \ \ (\text{clipped to } 0)$$

This is negative — so probability is effectively **0** for a small attacker.

**Step 4 — For $\alpha = 0.45$ (just under 50%):**

$$P_{\text{success}}(z=6) = 1 - \left(\frac{0.55}{0.45}\right)^{6} \approx 1 - 5.49 = -4.49 \ \ (\text{still } \approx 0)$$

**Step 5 — For $\alpha = 0.55$ (majority attacker):**

$$P_{\text{success}}(z=6) = 1 - \left(\frac{0.45}{0.55}\right)^{6} \approx 1 - 0.249 = 0.751$$

**Final Result (boxed for exam):**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{success}} = \begin{cases} 1 - \left(\dfrac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}\right)^{z} & \alpha > 0.5 \\ 0 & \alpha \leq 0.5 \end{cases}}$$

This is the **core security argument** of Bitcoin: an attacker needs sustained majority hashrate, which costs tens of billions of USD in hardware and electricity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Block Reward Halving Schedule

**Step 1.** Initial reward $= 50$ BTC. Halving every 210,000 blocks.

$$\text{Reward at halving } k = 50 \cdot 2^{-k}$$

**Step 2.** Sum of geometric series for total supply:

$$S = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-k} = 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 1/2} = 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2 = 21{,}000{,}000$$

**Step 3.** The series converges because of integer truncation. Final halving yields 0 BTC (around year **2140**). Total supply = **21 million BTC** (exactly 20,999,999.9769 in practice).

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{S_{\text{total}} = 21{,}000{,}000\ \text{BTC}}$$

---

## 3.5 KTU 14-Mark Problem Solver: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** Suppose the network hashrate is $h = 6 \times 10^{19}$ H/s and current difficulty $D = 1.0 \times 10^{13}$. How long on average to find a block?

**Solution using our formula:**

$$E[T_{\text{block}}] = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{h} = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{13}) \cdot (4.295 \times 10^{9})}{6 \times 10^{19}}$$

$$= \frac{4.295 \times 10^{22}}{6 \times 10^{19}} = 715.8\ \text{seconds} \approx 11.93\ \text{minutes}$$

This is close to the 10-minute target, indicating difficulty is slightly too low — it will increase at the next 2016-block adjustment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Trick:** The question "expected time" almost always wants you to use $E[T] = D \cdot 2^{32} / h$, not just divide. Always show the explicit substitution with the factor of $2^{32} \approx 4.295 \times 10^{9}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bitcoin Mining Process Flowchart

This diagram shows the iterative mining loop and the role of every block-header field. It uses safe alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters (no reserved keywords).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Miner receives Mempool] --> A2[Select High-Fee Transactions]
    A2 --> A3[Build Merkle Tree and Compute Root]
    A3 --> A4[Assemble 80-Byte Block Header]
    A4 --> A5[Set Nonce to Zero]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Double SHA-256]
    A6 --> A7{Hash Less Than Target T?}
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Broadcast Valid Block]
    A7 -- No --> A9[Increment Nonce or Extra Nonce]
    A9 --> A6
    A8 --> A10[Peer Nodes Verify in under 1 ms]
    A10 --> A11[Block Appended to Chain]
    A11 --> A12[Miner Receives Block Reward and Fees]
    A12 --> A1
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The inner loop `A6 → A7 → A9` is executed ~10²² times per block at current difficulty.
- Only the **80-byte header** is hashed — the body (transactions) is committed to via `merkle_root` only.
- When `nonce` (4 bytes) is exhausted, miners modify the `coinbase` script (extra-nonce), changing the `merkle_root`, then restart from `nonce = 0`.

---

## 4.2 Block Header Structure (Byte-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Version 4 bytes] --> B2[Previous Block Hash 32 bytes]
    B2 --> B3[Merkle Root 32 bytes]
    B3 --> B4[Timestamp 4 bytes]
    B4 --> B5[Bits Target Encoding 4 bytes]
    B5 --> B6[Nonce 4 bytes]
    B6 --> B7[Total 80 bytes]
    B7 --> B8[Double SHA-256 Hash 256 bits]
```

| Field | Size | Mutated by Miner? | Purpose |
|-------|------|-------------------|---------|
| Version | 4 B | ❌ No | Soft-fork signaling (BIP-9 bits) |
| Prev Hash | 32 B | ❌ No | Chain linkage |
| Merkle Root | 32 B | ✅ Yes (via extra-nonce) | Commits to all TXs |
| Timestamp | 4 B | ✅ Yes | Median-time-past constraint |
| Bits | 4 B | ❌ No (auto) | Encodes target `T` |
| Nonce | 4 B | ✅ Yes (primary) | Direct search space |

---

## 4.3 Difficulty Adjustment Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Network Measures 2016 Block Time Window] --> C2{Window Greater than 14 days?}
    C2 -- Yes --> C3[Decrease Target T, Decrease Difficulty]
    C2 -- No --> C4{Window Less than 7 days?}
    C4 -- Yes --> C5[Increase Target T, Increase Difficulty]
    C4 -- No --> C6[Keep Difficulty Unchanged]
    C3 --> C7[Update n_bits in Next Block]
    C5 --> C7
    C6 --> C7
    C7 --> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4× Cap:** To prevent a sudden hashrate crash (e.g., China mining ban, May 2021) from making blocks too slow, Bitcoin caps the *increase* in difficulty at 4× per period. The *decrease* is also capped at 4× to prevent gaming.

---

## 4.4 Hash Puzzle Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Full 256-Bit Hash Space] --> D2[Valid Region Smaller than Target T]
    D2 --> D3[Target Has Leading Zeros in Display]
    D3 --> D4[Lower Target Smaller Valid Region Harder Puzzle]
    D4 --> D5[Higher Target Larger Valid Region Easier Puzzle]
```

**Interpretation:** A higher target means the puzzle is *easier* (larger success region). Difficulty $D$ is the *inverse* — so when difficulty rises, target shrinks, and the required leading zeros increase (e.g., 19 leading zeros ≈ current Bitcoin difficulty).

---

## 4.5 51% Attack Probability Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Attacker With Fraction alpha of Hashrate] --> E2{alpha > 0.5?}
    E2 -- No --> E3[Probability of Catch-Up equals 0]
    E2 -- Yes --> E4[Compute Gamblers Ruin Formula]
    E4 --> E5[P success = 1 minus 1 minus alpha over alpha to power z]
    E5 --> E6[Exponentially Decays with Deficit z]
    E6 --> E7[Attacker Still Pays Real World Energy Cost]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Diagram 4.5:** Even with $\alpha > 0.5$, the attacker must expend real electricity. The economic security argument is that the *cost of attack* (hardware + electricity) exceeds any plausible *reward from double-spending* once the market capitalization of Bitcoin is large enough. This is the **"thermodynamic security"** claim.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define Proof of Work. List any two hash function properties essential for Bitcoin mining.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Proof of Work (PoW)** is a consensus protocol in which a participant must demonstrate that a non-trivial amount of computational effort has been expended to produce a result that others can verify cheaply. In Bitcoin, the miner finds a **nonce** such that the double SHA-256 hash of the block header is below a target value `T`.
>
> Two essential properties:
> 1. **Pre-image resistance** — given a hash output, finding the input is infeasible.
> 2. **Puzzle-friendliness** — no shortcut exists; brute-force search over the nonce space is the optimal strategy.
>
> *(Auxiliary accepted: collision resistance.)* ✅

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**
**Why does Bitcoin use double SHA-256 (SHA-256 ∘ SHA-256) instead of a single hash?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Defense against length-extension attacks:** SHA-256, like all Merkle–Damgård constructions, is vulnerable to length extension if used naïvely in a Merkle tree. Hashing the SHA-256 output (which is 256 bits, the full block size of SHA-256) effectively closes this attack vector.
> 2. **Extra safety margin:** Should a minor cryptanalytic weakness ever be found in SHA-256, the outer hash provides an additional layer.
> 3. **Negligible performance cost:** SHA-256 is extremely fast, so doubling it imposes a marginal (~2×) overhead on the overall mining process.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Format:** KTU ESE Module Internal Choice — you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each.

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU 2024 Scheme, Model Paper Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain the Bitcoin mining process in detail. Include a description of the block header structure and the role of the nonce. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The current network hashrate is $h = 5.5 \times 10^{19}$ H/s and the difficulty is $D = 9.5 \times 10^{12}$. Calculate (i) the expected time to find a block, and (ii) the expected number of hash trials required. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

1. **Block header structure (3 marks):** The 80-byte block header contains: *version* (4 B), *previous block hash* (32 B), *merkle root* (32 B), *timestamp* (4 B), *n_bits* (4 B), *nonce* (4 B). The full body containing transactions is NOT hashed directly; instead, transactions are committed via the Merkle root.
2. **Mining process (3 marks):** The miner assembles the candidate block, then iteratively varies the nonce and recomputes the double SHA-256 of the 80-byte header. When the result falls below target $T$, the block is valid and broadcast. Peer nodes verify by performing a single hash and checking the inequality.
3. **Role of nonce and extra-nonce (1 mark):** The 4-byte nonce provides $2^{32} \approx 4.3 \times 10^9$ direct trials. Once exhausted, miners modify the coinbase transaction's arbitrary data (the *extra-nonce*), which changes the merkle root, effectively giving a fresh $2^{32}$ search space.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Expected time to find a block:**

$$E[T_{\text{block}}] = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{h}$$

$$= \frac{9.5 \times 10^{12} \times 4.294967296 \times 10^{9}}{5.5 \times 10^{19}}$$

$$= \frac{4.0802 \times 10^{22}}{5.5 \times 10^{19}} \approx 741.86\ \text{seconds} \approx 12.36\ \text{minutes}$$

**(2 marks)** for substitution; **(1 mark)** for numerical answer with units.

**(ii) Expected number of hash trials:**

$$E[N] = D \cdot 2^{32} = 9.5 \times 10^{12} \times 4.294967296 \times 10^{9}$$

$$\approx 4.08 \times 10^{22}\ \text{hash trials}$$

**(2 marks)** for formula; **(2 marks)** for evaluation.

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]**

> **(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the difficulty adjustment algorithm in Bitcoin. Why is it necessary, and what are the bounds? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An attacker controls $\alpha = 0.40$ of the network hashrate. They attempt a double-spend attack against a transaction with $z = 6$ block confirmations. Compute the probability of attack success using Nakamoto's Gambler's Ruin formula. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

1. **Definition and necessity (2 marks):** The DAA recalibrates the target `T` every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks) so that average block time remains **10 minutes**, regardless of total network hashrate fluctuations. Without it, hashrate growth would make blocks too fast, breaking issuance schedule and confirmation latency.
2. **Algorithm (3 marks):**
$$\text{new target} = \text{old target} \times \frac{\text{actual time of last 2016 blocks}}{2016 \times 10 \text{ minutes}}$$
3. **Bounds (1 mark):** If actual time $< 7$ days → difficulty can increase at most **4×**; if $> 14$ days → difficulty can decrease at most **4×**. This prevents timestamp manipulation.
4. **Diagram (1 mark):** Use the feedback loop from Section 4.3.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

Since $\alpha = 0.40 < 0.5$, the attacker is in the minority.

By the Gambler's Ruin formula:

$$P_{\text{success}} = 1 - \left(\frac{1 - \alpha}{\alpha}\right)^{z} = 1 - \left(\frac{0.6}{0.4}\right)^{6} = 1 - 1.5^{6}$$

$$1.5^{6} = 1.5^2 \times 1.5^2 \times 1.5^2 = 2.25 \times 2.25 \times 2.25 = 11.39$$

$$P_{\text{success}} = 1 - 11.39 = -10.39 \rightarrow \text{clipped to } 0$$

**(4 marks)** for formula and substitution; **(1 mark)** for numerical evaluation; **(2 marks)** for the comment below.

**Comment:** A 40% minority attacker has effectively **0% probability** of catching up a 6-block deficit. Even increasing confirmations to 20 or 100 does not help the attacker. This is the core security guarantee: with $\alpha < 0.5$, the honest chain grows the lead stochastically. ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $2^{32}$ factor:** Many students write $E[T] = D / h$. This is WRONG. Always include $2^{32} \approx 4.295 \times 10^9$ — that's what makes the result come out to 10 minutes instead of microseconds. **(−2 marks typical)**
> 2. **Confusing target and difficulty:** Higher target = *easier* puzzle. Higher difficulty = *harder* puzzle. Mixing these up in the DAA question is a classic error. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Gambler's Ruin sign error:** The formula is $1 - ((1-\alpha)/\alpha)^z$, NOT $1 + ...$ or $1 - ((\alpha)/(1-\alpha))^z$. Show the bracket ratio clearly. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Missing the 4× cap explanation:** DAA questions often require stating both the *lower bound* (7 days) and the *upper bound* (14 days) — students often mention only one. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **No units in numerical answer:** Always write *seconds* / *minutes* / *hashes* explicitly. KTU examiners deduct for ambiguous units. **(−0.5 to 1 mark)**

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **High-density, exam-focused summary — review before entering the hall:**

- [x] **PoW Definition:** Miners find a **nonce** such that $\text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{header})) < T$.
- [x] **Block header = 80 bytes** = version ‖ prev_hash ‖ merkle_root ‖ time ‖ bits ‖ nonce.
- [x] **Double SHA-256** is used (defense vs. length-extension attack).
- [x] **Three hash properties:** pre-image resistance, collision resistance, puzzle-friendliness.
- [x] **Target $T$** ∈ $[1, 2^{256} - 1]$ — small $T$ = hard puzzle.
- [x] **Difficulty $D = D_1 / T$** — large $D$ = hard puzzle (inverse of target).
- [x] **Key formula 1:** Expected hashes per block $= 2^{32} \cdot D$.
- [x] **Key formula 2:** Expected block time $= D \cdot 2^{32} / h$ (seconds).
- [x] **DAA:** Re-targets every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks); cap **4× per adjustment**.
- [x] **DAA formula:** $\text{new target} = \text{old target} \times \frac{\text{actual time}}{20160\ \text{min}}$.
- [x] **51% Attack probability:** $P = 1 - ((1-\alpha)/\alpha)^z$ for $\alpha > 0.5$; else $0$.
- [x] **Asymmetric puzzle:** Hard to find, trivial to verify (<1 ms).
- [x] **Block reward halving:** Every 210,000 blocks; starts at 50 BTC, currently **3.125 BTC** (2024 halving).
- [x] **Max supply:** **21,000,000 BTC** (convergent geometric series).
- [x] **Genesis block embedded message:** *"The Times 03/Jan/2009..."* — political commentary.
- [x] **Origin:** Hashcash (Adam Back, 1997) → Bitcoin (Nakamoto, 2008).
- [x] **Current difficulty ≈ 1 × 10¹³**; current network hashrate ≈ 6 × 10¹⁹ H/s (check live).
- [x] **Security argument:** Attacker must pay real-world energy; cost-of-attack > cost-of-honest-mining at equilibrium.
- [x] **No central authority** — Sybil resistance achieved by economic cost, not identity verification.

> 🎯 **Top 3 Memory Anchors for Last-Minute Recall:**
> 1. **Puzzle:** $\text{hash} < T$ — period.
> 2. **Time:** $D \cdot 2^{32} / h$ — that's the magic number.
> 3. **Attack:** $1 - ((1-\alpha)/\alpha)^z$ — only works for $\alpha > 0.5$.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Proof of Work (PoW) — Refresher

**Proof of Work (PoW)** is a Sybil-resistant consensus mechanism introduced by Cynthia Dwork and Moni Naor (1993) and adapted for digital cash by Satoshi Nakamoto (2008). Miners compete to find a *nonce* $\ N $ such that the SHA-256 (or equivalent) hash of the block header is less than a network-defined target $\ T $.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *PoW is a cryptographic consensus protocol in which participants (miners) must expend verifiable computational effort to propose a valid block. The block is accepted by the network if and only if its hash satisfies the difficulty condition:*
>
> $$\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header} \,\|\, \text{Nonce}) \, < \, T$$

The difficulty $\ D $ is dynamically adjusted so that the expected time between blocks remains near **10 minutes** for Bitcoin.

## 1.2 What are "Attacks on PoW"?

**Attacks on PoW** refer to adversarial strategies that exploit the probabilistic nature of hash-based consensus, the economic incentives, or the peer-to-peer network layer to subvert the integrity guarantees of the blockchain (immutability, double-spend resistance, fair reward distribution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are PoW systems attacked?**
> Because PoW replaces a trusted central authority with a *computational majority*. If an adversary amasses that majority — even temporarily — the "trust" is broken.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lottery Tower" Model

Imagine a giant skyscraper filled with **trillion** lockers. Every miner buys an infinite number of keys. To win the block reward, your key must open *one specific locked locker* chosen uniformly at random.

- **Honest mining** = Each miner tries keys independently, hoping to be the first. Whoever finds the key gets the prize.
- **51% attack** = One person secretly owns **more than half the keys in the world**. They will *almost always* open the locker first and can rewrite history.
- **Selfish mining** = A miner finds the key, but **hides the locker** from others. They keep working on the *next* locker secretly. When the honest miners finally find theirs, the selfish miner reveals a longer hidden chain — invalidating honest work.
- **Double-spend / Race attack** = A customer pays for a coffee *and simultaneously* tries to pay themselves the same money on a parallel chain. If their parallel chain overtakes the coffee chain, the coffee payment vanishes.

## 1.4 Comprehensive Taxonomy of PoW Attacks

| # | Attack Name | Layer Targeted | Primary Goal |
|---|-------------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | **51% / Majority Hash Attack** | Consensus | Double-spend, censor transactions |
| 2 | **Selfish Mining** | Consensus / Incentives | Disproportionate block rewards |
| 3 | **Finney Attack** | Transaction Acceptance | Zero-confirmation double-spend |
| 4 | **Race Attack** | Mempool / P2P | Zero-confirmation double-spend |
| 5 | **Vector76 Attack** | Mempool / P2P | Double-spend on weakly confirmed TXs |
| 6 | **Eclipse Attack** | P2P Network | Isolate a victim node |
| 7 | **Long-Range Attack** | Consensus (weak subjectivity) | Rewrite old history |
| 8 | **DDoS on Mining Pools** | Network / Pools | Cripple honest hash rate |
| 9 | **Block Withholding (BWH)** | Mining Pools | Sabotage pool revenue |
| 10 | **Timejacking Attack** | Network time | Slow down victim mining |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:**
> The three **most frequently tested** attacks in KTU ESE are: (a) **51% Attack**, (b) **Selfish Mining**, and (c) **Double-Spend Family (Finney / Race / Vector76)**. Master their probability math.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 51% (Majority Hash) Attack

### Concept
If a single entity controls more than **50%** of the network's total hash rate, they can consistently outpace the honest chain. They can:
1. **Double-spend** their own coins by sending TX A to a merchant, mining a parallel private chain where TX A is replaced by TX B (sending coins back to themselves), then publishing the longer chain.
2. **Censor** specific transactions or addresses.
3. **Reverse** recently confirmed transactions.

### Why it Works — Step-by-Step Logic

1. Honest network hashrate = $\ H_h $, attacker hashrate = $\ H_a $.
2. Total hashrate $\ H_{tot} = H_h + H_a $.
3. Attacker fraction $\ \alpha = \dfrac{H_a}{H_{tot}} $, honest fraction $\ \beta = 1 - \alpha $.
4. Both miners discover blocks via a **Poisson process** with mean rates $\ \lambda_a = \alpha \lambda $ and $\ \lambda_h = \beta \lambda $.
5. If the attacker privately mines a chain of length $\ z $ before the honest chain reaches length $\ z $, the attacker publishes and overwrites the honest chain.

### Probability the Attacker Catches Up (z-block lead)

The probability that an attacker starting with $\ z $ confirmed blocks can catch up to the honest chain is modelled as a **gambler's ruin** problem (Nakamoto, 2008):

$$P_{\text{catchup}} \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \alpha \leq \beta \\[8pt] \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{z} \binom{z+k-1}{k}\,(p)^{z}\,(1-p)^{k} & \text{if } \alpha > \beta \end{cases}$$

where $\ p = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta} $ and the attacker wins if their Poisson process overtakes the honest one within the gap.

For large $\ z $, the closed-form approximation is:

$$P_{\text{catchup}} \;\approx\; 1 \;-\; \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^{\!z}$$

where $\ q = 1 - p $. When $\ \alpha \le 0.5 $ (i.e., $\ q \ge p $), the probability **exponentially decays to zero** with $\ z $.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the *theoretical foundation* behind the "6-confirmation rule" for Bitcoin. Each confirmation exponentially reduces the attacker's probability of rewriting history.

### Cost Barrier

The economic cost of mounting a sustained 51% attack on Bitcoin is estimated in **billions of USD** (electricity + ASIC hardware), making it economically irrational for profit-seeking attackers (assuming coin value collapses after attack disclosure — a key game-theoretic deterrent).

---

## 2.2 Selfish Mining (Eyal & Sirer, 2013)

### Concept
A mining pool with $\ \alpha < 0.5 $ of the hash rate can still earn **disproportionate** rewards ($\ > \alpha $) by strategically *withholding* discovered blocks and only publishing them to invalidate honest miners' work.

### Operational Steps

1. **Step 1:** Honest miners find a block → broadcast it.
2. **Step 2:** Selfish miner finds a block → *keeps it secret* (state = lead 1).
3. **Step 3:** If selfish miner finds another block (state = lead 2), they continue; honest work on lead-1 chain is wasted.
4. **Step 4:** When honest miners close the gap to lead 1, selfish miner reveals their lead-2 chain. Honest network switches (longest-chain rule).
5. **Step 5:** If honest miners find a competing block during the selfish miner's lead-1 state, *both chains* propagate and there is a 50/50 tie, broken by network propagation advantage.

### Eyal–Sirer Revenue Formula

With $\ \alpha $ = selfish pool's hash rate fraction and $\ \gamma $ = ratio of honest miners that connect to the selfish pool (propagation advantage), the **pool's revenue ratio** is:

$$R_{\text{pool}} \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha\,(1-\alpha)^{2}\,(4\alpha + \gamma(1-2\alpha)) \;-\; \alpha^{3}}{1 \;-\; \alpha\,(1 + (2-\alpha)\alpha)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Condition for Profitability of Selfish Mining:**
> A pool becomes profitable to attack when its revenue $\ R_{\text{pool}} > \alpha $. Solving the above yields the well-known Eyal–Sirer threshold:
>
> $$\alpha \;>\; \dfrac{1 - \gamma}{3 - 2\gamma}$$
>
> For $\ \gamma = 0 $ (no propagation advantage), the threshold is $\ \alpha > \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 33.3\% $ — a devastating result because it is **far below 50%**.

---

## 2.3 The Double-Spend Attack Family

### 2.3.1 Finney Attack
- **Pre-condition:** Attacker is a miner who has mined a block containing TX A (sending coins to themselves) but has *not yet broadcast it*.
- **Step 1:** Attacker purchases goods from a merchant accepting 0-confirmation TXs.
- **Step 2:** Merchant ships goods immediately.
- **Step 3:** Attacker broadcasts the previously mined block, invalidating the payment to the merchant.
- **Risk:** A competing honest miner could mine the payment block first, invalidating the attacker's pre-mined block.

### 2.3.2 Race Attack
- **Pre-condition:** Merchant accepts 0-confirmation payments.
- **Step 1:** Attacker sends TX A to merchant *and* TX B (conflict, sending same coins to self) to different parts of the network.
- **Step 2:** If merchant sees TX A first, ships goods. Attacker's TX B gets confirmed in a later block, and TX A is invalidated as a double-spend.
- **Mitigation:** Listen for *first-seen* policy + use of replace-by-fee (RBF) flags.

### 2.3.3 Vector76 Attack
- A hybrid combining elements of Finney + Race.
- Attacker mines a block containing TX B (self-payment) with high difficulty but only propagates a *partially valid* version, then races against the merchant.
- Effective against merchants accepting **1–2 confirmations**.

---

## 2.4 Eclipse Attack

The attacker monopolizes *all* peer connections of a victim node by controlling the victim's IP address space (often via botnets or BGP hijacking). Once eclipsed, the victim sees an **alternate reality** of the blockchain.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Eclipse attack is a *network-layer* attack, not a consensus attack. It is often used as a *prelude* to 51% or selfish mining because it isolates a target's hash power.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Domain |
|---|---------|---------|--------|
| 1 | $\ P_{\text{catchup}} \approx 1 - (q/p)^{z} $ | Attacker catch-up probability after $\ z $ confirmations | $\ 0 \le z \in \mathbb{Z}^{+} $ |
| 2 | $\ \alpha = H_a / H_{tot} $ | Attacker's hash power fraction | $\ 0 \le \alpha \le 1 $ |
| 3 | $\ \lambda = 1/T_{\text{block}} $ | Block discovery rate (Poisson) | Bitcoin: $\ T = 600\ \text{s} $ |
| 4 | $\ R_{\text{pool}} = \frac{\alpha(1-\alpha)^{2}(4\alpha + \gamma(1-2\alpha)) - \alpha^{3}}{1 - \alpha(1 + (2-\alpha)\alpha)} $ | Selfish mining revenue ratio | $\ 0 < \alpha < 0.5 $ |
| 5 | $\ \alpha > (1-\gamma)/(3-2\gamma) $ | Selfish mining profitability threshold | $\ 0 \le \gamma \le 1 $ |
| 6 | $\ P(\text{find next}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot t} $ | Probability of finding a block in time $\ t $ | Exponential waiting time |
| 7 | $\ \text{Cost}_{\text{attack}} = P_{\text{hash}} \times P_{\text{electricity}} \times t $ | Total economic cost of 51% attack | Engineering economics |
| 8 | $\ \text{Security} \propto e^{-c \cdot z} $ | Confirmation security grows exponentially | Probabilistic finality |

> [!NOTE]
> All vertical bars in this table have been replaced with `\vert`/`\mid` macros to prevent Markdown parser breakage. Use $\ \mid x \mid $ for absolute value in your answer sheets.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Cryptocurrency Security Audits:** Quantifying the cost of attacking a PoW chain (e.g., for altcoin feasibility studies).
- **Insurance & Risk Modelling:** Exchanges like Bitfinex use these formulas to set confirmation requirements (typically **6 for BTC, 12 for ETH**, 100+ for smaller coins).
- **Network Protocol Design:** Designing anti-eclipse peer-selection algorithms (e.g., Bitcoin Core's `addrdb`).
- **Game-Theoretic Mechanism Design:** Designing *penalty mechanisms* (used in PoS as a deterrent) that originated in selfish mining analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nakamoto's 51% Catch-up Probability

### Setup
- Attacker hash fraction $\ \alpha $, honest fraction $\ \beta = 1 - \alpha $.
- Attacker is $\ z $ blocks **behind** the honest chain and must catch up.
- Let $\ q_{z} $ = probability the attacker **ever** catches up given a $\ z $-block deficit.

### Recursive Relation (Gambler's Ruin)
The attacker's state transitions when either side finds a block:

$$q_{z} \;=\; \alpha \cdot q_{z+1} \;+\; \beta \cdot q_{z-1}$$

Boundary conditions:
- $\ q_{0} = 1 $ (already caught up).
- $\ \lim_{z \to \infty} q_{z} = 0 $ (infinite deficit impossible to overcome when $\ \alpha < 0.5 $).

### Solving the Recurrence
Assume solution of the form $\ q_{z} = r^{z} $:

$$r^{z} \;=\; \alpha \cdot r^{z+1} \;+\; \beta \cdot r^{z-1}$$

Dividing by $\ r^{z-1} $:

$$1 \;=\; \alpha \cdot r^{2} \;+\; \beta$$

$$\alpha \cdot r^{2} \;-\; 1 \;+\; \beta \;=\; 0$$

$$r^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1 - \beta}{\alpha} \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}\right)$$

$$r \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}}$$

General solution: $\ q_{z} = A \cdot r^{z} + B $. Apply $\ \lim_{z \to \infty} q_z = 0 $ (since $\ r < 1 $ when $\ \alpha < \beta $) $\ \Rightarrow \ A = 0 $. Apply $\ q_0 = 1 \Rightarrow B = 1 $.

But for $\ \alpha > \beta $, the attacker has positive drift; the bounded solution requires truncation. Nakamoto derives the **finite-sum** form:

$$P_{\text{catchup}} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{z} \binom{z + k - 1}{k}\,\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}\right)^{\!z}\,\left(1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}\right)^{\!k}$$

For practical purposes (small $\ z $, large $\ \beta $), the asymptotic is:

$$P_{\text{catchup}} \;\approx\; \left(\dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}\right)^{\!z} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^{\!z}$$

where $\ p = \alpha / \beta $ and $\ q = 1 - p $.

### Numerical Verification

| Confirmations $\ z $ | $\ \alpha = 0.1 $ | $\ \alpha = 0.3 $ | $\ \alpha = 0.45 $ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $\ 1.11 \times 10^{-1} $ | $\ 4.29 \times 10^{-1} $ | $\ 8.18 \times 10^{-1} $ |
| 3 | $\ 1.37 \times 10^{-3} $ | $\ 7.89 \times 10^{-2} $ | $\ 5.47 \times 10^{-1} $ |
| 6 | $\ 1.88 \times 10^{-5} $ | $\ 6.23 \times 10^{-3} $ | $\ 3.00 \times 10^{-1} $ |
| 10 | $\ 2.66 \times 10^{-8} $ | $\ 1.32 \times 10^{-4} $ | $\ 1.25 \times 10^{-1} $ |

> [!NOTE]
> This table is **interpolated** from the asymptotic decay and confirms why Bitcoin's "6-confirmation rule" yields a $\ < 0.001\% $ attack probability at $\ \alpha = 0.1 $.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Selfish Mining Revenue (Eyal–Sirer)

### Markov Chain States
Define the state as the **lead** of the selfish miner's secret chain:
- State 0: No lead.
- State 1: Selfish miner ahead by 1 block.
- State 2: Selfish miner ahead by 2 blocks.
- State $k$: Selfish miner ahead by $k$ blocks.
- State 0′: Tie after honest miner found a competing block.

### Transition Probabilities
- $\ P(\text{Selfish finds block} \mid \text{state 0}) = \alpha $.
- $\ P(\text{Honest finds block} \mid \text{state 0}) = 1 - \alpha $.
- $\ P(\text{Selfish finds 2 in a row from state 0}) = \alpha^{2} $.
- $\ P(\text{Honest finds 1 during state 1}) = (1 - \alpha)\,(1 - \gamma) + \alpha\,(1 - \gamma)\cdot 0 + \ldots $ (see Eyal–Sirer for full derivation).

### Revenue Computation
The expected revenue per cycle is the sum of revenues from each terminal state weighted by their stationary probabilities. The closed-form result (Eyal & Sirer, *Majority is not Enough: Bitcoin Mining is Vulnerable*, 2013) is:

$$R_{\text{pool}} \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha\,(1-\alpha)^{2}\,(4\alpha + \gamma(1-2\alpha)) - \alpha^{3}}{1 - \alpha\,(1 + (2-\alpha)\alpha)}$$

### Threshold Derivation
Set $\ R_{\text{pool}} = \alpha $ and solve for $\ \alpha $ in terms of $\ \gamma $:

$$\alpha\,(1-\alpha)^{2}\,(4\alpha + \gamma(1-2\alpha)) - \alpha^{3} \;=\; \alpha\,\bigl[1 - \alpha\,(1 + (2-\alpha)\alpha)\bigr]$$

Divide both sides by $\ \alpha $ (assuming $\ \alpha \ne 0 $):

$$(1-\alpha)^{2}\,(4\alpha + \gamma(1-2\alpha)) - \alpha^{2} \;=\; 1 - \alpha\,(1 + (2-\alpha)\alpha)$$

Expand $\ (1-\alpha)^{2} = 1 - 2\alpha + \alpha^{2} $:

$$(1 - 2\alpha + \alpha^{2})\,(4\alpha + \gamma - 2\gamma\alpha) \;=\; 1 - \alpha - 2\alpha^{2} + \alpha^{3} + \alpha^{2}$$

Expand LHS:

$$4\alpha + \gamma - 2\gamma\alpha - 8\alpha^{2} - 2\gamma\alpha + 4\gamma\alpha^{2} + 4\alpha^{3} + \gamma\alpha^{2} - 2\gamma\alpha^{3} \;=\; 1 - \alpha - \alpha^{2} + \alpha^{3}$$

Collect terms in $\ \alpha $: After algebraic simplification (dropping higher-order terms for clarity), the dominant linear term gives:

$$4\alpha + \gamma(1 - 4\alpha) \;\approx\; 1 - \alpha$$

$$\gamma - 4\gamma\alpha \;=\; 1 - 5\alpha$$

$$\alpha\,(5 - 4\gamma) \;=\; 1 - \gamma$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{1 - \gamma}{5 - 4\gamma}}$$

For $\ \gamma = 0 $: $\ \alpha = 1/5 = 0.2 $. **However**, the Eyal–Sirer paper (with full nonlinear expansion) yields the **canonical threshold**:

$$\alpha_{\text{crit}} \;=\; \dfrac{1 - \gamma}{3 - 2\gamma}$$

This $\ 1/3 $ bound for $\ \gamma = 0 $ is the celebrated result.

> [!WARNING]
> Students often confuse the two simplified forms. The **correct** canonical Eyal–Sirer threshold is $\ \alpha > (1-\gamma)/(3-2\gamma) $, **not** the linearized version. Examiners frequently award full marks only for the correct canonical form.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Attack Comparison

| Parameter | 51% Attack | Selfish Mining | Finney | Race | Vector76 |
|-----------|:----------:|:--------------:|:------:|:----:|:--------:|
| Required Hash Power | $\ > 50\% $ | $\ > 33.3\% $ | 1 block | None | 1 block |
| Confirmations Targeted | All | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1–2 |
| Double-Spend Capable | ✓ | ✗ (revenue only) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Persistent Disruption | ✓ | ✗ (cyclic) | ✗ (one-shot) | ✗ | ✗ |
| Difficulty to Execute | Extreme | Moderate | Easy | Easy | Moderate |
| Network Layer Needed | No | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Bitcoin Network Vulnerable Today | Theoretically (~$20B cost) | Theoretically | Yes (merchants using 0-conf) | Yes | Limited |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The 51% Attack Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker sends TX_A to Merchant] --> B[Merchant ships goods at 0-conf]
    B --> C[Attacker mines private chain with TX_B replacing TX_A]
    C --> D{Private chain length z >= Honest chain length z}
    D -- No --> E[Continue private mining]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Publish private chain to network]
    F --> G[Honest nodes switch to longer chain]
    G --> H[TX_A invalidated, attacker recovers funds]
    H --> I[Double-spend SUCCESSFUL]
```

## 4.2 Selfish Mining State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State0
    State0 --> State1 : Selfish finds block alpha
    State0 --> HonestWin : Honest finds block 1 minus alpha
    State1 --> State2 : Selfish finds next block alpha
    State1 --> Tie : Honest finds block 1 minus alpha
    State2 --> State3 : Selfish finds block alpha
    State2 --> Publish : Honest catches up 1 minus alpha
    State3 --> State4 : Selfish finds block alpha
    State3 --> Publish : Honest catches up 1 minus alpha
    Tie --> SelfishWins : Other honest miners choose selfish chain gamma
    Tie --> HonestWins : Other honest miners choose honest chain 1 minus gamma
    Publish --> [*]
    SelfishWins --> [*]
    HonestWins --> [*]
    HonestWin --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Double-Spend Attack Family Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Finney_Attack
        F1[Miner pre-mines block with TX_B] --> F2[Send TX_A to merchant]
        F2 --> F3[Merchant accepts 0-conf]
        F3 --> F4[Attacker publishes pre-mined block]
        F4 --> F5[Double-spend complete]
    end
    subgraph Race_Attack
        R1[Broadcast TX_A to merchant] --> R2[Broadcast TX_B to network]
        R2 --> R3[If merchant sees TX_A first]
        R3 --> R4[Merchant ships goods]
        R4 --> R5[TX_B confirms, TX_A invalid]
    end
    subgraph Vector76_Attack
        V1[Attacker mines block with TX_B] --> V2[Send TX_A to merchant with high fee]
        V2 --> V3[Merchant waits 1-2 confs]
        V3 --> V4[Attacker reveals pre-mined block]
        V4 --> V5[Chain reorganization double-spend]
    end
```

## 4.4 Eclipse Attack Network Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Honest_Network
        H1[Honest Node A]
        H2[Honest Node B]
        H3[Honest Node C]
    end

    end

        E1[Evil Peer 1]
        E2[Evil Peer 2]
        E3[Evil Peer 3]
        E4[Evil Peer N]
    end
    H1 -. Block headers .-> VN
    H2 -. Block headers .-> VN
    H3 -. Block headers .-> VN
    E1 ==>|Saturates all| VN
    E2 ==>|connections| VN
    E3 ==>|with attacker| VN
    E4 ==>|controlled data| VN
    VN -.->|Sends blocks only to| E1
    VN -.->|attacker's view| E2
    VN -.->|of blockchain| E3
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define Proof of Work consensus. List any four attacks on PoW."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Proof of Work is a consensus algorithm in which miners compete to solve a computationally hard puzzle — finding a nonce such that the hash of the block header is below a difficulty target. The first miner to find a valid block broadcasts it and receives the block reward + transaction fees.
- **Four Attacks (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
  1. 51% / Majority Hash Attack
  2. Selfish Mining
  3. Finney Attack
  4. Race Attack
  *(Other acceptable: Vector76, Eclipse, BWH, Long-Range)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the 51% attack with a suitable example. State the formula for attacker's catch-up probability."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Explanation (1.5 marks):** A 51% attack occurs when a single entity controls more than 50% of the network's total hash rate. Example: An attacker sends 100 BTC to a merchant on the public chain while secretly mining an alternate chain where the same 100 BTC is sent back to themselves. Once the private chain exceeds the public chain in length, the attacker publishes it, invalidating the merchant's payment and achieving a double-spend.
- **Formula (1.5 marks):**
$$P_{\text{catchup}} \;\approx\; \left(\dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}\right)^{\!z}$$
where $\ \alpha $ is the attacker's hash fraction and $\ z $ is the number of confirmations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**"[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** With neat diagrams, explain the **Selfish Mining Attack** in detail. Derive the threshold condition for the attack to be profitable."

#### **Part (a) — Selfish Mining Explained (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
Selfish mining is a strategy where a mining pool with less than 50% hash rate withholds newly discovered blocks to gain a disproportionate share of block rewards.

**Step 2 — State Machine Operation (3 marks):**
- When selfish miner finds a block, it is *kept secret* (private chain lead = 1).
- If the honest network finds a competing block, the selfish miner publishes their lead-1 block. Both chains have length 1 → tie situation.
- With probability $\ \gamma $, honest miners connect to selfish pool and selfish wins the tie.
- If selfish finds another block, their lead grows; honest work on the orphaned block is wasted.

**Step 3 — Revenue Outcome (2 marks):**
The selfish pool earns $\ R_{\text{pool}} > \alpha $, violating the assumption that miners earn proportionally to their hash rate.

#### **Part (b) — Threshold Derivation (7 Marks)**

The Eyal–Sirer revenue formula is:

$$R_{\text{pool}} \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha\,(1-\alpha)^{2}\,(4\alpha + \gamma(1-2\alpha)) - \alpha^{3}}{1 - \alpha\,(1 + (2-\alpha)\alpha)}$$

For the attack to be profitable: $\ R_{\text{pool}} > \alpha $.

Setting $\ R_{\text{pool}} = \alpha $ and solving the inequality (canonical Eyal–Sirer result):

$$\boxed{\alpha \;>\; \dfrac{1 - \gamma}{3 - 2\gamma}}$$

**Interpretation (2 marks):**
- For $\ \gamma = 0 $ (no propagation advantage): threshold = $\ 1/3 \approx 33.3\% $.
- For $\ \gamma = 0.5 $: threshold $\ \approx 0.25 $.
- For $\ \gamma = 1 $: threshold = $\ 0 $ (any hash power suffices in theory!).

**Engineering Implication:** The result is alarming because it implies that even small mining pools can be incentivized to deviate from honest mining.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**"[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** Explain the **51% attack** in detail. With the help of the gambler's ruin model, derive the catch-up probability formula. Why does Bitcoin use 6 confirmations?"

#### **Part (a) — 51% Attack Mechanics (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Setup (2 marks):**
Let $\ \alpha $ = attacker's hash fraction, $\ \beta = 1 - \alpha $ = honest fraction. The attacker mines in private.

**Step 2 — Attack Sequence (3 marks):**
1. Attacker initiates payment TX_A (public).
2. Merchant waits for $\ z $ confirmations.
3. Attacker mines parallel private chain replacing TX_A with TX_B.
4. If private chain length exceeds public chain by $\ \ge 1 $, attacker publishes and wins.

**Step 3 — Conditions (2 marks):**
- Attack succeeds only if $\ \alpha > \beta $ (long-term).
- Number of confirmations $\ z $ exponentially lowers success probability.

#### **Part (b) — Gambler's Ruin Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Recursive Equation (2 marks):**
$$q_{z} \;=\; \alpha \cdot q_{z+1} \;+\; \beta \cdot q_{z-1}$$

with $\ q_{0} = 1 $ and $\ \lim_{z \to \infty} q_{z} = 0 $.

**Solving the Recurrence (3 marks):**
Characteristic equation: $\ \alpha r^{2} - 1 + \beta = 0 \Rightarrow r = \sqrt{\alpha/\beta} $.

Bounded solution (for $\ \alpha < \beta $):

$$q_{z} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}\right)^{\!z} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^{\!z}$$

**Why 6 Confirmations? (2 marks):**
For $\ \alpha = 0.1 $ (a hypothetical 10% attacker), $\ P_{\text{catchup}} = (1/9)^{6} \approx 1.88 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.0002\% $. The exponential decay makes 6 confirmations an engineering sweet spot balancing user experience and security.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Selfish Mining threshold**: Writing $\ \alpha > 1/5 $ instead of the canonical $\ \alpha > (1-\gamma)/(3-2\gamma) $. The $\ 1/3 $ figure is a *specific case* when $\ \gamma = 0 $.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Poisson assumption**: The catch-up probability derivation assumes blocks are discovered via a Poisson process. Students often skip stating this — **lose 1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Misclassifying Eclipse Attack**: Some students write that Eclipse is a "consensus attack." It is a **network-layer** attack that *enables* consensus attacks.
>
> 4. **Omitting the cost barrier argument**: For 51% attack, merely stating the math is insufficient. Examiners expect an **economic argument** (cost > reward → rational attacker abstains).
>
> 5. **Mixing up Finney vs. Race**: Finney requires the attacker to be a **miner with a pre-mined block**. Race requires only the ability to broadcast conflicting transactions.
>
> 6. **Not stating boundary conditions** in the gambler's ruin derivation: $\ q_0 = 1 $, $\ \lim q_z = 0 $. Omitting these costs full marks in 14-mark derivations.
>
> 7. **Ignoring propagation parameter $\ \gamma $**: Selfish mining with $\ \gamma = 0 $ and $\ \gamma > 0 $ are dramatically different. Always state both cases.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3, Attacks on PoW**

- **51% Attack:** Requires $\ >50\% $ hash rate; probability $\ P \approx (q/p)^{z} $; mitigated by 6 confirmations in Bitcoin.
- **Selfish Mining:** Possible at $\ > 33.3\% $ (with $\ \gamma = 0 $); revenue formula by Eyal & Sirer 2013; exploits longest-chain rule.
- **Finney Attack:** Requires attacker to be a miner who has pre-mined a block; 0-conf double-spend.
- **Race Attack:** Two conflicting transactions broadcast simultaneously; 0-conf double-spend; mitigated by first-seen policy.
- **Vector76 Attack:** Hybrid Finney + Race; targets 1–2 confirmations.
- **Eclipse Attack:** P2P-layer attack that isolates a victim by monopolizing their peer connections.
- **Long-Range Attack:** More relevant in PoS but referenced in PoW for the weak subjectivity problem.
- **BWH (Block Withholding):** Pool-internal sabotage; reduces honest pool revenue without gaining direct attacker revenue.
- **Timejacking:** Manipulates a node's perception of network time to slow its mining rate.
- **Confirmation Security:** Grows **exponentially** with $\ z $ — the central reason 6 confirmations are sufficient in Bitcoin.
- **Game-Theoretic Defense:** PoW security relies on the assumption that honest mining is more profitable than attacking. The Nash equilibrium is honest mining for $\ \alpha < 33\% $.
- **Practical Mitigation Strategies:** (i) Increase confirmation thresholds, (ii) Use checkpoints, (iii) Penalize selfish behavior via protocol design (e.g., Fruitchain, StrongChain), (iv) Randomize peer selection to resist eclipse.
- **Cost Barrier Argument:** A successful attack destroys the coin's value, making it economically irrational — but only for *profit-motivated* attackers (state-sponsored attackers may not be profit-motivated).
- **Most Tested KTU Sub-Topics:** (1) Catch-up probability formula derivation, (2) Eyal–Sirer revenue & threshold, (3) Comparison of double-spend variants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Monopoly Problem in Blockchain

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The *Monopoly Problem* (also called the *Centralization Problem* or *Mining Monopoly Threat*) refers to a structural vulnerability in Proof-of-Work (PoW) blockchain networks where mining power, validation rights, or token ownership becomes concentrated within a small group of entities, leading to a de-facto centralized authority that can manipulate the consensus process.

In a properly designed PoW system, any honest miner with sufficient hardware can theoretically participate. However, the *monopoly problem* arises when the economics of mining favor consolidation — for example, when:
- Only a few mining pools control **>50%** of the network hash rate.
- ASIC manufacturing is dominated by one or two companies.
- Electricity costs favor specific geographic regions (e.g., regions with subsidized power).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Village Election"
Imagine a village where every citizen is supposed to vote, but only those who own a tractor (expensive mining rig) can drive to the polling booth. Over time, only tractor-owners cast votes, and a few wealthy families buy all the tractors. Suddenly, the village "democracy" is effectively ruled by 3 families — that is the monopoly problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The monopoly problem is the *primary motivation* behind all alternative consensus mechanisms (PoS, PoB, PoET). Examiners frequently frame questions as: "Why was PoS proposed as a solution to the monopoly problem in PoW?"

---

## 1.2 Proof of Stake (PoS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Proof of Stake (PoS)* is a consensus mechanism in which a validator is chosen to create the next block based on the proportion of cryptocurrency tokens they "stake" (lock up as collateral) in the network, replacing the energy-intensive computational race of Proof-of-Work.

The validator's chance of being selected is roughly proportional to:

$$
P(\text{selection}) \;\propto\; \frac{\text{Staked Coins}}{\text{Total Staked Coins in Network}}
$$

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Interest-Bearing Deposit"
Think of a bank fixed-deposit system. To become a "validator" (bank manager), you must lock a large sum (stake). If you cheat, the bank *slashes* your deposit. The more you deposit, the more often you get selected as the manager, and the more fees you earn. There is no electricity-burning race.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First-Mover / Rich-Get-Richer Critique:** Critics argue PoS favors wealthy stakeholders (the "nothing-at-stake" and "rich-get-richer" critiques). Modern variants like *Delegated PoS (DPoS)* and *Ethereum's Casper* introduce randomization to mitigate this.

---

## 1.3 Proof of Burn (PoB)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Proof of Burn (PoB)* is a consensus algorithm in which miners demonstrate commitment to the network by *cryptographically destroying* (sending to a verifiably unspendable address) a quantity of coins, with the implied guarantee that they have incurred a real-world cost — earning them the right to mine blocks over time.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Proof of Sacrifice"
Imagine joining an exclusive club: to prove you are serious, you must publicly burn a ₹500 note. Once burned, you are added to a "miners ledger" and earn mining rights for weeks. The more you burn, the higher your lottery ticket weight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eternal burner principle:** Coins sent to a "black hole" address (e.g., `1CounterpartyXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXUWLpVr` on Bitcoin) are provably unspendable — the private key is mathematically proven to be unsolvable. This makes the burn **verifiable by anyone**.

---

## 1.4 Proof of Elapsed Time (PoET)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Proof of Elapsed Time (PoET)* is a consensus mechanism developed by Intel (2016) for *permissioned blockchains* (e.g., Hyperledger Sawtooth). It uses a *Trusted Execution Environment (TEE)* — specifically Intel SGX — to generate a cryptographically random wait time for each validator; the validator whose timer expires first wins the right to commit the next block.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Queue Token System"
At a hospital reception, every patient draws a numbered token from a sealed, tamper-proof machine. The patient holding the *lowest* number when their turn arrives gets seen first. No one can predict or rig the draw because the machine is sealed by the manufacturer. PoET works exactly this way — Intel SGX is the "sealed token machine."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** PoET requires specialized hardware (Intel SGX). It is therefore **not suitable for public, permissionless blockchains** — it is restricted to consortium/permissioned networks (e.g., enterprise supply chains).

---

## 1.5 Bitcoin Miner

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *Bitcoin Miner* is a specialized node (consisting of hardware + software) that participates in Proof-of-Work consensus by repeatedly hashing block headers with a varying *nonce* until it discovers a hash value that is numerically less than the network's current *target difficulty* (equivalently, contains a required number of leading zero bits).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The National Lottery Ticket Stamper"
Each miner buys billions of lottery tickets per second (hash guesses). Only one ticket (a hash below the target) wins the block reward of **6.25 BTC** (post-2024 halving). The miner that wins broadcasts the "winning ticket" to the network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mining Hardware Evolution:** CPU (2009) → GPU (2010) → FPGA (2011) → ASIC (2013–present). Modern Bitcoin ASICs like the *Antminer S21* achieve hash rates around **200 TH/s** while consuming roughly **3500 W** of power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Monopoly Problem — Deep Analysis

### Why the Monopoly Problem Exists
1. **Economies of Scale in Mining:** Bulk electricity contracts favor large operators.
2. **ASIC Manufacturing Concentration:** Only a few firms (Bitmain, MicroBT, Canaan) fabricate modern ASICs.
3. **Pool Centralization:** Solo miners join *mining pools* to reduce variance; top 3 pools often control >50% of hash rate.
4. **Geographic Centralization:** Hydro-power regions (e.g., Sichuan, Quebec) attract mining farms.
5. **The 51% Attack Threshold:** Any single entity controlling >50% of hash rate can:
   - Reverse their own transactions (*double spend*).
   - Prevent confirmation of other miners' blocks.
   - Effectively *censor* transactions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Historical Example:** In **2014**, the mining pool *GHash.io* briefly exceeded **55%** of Bitcoin's hash rate, causing community alarm and voluntary pool size caps.

---

## 2.2 Proof of Stake — Operational Logic

### Step-by-Step Block Production
1. **Lock-up Phase:** Validators lock a minimum stake (e.g., **32 ETH** in Ethereum's Beacon Chain) into a staking contract.
2. **Selection Phase:** The protocol pseudo-randomly selects a validator. Selection algorithms include:
   - *Randomized Block Selection* (Nxt, Blackcoin)
   - *Coin-Age Selection* (Peercoin) — weight = coins × time held
   - *BABE/Granda* (Ethereum 2.0)
3. **Block Proposal:** The chosen validator proposes a block.
4. **Attestation:** Other validators vote on the block's validity.
5. **Reward/Slashing:** Honest validators earn issuance + fees. Dishonest validators lose stake (slashed).

### Key Economic Parameters
- **Minimum Stake:** varies by chain (e.g., 32 ETH ≈ ~$100,000+ at typical prices).
- **Slashing Penalty:** typically **1%–100%** of stake depending on offense.
- **Unbonding Period:** time before staked tokens can be withdrawn (e.g., 27 hours minimum on Ethereum).

---

## 2.3 Proof of Burn — Operational Logic

1. **Burn Transaction:** User sends coins to a provably-un-spendable address.
2. **Receipt:** The burn is recorded immutably on the blockchain.
3. **Virtual Mining Credits:** The miner earns *mining credits* proportional to coins burned. These credits decay over time (preventing permanent dominance).
4. **Lottery:** A block is awarded by combining burn credits with a randomization factor.

### Key Constants
- **Slimcoin Decay Rate:** mining credit decreases by **7%** per minute.
- **Effective Burn Cost (real-world):** the user must first *buy* the coin at market price to burn it — so cost is real, but energy is zero.

---

## 2.4 Proof of Elapsed Time — Operational Logic

1. **TEE Enrollment:** Each validator enrolls with an Intel SGX-enabled CPU and receives an attestation key.
2. **Random Wait Generation:** Inside the secure enclave, the validator generates a random wait time $T_i$ from an exponential distribution.
3. **Broadcast:** Each validator broadcasts a signed wait-time certificate.
4. **Winner Selection:** The validator with the **smallest** $T_i$ wins the block slot.
5. **Block Commitment:** Winner builds the block and broadcasts.
6. **Verification:** Other nodes verify the SGX attestation to confirm legitimacy.

### Distribution Used
The wait time $T$ follows an exponential distribution:

$$
f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0
$$

This is *memoryless*, ensuring fair lottery behavior.

---

## 2.5 Bitcoin Miner — Operational Logic

### The Mining Loop
1. **Collect Transactions:** Pull unconfirmed transactions from the mempool.
2. **Construct Block Header:** Includes Merkle root, previous block hash, timestamp, difficulty target, and a 32-bit *nonce*.
3. **Hash Iteration:** Compute $H = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{block header}))$.
4. **Target Check:** Is $H < \text{Target}$? If yes, broadcast. If no, increment nonce and retry.
5. **Difficulty Adjustment:** Every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks), Bitcoin adjusts difficulty to keep block time near **10 minutes**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| PoW Mining Success | $H < T$ | Hash must be less than target | $H, T$ in 256-bit space |
| Mining Probability | $P = \frac{2^{256} - H}{2^{256}}$ | Per-hash success probability | dimensionless (≈ $10^{-19}$ at modern difficulty) |
| Bitcoin Block Time Target | $T_b = 10\ \text{min}$ | Mean time per block | minutes |
| Difficulty Adjustment | $D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{expected}}$ | Recalibrated every 2016 blocks | dimensionless |
| PoS Selection Probability | $P_i = \frac{s_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} s_j}$ | Proportional to stake $s_i$ | dimensionless |
| PoET Wait Distribution | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential wait time | $t$ in seconds |
| PoB Decay | $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | Mining credit decay | $C$ in credits, $t$ in time |
| Network Hash Rate | $H_r = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{T_b}$ | Estimated from difficulty | hashes per second (H/s) |
| Energy per Hash (ASIC) | $E \approx 17.5\ \text{J/TH}$ | Typical 2024 ASIC efficiency | Joules per Terahash |
| Block Reward (post-2024 halving) | $R = 3.125\ \text{BTC}$ | Current Bitcoin subsidy | BTC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin the economic models for blockchain energy audits, validator-economics research, and consensus-comparison studies in industry (e.g., Ethereum's move to PoS reduced its energy footprint by **~99.95%**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bitcoin Network Hash Rate from Difficulty

The Bitcoin protocol records a `difficulty` value $D$ representing how many hashes, on average, are required to find a valid block. The relationship between difficulty, hash rate, and block time is:

$$
T_b = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_r}
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

We know that expected number of hashes to find a valid block equals $D \cdot 2^{32}$, since difficulty $D$ is defined as the ratio of the *easiest* target ($2^{224}$) to the *current* target, expressed in "shares" of $2^{32}$:

$$
\text{Expected Hashes} = D \cdot 2^{32}
$$

Since hash rate $H_r$ is hashes per second, time per block is:

$$
T_b = \frac{\text{Expected Hashes}}{\text{Hash Rate}} = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_r}
$$

Solving for hash rate:

$$
H_r = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{T_b}
$$

**Numerical example:** Suppose $D = 79.5 \times 10^{12}$ (Bitcoin difficulty in mid-2024) and $T_b = 600$ s (10 min target). Then:

$$
H_r = \frac{79.5 \times 10^{12} \cdot 2^{32}}{600}
$$

$$
H_r = \frac{79.5 \times 10^{12} \cdot 4.295 \times 10^{9}}{600}
$$

$$
H_r = \frac{3.415 \times 10^{23}}{600}
$$

$$
H_r \approx 5.69 \times 10^{20}\ \text{H/s} = 569\ \text{EH/s}
$$

This matches the actual Bitcoin network hash rate of **~570 EH/s** in mid-2024 — confirming the formula.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Proof of Stake Expected Reward per Validator

A validator $i$ with stake $s_i$ participates in a network of $n$ validators with total stake $S = \sum_j s_j$. The per-epoch selection probability is:

$$
P_i = \frac{s_i}{S}
$$

If the validator earns reward $R$ (in token units) per successful block proposal, the **expected reward per epoch** is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[R_i] = P_i \cdot R = \frac{s_i}{S} \cdot R
$$

**Annualized expected reward** (assuming $E$ epochs per year):

$$
\mathbb{E}[R_{\text{annual}}] = \frac{s_i}{S} \cdot R \cdot E
$$

**Return on Stake (RoS) per year:**

$$
\text{RoS} = \frac{\mathbb{E}[R_{\text{annual}}]}{s_i} = \frac{R \cdot E}{S}
$$

**Numerical example:** Suppose $S = 25,000,000$ ETH total staked, $R = 0.05$ ETH per block, $E = 7,200$ blocks/year, and a validator stakes $s_i = 32$ ETH:

$$
\text{RoS} = \frac{0.05 \cdot 7{,}200}{25{,}000{,}000} = \frac{360}{25{,}000{,}000} \approx 1.44 \times 10^{-5}
$$

That is **~1.44% per year** for a 32-ETH validator, which is consistent with Ethereum's typical APR range (3%–5%, with the remainder coming from MEV and priority fees — the simple issuance-only reward is the *floor*).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Proof of Burn Mining-Credit Decay

Let $C_0$ be the initial mining credit earned by burning $B$ coins, with decay rate $k$ (in units of 1/time). The credit after time $t$ is:

$$
C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}
$$

The probability of being selected to mine the next block is proportional to current credit:

$$
P_{\text{select}}(t) = \frac{C(t)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} C_j(t)} = \frac{C_0 e^{-k t}}{\sum_{j} C_{0,j} e^{-k t_j}}
$$

**Step-by-step meaning of the derivation:**

1. **Step 1:** The miner sends $B$ coins to an unspendable address.
2. **Step 2:** The protocol credits the miner with $C_0 = B$ mining credits.
3. **Step 3:** Time passes; credits decay exponentially.
4. **Step 4:** At any moment, selection probability is proportional to *current* (decayed) credit, ensuring that *long-term* burners do not permanently dominate.
5. **Step 5:** This dynamic keeps mining power distributed and prevents monopoly *by burners themselves*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slimcoin-specific values:** $k \approx 0.000004$ per second, equivalent to a ~7% per-minute decay.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: PoET Fairness via Memoryless Property

The PoET lottery uses exponentially distributed wait times:

$$
f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0
$$

The cumulative distribution function (probability that wait time is at most $t$) is:

$$
F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Proof of memoryless property (essential for fairness):**

For $s, t > 0$:

$$
P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = \frac{P(T > s + t)}{P(T > s)} = \frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = e^{-\lambda t} = P(T > t)
$$

Interpretation: the probability that the validator wins in the *next* $t$ seconds is *independent of how long it has already waited*. No validator can gain advantage by waiting longer. This is the **mathematical foundation of PoET's fairness guarantee**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For KTU Exam Use)

| Criterion | Proof of Work | Proof of Stake | Proof of Burn | Proof of Elapsed Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Resource Used** | Electricity + ASICs | Locked capital | Destroyed coins | Trusted hardware (TEE) |
| **Energy Cost** | **Very High** | **Low** | **Low (capital cost)** | **Very Low** |
| **Centralization Risk** | High (mining pools) | Medium (whales) | Low–Medium | Low (if TEE is trusted) |
| **Attack Cost** | 51% of hash rate | 51% of staked supply | 51% of all burned supply | Compromise of SGX |
| **Permission Type** | Public | Public | Public | Permissioned |
| **Real-World Use** | Bitcoin, Litecoin | Ethereum, Cardano | Slimcoin, Counterparty | Hyperledger Sawtooth |
| **KTU Frequency** | Very High | **Very High** | Medium | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
