# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 3]**

### Q1. Define the Monopoly Problem in the context of blockchain consensus. Mention any one real-world incident that demonstrated this risk. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> The *Monopoly Problem* in blockchain refers to a situation where mining power, validation rights, or token ownership becomes concentrated within a small group of entities, threatening the decentralized nature of the network. A single entity controlling **>50%** of the hash rate can perform a *51% attack*, enabling double-spending, transaction censorship, and chain reorganization.
>
> *Real-world incident:* In **2014**, the Bitcoin mining pool *GHash.io* briefly exceeded **55%** of total network hash rate, prompting the community to discourage pool centralization.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Proof of Stake and Proof of Burn in terms of resource consumption. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Proof of Stake | Proof of Burn |
|---|---|---|
| Resource | Locked/collateralized capital | Permanently destroyed coins |
| Recovery of Resource | Yes (after unbonding) | No (irreversible) |
| Energy Use | Negligible | Negligible |
| Economic Effect | Opportunity cost of locking | Absolute capital destruction |
| Real-world Chain | Ethereum, Cardano | Slimcoin, Counterparty |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the operational flow of the **Proof of Stake (PoS)** consensus mechanism. How does PoS address the monopoly problem inherent in Proof of Work? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Proof of Elapsed Time (PoET)** mechanism in detail. Explain the role of Intel SGX and discuss why PoET is limited to permissioned blockchains. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Step 1 — Stake Lock-up:** Validators deposit a minimum amount of the network's native token (e.g., **32 ETH** in Ethereum's Beacon Chain) into a staking smart contract as collateral.

**Step 2 — Validator Set Registration:** The deposited tokens register the validator in the active set, indexed by an *epoch-based* system (e.g., 32 slots per epoch in Ethereum).

**Step 3 — Pseudo-Random Selection:** A pseudo-random mechanism (e.g., *RANDAO + VDF* in Ethereum, *Coin-Age* in Peercoin) selects one validator per slot. Probability:

$$
P_i = \frac{s_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} s_j}
$$

**Step 4 — Block Proposal:** The selected validator constructs a block from pending transactions, signs it, and broadcasts it to the network.

**Step 5 — Attestation & Finality:** Other validators verify the block and submit attestations. Once **2/3 of total staked ETH** attests, the block is *finalized* (Casper FFG rule).

**Step 6 — Rewards & Slashing:**
- **Reward:** Validator receives block reward + transaction fees + MEV share.
- **Slashing:** Dishonest behavior (e.g., double-signing) results in loss of **≥1%** of stake, with full loss of **100%** for severe offenses.

**Step 7 — How PoS Solves Monopoly Problem:**
- *Energy-asymmetrical attack:* Launching a 51% attack requires acquiring **>50% of the total staked supply** (multi-billion dollar cost), which is economically irrational — the attacker would devalue their own holdings.
- *Self-punishing mechanism:* The attacker's stake is slashed if caught, providing cryptographic economic disincentive.
- *Lower barrier to entry:* Validators need only capital (not ASIC hardware + electricity), enabling broader participation.

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Step 1 — Hardware Prerequisite:** Each validator must run a *Trusted Execution Environment* (TEE), specifically **Intel SGX**, which creates an isolated memory region (enclave) inaccessible even to the host operating system.

**Step 2 — Attestation Enrollment:** On startup, the SGX enclave produces a *remote attestation quote*, signed by Intel, proving the hardware is genuine and the software hash matches the expected PoET binary.

**Step 3 — Random Wait Generation:** Inside the enclave, the validator draws a random wait time $T_i$ from the exponential distribution:

$$
f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0
$$

**Step 4 — Broadcast:** The validator signs $(T_i, \text{validator\_id})$ and broadcasts it on-chain.

**Step 5 — Winner Election:** At the end of the round, the validator with the **smallest** $T_i$ wins the right to commit the next block.

**Step 6 — Verification:** Other nodes verify:
- The SGX attestation quote is valid.
- The signed $T_i$ matches the on-chain record.
- The block is well-formed.

**Step 7 — Why PoET is limited to Permissioned Blockchains:**
- *Hardware Trust Dependency:* PoET assumes Intel SGX is uncompromised. A backdoor in Intel hardware would collapse the security model. Public chains cannot rely on a single hardware vendor.
- *Validator Enrollment:* Public chains must allow *anyone* to participate; PoET requires pre-screened, SGX-equipped nodes.
- *Use Case Fit:* Permissioned networks (e.g., supply chain consortia, Hyperledger Sawtooth) have known, pre-vetted participants — exactly the scenario where hardware trust is acceptable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** What is the **Monopoly Problem** in Proof of Work? List four key factors that lead to mining centralization, and explain the **51% Attack** with its mathematical basis. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain **Proof of Burn (PoB)** with its operational phases. How does the credit-decay function in PoB help prevent long-term monopoly by large burners? *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Step 1 — Definition of Monopoly Problem:**
The Monopoly Problem refers to the gradual concentration of mining power (hash rate) in a small number of entities, which undermines the decentralized trust assumption of blockchain networks.

**Step 2 — Four Key Factors Leading to Mining Centralization:**

1. **Economies of Scale in Hardware Procurement:** Bulk orders of ASIC miners receive significant discounts, favoring large mining farms.
2. **Electricity Cost Asymmetry:** Industrial miners negotiate power purchase agreements (PPAs) at rates as low as **2–3 cents/kWh**, while residential miners pay **10–15 cents/kWh**.
3. **Mining Pool Concentration:** Solo miners join pools to reduce reward variance. Top pools (e.g., Foundry USA, AntPool) often capture >50% of hash rate.
4. **Geographic Centralization:** Mining gravitates to regions with cheap, often subsidized, renewable energy (e.g., hydroelectric in Sichuan, geothermal in Iceland, stranded energy in Texas).

**Step 3 — The 51% Attack — Mechanism:**
If an attacker controls more than 50% of total network hash rate $H_{\text{total}}$, they can:
- *Double Spend:* Mine a private fork where they send coins to a victim, then re-mine those blocks on the main chain to themselves.
- *Censor Transactions:* Refuse to include specific transactions in their blocks.
- *Block Other Miners:* Orphan competitors' valid blocks by extending their own chain.

**Step 4 — Mathematical Basis:**
The probability that an honest miner finds the next block, given the attacker's share $\alpha$ of hash rate, is:

$$
P_{\text{honest finds next}} = 1 - \alpha
$$

The probability of the attacker successfully extending their private fork by $z$ blocks before honest miners extend the public chain is modeled by a *Gambler's Ruin* problem:

$$
P_{\text{attack success}} = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \alpha > 0.5 \\
\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}\right)^z & \text{if } \alpha < 0.5
\end{cases}
$$

For $\alpha = 0.51$ and $z = 6$ confirmations:

$$
P = \left(\frac{0.51}{0.49}\right)^6 = (1.0408)^6 \approx 0.272 = 27.2\%
$$

A 27% chance of double-spend after 6 confirmations demonstrates why exchanges typically wait for **≥6 confirmations** for high-value Bitcoin transactions.

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Concept:**
Proof of Burn is a consensus mechanism in which miners demonstrate long-term commitment to the network by *destroying* (sending to a provably unspendable address) a quantity of cryptocurrency. The act of burning represents a real, irreversible economic sacrifice, analogous to PoW's electricity expenditure but with zero ongoing energy cost.

**Step 2 — Operational Phases:**

*Phase 1 — Acquisition:* The miner first purchases the coin (e.g., Slimcoin, Bitcoin) on an exchange at the prevailing market price. This is a real capital cost.

*Phase 2 — Burn Transaction:* The miner constructs a transaction sending the coins to a *black-hole address* (e.g., for Bitcoin, the address `1CounterpartyXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXUWLpVr` is mathematically proven unspendable). The transaction is broadcast and confirmed on-chain.

*Phase 3 — Credit Issuance:* The PoB protocol reads the burn event and credits the miner's account with *mining credits* proportional to the burned amount: $C_0 = B$ (where $B$ is the number of coins burned).

*Phase 4 — Block Lottery:* In each round, a random number is combined with the miner's current credit balance to select the block producer. Selection probability:

$$
P_{\text{select}} = \frac{C(t)}{\sum_{j} C_j(t)}
$$

*Phase 5 — Block Reward:* The selected miner proposes the next block and receives the block reward plus fees, paid in freshly minted coins.

**Step 3 — Credit Decay Function:**
Mining credits decay exponentially with time:

$$
C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}
$$

where $k$ is the decay constant. In Slimcoin, $k$ corresponds to a **~7% per-minute** decay rate.

**Step 4 — Why Decay Prevents Monopoly:**

1. **Time-Weighted Equality:** A miner who burned 1000 coins a year ago now has only a tiny fraction of the original credit. A miner who burned 100 coins today has more current credit. This balances the system in favor of *recent commitment*.

2. **No Permanent Dominance:** Even early adopters cannot lock in mining power indefinitely. They must *re-burn* coins periodically to maintain their position.

3. **Capital Recycling:** Since credits decay, large burners must continuously destroy capital, preventing the *rich-get-richer* effect seen in some Proof of Stake implementations.

4. **Game-Theoretic Stability:** The expected value of burning equals the expected mining reward minus the opportunity cost of destroyed capital. This creates a Nash equilibrium where rational miners do not over-accumulate burns.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing *Proof of Stake* with *Proof of Authority* (PoA). PoA uses validator identity, not stake.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Saying "PoB consumes electricity" — it does not. The cost is *capital destruction*, not energy.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting that PoET *requires* Intel SGX and is therefore *not* permissionless.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Stating the block reward as **6.25 BTC** without mentioning the **April 2024 halving** that reduced it to **3.125 BTC**.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Writing the PoS selection formula as $P_i = s_i$ instead of the correct proportional form $P_i = s_i / \sum_j s_j$.
> - **Common Mistake #6:** Omitting the difference between *transaction finality* in PoW (probabilistic) vs. PoS (often absolute, after finality gadget).
> - **Valuation Tip:** Always include at least one **real-world chain example** in 14-mark answers — examiners reward applied understanding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Sub-topic Bundle**

- ☐ **Monopoly Problem:** Concentration of hash rate / stake / burn credits threatening decentralization; mitigated by 51% attack economics.
- ☐ **51% Attack Threshold:** Any consensus mechanism can be subverted by >50% control of its *primary resource* (hash, stake, burn, or TEE trust).
- ☐ **Proof of Stake (PoS):** Validators selected by stake proportion; slashing as economic deterrent; energy-efficient (~99.95% savings over PoW).
- ☐ **Proof of Burn (PoB):** Miners destroy coins at provably-unspendable addresses; credit decays exponentially; long-term dominance prevented.
- ☐ **Proof of Elapsed Time (PoET):** Intel SGX-based random wait lottery; permissioned only; memoryless exponential distribution.
- ☐ **Bitcoin Miner:** ASIC hardware; double-SHA-256; 10-min target; 2016-block difficulty adjustment; current reward **3.125 BTC** post-April 2024 halving.
- ☐ **Mining Pools:** Combine hash rate; reduce variance; *cause* of the monopoly problem in practice.
- ☐ **Energy vs. Capital Trade-off:** PoW = energy cost; PoS = capital lock; PoB = capital destruction; PoET = hardware trust.
- ☐ **Real-world Deployments:** Bitcoin (PoW), Ethereum (PoS), Slimcoin (PoB), Hyperledger Sawtooth (PoET).
- ☐ **Key Formulae to Memorize:** $P_i = s_i / S$, $H_r = D \cdot 2^{32} / T_b$, $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$, $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ☐ **Finality Difference:** PoW = *probabilistic*; PoS (Casper) = *absolute* after finalization.
- ☐ **Nothing-at-Stake Problem:** Validators can vote on multiple forks in PoS without cost — solved by *slashing*.
- ☐ **Long-Range Attack Risk:** Old validators rewriting history — solved by *weak subjectivity checkpoints* (Ethereum).
- ☐ **Permissionless vs. Permissioned:** PoW/PoS/PoB are *permissionless*; PoET is *permissioned* (consortium-grade).
- ☐ **Exam Mantra:** Always tie your answer to *decentralization*, *security model*, and *real-world deployment*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mining Difficulty
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mining Difficulty

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mining Difficulty** is a network-wide, dynamically-adjusted metric in Proof-of-Work (PoW) blockchains (notably Bitcoin) that quantifies the computational effort required for a miner to produce a valid block. Formally, it is defined as the ratio of the **maximum possible target** to the **current target threshold** that a valid block hash must satisfy.

Mathematically, the mining difficulty $\mathcal{D}$ is expressed as:

$$\mathcal{D} \;=\; \frac{T_{\max}}{T_{\text{current}}}$$

where $T_{\max}$ is the highest possible target (corresponding to the minimum difficulty of 1) and $T_{\text{current}}$ is the current 256-bit target stored in the block header.

A block is considered valid if its double-SHA-256 hash is numerically less than or equal to the current target. A **smaller target** therefore implies a **higher difficulty**, because valid hashes become statistically rarer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mining Difficulty is a *self-regulating consensus parameter* that ensures a constant average block generation interval (e.g., **10 minutes** in Bitcoin) regardless of the total computational power (hash rate) on the network.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Digital Gold Rush

Imagine a vast desert where gold nuggets are hidden. On Day 1, only a few miners (workers) are searching, and finding a nugget is relatively easy. As word spreads and thousands more miners arrive, the desert gets *crowded* — and each individual miner finds fewer nuggets per day.

To keep the *total gold produced per day* constant (as promised by the system), the governing body of the desert makes the gold **harder to find** — perhaps by burying it deeper or scattering it in more places. This is precisely what the mining difficulty does in Bitcoin:

- **More miners join** → hash rate rises → difficulty is automatically **increased** (target lowered).
- **Miners leave** → hash rate falls → difficulty is automatically **decreased** (target raised).

The system thus guarantees a predictable coin issuance schedule, decoupled from physical hardware progress.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Boundary Values

| Metric | Value (Bitcoin) |
|---|---|
| **Target Block Time** | **10 minutes** |
| **Difficulty Adjustment Period** | **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks) |
| **Maximum Difficulty Change per Period** | **Factor of 4 (4× up or ¼ down)** |
| **Genesis Block Difficulty** | **1** |
| **Hash Function** | **SHA-256 (double application)** |
| **Target Bit Length** | **256 bits** |
| **Compact Representation in Block** | "nBits" (4 bytes) |

> [!NOTE]
> The term *difficulty* is dimensionless. Genesis block of Bitcoin has $\mathcal{D}=1$. As of mid-2024, Bitcoin's difficulty exceeds **80 trillion** ($\approx 8 \times 10^{13}$), reflecting massive global hash power.

## 1.4 Why Mining Difficulty is Essential

1. **Monetary Supply Control:** Satoshi Nakamoto designed Bitcoin to release a *fixed* supply schedule. Without difficulty adjustment, faster hardware would release all 21 million BTC in months.
2. **Security:** High difficulty makes a **51% attack** economically prohibitive.
3. **Block Time Stabilization:** Maintains the **10-minute** target for transaction confirmations.
4. **Decentralization Incentive:** As difficulty rises, only efficient miners (low electricity costs) remain profitable, but the *protocol* does not discriminate — anyone can join.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Target Threshold ($T$) — Heart of Difficulty

Every block header in Bitcoin contains a 4-byte field called **nBits**, which is a compact (exponent–mantissa) representation of the 256-bit target $T$. The mining software translates nBits into a full 256-bit number against which the candidate block hash is compared.

A hash $H$ is **valid** if and only if:

$$H \;\leq\; T$$

Because SHA-256 produces uniformly distributed outputs across the 256-bit space, the probability of a single hash being valid is:

$$P(\text{success per hash}) \;=\; \frac{T + 1}{2^{256}}$$

The **expected number of hashes** to find a valid block is therefore:

$$E[\text{hashes per block}] \;=\; \frac{2^{256}}{T + 1}$$

The mining **difficulty** $\mathcal{D}$ is then:

$$\mathcal{D} \;=\; \frac{2^{224}}{T + 1} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad T \;\approx\; \frac{2^{224}}{\mathcal{D}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant $2^{224}$ (instead of $2^{256}$) is used because Bitcoin's pool/genesis difficulty = 1 corresponds to a target that has 32 leading zero bits, i.e., $T_{\max} \approx 2^{224}$. This simplifies human-readable difficulty reporting.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Difficulty Adjustment

Bitcoin recalculates the difficulty at the end of every **2016-block epoch**. The algorithm proceeds as:

1. **Measure** the actual time $T_{\text{actual}}$ spent mining the last 2016 blocks.
2. **Compute the ratio** of actual time to the ideal 2-week target (20160 minutes):

   $$r \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{20160 \text{ minutes}}$$

3. **Adjust the target**:

   $$T_{\text{new}} \;=\; T_{\text{old}} \times r$$

   Equivalently, the new difficulty becomes:

   $$\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \;=\; \frac{\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}}}{r} \;=\; \mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \times \frac{20160}{T_{\text{actual}}}$$

4. **Clamp the result** to prevent abrupt shocks:

   $$\frac{1}{4} \,\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \;\leq\; \mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \;\leq\; 4 \,\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}}$$

   i.e., a maximum **4× change** per adjustment epoch, regardless of the hash rate volatility.

5. **Store** the new target in compact form (nBits) in the next block's header.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following cheat-sheet table is the single most-tested reference for Mining Difficulty questions in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Difficulty Definition | $\mathcal{D} = T_{\max} / T_{\text{current}}$ | Higher $\mathcal{D}$ $\Rightarrow$ smaller target $\Rightarrow$ harder puzzle |
| 2 | Hash Success Probability | $P = (T+1)/2^{256}$ | Approx. $T/2^{256}$ for large $T$ |
| 3 | Expected Hashes per Block | $E[N] = 2^{256}/(T+1)$ | Work required per block |
| 4 | Difficulty–Target Link | $\mathcal{D} = 2^{224}/(T+1)$ | Bitcoin-specific constant $2^{224}$ |
| 5 | New Target Formula | $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \cdot (T_{\text{actual}}/20160)$ | Core adjustment equation |
| 6 | New Difficulty Formula | $\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} = \mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \cdot (20160 / T_{\text{actual}})$ | Inverse of ratio above |
| 7 | Hash Rate Relation | $H \;(\text{H/s}) = \mathcal{D} \cdot 2^{32} / t_{\text{block}}$ | Network hash rate |
| 8 | Adjustment Clamp | $0.25\,\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \le \mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \le 4\,\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}}$ | Safety bound (4× cap) |
| 9 | Block Count per Epoch | $N_{\text{epoch}} = 2016$ | Roughly 2 weeks at 10 min/block |
| 10 | Compact nBits Encoding | $\text{nBits} = \text{exponent} \cdot 2^{24} + \text{mantissa}$ | 3-byte mantissa + 1-byte exponent |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, division and absolute-value notations use $\diagup$ or $\vert$ (or `mid`) — **never** use the bare pipe `|` character — to avoid breaking markdown table rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mining-difficulty concept transcends cryptocurrency. It is fundamentally a **closed-loop negative-feedback control system** with the following engineering analogues:

- **CPU Frequency Scaling (DVFS):** Modern processors throttle based on temperature — analogous to the network throttling block production via difficulty.
- **TCP Congestion Control:** Window size shrinks when packet loss rises; difficulty rises when block times fall.
- **Power-Grid Demand Response:** Load-shedding is "difficulty rising" in response to over-generation.
- **Mining Difficulty in Industry:** Used in PoW altcoins (Litecoin, Dogecoin, Monero) with period constants tuned to their respective block times.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Probability of Hash Success from Target

**Given:** A uniformly distributed 256-bit hash function $H$ over $[0, 2^{256}-1]$.

**Find:** Probability that a single hash attempt falls below target $T$.

### Step 1: Total Sample Space

The hash function $H$ maps the block header to any of the $2^{256}$ possible 256-bit integers, each with equal probability.

$$P(H = h) \;=\; \frac{1}{2^{256}}, \quad \forall h \in [0, 2^{256}-1]$$

### Step 2: Count Favorable Outcomes

The number of "valid" hashes satisfying $H \le T$ is exactly $T+1$ (since $H$ is an integer in $[0, T]$).

### Step 3: Compute Probability

By classical probability:

$$P(\text{valid}) \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Total outcomes}} \;=\; \frac{T+1}{2^{256}}$$

For very large $T$ (e.g., $T \approx 2^{224}$), the $+1$ is negligible, so:

$$\boxed{\,P(\text{valid per hash}) \;\approx\; \frac{T}{2^{256}}\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Number of Hashes per Block

The mining process is a **Bernoulli trial** with success probability $p = (T+1)/2^{256}$. The number of trials $N$ until first success follows a **Geometric distribution** with expected value:

$$E[N] \;=\; \frac{1}{p} \;=\; \frac{2^{256}}{T+1}$$

Substituting the difficulty relation $T+1 = 2^{224}/\mathcal{D}$:

$$E[N] \;=\; \frac{2^{256}}{2^{224}/\mathcal{D}} \;=\; \mathcal{D} \cdot 2^{32}$$

Therefore, **on average, a miner must perform $2^{32} \cdot \mathcal{D}$ hash computations** to find a valid block.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Difficulty Adjustment Equation

**Given:** Previous 2016 blocks took $T_{\text{actual}}$ minutes (instead of the target 20160 min).

**Find:** New target $T_{\text{new}}$ and new difficulty $\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}}$.

### Step 1: Relate Hash Rate to Difficulty

Total hashes performed in time $T_{\text{actual}}$ to mine 2016 blocks:

$$N_{\text{hashes}} \;=\; 2016 \cdot E[N] \;=\; 2016 \cdot \mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \cdot 2^{32}$$

### Step 2: Express Hash Rate

$$H \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{hashes}}}{T_{\text{actual}}} \;=\; \frac{2016 \cdot \mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \cdot 2^{32}}{T_{\text{actual}}} \quad [\text{hashes/min}]$$

### Step 3: Required Hash Rate to Maintain 10-min Block

For the next 2016 blocks to be mined in exactly 20160 minutes:

$$H_{\text{target}} \;=\; \frac{2016 \cdot \mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \cdot 2^{32}}{20160}$$

### Step 4: Equate the Two (Same Network Hash Rate)

$$\frac{2016 \cdot \mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \cdot 2^{32}}{T_{\text{actual}}} \;=\; \frac{2016 \cdot \mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \cdot 2^{32}}{20160}$$

### Step 5: Simplify

The constants $2016$ and $2^{32}$ cancel:

$$\frac{\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{actual}}} \;=\; \frac{\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}}}{20160}$$

### Step 6: Solve for $\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}}$

$$\boxed{\,\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \;=\; \mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{20160}{T_{\text{actual}}}\,}$$

Equivalently, since $T = 2^{224}/\mathcal{D}$:

$$\boxed{\,T_{\text{new}} \;=\; T_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{20160}\,}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Fast Network (Difficulty Rises)

**Scenario:** 2016 blocks were mined in **10,080 minutes** (7 days instead of 14).

### Given Values

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} = 50{,}000{,}000{,}000{,} \quad T_{\text{actual}} = 10080 \text{ min}$$

### Calculation

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \;=\; 50 \times 10^{12} \times \frac{20160}{10080} \;=\; 50 \times 10^{12} \times 2 \;=\; 100 \times 10^{12}$$

**Interpretation:** The hash rate doubled during the previous period. To restore 10-minute block times, the network **doubles** the difficulty. ✓

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Slow Network (Difficulty Falls)

**Scenario:** 2016 blocks were mined in **30,240 minutes** (21 days).

### Given Values

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} = 50 \times 10^{12}, \quad T_{\text{actual}} = 30240 \text{ min}$$

### Calculation

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \;=\; 50 \times 10^{12} \times \frac{20160}{30240} \;=\; 50 \times 10^{12} \times 0.6667 \;\approx\; 33.33 \times 10^{12}$$

**Interpretation:** Hash rate dropped by 50%; difficulty drops by ~33% to compensate.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 3 — 4× Clamp Triggered

**Scenario:** 2016 blocks were mined in **1,000 minutes** (an extreme hash rate spike).

### Calculation

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{new, raw}} \;=\; 50 \times 10^{12} \times \frac{20160}{1000} \;=\; 50 \times 10^{12} \times 20.16 \;=\; 1.008 \times 10^{15}$$

### Apply Clamp

Upper bound $= 4 \times 50 \times 10^{12} = 2 \times 10^{14}$.

Since $1.008 \times 10^{15} > 2 \times 10^{14}$, the **clamp activates**:

$$\boxed{\,\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \;=\; 2 \times 10^{14}\,}$$

This safeguard prevents catastrophic shock to the network (e.g., if a new ASIC generation suddenly appears mid-epoch).

## 3.7 Derivation 4 — Hash Rate from Difficulty

**Given:** A miner observes that blocks are currently being produced every $t_{\text{block}}$ minutes, with current difficulty $\mathcal{D}$.

**Find:** Network hash rate $H$ (in H/s).

### Derivation

From Section 3.2, expected hashes per block $= \mathcal{D} \cdot 2^{32}$. Convert block time to seconds: $t_{\text{block}} \times 60$. Therefore:

$$H \;=\; \frac{\mathcal{D} \cdot 2^{32}}{t_{\text{block}} \times 60} \quad [\text{hashes/second}]$$

### Example

If $\mathcal{D} = 80 \times 10^{12}$ and $t_{\text{block}} = 10 \text{ min} = 600 \text{ s}$:

$$H \;=\; \frac{80 \times 10^{12} \times 2^{32}}{600} \;\approx\; \frac{80 \times 10^{12} \times 4.295 \times 10^{9}}{600} \;\approx\; 5.73 \times 10^{20} \text{ H/s}$$

This is roughly **573 EH/s** (exahashes per second) — consistent with the real Bitcoin network in 2024.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Mining Difficulty Adjustment Cycle

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the closed-loop feedback control logic of the Bitcoin difficulty adjustment mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of 2016-Block Epoch] --> B[Miners Compete Globally with SHA-256 Hash Power]
    B --> C[Each Miner Constructs Block Header + Nonce]
    C --> D{Hash <= Target Threshold?}
    D -- No --> E[Increment Nonce / Change Merkle Root / Update Timestamp]
    E --> D
    D -- Yes --> F[Broadcast Valid Block to Network]
    F --> G[Other Nodes Verify and Accept Block]
    G --> H[Append Block to Local Copy of Blockchain]
    H --> I{Block Count of Epoch == 2016?}
    I -- No --> B
    I -- Yes --> J[Measure Total Actual Time T_actual of Last 2016 Blocks]
    J --> K[Compute Ratio r = T_actual / 20160 minutes]
    K --> L{Clamp r to within 0.25 to 4.0 Range}
    L --> M[Compute New Target T_new = T_old x r]
    M --> N[Encode New Target into 4-Byte nBits Field]
    N --> O[Broadcast Updated Difficulty to All Nodes]
    O --> P[Start New 2016-Block Epoch with New Difficulty]
    P --> B
```

## 4.2 Block Header Anatomy (nBits Location)

The compact representation of the difficulty target is stored in the **nBits** field of every block header.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BH[Block Header 80 Bytes Total]
        V[Version - 4 Bytes]
        P[Previous Block Hash - 32 Bytes]
        MR[Merkle Root - 32 Bytes]
        T[Timestamp - 4 Bytes]
        NB[nBits - 4 Bytes - DIFFICULTY TARGET]
        N[Nonce - 4 Bytes]
    end
    V --> P --> MR --> T --> NB --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **nBits** field is a 4-byte compact encoding: the leading byte is the *exponent* and the trailing 3 bytes are the *mantissa*. For example, nBits `0x1d00ffff` decodes to a target of `0x00ffff * 2^(8*(0x1d-3))`.

## 4.3 Difficulty vs. Hash Rate Relationship

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Total Network Hash Rate H] -->|Rises Sharply| B[Block Time Falls Below 10 min]
    B --> C[2016 Blocks Mined Faster than 2 Weeks]
    C --> D[Difficulty Increases 4x Cap]
    D --> E[Block Time Returns Toward 10 min Target]
    E --> A
    A2[Total Network Hash Rate H Decreases] -->|Falls| B2[Block Time Rises Above 10 min]
    B2 --> C2[2016 Blocks Mined Slower than 2 Weeks]
    C2 --> D2[Difficulty Decreases 4x Cap]
    D2 --> E2[Block Time Returns Toward 10 min Target]
    E2 --> A2
```

## 4.4 Mining-Difficulty Feedback Loop as Engineering Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reference Block Time 10 min] --> S[Subtractor Compares]
    O[Observed Block Time] --> S
    S --> E[Error Signal e = Reference - Observed]
    E --> CTRL[Controller Difficulty Adjustment Algorithm]
    CTRL --> U[Control Signal New Target T_new]
    U --> PL[Plant - Mining Network Hash Power]
    PL --> O
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **negative feedback proportional controller** with a discrete (every 2016 blocks) update period and a saturation limit (4× clamp). It is functionally equivalent to industrial **P-only control with anti-windup protection**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define mining difficulty. How is it related to the target threshold in Bitcoin?**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Mining difficulty is a measure of how hard it is to find a hash below a given target during the Proof-of-Work mining process. It quantifies the average number of hash attempts required to produce a valid block.

**Mathematical Relation (2 Marks):** It is inversely proportional to the target threshold:

$$\mathcal{D} \;=\; \frac{T_{\max}}{T_{\text{current}}}$$

where $T_{\max}$ is the maximum (genesis) target and $T_{\text{current}}$ is the current 256-bit target stored in the block's `nBits` field. A smaller target implies higher difficulty because valid hashes become statistically rarer. For the genesis block, $\mathcal{D}=1$ since $T_{\text{current}}=T_{\max}$.

---

### **Q2. Why does Bitcoin's mining difficulty adjust every 2016 blocks?**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Purpose (1 Mark):** To maintain a constant average block generation time of **10 minutes** irrespective of the total network hash rate.
2. **Mechanism (1 Mark):** Every 2016 blocks (~2 weeks) the protocol measures the actual time taken and adjusts the target as $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times (T_{\text{actual}}/20160)$.
3. **Need (1 Mark):** Without adjustment, faster hardware (ASICs) would shrink block times, accelerate coin emission beyond the planned supply schedule, and compromise network security against double-spend attacks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Explain the Bitcoin mining difficulty adjustment algorithm in detail. A mining pool observes that the last 2016 blocks took 12096 minutes instead of the ideal 20160 minutes. If the old difficulty was 60 trillion, calculate the new difficulty. Also explain why the 4× clamp is applied.**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Initialization [1 Mark]:**
At every 2016-block boundary, a Bitcoin node records the timestamp of the first block and the latest block of the just-completed epoch.

**Step 2 — Time Measurement [1 Mark]:**
The actual time $T_{\text{actual}}$ spent to mine the 2016 blocks is computed from these timestamps.

**Step 3 — Ratio Computation [1 Mark]:**
A ratio is formed:

$$r \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{20160 \text{ minutes}}$$

**Step 4 — Target Update [2 Marks]:**
The new 256-bit target is computed as:

$$T_{\text{new}} \;=\; T_{\text{old}} \times r$$

Equivalently, the new difficulty is:

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \;=\; \frac{\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}}}{r} \;=\; \mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \times \frac{20160}{T_{\text{actual}}}$$

**Step 5 — Clamping [1 Mark]:**
The new difficulty is bounded:

$$0.25\,\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \le \mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \le 4\,\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}}$$

**Step 6 — Propagation [1 Mark]:**
The new target is encoded into 4-byte `nBits` and included in the next block, signalling the updated difficulty to all nodes.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation & Clamp Justification [7 Marks]**

**Given:**

$$T_{\text{actual}} = 12096 \text{ min}, \quad \mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} = 60 \times 10^{12}$$

**Calculation [4 Marks]:**

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \;=\; 60 \times 10^{12} \times \frac{20160}{12096}$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{20160}{12096} \;=\; \frac{20160 \div 1008}{12096 \div 1008} \;=\; \frac{20}{12} \;=\; \frac{5}{3} \;\approx\; 1.6667$$

Therefore:

$$\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \;=\; 60 \times 10^{12} \times \frac{5}{3} \;=\; 100 \times 10^{12} \;=\; 10^{14}$$

**Interpretation [1 Mark]:** The network's hash rate was higher than expected (blocks came 40% faster), so the network increased difficulty by a factor of $5/3$ to bring the block time back to 10 minutes.

**Why 4× Clamp is Applied [2 Marks]:**
- **Security against manipulation:** Without a clamp, a malicious miner with majority hash power could trigger wild difficulty oscillations.
- **Predictability:** A bounded change rate (max 4× per ~2 weeks = $\sim 8\times$/month) gives miners, investors, and ASIC manufacturers a stable economic environment.
- **Stability during hardware shocks:** Sudden appearance of new ASICs (e.g., Bitmain S19 series) cannot instantly drive the difficulty to astronomically high values.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice. With a suitable block-level diagram, explain the components of a Bitcoin block header and show how the `nBits` field encodes the difficulty target. Derive the relationship between hash rate, difficulty, and block time. If the network hashrate is 600 EH/s and the current block time is observed to be 8 minutes, what is the approximate network difficulty? Assume $1 \text{ EH} = 10^{18} \text{ H/s}$.**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — Block Header & nBits Encoding [7 Marks]**

**Block Header Components [3 Marks]:**

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 4 bytes | Protocol version |
| Previous Block Hash | 32 bytes | Links to parent block |
| Merkle Root | 32 bytes | Hash of all transactions |
| Timestamp | 4 bytes | Time of mining |
| **nBits** | **4 bytes** | **Compact encoding of difficulty target** |
| Nonce | 4 bytes | Counter incremented during mining |

**nBits Encoding [4 Marks]:**
The `nBits` field uses scientific notation in base-256. The first byte is the exponent $e$ and the next three bytes form the mantissa $m$ (coefficient). The full target is:

$$T \;=\; m \cdot 2^{8(e-3)}$$

For example, Bitcoin's `nBits = 0x1d00ffff` (genesis) decodes as:

- $e = 0x1d = 29$
- $m = 0x00ffff = 65535$

$$T_{\max} \;=\; 65535 \cdot 2^{8(29-3)} \;=\; 65535 \cdot 2^{208}$$

This is the maximum target (minimum difficulty = 1).

#### **Part (b) — Derivation & Numerical Computation [7 Marks]**

**Derivation [4 Marks]:**

Expected hashes per block:

$$E[N] \;=\; \frac{2^{256}}{T+1} \;\approx\; \mathcal{D} \cdot 2^{32}$$

Total network hashes in time $t_{\text{block}}$ (in seconds) equals:

$$H \cdot t_{\text{block}} \;=\; \mathcal{D} \cdot 2^{32}$$

Solving for difficulty:

$$\mathcal{D} \;=\; \frac{H \cdot t_{\text{block}}}{2^{32}}$$

**Numerical Calculation [3 Marks]:**

**Given:**

$$H = 600 \text{ EH/s} = 600 \times 10^{18} \text{ H/s}, \quad t_{\text{block}} = 8 \text{ min} = 480 \text{ s}$$

Compute:

$$\mathcal{D} \;=\; \frac{600 \times 10^{18} \times 480}{2^{32}} \;=\; \frac{2.88 \times 10^{23}}{4.295 \times 10^{9}} \;\approx\; 6.7 \times 10^{13}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\,\mathcal{D} \;\approx\; 67 \text{ trillion}\,}$$

**Sanity Check [Bonus 0 Marks — for understanding]:** Block time is 8 min (faster than 10 min target), so difficulty should be higher than the equilibrium. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points (Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 4× clamp:** Students compute the raw new difficulty but forget to apply $\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \le 4\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}}$. **(-1 to -2 marks).**
> 2. **Inverting the ratio:** Writing $\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} = \mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \times (T_{\text{actual}}/20160)$ instead of $\times (20160/T_{\text{actual}})$. **(-2 marks).**
> 3. **Confusing target and difficulty:** They are inverse; stating that a higher target means higher difficulty loses marks. **(-1 mark).**
> 4. **Using $2^{256}$ instead of $2^{224}$:** The constant depends on the genesis target. For Bitcoin's "difficulty-1" definition, use $2^{224}$. **(-1 mark).**
> 5. **Omitting the nBits encoding explanation** in long-answer questions. KTU examiners expect at least 2-3 lines on the 3-byte mantissa + 1-byte exponent format.
> 6. **Unit mistakes:** Reporting hash rate in EH/s without showing the conversion $1 \text{ EH/s} = 10^{18} \text{ H/s}$. **(-1 mark).**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Mining difficulty** is the network-wide measure of computational effort required to find a valid block hash.
- ✅ **Mathematical core:** $\mathcal{D} = T_{\max}/T_{\text{current}}$; higher $\mathcal{D}$ $\Leftrightarrow$ smaller $T$ $\Leftrightarrow$ harder puzzle.
- ✅ **Validity condition:** Block hash $H \le T$ (current target).
- ✅ **Probability of valid hash:** $P = (T+1)/2^{256} \approx T/2^{256}$.
- ✅ **Expected work per block:** $\mathcal{D} \cdot 2^{32}$ hash computations.
- ✅ **Adjustment period:** every **2016 blocks** (≈ 2 weeks).
- ✅ **Adjustment formula:** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} = \mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \times (20160/T_{\text{actual}})$.
- ✅ **4× Clamp:** $\frac{1}{4}\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}} \le \mathcal{D}_{\text{new}} \le 4\mathcal{D}_{\text{old}}$.
- ✅ **Genesis difficulty = 1;** current Bitcoin difficulty $\approx 80$ trillion (2024).
- ✅ **nBits field** is the 4-byte compact encoding of the 256-bit target, in exponent-mantissa form.
- ✅ **Hash-rate formula:** $H = (\mathcal{D} \cdot 2^{32})/t_{\text{block}}$ (with $t_{\text{block}}$ in seconds).
- ✅ **Engineering interpretation:** Mining difficulty is a **closed-loop negative-feedback controller** with discrete update period and saturation limit.
- ✅ **Security role:** Higher difficulty = more expensive 51% attack = stronger network.
- ✅ **Supply control:** Difficulty ensures Bitcoin's 21 million coin cap is reached around year 2140, not earlier.
- ✅ **Real-world use:** Same principle used in Litecoin (2016 blocks, 2.5 min target), Dogecoin, Monero, etc.
- ✅ **Key terminology:** Target, Difficulty, nBits, Epoch, Hash Rate, Nonce, Genesis Block, ASIC, Clamp.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mining Pool, Permissioned Model and Use Cases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mining Pool, Permissioned Model and Use Cases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mining Pool — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Mining Pool (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A *mining pool* is a **cooperative grouping of individual cryptocurrency miners** who pool their **computational resources (hashrate)** over a network to **jointly solve cryptographic puzzles**, **increase the probability of finding a valid block**, and **share the block rewards** in proportion to the contributed work (measured in *valid shares*).

Each miner in the pool is called a **pool miner** or **hash contributor**. The pool is coordinated by a **pool operator** who runs a **pool server** (a.k.a. *stratum server* or *getwork relay*) that distributes **block templates** and collects **shares** from each miner.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Group Lottery

Imagine **100 friends** each buying **1 lottery ticket** independently. Each has only a **1% chance** of winning. If instead they **pool money and buy 100 tickets together**, their **collective chance rises to 100× higher**, but the **winnings must be split**. A mining pool works **exactly like this**:

| Lottery Analogy | Mining Pool Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Each friend buying a ticket | Each miner contributing hashrate |
| Pooled money → 100 tickets | Pooled hashrate → higher block-finding probability |
| Prize split equally | Reward split by valid shares |
| The friend who organized the pool | The **Pool Operator** |
| Ticket stubs as proof of participation | **Cryptographic Shares** submitted to pool |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mathematical breakthrough of mining pools is the **Law of Large Numbers**: the variance of reward is reduced as $\sqrt{N}$ where $N$ is the number of independent miners, while the **mean reward rate** stays constant. This converts a high-variance lottery into a **predictable income stream**.

---

## 1.2 Permissioned Blockchain Model — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Permissioned Blockchain (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A *permissioned blockchain* (also called a **consortium blockchain** or **private blockchain**) is a distributed ledger where **participation rights** — including the ability to **read**, **submit transactions**, and **validate blocks** — are **restricted to pre-approved, known, and authenticated entities** governed by an **identity layer** and an **access control policy**.

Unlike Bitcoin's *permissionless* design, a permissioned network is **NOT open to the public**. Every node carries a **cryptographic identity (X.509 certificate, Membership Service Provider identity, etc.)** issued by a **central authority or consortium**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Corporate Intranet vs. The Public Internet

Think of the **permissionless blockchain as the Public Internet**:
- Anyone can connect, host a server, and publish content.
- No central authority issues credentials.

The **permissioned blockchain is the Corporate Intranet / Bank Network**:
- You need a **badge, an employee ID, and firewall clearance** to enter.
- Every action is **logged, signed, and traceable** to a real identity.
- The company (or consortium) decides **who can do what**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Permissioned blockchains **sacrifice decentralization for performance and compliance**. They can achieve **thousands of TPS** (transactions per second) using **PBFT, Raft, or Istanbul BFT** consensus — far beyond Bitcoin's ~7 TPS — because the consensus set is small and known.

---

## 1.3 Use Cases — Why Permissioned Models Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The **Hyperledger Fabric** (Linux Foundation), **R3 Corda**, **Quorum** (J.P. Morgan), and **Hyperledger Besu** are the **four flagship permissioned platforms** expected in the KTU 2024 syllabus for enterprise use cases.

| Sector | Permissioned Use Case | Why Permissioned? |
|---|---|---|
| **Banking & Finance** | Cross-border settlements (e.g., **Marco Polo Network**) | KYC/AML compliance, regulator visibility |
| **Supply Chain** | Walmart–IBM **Food Trust** (mango traceability) | Confidential pricing, B2B privacy |
| **Healthcare** | **MediLedger** (drug provenance) | HIPAA compliance, patient data privacy |
| **Government** | **Estonia e-Residency / KSI Blockchain** | Citizen data sovereignty |
| **Insurance** | B3i (Blockchain Insurance Industry Initiative) | Smart-contract automation among insurers |
| **Trade Finance** | **we.trade** (IBM + 7 European banks) | Counter-party risk reduction |

---

## 1.4 Standard Performance & Security Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key **standard metrics** used in the KTU 2024 scheme for evaluating these models:
> - **Hashrate ($H$):** Measured in **H/s (hashes per second)** — typically expressed as **TH/s** ($10^{12}$ H/s) or **EH/s** ($10^{18}$ H/s) for modern Bitcoin mining.
> - **Block Time ($t_{block}$):** Bitcoin target = **10 minutes**; Ethereum (pre-Merge) = **~13 seconds**; Hyperledger Fabric = **< 1 second**.
> - **TPS (Transactions Per Second):** Bitcoin ≈ **7 TPS**; Ethereum ≈ **30 TPS**; Hyperledger Fabric ≈ **3000+ TPS**.
> - **Difficulty ($D$):** Re-adjustment target — Bitcoin = **2016 blocks ≈ 2 weeks**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mining Pool — Operational Architecture

A mining pool operates on a **client–server model** with the following components:

### Pool Architecture Components (in order of request flow):

1. **Pool Server (Stratum Server):** Maintains a connection to the **full Bitcoin node** (or relevant chain node). Fetches the **block template** (candidate block with current tip, merkle root, difficulty target).
2. **Work Distribution:** The server splits the candidate block header's **nonce range** and **extra-nonce space** among connected miners using the **Stratum Protocol (v1/v2)**.
3. **Share Submission:** Miners return **shares** — valid PoW solutions computed at a **reduced difficulty** (e.g., $D_{share} = D_{block} / 1000$).
4. **Share Validation:** The pool checks each share against the target: $\text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{header})) < T_{share}$.
5. **Reward Engine:** When a share also satisfies $D_{block}$, the pool **broadcasts the full block** to the network and **distributes the reward**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why reduced share difficulty?** A miner working at full network difficulty ($D_{block}$) would **never** find a block alone in a reasonable time. Lowering the target threshold means a miner can submit **partial proofs** (shares) that are *not* valid blocks but **prove work was done** — this is the basis for **fair reward distribution**.

---

## 2.2 Mining Pool Reward Distribution Methods — The Core "Why"

Different reward schemes balance the trade-off between **expected reward (mean)** and **reward variance (risk)**. The KTU 2024 scheme frequently tests these.

### Method 1 — Pay-Per-Share (PPS)

The pool **pays a fixed amount per valid share**, regardless of whether the pool finds a block. The pool operator absorbs the variance risk.

$$R_{PPS} = \frac{B + (n-1)\cdot f}{D_{block} / D_{share}}$$

Where:
- $B$ = block reward (subsidy + fees)
- $n$ = number of blocks in confirmation maturity period
- $f$ = average transaction fee per block
- $D_{block}$ = network block difficulty
- $D_{share}$ = share difficulty

> Miners get **predictable, low-variance income**, but the pool charges **high fees (typically 3–8%)** to compensate for risk.

### Method 2 — Proportional (PROP)

When a block is found, the reward is split **proportionally to the number of shares contributed** in the **current round**.

$$R_{PROP,i} = B \cdot \frac{S_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} S_j}$$

Where $S_i$ = number of shares submitted by miner $i$, and $N$ = total pool miners.

> Disadvantage: vulnerable to **"pool hopping"** — miners switch pools mid-round to maximize expected reward (an exploitable behavior).

### Method 3 — Pay-Per-Last-N-Shares (PPLNS)

A sliding window of the **last $N$ shares** (regardless of round boundaries) determines reward distribution.

$$R_{PPLNS,i} = B \cdot \frac{S_i^{window}}{S_{total}^{window}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PPLNS **resists pool hopping** and gives **higher long-term expected payoff**, but introduces **higher variance** than PPS.

### Method 4 — Score-Based

Each share is assigned a **score** that increases with the time since the start of the round. This penalizes late-round joiners.

$$\text{Score}(t) = \gamma^{t}$$

Where $\gamma$ is a smoothing constant ($0 < \gamma < 1$) and $t$ is elapsed round time. Late shares are **worth less**, discouraging hoppers.

### Method 5 — SOLO Mining (No Pool)

Miner keeps **100% of block reward** but expects $E[T] = \frac{2^{32} \cdot D}{H}$ seconds between blocks. Variance is enormous.

---

## 2.3 Mathematical Foundation — Expected Block Time & Probability

### Solo Miner Expected Time to Find a Block

$$E[T_{solo}] = \frac{2^{32} \cdot D_{block}}{H_{solo}}$$

Where $H_{solo}$ is the miner's hashrate in H/s and $D_{block}$ is the current network difficulty (in "difficulty-1 units" where the genesis difficulty is 1).

### Pool Miner's Effective Block Time

$$E[T_{pool}] = \frac{2^{32} \cdot D_{block}}{H_{pool,total}}$$

> As $H_{pool,total} \gg H_{solo}$, the pool finds blocks **far more frequently** — distributing the reward.

### Probability of Finding a Block in Time $T$

For a Poisson process with rate $\lambda = H / (2^{32} \cdot D_{block})$:

$$P(\text{at least one block in time } T) = 1 - e^{-\lambda T}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Formula | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E[T_{solo}] = \frac{2^{32} \cdot D}{H}$ | $D$ = difficulty, $H$ = hashrate | Expected time for a solo miner to find a block |
| 2 | $E[R_{solo}] = B \cdot \frac{H_{solo}}{H_{total}}$ | $B$ = block reward | Solo miner's long-term expected reward |
| 3 | $R_{PPS} = \frac{B}{D_{block}/D_{share}}$ | $B$ = block reward | Fixed payment per share |
| 4 | $R_{PROP,i} = B \cdot \frac{S_i}{S_{total}}$ | $S_i$ = shares by miner $i$ | Proportional distribution |
| 5 | $R_{PPLNS,i} = B \cdot \frac{S_i^{N}}{S_{total}^{N}}$ | $N$ = window size | PPLNS sliding window reward |
| 6 | $P(\text{success in } T) = 1 - e^{-H T / (2^{32} D)}$ | — | Block-finding probability over horizon $T$ |
| 7 | $\text{Var}(R_{pool}) = \text{Var}(R_{solo}) / N$ | $N$ = number of pool members | Variance reduction by pool |
| 8 | $\sigma_{pool} = \sigma_{solo} / \sqrt{N}$ | — | Standard deviation reduction |
| 9 | $TPS_{theoretical} = \frac{\text{block size (bytes)}}{\text{tx size (bytes)} \cdot t_{block}}$ | — | Transaction throughput |
| 10 | $\text{Energy} = P \cdot T$ | $P$ = power (W), $T$ = time (s) | Mining electricity cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In exam answers, **always** state the **units** explicitly: hashrate in **H/s** (or TH/s, EH/s), time in **seconds**, reward in **coins/satoshis**, and TPS in **transactions/second**.

---

## 2.5 Permissioned Model — Why It Exists (Engineering Rationale)

Permissioned blockchains solve **three real problems** that permissionless chains cannot address in enterprise contexts:

### Problem 1 — Regulatory Compliance
Banks and hospitals must comply with **KYC, AML, HIPAA, GDPR**. Permissionless chains have **pseudonymous, irreversible** transactions — legally incompatible.

### Problem 2 — Transaction Throughput
Permissionless chains trade throughput for decentralization. PBFT-based permissioned chains achieve **$\mathcal{O}(n^2)$** message complexity but with $n$ small ($\leq$ 50), throughput is **high**.

### Problem 3 — Confidentiality
Permissionless chains have **public state**. Enterprises require **private channels** (Hyperledger Fabric's *Private Data Collections*) or **zero-knowledge proofs** for confidential business data.

### Consensus Comparison (Permissioned vs Permissionless)

| Property | Permissionless (PoW) | Permissioned (PBFT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Throughput** | ~7 TPS | ~3000+ TPS |
| **Latency** | ~10 min finality | ~1–2 sec finality |
| **Identity** | Pseudonymous | Verified (X.509) |
| **Energy** | Massive (PoW) | Minimal |
| **Consensus** | Nakamoto (probabilistic) | PBFT (deterministic) |
| **Forking** | Probabilistic (allows forks) | Deterministic (no forks) |
| **Use** | Crypto, public ledgers | Enterprise, consortium |

> [!NOTE]
> The **deterministic finality** of PBFT means **no forks** — once a block is committed, it is final. This is critical for **legal and financial** applications where **reversal is unacceptable**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Variance Reduction in Mining Pools

### Setup
Let $X_i$ be a Bernoulli random variable for the event that miner $i$ finds a block in time interval $T$.

$$P(X_i = 1) = p_i = \frac{H_i}{H_{total}}, \quad P(X_i = 0) = 1 - p_i$$

### Expected Block Reward for a Solo Miner

For a single miner with hashrate $H_i$ in a network of total hashrate $H_{total}$:

$$E[R_{solo,i}] = B \cdot p_i = B \cdot \frac{H_i}{H_{total}}$$

This is the **long-run mean** reward per round.

### Variance of Solo Reward

Since $X_i$ is Bernoulli, the variance is:

$$\text{Var}(R_{solo,i}) = B^2 \cdot p_i(1 - p_i)$$

For an individual miner, $p_i \ll 1$ (e.g., $p_i \approx 10^{-18}$ for a small miner), so:

$$\text{Var}(R_{solo,i}) \approx B^2 \cdot p_i = B^2 \cdot \frac{H_i}{H_{total}}$$

### Pool Miner's Variance

When a miner joins a pool of $N$ equal members (each with hashrate $H_i$), the pool's effective hashrate is $N \cdot H_i$. The probability of the **pool** finding a block rises to:

$$p_{pool} = N \cdot p_i \ll 1 \text{ (still small, but } N \text{ times larger)}$$

The miner's expected reward stays the same: $E[R_{pool,i}] = B \cdot p_i$.

However, the miner's **variance** drops because the pool's reward is split into $N$ pieces, but paid out **every time the pool finds a block** (much more frequently than solo):

$$\text{Var}(R_{pool,i}) = \frac{B^2 \cdot p_{pool} \cdot (1 - p_{pool})}{N^2}$$

Substituting $p_{pool} = N \cdot p_i$:

$$\text{Var}(R_{pool,i}) = \frac{B^2 \cdot N \cdot p_i \cdot (1 - N p_i)}{N^2} = \frac{B^2 \cdot p_i (1 - N p_i)}{N}$$

For $N p_i \ll 1$ (typical):

$$\text{Var}(R_{pool,i}) \approx \frac{B^2 \cdot p_i}{N} = \frac{\text{Var}(R_{solo,i})}{N}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(R_{pool}) = \frac{\text{Var}(R_{solo})}{N}, \quad \sigma_{pool} = \frac{\sigma_{solo}}{\sqrt{N}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **standard deviation shrinks by $\sqrt{N}$** while the **mean is preserved** — this is the **Law of Large Numbers** applied to mining. A pool of **100 equal miners** gives a $\sqrt{100} = 10\times$ reduction in reward volatility.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pool Hopping Attack on Proportional Reward

### Setup
Consider a Proportional pool where a round starts at $t = 0$ and ends when a block is found at $t = T$. The expected time to find a block is $E[T] = 1/\lambda$ where $\lambda = H_{pool} / (2^{32} D)$.

### Per-Share Expected Reward
A miner who contributes a share at time $t$ has expected payoff:

$$E[\text{payoff per share at time } t] = \frac{B}{S_{total}(t)} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

Because the longer the round lasts, the more shares accumulate, **diluting** the per-share reward. Miners who join **late** get shares worth **more**, while miners who join **early** get shares worth **less** (their shares are diluted by future joiners).

### Attack
A rational miner ("hopper") joins **only when $t$ is large** (late in the round) to maximize per-share expected payoff, then **switches to another pool** when a new round starts.

### Mitigation
The **Score-Based** scheme modifies the per-share value:

$$\text{Score}(t) = \gamma^{t}, \quad \gamma \in (0,1)$$

Late-round shares are **devalued exponentially**, neutralizing the hopper's advantage. **PPLNS** also resists hopping because the window **straddles round boundaries**, and shares submitted just before a block find are **rewarded in the next round's window**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Permissioned Model's PBFT Consensus (Step-by-Step)

Permissioned blockchains typically use **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)**. The KTU syllabus often asks for the **phases** of PBFT.

### Setup
$N$ replicas, $f$ Byzantine (malicious) faults. PBFT tolerates $N \geq 3f + 1$.

### Phase 1 — Pre-Prepare
The **primary (leader)** assigns a sequence number $n$ to a client request $m$ and broadcasts a **PRE-PREPARE** message to all backups.

### Phase 2 — Prepare
Each backup **verifies** the PRE-PREPARE (signature, view number, sequence) and broadcasts a **PREPARE** message. When a replica receives **$2f$ matching PREPAREs**, it enters the *prepared* state.

### Phase 3 — Commit
The replica broadcasts a **COMMIT** message. When a replica receives **$2f + 1$ matching COMMITs**, it **executes** the request and sends a **REPLY** to the client.

### Mathematical Justification of $N \geq 3f + 1$

Consider the worst case: $f$ nodes are **Byzantine** and may send conflicting messages. To guarantee all **honest** nodes reach the same decision:

- Need at least $f + 1$ honest nodes to form a **majority**.
- Total nodes $N = (\text{honest}) + (\text{Byzantine}) \geq (f + 1) + f = 2f + 1$.

But in the **Prepare** phase, a replica needs **$2f$ matching PREPAREs** to commit. These must come from **distinct** replicas (else Byzantine can fake votes). Among the $N - f$ honest replicas, the maximum number that can *fail to send* a matching PREPARE is $f$ (those are the dishonest ones). So:

$$\text{Honest replicas sending matching PREPARE} \geq (N - f) - f = N - 2f$$

For a quorum, we need $N - 2f \geq f + 1$, giving:

$$N \geq 3f + 1$$

**Example for KTU-style exam:**

- If $N = 4$, then $f \leq 1$ (3f+1 = 4 ✓).
- If $N = 10$, then $f \leq 3$ (3f+1 = 10 ✓).
- If $N = 7$, then $f \leq 2$ (3f+1 = 7 ✓).

### Throughput of PBFT

$$TPS_{PBFT} \approx \frac{\text{Block size in transactions}}{t_{commit}}, \quad t_{commit} \approx \text{RTT} \cdot 3$$

With intra-data-center RTT $\approx$ 1 ms, $t_{commit} \approx 3$ ms. With block size = 500 transactions:

$$TPS \approx \frac{500}{0.003} \approx 1.67 \times 10^{5} \text{ TPS (theoretical)}$$

Practical Fabric throughput: **~3000 TPS** due to transaction execution overhead, endorsement policy, and ordering service bottlenecks.

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mining Pool Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Full Blockchain Node] -->|Fetch block template| B[Stratum Pool Server]
    B -->|Distribute work unit| C1[Miner Node 1]
    B -->|Distribute work unit| C2[Miner Node 2]
    B -->|Distribute work unit| C3[Miner Node 3]
    B -->|Distribute work unit| C4[Miner Node N]
    C1 -->|Submit share at shareDiff| D[Share Aggregator]
    C2 -->|Submit share at shareDiff| D
    C3 -->|Submit share at shareDiff| D
    C4 -->|Submit share at shareDiff| D
    D -->|Validate shares| E{Share meets blockDiff?}
    E -->|No| F[Record contribution to ledger]
    E -->|Yes| G[Pool broadcasts full block to network]
    G -->|Block accepted| H[Distribute reward via PPS or PPLNS]
    F -->|Continue mining| C1
    F -->|Continue mining| C2
    F -->|Continue mining| C3
    F -->|Continue mining| C4
    H -->|Payout in BTC| I[Miner Wallets]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Stratum Pool Server** is the **single point of trust** — a critical centralization risk.
- The **Share Aggregator** maintains the **share ledger** (an internal database) for fair reward distribution.
- Miners never see the full block — they receive a **header template + extra-nonce** and search for valid nonces.

---

## 4.2 Permissioned Blockchain Network — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IdentityLayer["Identity & Membership Layer"]
        A1[Certificate Authority X.509] --> A2[Membership Service Provider]
        A2 --> A3[Issue Identities to Peers]
    end

    subgraph EndorserLayer["Endorsement Layer"]
        B1[Endorser Peer 1] --> B2[Chaincode Execution]
        B3[Endorser Peer 2] --> B2
        B4[Endorser Peer 3] --> B2
    end

    subgraph OrderingLayer["Ordering Service"]
        C1[Orderer Node 1]
        C2[Orderer Node 2]
        C3[Orderer Node 3]
    end

    subgraph CommittingLayer["Committing Layer"]
        D1[Committing Peer A]
        D2[Committing Peer B]
        D3[Committing Peer C]
    end

    subgraph LedgerLayer["Distributed Ledger"]
        E1[(World State DB)]
        E2[(Blockchain)]
    end

    A3 --> B1
    A3 --> B3
    A3 --> B4
    B2 -->|Endorsed transaction| C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 -->|Ordered block| D1
    C3 -->|Ordered block| D2
    C3 -->|Ordered block| D3
    D1 --> E1
    D1 --> E2
    D2 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> E1
    D3 --> E2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Hyperledger Fabric** architecture: transactions are first **endorsed** (executed privately on endorsing peers), then **ordered** (sequenced by orderer nodes), then **committed** to the ledger.
- The **MSP (Membership Service Provider)** binds every action to a **verified identity** — no anonymous participation.

## 4.4 Sequence Flow — PBFT Consensus (3-Phase Commit)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant P as Primary Leader
    participant B1 as Backup 1
    participant B2 as Backup 2
    participant B3 as Backup 3

    C->>P: Send request m
    P->>B1: PRE-PREPARE n v m
    P->>B2: PRE-PREPARE n v m
    P->>B3: PRE-PREPARE n v m

    B1->>P: PREPARE n v digest
    B1->>B2: PREPARE n v digest
    B1->>B3: PREPARE n v digest
    B2->>P: PREPARE n v digest
    B2->>B1: PREPARE n v digest
    B2->>B3: PREPARE n v digest
    B3->>P: PREPARE n v digest
    B3->>B1: PREPARE n v digest
    B3->>B2: PREPARE n v digest

    Note over B1,B3: 2f matching PREPAREs received

    B1->>B2: COMMIT n v digest
    B1->>B3: COMMIT n v digest
    B2->>B1: COMMIT n v digest
    B2->>B3: COMMIT n v digest
    B3->>B1: COMMIT n v digest
    B3->>B2: COMMIT n v digest

    Note over B1,B3: 2f+1 matching COMMITs

    B1->>C: REPLY result
    B2->>C: REPLY result
    B3->>C: REPLY result
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Three rounds of message exchange: **Pre-Prepare → Prepare → Commit**.
- Total messages: $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ per request, but with small $N$ (10–50 nodes), this is tractable.
- Finality is **deterministic** — once the client receives $f + 1$ matching REPLYs, the request is committed irreversibly.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]

> **Q1.** Define a *mining pool*. List **any three** popular reward distribution methods used in mining pools.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A mining pool is a **cooperative group of cryptocurrency miners** who combine their **computational resources (hashrate)** to **jointly solve PoW puzzles** and **share block rewards** in proportion to the **contributed valid shares**.

Three reward distribution methods:

1. **Pay-Per-Share (PPS):** Fixed payment per share; pool operator absorbs variance risk. *(1 mark)*
2. **Proportional (PROP):** Reward split proportional to shares in a single round. *(1 mark)*
3. **Pay-Per-Last-N-Shares (PPLNS):** Sliding window of last $N$ shares determines payout. *(1 mark)*

> *(Alternative acceptable: Score-Based, FPPS — Full Pay Per Share, ESMPPS.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

> **Q2.** Differentiate between **permissioned** and **permissionless** blockchains. Give **one example** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Permissionless | Permissioned |
|---|---|---|
| **Access** | Open, anyone can join | Restricted, identity-verified |
| **Consensus** | PoW / PoS (Nakamoto) | PBFT / Raft (deterministic) |
| **Throughput** | Low (~7–30 TPS) | High (~3000+ TPS) |
| **Example** | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Hyperledger Fabric, Corda |

*(1 mark for each correct row; 1 mark for valid examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks — Internal Choice Option]

> **Q (A).** *(a)* Explain the **architecture and working of a mining pool** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **PPS** and **PPLNS** reward distribution schemes in detail. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A solo miner with hashrate **120 TH/s** operates in a network of total hashrate **600 EH/s**. Current block reward is **6.25 BTC** and difficulty yields an average block time of **600 seconds**. Calculate: (i) the **expected time** for this miner to find a block solo, (ii) the miner's **expected reward per day** in BTC, (iii) the **probability** that the miner finds at least one block in 30 days. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Architecture (3 marks):**

A mining pool consists of:
- **Pool Server (Stratum Server):** Connected to a full blockchain node; distributes work.
- **Pool Miners:** Receive work units; compute PoW; submit shares.
- **Share Aggregator:** Validates shares, updates internal ledger.
- **Reward Engine:** Computes payouts based on chosen scheme.
- **Pool Wallet:** Holds rewards until distribution maturity (typically 100 confirmations).

**Working (2 marks):**
1. Pool server fetches a candidate block template.
2. Server splits the **nonce range + extra-nonce** and distributes to miners.
3. Miners iterate nonces, computing SHA-256(SHA-256(header)) and checking against a **share difficulty target** (lower than network).
4. Each valid share is submitted to the pool.
5. If any share's hash also satisfies the **network difficulty**, the pool broadcasts the full block.
6. Reward is distributed per the chosen method.

**PPS (1 mark):**
$$R_{PPS} = \frac{B}{D_{block} / D_{share}}$$
Pool pays a **fixed amount per share** regardless of whether the pool finds a block. Pool absorbs risk → **higher fees (3–8%)**.

**PPLNS (1 mark):**
$$R_{PPLNS,i} = B \cdot \frac{S_i^{window}}{S_{total}^{window}}$$
A **sliding window of last $N$ shares** is used. Higher long-term expected reward, lower variance than solo, **resistant to pool hopping**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $H_{solo} = 120 \text{ TH/s} = 120 \times 10^{12} \text{ H/s}$
- $H_{total} = 600 \text{ EH/s} = 600 \times 10^{18} \text{ H/s}$
- $B = 6.25 \text{ BTC}$
- Average block time $E[T_{block}] = 600 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Derive the network difficulty constant:**

$$H_{total} = \frac{2^{32} \cdot D}{E[T_{block}]}$$

$$2^{32} \cdot D = H_{total} \cdot E[T_{block}] = (600 \times 10^{18}) \cdot 600 = 3.6 \times 10^{23}$$

$$2^{32} \cdot D = 3.6 \times 10^{23}$$

**Step (i) — Expected time for solo miner to find a block:**

$$E[T_{solo}] = \frac{2^{32} \cdot D}{H_{solo}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{23}}{120 \times 10^{12}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{23}}{1.2 \times 10^{14}} = 3.0 \times 10^{9} \text{ seconds}$$

**Convert to years:**

$$E[T_{solo}] = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{9}}{365.25 \times 24 \times 3600} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{9}}{3.156 \times 10^{7}} \approx 95.05 \text{ years}$$

> **The solo miner expects to find a block only once every ~95 years.** *(2 marks)*

**Step (ii) — Expected reward per day:**

The fraction of total hashrate controlled by the miner:

$$f_{hash} = \frac{H_{solo}}{H_{total}} = \frac{120 \times 10^{12}}{600 \times 10^{18}} = \frac{120}{600 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \times 10^{-7}$$

Number of blocks found per day network-wide:

$$N_{blocks/day} = \frac{86400}{600} = 144 \text{ blocks/day}$$

Expected BTC earned per day:

$$E[R_{day}] = f_{hash} \cdot N_{blocks/day} \cdot B = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 144 \times 6.25$$

$$E[R_{day}] = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ BTC/day} = 0.00018 \text{ BTC/day}$$

> *(2 marks)*

**Step (iii) — Probability of finding $\geq 1$ block in 30 days:**

For a Poisson process with rate $\lambda = E[R_{day}]/B$ in units of "blocks per day" from the miner's perspective:

$$\lambda = f_{hash} \cdot N_{blocks/day} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 144 = 2.88 \times 10^{-5} \text{ blocks/day}$$

Expected blocks in 30 days:

$$E[N_{30}] = \lambda \cdot 30 = 2.88 \times 10^{-5} \times 30 = 8.64 \times 10^{-4}$$

Probability of at least one block in 30 days (Poisson distribution):

$$P(N \geq 1) = 1 - P(N = 0) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} = 1 - e^{-8.64 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Using Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ for small $x$:

$$P(N \geq 1) \approx 1 - (1 - 8.64 \times 10^{-4}) = 8.64 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.0864\%$$

> **There is only a 0.0864% chance the miner finds a block in 30 days.** This is why **pool mining is essential** for predictable income. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Internal Choice Option]

> **Q (B).** *(a)* Describe the **permissioned blockchain model** in detail. Compare it with the **permissionless model** along the dimensions of **consensus, throughput, identity, and finality**. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Explain **PBFT consensus** in detail with its three phases. If a network has **10 nodes**, what is the **maximum number of Byzantine (malicious) nodes** that PBFT can tolerate? Justify your answer with the formula $N \geq 3f + 1$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
A permissioned blockchain is a distributed ledger where **read, write, and validate permissions** are restricted to **pre-authenticated participants** governed by an **identity layer (e.g., X.509 certificates issued by an MSP)**.

**Key Characteristics (2 marks):**
- **Identity Verification:** Every node has a cryptographically verified identity.
- **Selective Access:** Read/write/validate can be separated (e.g., only banks can submit transactions; regulator can read all).
- **Centralized Governance:** A consortium or single authority manages the membership.
- **Confidentiality:** Channels, private data collections, and zero-knowledge proofs support privacy.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Dimension | Permissionless (Bitcoin, Ethereum) | Permissioned (Hyperledger, Corda) |
|---|---|---|
| **Consensus** | Nakamoto (PoW/PoS), probabilistic | PBFT, Raft, deterministic |
| **Throughput** | 7–30 TPS | 1000–3000+ TPS |
| **Identity** | Pseudonymous (public key) | Verified (X.509, MSP) |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic (6 confirmations ≈ 1 hr) | Deterministic (~1–2 sec) |
| **Energy** | High (PoW) | Low |
| **Examples** | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Hyperledger Fabric, Corda, Quorum |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**PBFT — Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (5 marks):**

A consensus protocol designed by Castro & Liskov (1999) to tolerate **Byzantine failures** in asynchronous networks with deterministic finality.

**Phase 1 — Pre-Prepare:**
- The **primary (leader)** assigns a **sequence number** $n$ to a client request $m$ and broadcasts a **PRE-PREPARE** message $(PRE-PREPARE, v, n, digest(m))$ to all backups.
- $v$ = current view number; $n$ = sequence number; $digest(m)$ = cryptographic digest of the request.

**Phase 2 — Prepare:**
- Each backup **verifies** the PRE-PREPARE (correct view, valid signature, sequence number in window).
- Backups broadcast **PREPARE** messages to all other replicas.
- A backup enters the *prepared* state upon receiving **$2f$ matching PREPAREs** with the same view, sequence number, and digest.

**Phase 3 — Commit:**
- Backups broadcast **COMMIT** messages.
- A backup enters the *committed-local* state upon receiving **$2f + 1$ matching COMMITs**.
- The request is **executed**, and a **REPLY** is sent to the client.
- The client accepts the result upon receiving **$f + 1$ matching REPLYs** from different replicas (ensuring at least one honest replica executed the request).

**Why $2f + 1$?**
With $f$ Byzantine nodes, the worst case has $f$ nodes refusing to respond. From the remaining $N - f$ honest nodes, the replica needs a **quorum** of $2f + 1$ matching messages, so:

$$N - f \geq 2f + 1 \implies N \geq 3f + 1$$

**Byzantine Tolerance for $N = 10$ (2 marks):**

$$10 \geq 3f + 1 \implies 3f \leq 9 \implies f \leq 3$$

> **The network can tolerate up to 3 malicious (Byzantine) nodes out of 10.** *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistake:**
> - Students often confuse $2f$ and $2f+1$ in PBFT. **Remember:** the **Prepare** phase requires **$2f$ matching messages** to enter the prepared state, and the **Commit** phase requires **$2f + 1$ matching messages** (including the replica's own) to commit.
> - In the final client reply, **$f + 1$ matching REPLYs** suffice (not $2f + 1$).
> - Many students write $N \geq 2f + 1$ — this is **incorrect**; the correct bound is $N \geq 3f + 1$ because the protocol must survive a simultaneous **view change** scenario.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Mining Pool, Permissioned Model & Use Cases**

### Mining Pool Essentials
- ☐ Mining pools are formed to **reduce reward variance** for individual miners via the **Law of Large Numbers** ($\sigma \to \sigma / \sqrt{N}$).
- ☐ A **share** is a PoW solution at **reduced difficulty** ($D_{share} < D_{block}$) submitted to the pool.
- ☐ **PPS** = fixed payment per share; pool absorbs variance; **high fees**.
- ☐ **PROP** = proportional to shares per round; vulnerable to **pool hopping**.
- ☐ **PPLNS** = sliding window of last $N$ shares; **resists pool hopping**; higher long-term EV.
- ☐ **Score-Based** = late shares worth less; smooths reward distribution.
- ☐ **Stratum Protocol** is the standard communication protocol between pool server and miners.
- ☐ The **pool operator** is a **centralization risk** (single point of trust).

### Solo Mining Math — Must Memorize
- ☐ $E[T_{solo}] = \frac{2^{32} \cdot D}{H}$
- ☐ $E[R_{solo}] = B \cdot H / H_{total}$
- ☐ $P(\geq 1 \text{ block in time } T) = 1 - e^{-\lambda T}$ where $\lambda = H / (2^{32} D)$

### Permissioned Blockchain Essentials
- ☐ **Identity is verified** (X.509, MSP) — no anonymous participation.
- ☐ **Consensus** is typically **PBFT, Raft, or Istanbul BFT** — deterministic finality.
- ☐ PBFT requires **$N \geq 3f + 1$** to tolerate $f$ Byzantine nodes.
- ☐ **No forks** in PBFT — transactions are final after a single round.
- ☐ Throughput: **~3000+ TPS** vs Bitcoin's ~7 TPS.
- ☐ Examples: **Hyperledger Fabric, R3 Corda, Quorum, Hyperledger Besu**.

### Hyperledger Fabric Pipeline (3-Stage)
- ☐ **Endorse** (private execution on endorsing peers) → **Order** (sequence transactions) → **Commit** (validate and append to ledger).

### Use Case Highlights
- ☐ **Finance:** Corda (cross-border), Quorum (settlement), we.trade (trade finance).
- ☐ **Supply Chain:** IBM Food Trust (Walmart mangoes), MediLedger (pharma).
- ☐ **Healthcare:** MedRec (MIT), patient data sovereignty.
- ☐ **Government:** Estonia KSI (e-residency), Sweden land registry (ChromaWay).
- ☐ **Insurance:** B3i (Blockchain Insurance Industry Initiative).
- ☐ **CBDC:** China e-CNY, Bahamas Sand Dollar — permissioned/hybrid.

### Common KTU Exam Traps
- ☐ Confusing **PoW (probabilistic finality)** with **PBFT (deterministic finality)**.
- ☐ Forgetting that **Hyperledger Fabric has NO native cryptocurrency** (no mining!).
- ☐ Saying "Bitcoin uses PBFT" — **WRONG**; Bitcoin uses **Nakamoto consensus**.
- ☐ Saying "$N \geq 2f + 1$" for PBFT — **WRONG**; correct is $N \geq 3f + 1$.
- ☐ Confusing **pool hopping** (cheating strategy) with **hash hopping** (nonexistent term).
- ☐ Treating **Corda** as a generalized blockchain — Corda is a **distributed ledger technology (DLT)** with **no global chain**, only point-to-point transactions.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SP3D":** For permissioned blockchains remember the **4 pillars**: **S**peed (high TPS), **P**rivacy (confidential channels), **P**ermission (identity-based), **D**eterminism (PBFT finality). For mining pools remember **"L-S-P"**: **L**aw of Large Numbers, **S**hares (reduced difficulty), **P**ool Hopping (PROP vulnerability).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethereum Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethereum Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of the Ethereum Network

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *The **Ethereum Network** is a decentralized, open-source blockchain platform featuring smart contract functionality. It is essentially a deterministic state machine (the **Ethereum Yellow Paper** formalization) that transitions from one valid state to another based on a globally agreed set of transactions, executed within a quasi-Turing-complete virtual environment called the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)**.*

In simpler academic terms, Ethereum extends the Bitcoin-style distributed ledger by providing a **programmable blockchain** — a global, single-instance computer that any participant can use to run applications whose state is verifiably agreed upon by every node on the network.

| Term | Meaning (Exam-Worthy) |
|---|---|
| **EVM** | The runtime environment that executes smart contract bytecode. |
| **Ether (ETH)** | The native cryptocurrency used to pay for computation (gas). |
| **Gas** | The unit measuring the computational effort required to execute an operation. |
| **Smart Contract** | Immutable code deployed at an address on the EVM. |
| **Account** | The basic unit of state — either an EOA or a Contract Account. |

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Ethereum

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU question on Ethereum architecture is essentially testing these three pillars. Memorize this triangle — it appears in almost every Module 4 paper.

1. **Decentralized Applications (DApps)** — front-end + smart contract backend.
2. **Smart Contracts** — self-executing programs on the EVM.
3. **Ethereum Blockchain** — the shared, tamper-proof ledger of state.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Global Village Computer"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a single, gigantic laptop** that belongs to no one and everyone at the same time. Any person in the world can upload a program (smart contract) to this laptop. Once uploaded, **no one — not even the original uploader — can delete or modify it**. When someone wants to run that program, they pay a tiny rent in *Ether* (the village currency) proportional to how much CPU time and memory the program consumes (*Gas*). The laptop is honest, deterministic, and lives forever — that laptop is the **EVM**, and the village is the **Ethereum Network**.

This analogy maps perfectly to the engineering view:
- The **CPU + RAM** of the laptop = **EVM**
- The **electricity bill** = **Gas fee (in ETH)**
- The **installed apps** = **Smart contracts**
- The **village's shared rulebook** = **Consensus protocol (now PoS, post-Merge)**
