> 1. **Confusing Keccak-256 with SHA-3:** Many students write "SHA-256" or "SHA3-256" when describing Ethereum's hash. Mist (and all of Ethereum) uses **Keccak-256**, the pre-NIST-finalization variant. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 2. **Forgetting the `0x04` prefix strip:** When hashing the public key, Mist strips the leading `0x04` byte (uncompressed point indicator). Hashing it *with* the prefix gives a **completely different address**. **(Lose 0.5–1 mark)**
> 3. **Confusing "full node" with "miner":** Mist is a full node but **does not mine**. It only validates blocks. Examiners may deduct marks if you claim Mist participates in PoW. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**
> 4. **Mixing up Mist and MetaMask:** Mist is *not* a browser extension. It is a **standalone desktop application**. Saying "Mist is a Chrome extension" is factually wrong. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Mist Browser = DApp browser + Ethereum wallet** developed by the Ethereum Foundation, released in **2016**, deprecated in **March 2019**.
- ✅ Built on **Electron (Chromium + Node.js)**; runs **Geth** as a backend full node.
- ✅ Communicates with Geth via **JSON-RPC** over **IPC socket** (`geth.ipc`) or **HTTP port 8545**.
- ✅ Uses **Keystore V3 (Web3 Secret Storage)** for wallet file encryption.
- ✅ **Encryption cipher:** AES-128-CTR; **KDF:** scrypt ($N=2^{18}$, $r=8$, $p=1$); **MAC:** Keccak-256 over `(derivedKey[16:32] || ciphertext)`.
- ✅ **Address derivation:** $A = \text{Keccak256}(K_{pub})[12:32]$ → 20-byte address → EIP-55 checksummed.
- ✅ **Private key = 256-bit scalar**; **Public key** = $K_{priv} \times G$ on **secp256k1**.
- ✅ **Transaction signing:** ECDSA over $\text{Keccak256}(\text{RLP}(T))$.
- ✅ **Ether units:** 1 ETH = $10^{18}$ Wei; base unit is **Wei**.
- ✅ **Why deprecated:** Massive ~1 TB blockchain sync, slow UX, security complexity, rise of MetaMask and hardware wallets.
- ✅ **Successors:** **MetaMask** (browser extension, light client), **MyEtherWallet / MyCrypto** (web-based, light client), **Ledger/Trezor** (hardware cold storage).
- ✅ **Historical significance:** Mist pioneered the **`web3` object injection** pattern that all modern DApp browsers use.
- ✅ **Full node vs. Light node** is a **trust-vs-usability tradeoff** — a frequently asked KTU conceptual question.
- ✅ Mist stored keystore files at `~/.ethereum/keystore/UTC--<date>--<address>` (Linux/macOS) or `%APPDATA%\Ethereum\keystore\` (Windows).

---

**End of Module 4 — Mist Browser Notes**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, aligned with Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L5).*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ether

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Ether

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ether (ETH)** is the **native cryptographic token (cryptocurrency)** of the **Ethereum decentralized computing platform**, used as the fundamental unit of value to compensate validators for executing transactions, deploying smart contracts, and running decentralized applications (DApps) on the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Ether is positioned as the *computational fuel* that powers every state transition on the Ethereum ledger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Verbatim):**
> *Ether is the digital asset (crypto-fuel) that incentivizes network participants and prevents infinite loops in smart contract execution by quantifying every computational step in units called **Gas**.*

Formally, Ether exists at three logical levels:

1. **As a digital currency** – a tradable asset listed on exchanges.
2. **As a unit of account** – a measure for quoting gas prices (e.g., Gwei).
3. **As a staking/incentive asset** – bonded by validators under Proof-of-Stake (post-Merge consensus).

The official specification of Ether is defined in the **Ethereum Yellow Paper (Wood, 2014)** under the *G* denomination model.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Petrol of the Ethereum Engine"

Imagine Ethereum as a **giant, decentralized world-computer** that anyone can use, but no one owns. Every time you ask this computer to do *anything* — whether sending money, executing a smart contract, or registering a deed — it costs electricity. **Ether is the "petrol" you pour into the tank of this computer.**

| Real-World Analogy | Ethereum Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Petrol station | EOA-controlled wallet funding a transaction |
| Fuel quantity (litres) | **Gas** (computational units consumed) |
| Price per litre (₹/L) | **Gas Price** in Gwei |
| Total bill = litres × price/litre | **Transaction Fee = Gas Used × Gas Price** |
| Car engine | **EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine)** |
| Driver pressing the pedal | A signed transaction broadcasting opcode execution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Ether need a "fuel" model?** If computation were free, a malicious smart contract could loop forever and stall the entire network (a *Denial-of-Service* attack). By attaching a real-world cost to every opcode, Ether economically disincentivizes infinite or wasteful computation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics, Units & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Denomination Table — Memorize for KTU Board Exam!**

The smallest indivisible unit of Ether is the **Wei** (named after cryptographer Wei Dai). All other units are powers of 10 derived from Wei:

| Unit Name | Wei Equivalent | Scientific Notation | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wei** | $1$ Wei | $10^{0}$ | Base unit (smart contract math) |
| **Kwei (Babbage)** | $10^{3}$ Wei | $10^{3}$ | — |
| **Mwei (Lovelace)** | $10^{6}$ Wei | $10^{6}$ | — |
| **Gwei (Shannon)** | $10^{9}$ Wei | $10^{9}$ | **Gas price quotations** |
| **Microether (Szabo)** | $10^{12}$ Wei | $10^{12}$ | — |
| **Milliether (Finney)** | $10^{15}$ Wei | $10^{15}$ | — |
| **Ether (ETH)** | $10^{18}$ Wei | $10^{18}$ | **Mainnet trading unit** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse *Gwei* (used for gas price) with *Giga-Wei*. **1 Gwei = $10^9$ Wei**, while 1 Ether = $10^{18}$ Wei. Always convert through the Wei base.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tripartite Architecture of Ether

Ether does not exist in isolation; it operates at the intersection of three architectural pillars that the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests.

### 2.1.1 The Account Model

Ethereum uses an **account-based model** (contrary to Bitcoin's UTXO model). There are two types of accounts:

- **Externally Owned Accounts (EOA)** — Controlled by a *private key*. Holds an Ether balance and a *nonce* (transaction counter).
- **Contract Accounts** — Controlled by *executable code* (smart contract bytecode). Holds an Ether balance, a *storage* root, and a *code hash*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Account-Based for Ethereum?** Smart contracts need persistent state. An account-based ledger maps neatly to the concept of a *stateful object* with code + storage + balance, which the EVM can manipulate atomically.

### 2.1.2 The State Transition Function

Each block consumed by the EVM applies a **state transition function** $\mathcal{Y}$ that converts one valid world state into the next:

$$\mathcal{Y}(S, T) \rightarrow S'$$

where:
- $S$ = current world state (mapping of all $2^{160}$ addresses to account states)
- $T$ = the new block of valid transactions
- $S'$ = the resulting new world state

---

## 2.2 The Gas Mechanism — The Engine of Ether Economics

### 2.2.1 Why Gas Exists

Ethereum opcodes are not free. Each low-level EVM instruction (e.g., `ADD`, `MSTORE`, `SHA3`) has a **predefined gas cost** hardcoded in the protocol. A simple transfer consumes **21,000 gas**, while a complex Uniswap swap may consume **>250,000 gas**.

### 2.2.2 The EIP-1559 Fee Market

After the **London Hard Fork (August 2021)**, Ethereum adopted **EIP-1559**, replacing the old first-price auction with a hybrid mechanism featuring:

- A dynamically adjusting **base fee** (burned, not paid to validators).
- An optional **priority tip** (incentive for validators).
- A variable **block size** (max 2× the gas target of **15 million gas/block**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:** The base fee is *burned* (destroyed) — this is Ethereum's deflationary mechanism. Combined with staking issuance, this produces a fluctuating net Ether supply.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — All Key Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **canonical cheat sheet** for solving numerical problems on Ether in the KTU University Exam. Master every row.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units | Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ether Denomination Conversion** | $N_{\text{ETH}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{Wei}}}{10^{18}}$ | ETH | $N_{\text{Wei}}$ = number of Wei |
| 2 | **Total Transaction Fee (Legacy)** | $\text{TxFee} = G_{\text{used}} \times P_{\text{gas}}$ | Wei | $G_{\text{used}}$ = gas consumed, $P_{\text{gas}}$ = gas price in Wei |
| 3 | **Total Transaction Fee (EIP-1559)** | $\text{TxFee} = G_{\text{used}} \times (B + T)$ | Wei | $B$ = base fee, $T$ = priority tip (both in Wei/gas) |
| 4 | **Wei to Gwei** | $N_{\text{Gwei}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{Wei}}}{10^{9}}$ | Gwei | — |
| 5 | **Base Fee Adjustment (EIP-1559)** | $B_{n+1} = B_n + \left\lfloor \dfrac{B_n \times (G_{\text{block}} - G_{\text{target}})}{G_{\text{target}} \times 8} \right\rfloor$ | Wei/gas | $G_{\text{block}}$ = gas used in last block; $G_{\text{target}} = 15 \times 10^6$ |
| 6 | **Block Gas Limit** | $G_{\text{limit}} = 2 \times G_{\text{target}} = 30 \times 10^{6}$ | gas | Hard upper bound per block |
| 7 | **Max Fee Per Gas (User Cap)** | $\text{MaxFee} = B_{n+1} + T$ | Wei/gas | User-specified ceiling to bound spending |
| 8 | **Validator Reward (Beacon Chain)** | $R = C + T_{\text{tip}} - P$ | Wei | $C$ = consensus reward, $P$ = penalty (slashing) |
| 9 | **Staking Requirement (Validator)** | $V_{\text{stake}} = 32 \text{ ETH}$ | ETH | Minimum bond to run a validator |
| 10 | **State Transition** | $\mathcal{Y}(S, T) \rightarrow S'$ | — | Maps world state $S$ to $S'$ via transactions $T$ |
| 11 | **Address Space Cardinality** | $\vert A \vert = 2^{160}$ | accounts | All possible 20-byte Ethereum addresses |
| 12 | **Account Nonce Increment** | $n_{i+1} = n_i + 1$ | integer | Prevents replay attacks |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Integrity Rule:** All absolute-value bars are escaped as `\vert` to preserve table rendering. Never use raw `|` inside table cells.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Ether gas model has direct analogues in **queueing theory**, **cloud computing pricing**, and **embedded systems scheduling**:

- **Cloud Computing** — AWS Lambda charges per GB-second; Ethereum charges per *gas unit*. Both are **pay-per-instruction** pricing models.
- **Resource-Starved Scheduling** — Gas limits on blocks function as a **CPU quota per slot**, similar to time-sharing OS schedulers.
- **Anti-Spam Economics** — The cost of a transaction is a *micropayment barrier* against Sybil flooding, the same principle behind Postmark email-spam prevention.

> [!NOTE]
> The 21,000 gas for a simple ETH transfer is not arbitrary; it is the sum of baseline transaction overhead (20,000 gas for intrinsic transaction cost) plus 2,100 gas × 10 (calldata bytes for a simple transfer), giving exactly 21,000 gas.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting Between Ether, Gwei, and Wei

The Ethereum denomination system is purely **decimal** (powers of 10), unlike Bitcoin's binary (powers of 8) satoshi system. We will derive the three most common conversions used in KTU numerical questions.

### 3.1.1 Given Data
Suppose a wallet displays a balance of $B = 3.275$ ETH, and the current gas price is $P = 47$ Gwei. The user wants to know the **total Wei** represented by 1 Ether.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Establish the Base Unit**

By definition, the smallest denomination of Ether is the **Wei**, and 1 Ether is defined as exactly $10^{18}$ Wei.

$$1 \; \text{ETH} = 10^{18} \; \text{Wei}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Gwei Relationship**

Since 1 Gwei = $10^{9}$ Wei, we can write the Gwei-to-ETH relationship by dividing powers of 10:

$$1 \; \text{ETH} = 10^{18} \; \text{Wei} = 10^{18-9} \times 10^{9} \; \text{Wei} = 10^{9} \; \text{Gwei}$$

Therefore:

$$1 \; \text{ETH} = 10^{9} \; \text{Gwei}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Conversion Function**

For any amount $N_{\text{ETH}}$ in Ether, the equivalent Wei value is:

$$N_{\text{Wei}} = N_{\text{ETH}} \times 10^{18}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Application**

Using $B = 3.275$ ETH:

$$N_{\text{Wei}} = 3.275 \times 10^{18} = 3.275 \times 10^{18} \; \text{Wei}$$

Convert to Gwei by dividing by $10^9$:

$$N_{\text{Gwei}} = \dfrac{3.275 \times 10^{18}}{10^{9}} = 3.275 \times 10^{9} \; \text{Gwei}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Multiply the Gwei result by $10^9$ to recover Wei: $3.275 \times 10^9 \times 10^9 = 3.275 \times 10^{18}$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: EIP-1559 Base Fee Update

The EIP-1559 base fee algorithm is a discrete-time feedback controller. The official formula is:

$$B_{n+1} = B_n \times \left(1 + \dfrac{G_{\text{block}} - G_{\text{target}}}{G_{\text{target}} \times 8}\right)$$

The floor function ensures it rounds to integer Wei. We will derive this step-by-step.

### 3.2.1 Setup

Let:
- $B_n$ = current block's base fee (Wei/gas)
- $B_{n+1}$ = next block's base fee
- $G_{\text{block}}$ = actual gas used in current block
- $G_{\text{target}} = 15 \times 10^6$ gas (network target)
- $D = 8$ = maximum per-block adjustment denominator

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the Congestion Ratio**

Define the deviation from target:

$$\Delta = \dfrac{G_{\text{block}} - G_{\text{target}}}{G_{\text{target}}}$$

If $G_{\text{block}} > G_{\text{target}}$ (congestion), $\Delta > 0$; base fee rises.
If $G_{\text{block}} < G_{\text{target}}$ (under-utilization), $\Delta < 0$; base fee falls.

**Step 2 — Bound the Maximum Change**

To prevent wild swings, divide by $D = 8$, bounding the per-block change to $\pm 12.5\%$:

$$\Delta_{\text{capped}} = \dfrac{\Delta}{8} = \dfrac{G_{\text{block}} - G_{\text{target}}}{8 \times G_{\text{target}}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Multiplicative Update**

The new base fee is the old base fee times $(1 + \Delta_{\text{capped}})$:

$$B_{n+1} = B_n \times \left(1 + \Delta_{\text{capped}}\right) = B_n \times \left(1 + \dfrac{G_{\text{block}} - G_{\text{target}}}{8 \times G_{\text{target}}}\right)$$

Rounding down to integer Wei gives the final EIP-1559 rule:

$$B_{n+1} = \left\lfloor B_n \times \left(1 + \dfrac{G_{\text{block}} - G_{\text{target}}}{8 \times G_{\text{target}}}\right) \right\rfloor$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example

Suppose $B_n = 30$ Gwei ($= 30 \times 10^9$ Wei/gas), and the previous block used $G_{\text{block}} = 22.5 \times 10^6$ gas (50% above the 15M target).

$$\Delta_{\text{capped}} = \dfrac{22.5 \times 10^6 - 15 \times 10^6}{8 \times 15 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 10^6}{120 \times 10^6} = 0.0625$$

$$B_{n+1} = 30 \times 10^9 \times (1 + 0.0625) = 30 \times 10^9 \times 1.0625 = 31.875 \times 10^9 \; \text{Wei/gas} \approx 31.875 \; \text{Gwei}$$

This matches the documented 12.5% maximum upward step. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Legacy vs. EIP-1559 Fee Markets

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is a *high-yield revision tool* for KTU Part B questions comparing transaction fee mechanisms.

| Parameter | Legacy Gas Auction (Pre-EIP-1559) | EIP-1559 (Post-London Fork) |
|---|---|---|
| **Pricing Mechanism** | First-price blind auction | Base fee + priority tip |
| **Fee Predictability** | Poor; user must guess | Excellent; base fee is protocol-public |
| **Base Fee Disposition** | Paid fully to miner | **Burned** (removed from supply) |
| **Tip Disposition** | Full payment to miner | Paid to validator as priority incentive |
| **Block Size** | Fixed gas limit (e.g., 12.5M) | Variable up to 2× target (15M → 30M) |
| **Overpayment Risk** | High | Low (with `MaxFeePerGas` cap) |
| **Monetary Policy Effect** | Inflationary under high demand | **Deflationary** when base fee burn > issuance |
| **User Transaction Syntax** | `gasPrice` field | `maxFeePerGas` + `maxPriorityFeePerGas` fields |
| **Default Wallet Behavior** | Send `gasPrice = 20 Gwei` | Send `MaxFee = base + 2 Gwei`, `Tip = 2 Gwei` |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Account Type Comparison

| Property | EOA (Externally Owned Account) | Contract Account |
|---|---|---|
| **Private Key** | ✓ Yes (controls signing) | ✗ No (code-controlled) |
| **Code Execution** | Triggers only | Always executes on call |
| **Initiates Transactions** | ✓ Yes | ✗ No (only reacts to incoming calls) |
| **Storage** | Empty by default | Persistent contract storage |
| **Ether Balance** | Yes | Yes |
| **Nonce** | Transaction count | Contract creation count |
| **Creation** | Free (off-chain keypair) | Costs gas (deploy tx) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Diagram — Ether's Role in the Ethereum Stack

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how Ether flows through the Ethereum stack — from user wallets to validators, and how it converts into gas that executes EVM opcodes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User A EOA Wallet] -->|Signs Tx with Private Key| txpool[Public Mempool]
    userB[User B EOA Wallet] -->|Signs Tx with Private Key| txpool
    userC[Contract Account] -->|Internal call triggers| txpool

    txpool -->|Validator picks| evmexec[EVM Execution Engine]

    evmexec -->|Charges| gasengine[Gas Accounting Module]
    gasengine -->|Reads Opcode Gas Cost| oplookup[Opcode Gas Table 21K for transfer]
    gasengine -->|Computes| feeformula[TxFee = Gas Used x BaseFee + Tip]

    feeformula -->|Burns BaseFee| burnpool[Burned Supply Sink]
    feeformula -->|Pays Tip| validator[Selected Validator Staking Pool]

    validator -->|Ether 32 ETH stake| beaconchain[Beacon Chain PoS Consensus]
    beaconchain -->|Attestations| finality[Finality Checkpoint Casper FFG]
    finality -->|Updates| worldstate[World State root in Patricia Trie]

    worldstate -->|Persists block| blockchain[EVM-Compatible Blockchain]

    userA -. Balance update .-> worldstate
    userB -. Balance update .-> worldstate
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The top of the diagram shows transaction *origins* (EOAs and contracts). The middle is the *EVM engine* and *gas accountant*. The bottom shows *where the fee money goes* (split between burning and the validator) and how the *world state* is updated. The dotted lines indicate asynchronous balance updates reflected back to wallets.

---

## 4.2 State Transition Flow Diagram

The following diagram shows the *complete lifecycle* of a single Ether transaction from broadcast to finality.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([User Initiates Tx]) --> noncecheck{Nonce valid and == current}
    noncecheck -- No --> reject([Reject: Replacement Underpriced])
    noncecheck -- Yes --> balancecheck{Balance >= Value + MaxFee x GasLimit}
    balancecheck -- No --> reject2([Reject: Insufficient Funds])
    balancecheck -- Yes --> sign{Sign with EOA Private Key}
    sign --> broadcast[Broadcast to P2P Network]
    broadcast --> mempool[Enters Mempool]
    mempool --> validator[Validator Selection VRF + RANDAO]
    validator --> exec[EVM Executes Tx on Local State Copy]
    exec -->|Gas consumed?| within[Gas Used <= GasLimit]
    within -- No --> revert([Revert but Tx Fee Still Paid])
    within -- Yes --> success([State Updated Successfully])
    success --> newstate[New World State Root in Block]
    revert --> newstate
    newstate --> attest[Other Validators Attest]
    attest --> finality[Finality after 2 Epochs ~12.8 min]
```

---

## 4.3 Ether Denomination Tree (Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ether[1 ETH = 10^18 Wei] --> finney[1 Finney = 10^15 Wei = 0.001 ETH]
    ether --> szabo[1 Szabo = 10^12 Wei = 0.000001 ETH]
    ether --> gwei[1 Gwei = 10^9 Wei]
    ether --> lovelace[1 Lovelace = 10^6 Wei = 1 Mwei]
    ether --> babbage[1 Babbage = 10^3 Wei = 1 Kwei]
    ether --> wei[1 Wei = 10^0 Wei]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — EIP-1559 Fee Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userfee[User Sets MaxFeePerGas and Tip] --> maxcap{MaxFeePerGas >= BaseFee + Tip}
    maxcap -- No --> err[Transaction Invalid Rejected by Mempool]
    maxcap -- Yes --> effective[Effective Gas Price = BaseFee + Tip]
    effective --> burncalc[Burned Amount = BaseFee x GasUsed]
    effective --> tipcalc[Validator Tip = Tip x GasUsed]
    burncalc --> burnaddr[Sent to 0x000...000 Dead Address]
    tipcalc --> validatordeliver[Credited to Proposing Validator]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the burner address?** The base fee is *minted into non-existence* by sending it to a provably unspendable address. This is a clean, verifiable, on-chain burn that reduces total Ether supply.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Define Ether. Why is it often called the "crypto-fuel" of the Ethereum network?**

**Model Answer:**

Ether (ETH) is the native cryptocurrency of the Ethereum blockchain, defined in the Ethereum Yellow Paper as a transferable digital asset denominated in Wei ($10^{18}$ Wei = 1 ETH). It is termed *crypto-fuel* because it is used to pay for the computation executed by the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)**. Every opcode executed consumes a quantified amount of **gas**, and gas is purchased using Ether. This dual-layer pricing (gas × price) creates a real-world economic cost for computation, preventing infinite loops, resource exhaustion, and denial-of-service attacks. Ether thus monetizes computational resources in a trustless, decentralized manner, enabling the secure execution of smart contracts and decentralized applications. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any four denominations of Ether and state one use of Gwei in the Ethereum network.**

**Model Answer:**

The four denominations of Ether are:
1. **Wei** — the base unit, equal to $10^0$ Wei; used internally in smart contracts for all arithmetic.
2. **Gwei (Shannon)** — equal to $10^9$ Wei; used to quote **gas prices** in wallets and block explorers.
3. **Finney (Milliether)** — equal to $10^{15}$ Wei; used historically for micro-pricing.
4. **Ether (ETH)** — equal to $10^{18}$ Wei; the mainnet trading unit listed on exchanges.

**Use of Gwei:** Gwei is the standard unit for quoting the **base fee and priority tip** under the EIP-1559 fee market, as a typical gas price lies in the range of 1–200 Gwei, which is human-readable, unlike raw Wei. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Pattern]**

**A.** (a) Explain the **EIP-1559 fee mechanism** in detail. Derive the formula for the base-fee update and show the disposition of the fee (burn vs. validator tip). [**7 marks**]
 (b) A user sends a transaction that consumes **85,000 gas**. The current **base fee is 25 Gwei** and the user adds a **priority tip of 2 Gwei**. Compute: (i) the **effective gas price**, (ii) the **total transaction fee** in Wei, Gwei, and ETH, and (iii) the **amount burned**. [**7 marks**]

#### Part A(a) — Model Solution

The **EIP-1559 (London Hard Fork)** fee mechanism replaced Ethereum's first-price auction with a hybrid system comprising a **base fee** and a **priority tip**. The base fee is **protocol-determined** and **burned**, while the priority tip goes to the validator.

**Base Fee Derivation:** Let $B_n$ be the current base fee, $G_{\text{block}}$ the gas used in the previous block, and $G_{\text{target}} = 15 \times 10^6$. The update rule is:

$$B_{n+1} = B_n \times \left(1 + \dfrac{G_{\text{block}} - G_{\text{target}}}{8 \times G_{\text{target}}}\right)$$

The denominator 8 limits the per-block change to a maximum of $\pm 12.5\%$, ensuring fee stability. **Disposition:** The base fee is **burned** (sent to address 0x0), permanently removing ETH from supply; this introduces a deflationary pressure. The **priority tip** is credited to the validator as an incentive for transaction inclusion. The user sets `maxFeePerGas` as a ceiling; any difference between the max fee and the actual effective price is refunded to the user. This mechanism improves fee predictability, reduces overpayment, and makes Ether potentially deflationary during high network usage. **(7 marks)**

#### Part A(b) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $G_{\text{used}} = 85{,}000$ gas
- $B = 25$ Gwei
- $T = 2$ Gwei

**(i) Effective Gas Price:**

$$\text{Effective} = B + T = 25 + 2 = 27 \text{ Gwei/gas}$$

**(ii) Total Transaction Fee:**

$$\text{TxFee} = G_{\text{used}} \times \text{Effective Price} = 85{,}000 \times 27 = 2{,}295{,}000 \text{ Gwei}$$

Convert to Wei (multiply by $10^9$):

$$\text{TxFee}_{\text{Wei}} = 2{,}295{,}000 \times 10^9 = 2.295 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei}$$

Convert to ETH (divide by $10^{18}$):

$$\text{TxFee}_{\text{ETH}} = \dfrac{2.295 \times 10^{15}}{10^{18}} = 2.295 \times 10^{-3} = 0.002295 \text{ ETH}$$

**(iii) Burned Amount (Base Fee × Gas Used):**

$$\text{Burned} = 85{,}000 \times 25 = 2{,}125{,}000 \text{ Gwei} = 2.125 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei} = 0.002125 \text{ ETH}$$

The validator receives the **tip portion**:

$$\text{Tip} = 85{,}000 \times 2 = 170{,}000 \text{ Gwei} = 0.00017 \text{ ETH}$$ **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[Internal Choice Alternative]**

**B.** (a) Differentiate between **EOA** and **Contract Accounts** in Ethereum. Explain the **state transition function** $\mathcal{Y}(S, T) \rightarrow S'$ with a labeled diagram. [**7 marks**]
 (b) A user initiates a transaction requiring a **maximum gas limit of 200,000 gas** at a **max fee per gas of 60 Gwei** and a **priority tip of 3 Gwei**. If the actual base fee is **52 Gwei** and the transaction uses **137,000 gas**, compute: (i) the **effective gas price**, (ii) the **total fee paid**, (iii) the **refund amount**, and (iv) the **amount burned**. [**7 marks**]

#### Part B(a) — Model Solution

| Property | EOA | Contract Account |
|---|---|---|
| Controlled by | Private key | Contract code |
| Can initiate tx | Yes | No |
| Has code | No | Yes (immutable once deployed) |
| Has storage | Optional | Yes (persistent) |
| Has nonce | Tx count | Contract creation count |

**State Transition Function:**

The Ethereum world state is a mapping of all $2^{160}$ addresses to account states. When a block $T$ of valid transactions is applied, the protocol computes:

$$\mathcal{Y}(S, T) \rightarrow S'$$

where $S$ is the pre-state and $S'$ is the post-state. Each transaction updates the balances, nonces, and storage of the touched accounts, producing a new Merkle Patricia Trie root. The diagram in Section 4.2 illustrates this flow. **(7 marks)**

#### Part B(b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $G_{\text{limit}} = 200{,}000$, $\text{MaxFee} = 60$ Gwei, $T = 3$ Gwei, $B = 52$ Gwei, $G_{\text{used}} = 137{,}000$.

**(i) Effective Gas Price:** The user pays the lower of `MaxFee` and `BaseFee + Tip`:

$$\min(60, 52 + 3) = \min(60, 55) = 55 \text{ Gwei/gas}$$

**(ii) Total Fee Paid:**

$$137{,}000 \times 55 = 7{,}535{,}000 \text{ Gwei} = 7.535 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei} = 0.007535 \text{ ETH}$$

**(iii) Refund:** The user pre-authorized up to $137{,}000 \times 60 = 8{,}220{,}000$ Gwei, so:

$$\text{Refund} = 8{,}220{,}000 - 7{,}535{,}000 = 685{,}000 \text{ Gwei} = 6.85 \times 10^{14} \text{ Wei} = 0.000685 \text{ ETH}$$

**(iv) Burned:** Base fee × gas used:

$$137{,}000 \times 52 = 7{,}124{,}000 \text{ Gwei} = 0.007124 \text{ ETH}$$ **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Gwei with Wei:** Many students forget to multiply by $10^9$ when converting to Wei. Always show the unit alongside the number.
> 2. **Forgetting the Refund in EIP-1559:** EIP-1559 *refunds* the difference between `maxFeePerGas` and the actual effective price. Students often compute the total as `MaxFee × GasUsed`, which is the *worst-case* scenario, not the *actual* fee.
> 3. **Mixing up Burned vs. Paid:** The base fee is **burned** (lost forever); only the **tip** goes to the validator. Mixing these two destroys the calculation.
> 4. **Wrong Formula for Base Fee Update:** Some students use additive ($+12.5\%$) instead of multiplicative. Memorize the multiplicative form: $B_{n+1} = B_n(1 + \Delta/8)$.
> 5. **Skipping Unit Conversion in Numericals:** KTU examiners award the *final* mark only if the units (Wei/Gwei/ETH) are stated. Always finish with "= X ETH".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin This on Your Wall!**

- ✅ **Ether (ETH)** is the **native cryptocurrency of Ethereum**, used to pay for computation (gas).
- ✅ **Smallest unit = Wei** ($10^0$); **1 ETH = $10^{18}$ Wei**; **1 Gwei = $10^{9}$ Wei** (used for gas price quotes).
- ✅ **Gas** is the *unit of computational effort*; each EVM opcode has a fixed gas cost (e.g., `ADD` = 3, `MSTORE` = 6, `SHA3` = 30+).
- ✅ A simple ETH transfer consumes exactly **21,000 gas** (intrinsic transaction cost).
- ✅ **Ethereum uses an account-based model** (not UTXO), with two account types: **EOA** (key-controlled) and **Contract Account** (code-controlled).
- ✅ **EIP-1559** replaced first-price auctions with a **base fee (burned) + priority tip (to validator)** model.
- ✅ **Base fee** adjusts ±12.5% per block based on congestion vs. the 15M gas target.
- ✅ **Validators must stake 32 ETH** under the post-Merge Proof-of-Stake consensus.
- ✅ **State transition** is the formal function $\mathcal{Y}(S, T) \rightarrow S'$, mapping pre-state to post-state via transactions.
- ✅ **Deflationary effect:** Base-fee burn can exceed new issuance during high demand, reducing total ETH supply.
- ✅ **Address space:** All Ethereum addresses are 20 bytes (160 bits), giving $2^{160}$ possible addresses.
- ✅ **Block gas limit:** 30 million gas (2× the 15M target), allowing variable block sizes.
- ✅ **Transaction field names (post-EIP-1559):** `maxFeePerGas`, `maxPriorityFeePerGas`, `gasLimit`, `to`, `value`, `data`, `nonce`.
- ✅ **Ether was pre-mined** with a 72 million ETH genesis supply, of which 12M were allocated to the development team/foundation.
- ✅ **Units named after cryptographers:** Wei Dai, Nick Szabo, Hal Finney, Ada Lovelace, Charles Babbage, Claude Shannon.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Solidity** is a high-level, object-oriented, statically-typed programming language specifically designed for writing **smart contracts** that run on the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)**. It was proposed in August 2014 by Gavin Wood, and its development is led by the Ethereum project team. The language supports inheritance, libraries, and complex user-defined types, and its syntax is heavily influenced by JavaScript, C++, and Python.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> Solidity forms the **contractual backbone** of Ethereum-based decentralized applications (DApps). Every DeFi protocol, NFT marketplace, and DAO (Decentralized Autonomous Organization) deployed on EVM-compatible chains is written in Solidity or a Solidity-derived language.

> [!NOTE]
> **EVM-Compatibility Statement**
> Solidity compiles to **EVM bytecode** and is **not Turing-incomplete by default** — it achieves Turing-completeness through its metering system called **Gas** (unit: **gwei / wei**, where $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ wei}$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Vending Machine"

Imagine a vending machine that you program once with a strict set of rules:

- **Smart Contract (Solidity Code)** = The internal circuitry of the machine.
- **State Variables** = The inventory and balance stored inside the machine.
- **Functions** = The buttons a customer can press.
- **Modifiers** = The security guards that check if the customer is allowed to press a button (e.g., sufficient coins inserted, correct age verified).
- **EVM** = The actual physical machine executing the logic.
- **Gas** = The electricity consumed; you must pay for the energy used, and the machine halts if you run out.

Just as a vending machine cannot lie about its inventory, a Solidity contract is **immutable once deployed** and its logic is enforced by every node in the Ethereum network.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Board Recall)

- **Solidity Version Used in 2024**: $\geq 0.8.x$ (built-in overflow/underflow protection).
- **EVM Word Size**: **256 bits** ($32$ bytes).
- **Wei**: $1$ wei $= 10^{-18}$ ETH (smallest unit).
- **Gas Limit (Block)**: **30,000,000** units.
- **Contract Deployment Cost**: Typically **$\geq 53,000$ gas** for a basic contract.
- **Source File Extension**: `.sol`
- **Compiler**: `solc` (Solidity compiler).

> [!TIP]
> Always declare a `pragma` directive in your Solidity file: `pragma solidity ^0.8.0;` — this tells the compiler which version to use. KTU examiners frequently test this line.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Solidity Smart Contract

A Solidity source file (`.sol`) is structured as a collection of the following components, each with a distinct role:

### 2.1.1 Pragma Directive
The `pragma` line specifies the compiler version. It is **mandatory** and appears at the top of every file.

### 2.1.2 State Variables
Variables permanently stored on the blockchain. They consume **storage slots** (each slot is **256 bits**). They form the **persistent memory** of the contract.

### 2.1.3 Functions
Executable units of code. They can be:
- **Read-only** (`view` / `pure`).
- **State-modifying** (default).
- **Payable** (can receive Ether).

### 2.1.4 Function Modifiers
Reusable pre-condition checks attached to functions. They enforce access control, validation, and reentrancy guards.

### 2.1.5 Events
Logging mechanisms that allow off-chain applications to listen for contract activity. They are declared with the `event` keyword and emitted using `emit`.

### 2.1.6 Structs, Enums, and Mappings
User-defined complex types that allow the contract to model real-world entities (e.g., a `User` struct, a `Status` enum, or a `mapping(address => uint256)` balance table).

## 2.2 Solidity Data Types — The "Why" and "How"

Solidity is **statically typed**, meaning the type of every variable must be declared at compile time. Types fall into two major categories:

### 2.2.1 Value Types
These are passed by value — copying creates an independent clone.
- **Booleans**: `bool` → `true` or `false`.
- **Integers**: `int8` to `int256` (signed) and `uint8` to `uint256` (unsigned). Default: `int` is shorthand for `int256`.
- **Address**: Holds a 20-byte Ethereum address. `address payable` can receive Ether.
- **Bytes**: Fixed-size `bytes1` to `bytes32`, plus dynamic `bytes`.
- **Enums**: User-defined discrete sets (e.g., `enum Status { Pending, Active, Closed }`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "256 bits"?** The EVM is a 256-bit machine. Choosing 256-bit integers maximizes efficiency because most EVM operations are natively 256-bit.

### 2.2.2 Reference Types
These are passed by reference — copying passes a pointer, not the data.
- **Strings**: Dynamic UTF-8 encoded data (`string`).
- **Arrays**: Fixed (`uint[5]`) or dynamic (`uint[]`). Can be `memory` or `storage`.
- **Structs**: Custom groupings of variables.
- **Mappings**: Hash-table-like key-value stores — `mapping(KeyType => ValueType)`.

> [!WARNING]
> Mappings **cannot be iterated** in Solidity. This is a frequent KTU exam question.

## 2.3 Function Visibility — Who Can Call Me?

Solidity functions have **four visibility specifiers**:

| Specifier | Callable From | Inheritance? | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| `public` | Anywhere (internal + external) | Yes | **"P" — Public Park** |
| `external` | Only outside the contract | No | **"E" — Exit only** |
| `internal` | This contract + derived contracts | Yes | **"I" — Inside family** |
| `private` | Only this contract | No | **"P" — Personal vault** |

## 2.4 Function State Mutability — What Can I Do?

| Mutability | Reads State? | Modifies State? | Receives Ether? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pure` | No | No | No |
| `view` | Yes | No | No |
| (default) | Yes | Yes | No |
| `payable` | Yes | Yes | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `pure` and `view` functions **do not require gas** when called externally (read-only calls). This is a high-yield KTU concept.

## 2.5 Gas Economics — The Mathematical Foundation

Every EVM operation has an associated **gas cost**. The total transaction fee paid by the user is:

$$
\text{TxFee} = \text{GasUsed} \times \text{GasPrice}
$$

where:
- $\text{GasUsed} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{opcode}_i \text{ cost})$
- $\text{GasPrice}$ is measured in **gwei** ($1 \text{ gwei} = 10^9 \text{ wei}$).

| Operation | Approximate Gas Cost | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| `SSTORE` (write to storage) | **20,000** (new slot) | Most expensive EVM op |
| `SLOAD` (read from storage) | **2,100** (warm) / **2,000** (cold) | Cold storage = costly |
| Addition (`ADD`) | **3** | Cheap arithmetic |
| Contract deployment | **32,000 + 200 × code bytes** | Base deployment cost |
| Transaction base | **21,000** | Floor for any tx |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Syntax | Memory Location | Gas Behavior | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Integer | `uint256` | Storage / Memory | Cheap arithmetic | High |
| Address | `address` | Storage | 20 bytes | High |
| Mapping | `mapping(K => V)` | Storage only | No iteration | High |
| Array (dynamic) | `uint[]` | Storage / Memory | `push` = 22,000 gas | Medium |
| String | `string` | Storage / Memory | Variable | Low |
| Payable function | `function() public payable` | — | Accepts Ether | High |
| `view` function | `function() public view` | — | Free externally | High |
| `pure` function | `function() public pure` | — | Free externally | High |
| Event emission | `emit EventName(args)` | Logs (not storage) | 375 gas + 375/log | Medium |
| Inheritance | `contract B is A` | Code reuse | Reduces deployment | Medium |
| Modifier | `modifier onlyOwner() { require(...); _; }` | — | Validates before fn | High |
| `require` | `require(condition, "msg")` | Reverts on false | Refunds unused gas | High |
| `assert` | `assert(condition)` | Reverts on false | Burns all gas | Medium |
| `revert` | `revert("msg")` | Reverts | Refunds unused gas | Medium |
| Fallback | `fallback() external payable` | Triggered on miss | Optional | Medium |
| Receive | `receive() external payable` | Plain ETH transfers | Optional | Low |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: Solidity powers over **$50 billion USD** in Total Value Locked (TVL) across DeFi protocols as of 2024. Mastering its gas optimization patterns is a direct path to becoming an Ethereum smart-contract auditor.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Derivation: How Gas Cost is Computed for a Storage Write

Consider a simple Solidity assignment: `balances[msg.sender] = 100;`

The EVM executes the following sequence, each step billed in gas:

$$
\text{TotalGas} = G_{\text{base}} + G_{\text{op}} \times N_{\text{op}} + G_{\text{storage}}
$$

**Step 1 — Transaction Base Cost**
Every transaction pays a flat base fee.

$$
G_{\text{base}} = 21{,}000 \text{ gas}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Keccak-256 Hash of the Storage Slot**
Solidity computes the storage slot for `mapping(address => uint256) balances[msg.sender]` by hashing the key with the slot index.

$$
\text{slot} = \text{keccak256}(\text{key} \cdot \text{slotIndex})
$$

This requires an `SHA3` opcode costing **30 gas** (base) + **6 gas per word** (for the input data). For a single address (32 bytes aligned to 64 bytes after padding), the cost is:

$$
G_{\text{hash}} = 30 + (6 \times 2) = 42 \text{ gas}
$$

**Step 3 — Read the Storage Slot (`SLOAD`)**
If the slot is cold (first access in the transaction):

$$
G_{\text{SLOAD, cold}} = 2{,}100 \text{ gas}
$$

If the slot is warm (accessed earlier in the same transaction):

$$
G_{\text{SLOAD, warm}} = 100 \text{ gas}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the Storage Slot (`SSTORE`)**
For a **new slot** (the value goes from zero to non-zero):

$$
G_{\text{SSTORE, new}} = 20{,}000 \text{ gas}
$$

For an **existing slot** (value updates):

$$
G_{\text{SSTORE, update}} = 5{,}000 \text{ gas (warm) or 2{,}900 (cold original)}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the Total**

$$
G_{\text{total}} = 21{,}000 + 42 + 2{,}100 + 20{,}000 = 43{,}142 \text{ gas}
$$

Thus, the user pays:

$$
\text{TxFee} = 43{,}142 \times \text{GasPrice (in gwei)} \times 10^{-9} \text{ ETH}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is precisely why Solidity developers pack multiple variables into a single 256-bit storage slot — to amortize the **20,000-gas** `SSTORE` cost.

## 3.2 Derivation: Function Selector and ABI Encoding

When an external party calls a Solidity function, the function must be uniquely identified by its **4-byte selector**.

**Step 1 — Compute the Keccak-256 Hash of the Function Signature**
Take the canonical signature (function name + parenthesized parameter types).

$$
H = \text{keccak256}(\text{"transfer(address,uint256)"})
$$

**Step 2 — Take the First 4 Bytes**

$$
\text{selector} = H[0{:}4]
$$

**Step 3 — Pack the Arguments (ABI Encoding)**
Each argument is padded to 32 bytes and concatenated.

$$
\text{calldata} = \text{selector} \mid\mid \text{arg}_1 \text{ (padded)} \mid\mid \text{arg}_2 \text{ (padded)} \mid\mid \cdots
$$

**Step 4 — EVM Dispatch**
The EVM compares the first 4 bytes of `calldata` to the selectors of all `external`/`public` functions. On match, it jumps to that function's bytecode entry point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Two functions with the same name and parameter types but different return types are **NOT allowed** in Solidity — they share the same selector. This is a guaranteed board question.

## 3.3 Walkthrough: A Full Solidity Smart Contract — Line by Line

The following annotated contract is the canonical "Ballot" example. Every line is explained for KTU 14-mark answers.

```solidity
// Step 1: Pragma — compiler version lock
pragma solidity ^0.8.0;

// Step 2: Contract declaration
contract Ballot {

    // Step 3: Struct — custom data type for a voter
    struct Voter {
        uint weight;
        bool voted;
        address delegate;
        uint vote;
    }

    // Step 4: Struct — proposal to be voted on
    struct Proposal {
        bytes32 name;
        uint voteCount;
    }

    // Step 5: State variable — chairperson is immutable
    address public chairperson;

    // Step 6: Mapping — voter registry keyed by address
    mapping(address => Voter) public voters;

    // Step 7: Dynamic array — list of proposals
    Proposal[] public proposals;

    // Step 8: Constructor — runs once at deployment
    constructor(bytes32[] memory proposalNames) {
        chairperson = msg.sender;
        voters[chairperson].weight = 1;

        // Step 9: Loop to initialize proposals
        for (uint i = 0; i < proposalNames.length; i++) {
            proposals.push(Proposal({
                name: proposalNames[i],
                voteCount: 0
            }));
        }
    }

    // Step 10: Modifier — only chairperson can grant rights
    modifier onlyChairperson() {
        require(msg.sender == chairperson, "Not authorized");
        _;  // Continue with function body
    }

    // Step 11: Function to give voting rights
    function giveRightToVote(address voter) public onlyChairperson {
        require(!voters[voter].voted, "Already voted");
        require(voters[voter].weight == 0, "Already has rights");
        voters[voter].weight = 1;
    }

    // Step 12: Function to delegate vote (delegation)
    function delegate(address to) public {
        Voter storage sender = voters[msg.sender];
        require(!sender.voted, "Already voted");
        require(to != msg.sender, "Self-delegation forbidden");
        require(voters[to].weight > 0 || voters[to].voted, "Delegate invalid");

        // Cycle detection
        address current = to;
        while (voters[current].delegate != address(0)) {
            current = voters[current].delegate;
            require(current != msg.sender, "Cycle detected");
        }

        sender.voted = true;
        sender.delegate = to;

        if (voters[to].voted) {
            proposals[voters[to].vote].voteCount += sender.weight;
        } else {
            voters[to].weight += sender.weight;
        }
    }

    // Step 13: Function to cast a vote
    function vote(uint proposal) public {
        Voter storage sender = voters[msg.sender];
        require(sender.weight > 0, "No voting right");
        require(!sender.voted, "Already voted");
        sender.voted = true;
        sender.vote = proposal;
        proposals[proposal].voteCount += sender.weight;
    }

    // Step 14: View function — returns winning proposal
    function winningProposal() public view returns (uint winningProposal_) {
        uint winningVoteCount = 0;
        for (uint p = 0; p < proposals.length; p++) {
            if (proposals[p].voteCount > winningVoteCount) {
                winningVoteCount = proposals[p].voteCount;
                winningProposal_ = p;
            }
        }
    }

    // Step 15: Function name — returns winner name
    function winnerName() public view returns (bytes32 winnerName_) {
        winnerName_ = proposals[winningProposal()].name;
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> This contract demonstrates **all** critical Solidity features KTU 2024 expects: pragma, structs, mappings, dynamic arrays, constructors, modifiers, `view` functions, `require` statements, and event-free read logic. Memorize its structure.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: How Inheritance Resolves Multiple Modifiers

Consider a 3-level inheritance chain.

$$
C \rightarrow B \rightarrow A
$$

where `B is A` and `C is B`. When `C` calls a function `foo()` with modifiers `m1` (in A) and `m2` (in B):

**Step 1 — Linearization (C3 Linearization in Solidity)**

$$
\text{MRO} = [C, B, A]
$$

**Step 2 — Modifier Order**
Modifiers execute in **inheritance-graph order, not declaration order**. The most-derived contract's modifier runs first.

**Step 3 — Body Execution**
The placeholder `_;` in the modifier jumps into the function body, which is executed last.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**: Students think modifiers run in declaration order. They run in **inheritance order, from most-derived to base**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Solidity Compilation & Deployment Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    src1[Solidity Source .sol file] --> pragma1[Pragma directive version lock]
    pragma1 --> imports1[Import statements and using directives]
    imports1 --> contract1[Contract definition struct event modifier function]
    contract1 --> compile1[solc compiler invocation]
    compile1 --> bytecode1[Bytecode EVM machine code]
    compile1 --> abi1[ABI Application Binary Interface JSON]
    bytecode1 --> tx1[Ethereum transaction with to address null and data payload]
    tx1 --> evm1[EVM execution on every node]
    evm1 --> deploy1[Contract address generated deterministically]
    deploy1 --> state1[State variables stored on-chain]
    abi1 --> frontend1[Frontend or backend using web3.js or ethers.js]
    frontend1 --> call1[Function calls encoded with selector and arguments]
    call1 --> evm1
    state1 --> evm1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Solidity Type System Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    types1[Solidity Types] --> val1[Value Types]
    types1 --> ref1[Reference Types]

    val1 --> b1[Boolean bool]
    val1 --> i1[Integers int and uint]
    val1 --> addr1[Address and address payable]
    val1 --> by1[Bytes fixed bytes1 to bytes32 and dynamic bytes]
    val1 --> en1[Enums]
    val1 --> add1[Address literals]

    ref1 --> str1[String]
    ref1 --> arr1[Arrays fixed and dynamic]
    ref1 --> struct1[Structs]
    ref1 --> map1[Mappings]
    ref1 --> user1[User Defined Value Types]

    arr1 --> mem1[Memory arrays]
    arr1 --> stor1[Storage arrays]
    arr1 --> cal1[Calldata arrays]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: EVM Execution Flow for a Solidity Function Call

```mermaid
graph TD
    call1[External call arrives with calldata] --> decode1[EVM decodes function selector first 4 bytes]
    decode1 --> match1{Selector matches known function}
    match1 --> no1[Revert with no matching function]
    match1 --> yes1[Load function entry into program counter]
    yes1 --> mod1[Execute modifiers in inheritance order]
    mod1 --> check1{Modifier require checks pass}
    check1 --> fail1[Revert and refund unused gas]
    check1 --> pass1[Execute function body]
    pass1 --> op1[Execute opcodes with gas deduction]
    op1 --> state1{State modifying operation}
    state1 --> yes2[Update state and emit events]
    state1 --> no2[Read only or pure function]
    yes2 --> ret1[Return data and unused gas refund]
    no2 --> ret1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Inheritance and Modifier Resolution

```mermaid
graph TD
    base1[Contract A base] --> m1[Modifier m1 require msg.sender is owner then _ end]
    base1 --> f1[Function foo with m1]

    child1[Contract B is A derived] --> m2[Modifier m2 require value greater than 0 then _ end]
    child1 --> g1[Function bar with m2]

    grandchild1[Contract C is B] --> h1[Function baz with both m1 and m2]

    h1 --> resolve1[C3 Linearization finds MRO C B A]
    resolve1 --> exec1[Execute m1 from most derived then m2 then function body]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Visual Cue**: When KTU asks for a "block diagram of Solidity compilation," draw or describe the source-code → bytecode → EVM → state-storage pipeline. The above 4.1 diagram is the **gold standard** for full 7-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Solidity. List any four features of Solidity. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Solidity is a high-level, statically-typed, object-oriented programming language designed for implementing smart contracts on the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM). Its syntax is influenced by JavaScript, C++, and Python.

**Four Features (2 marks, 0.5 each):**

1. **Statically typed** — variable types are checked at compile time, reducing runtime errors.
2. **Inheritance support** — contracts can inherit from multiple base contracts (capped to 9 in 0.8.x).
3. **Library support** — reusable code can be deployed once and called via `DELEGATECALL`.
4. **Event logging** — provides cheap on-chain logging for off-chain consumption.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 expects 4 specific points for a 3-mark "define + features" question. One-line definitions with bullet features fetch full marks.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between `memory` and `storage` data locations in Solidity. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `storage` | `memory` | `calldata` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Persistence** | Permanent on-chain | Temporary (per call) | Temporary (read-only) |
| **Gas cost** | High (SSTORE = 20,000) | Lower | Lowest |
| **Location** | Contract state | Function execution | Function arguments |
| **Mutability** | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| **Default for** | State variables | Function parameters (non-external) | External function params |

**Key Distinction (1 mark):** `storage` variables persist across transactions, while `memory` variables exist only for the lifetime of a function call and are wiped afterward.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Explain Solidity Data Types in Detail. Also explain function visibility and state mutability specifiers with examples. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Module 4]`

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Solidity Data Types (7 Marks)

**1. Value Types (3.5 Marks):**

- **Booleans (`bool`)**: Holds `true` or `false`. Operators: `!`, `&&`, `||`, `==`, `!=`.
- **Integers**: Signed `int8` to `int256` and unsigned `uint8` to `uint256`. Default is `int` (= `int256`) and `uint` (= `uint256`).
  - Example: `uint256 public maxSupply = 1000000;`
- **Address**: 20-byte Ethereum address. `address` for basic operations, `address payable` for receiving Ether.
  - Example: `address public owner;` and `address payable public recipient;`
- **Bytes**: Fixed-size `bytes1` to `bytes32`, and dynamic `bytes`.
  - Example: `bytes32 public hash;`
- **Enums**: User-defined discrete values. Stored as `uint8` internally.
  - Example: `enum State { Active, Inactive, Banned }`

**2. Reference Types (3.5 Marks):**

- **Strings (`string`)**: Dynamic UTF-8 data. Has no `.length` in older versions but supports `bytes(string).length`.
- **Arrays**:
  - **Fixed**: `uint[5] public fixedArray;` — length known at compile time.
  - **Dynamic**: `uint[] public dynamicArray;` — supports `.push()`, `.pop()`, `.length`.
- **Structs**: User-defined grouping of variables.
  - Example:
  ```solidity
  struct User {
      string name;
      uint age;
      address wallet;
  }
  ```
- **Mappings**: Hash-table key-value stores. **Cannot be iterated**.
  - Example: `mapping(address => uint256) public balances;`

---

#### Part (b) — Function Visibility and State Mutability (7 Marks)

**1. Visibility Specifiers (3.5 Marks):**

| Specifier | External Call | Internal Call | Inheritance | Other Contracts |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `external` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `internal` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `private` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |

**Example**:
```solidity
function read() public view returns (uint) { return storedData; }
function write(uint x) external { storedData = x; }
function _internalLogic() internal pure returns (uint) { return 42; }
function _secret() private { /* only this contract */ }
```

**2. State Mutability Specifiers (3.5 Marks):**

| Specifier | Reads State | Modifies State | Receives Ether | External Call Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `pure` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Free (off-chain) |
| `view` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Free (off-chain) |
| (default) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | Gas required |
| `payable` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Gas required |

**Example**:
```solidity
function add(uint a, uint b) public pure returns (uint) { return a + b; }
function getBalance() public view returns (uint) { return address(this).balance; }
function deposit() public payable { balances[msg.sender] += msg.value; }
```

---

### Question B: Explain the structure of a Solidity smart contract with a sample program. Also explain inheritance and modifiers in Solidity. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 – Module 4]`

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Smart Contract Structure (7 Marks)

A Solidity file (`.sol`) is structured as follows:

**1. Pragma Directive (1 Mark):**
```solidity
pragma solidity ^0.8.0;
```
Specifies the compiler version.

**2. Import Statements (1 Mark):**
```solidity
import "./SafeMath.sol";
```
Brings in code from other files.

**3. Contract Declaration (1 Mark):**
```solidity
contract MyToken { ... }
```

**4. State Variables (1 Mark):**
```solidity
uint256 public totalSupply;
mapping(address => uint256) public balanceOf;
```

**5. Constructor (1 Mark):**
```solidity
constructor(uint256 _initialSupply) {
    totalSupply = _initialSupply;
    balanceOf[msg.sender] = _initialSupply;
}
```

**6. Functions (1 Mark):**
```solidity
function transfer(address _to, uint256 _value) public returns (bool) {
    require(balanceOf[msg.sender] >= _value, "Insufficient balance");
    balanceOf[msg.sender] -= _value;
    balanceOf[_to] += _value;
    return true;
}
```

**7. Events (1 Mark):**
```solidity
event Transfer(address indexed from, address indexed to, uint256 value);
emit Transfer(msg.sender, _to, _value);
```

---

#### Part (b) — Inheritance and Modifiers (7 Marks)

**1. Inheritance (3.5 Marks):**

Solidity supports **single and multiple inheritance** (multi-level chain limited to 9 contracts in 0.8.x to prevent C3 linearization bugs).

**Example:**
```solidity
contract Ownable {
    address public owner;
    constructor() { owner = msg.sender; }
}

contract Pausable is Ownable {
    bool public paused;
    function pause() public { require(msg.sender == owner); paused = true; }
}

contract Token is Pausable {
    mapping(address => uint256) balances;
    function transfer(address to, uint amt) public {
        require(!paused, "Paused");
        balances[msg.sender] -= amt;
        balances[to] += amt;
    }
}
```

**Inheritance C3 Linearization** ensures the most-derived contract's functions are called first, preventing the "diamond problem" in multiple inheritance.

**2. Modifiers (3.5 Marks):**

Modifiers are **reusable pre-conditions** that can be attached to multiple functions.

**Example:**
```solidity
modifier onlyOwner() {
    require(msg.sender == owner, "Not the owner");
    _;  // Function body executes here
}

modifier validAddress(address _addr) {
    require(_addr != address(0), "Zero address");
    _;
}

function changeOwner(address newOwner) public onlyOwner validAddress(newOwner) {
    owner = newOwner;
}
```

**Order of Execution:** When multiple modifiers are stacked, they execute in **declaration order**, but the function body (denoted by `_`) executes at the position of `_` in each modifier.

**Common Modifiers in Practice:**
- `onlyOwner` — access control.
- `nonReentrant` — prevents reentrancy attacks.
- `whenNotPaused` — emergency stop.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark-Loss Hotspots**
> 1. **Confusing `public` and `external`**: External functions cannot be called internally without using `this.fn()`. This is a -1 mark penalty in 7-mark answers.
> 2. **Forgetting `payable`**: A function receiving Ether **must** be marked `payable`. Omitting it causes a compile error.
> 3. **Mapping iteration claim**: Writing "we can iterate over a mapping" is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Solidity version confusion**: Don't claim `uint8` is unsigned — it's `uint8` (unsigned) and `int8` (signed). Mixed signals cost 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing `pragma`**: Every `.sol` file must have a `pragma solidity` line at the top. Missing it = -0.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Solidity is a smart-contract language for EVM**, not for Bitcoin (Bitcoin uses Bitcoin Script / Taproot).
- **Two type categories**: Value types (passed by value) and Reference types (passed by pointer).
- **4 visibilities**: `public`, `external`, `internal`, `private`. **4 mutabilities**: `pure`, `view`, default, `payable`.
- **Storage** is permanent and expensive; **memory** is temporary and cheap; **calldata** is read-only and cheapest.
- **Mappings cannot be iterated** — design a counter pattern if iteration is required.
- **Inheritance** uses C3 linearization; modifiers execute in **inheritance order**, not declaration order.
- **Gas formula**: $\text{TxFee} = \text{GasUsed} \times \text{GasPrice}$. Writing to a new storage slot costs **20,000 gas**.
- **Constructor** runs once at deployment and has the same name as the contract in older versions, or is the `constructor` keyword in 0.4.22+.
- **Events** are cheap (375 gas + 375/log topic) and indexed parameters (up to 3) are searchable.
- **`require` vs `assert`**: `require` refunds unused gas; `assert` burns all remaining gas.
- **Function selector** = first 4 bytes of `keccak256(canonicalSignature)`.
- **`fallback()`** is triggered when no function selector matches; **`receive()`** is triggered for plain ETH transfers.
- **Solidity ≥ 0.8.x** has built-in overflow/underflow checks, eliminating the need for `SafeMath`.
- **`.sol` files** can declare **multiple contracts** but only the file-named contract is the "primary" one (with restrictions in deployment).
- **`msg.sender`** is the immediate caller; **`tx.origin`** is the original EOA — never use `tx.origin` for authorization.
- **EVM word size = 256 bits** → pack structs tightly to save gas.
- **`block.timestamp`, `block.number`, `block.coinbase`, `msg.value`, `gasleft()`** are global variables tested in board exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board-Exam Mantra**: "Solidity = EVM + static typing + gas accounting + immutable contracts." Anchor every answer in this triad and you will consistently score above 85% on Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blockchain Applications in Supply Chain Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Blockchain Applications in Supply Chain Management # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Supply Chain Management (SCM)** is the end-to-end coordination and integration of all activities involved in transforming raw materials into finished products and delivering them to the end consumer, encompassing suppliers, manufacturers, warehouses, transporters, retailers, and customers. > [!NOTE] > **Blockchain-based Supply Chain Management** refers to the deployment of distributed ledger technology (DLT) to record, verify, and share transactional data across all participants of a supply chain network, ensuring **immutability**, **transparency**, **traceability**, and **real-time provenance** of physical and digital assets as they move through the chain. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, blockchain for supply chain is positioned as a **permissioned or consortium blockchain** application (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric), rather than a fully public chain, since supply chain participants are pre-vetted, known business entities (B2B scenarios). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Mango from Farm to Fork" Story > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you buy a mango from a supermarket. Today, you have no idea which farm it came from, which pesticides were sprayed, or how long it sat in cold storage. Now, imagine every time that mango changed hands — farmer → wholesaler → transporter → retailer — a **tamper-proof digital receipt** was automatically written into a **shared, unalterable ledger** that anyone (including you, the consumer) could scan via a QR code. That is **Blockchain in Supply Chain Management**. Think of blockchain as a **Google Doc shared by everyone in the supply chain**, except: - **No one can delete or edit** a past entry. - Every new entry is **time-stamped and cryptographically signed**. - All participants see the **same single source of truth**. ## 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Measurements in Blockchain-SCM The following **key performance indicators (KPIs)** are standard benchmarks in industry deployments: | **Standard Metric** | **Typical Value/Range** | **Significance** | |---------------------|-------------------------|------------------| | Transaction Throughput (TPS) | **3,000 – 10,000 TPS** (Hyperledger Fabric) | Determines how many supply chain events can be recorded per second | | Block Confirmation Time | **0.5 – 5 seconds** | Time to finalize a transaction (Provenance, Food Trust) | | Data Immutability | **SHA-256 hash, 2^256 collision space** | Cryptographic strength of the ledger | | Network Participants | **10 – 500 nodes** | Typical consortium size in B2B supply chain | | Reduction in Disputes | **40 – 60%** | Empirically observed in IBM Food Trust deployments | | Recall Time | Reduced from **days to seconds** | Critical for food/pharma safety | ## 1.4 Syllabus Highlight — Why This Topic Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > As per the **KTU 2024 PECCT522 syllabus (Module 4)**, this topic bridges **distributed systems theory** with **real-world enterprise applications**. It is a high-weightage area for ESE (End Semester Examination) and frequently appears as a 14-mark analytical question testing a student's ability to **map SCM pain points to blockchain properties**. The core syllabus outcomes addressed are: - **CO4:** Apply blockchain concepts to real-world domains. - **CO5:** Evaluate the suitability of blockchain for enterprise use-cases. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Core Pain Points in Traditional Supply Chains (Pre-Blockchain) To understand *why* blockchain is needed, we must first catalogue the inherent weaknesses of legacy SCM systems: 1. **Opacity** — Each stakeholder maintains a **siloed, private database**. Information asymmetry is rampant. 2. **Susceptibility to Fraud** — Documents like Bills of Lading, Certificates of Origin, and Quality Reports can be **forged or back-dated**. 3. **Inefficient Dispute Resolution** — When product defects are detected, tracing liability is slow and expensive. 4. **Lack of Real-Time Visibility** — EDI (Electronic Data Interchange) systems have **latency of hours to days**. 5. **High Reconciliation Costs** — Multiple parties maintaining separate ledgers must reconcile at intervals, a process prone to error. 6. **Counterfeiting** — Luxury goods, pharmaceuticals, and electronics suffer massive losses (estimated **\$500+ billion annually** in counterfeiting globally). ## 2.2 Blockchain as the Solution — The Core 'Why' Blockchain addresses each pain point through specific architectural properties: | **Pain Point** | **Blockchain Property That Solves It** | |----------------|---------------------------------------| | Opacity | **Distributed Ledger** — single shared view across all participants | | Forgery | **Cryptographic Hashing & Immutability** — past records cannot be altered | | Slow Disputes | **Smart Contracts** — self-executing, self-evidencing logic | | Latency | **Real-time Consensus** — block finality in seconds | | Reconciliation Cost | **Single Source of Truth** — no separate ledgers to reconcile | | Counterfeiting | **Provenance Tracking** — every step is digitally signed and timestamped | ## 2.3 Architecture of a Blockchain Supply Chain Solution A typical blockchain-SCM system has **five conceptual layers**: ### Layer 1: Physical Asset Layer - IoT sensors (GPS, temperature, humidity, RFID) - QR codes / NFC tags / barcodes - Smart packaging ### Layer 2: Data Capture Layer (Oracles) - IoT gateways pushing sensor data - Manual entry via mobile apps - ERP system integration (SAP, Oracle) ### Layer 3: Blockchain Layer (Distributed Ledger) - **Consortium/Hyperledger Fabric** preferred for SCM - Smart contracts (chaincode) for business logic - Consensus: PBFT, Raft, or PoA (Proof of Authority) ### Layer 4: Integration Layer - APIs (REST, gRPC) for ERP/WMS/TMS integration - Middleware (e.g., Hyperledger Composer) ### Layer 5: Application Layer - Dashboards for stakeholders - Consumer-facing QR code verification apps - Audit & compliance tools ## 2.4 Step-by-Step Operational Logic — How a Transaction is Recorded The following bulleted logic steps describe the **end-to-end flow** of a supply chain event being written to the blockchain: 1. **Step 1 — Physical Event Occurs:** Goods are shipped, received, inspected, or scanned. 2. **Step 2 — Data Capture:** An IoT sensor or human operator records the event (timestamp, GPS, temperature, quantity, batch ID). 3. **Step 3 — Data Validation by Oracle:** The data is signed and pushed to the blockchain network via a trusted oracle (to prevent garbage-in-garbage-out). 4. **Step 4 — Smart Contract Execution:** A chaincode function (e.g., `recordShipment(batchID, sender, receiver, location)`) is triggered. 5. **Step 5 — Endorsement by Peers:** In Hyperledger Fabric, endorsing peer nodes execute the chaincode and cryptographically sign the result. 6. **Step 6 — Consensus & Block Formation:** Ordering service packages endorsed transactions into a block; consensus is reached via Raft/PBFT. 7. **Step 7 — Ledger Commit:** Block is appended to the distributed ledger; all peers update their copy. 8. **Step 8 — Event Notification:** Downstream systems (ERP, consumer app) are notified via event listeners. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes the **core mathematical and cryptographic primitives** you must know for blockchain-SCM questions. No vertical pipes (`|`) are used inside cells to avoid markdown breakage; absolute values use `\vert` notation. | **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Engineering Utility** | |-------------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | Cryptographic Hash | $H(m) = \text{SHA-256}(m)$ | Generates fixed-length fingerprint of a transaction; detects any tampering | | Block Hash | $H_b = H(H_{b-1} \Vert T_b \Vert \text{Nonce})$ | Links each block to the previous; ensures immutability | | Merkle Root | $R = H(H(T_1 \Vert T_2) \Vert H(T_3 \Vert T_4))$ | Efficient verification of large transaction batches in SCM | | Digital Signature | $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{sk}(H(m))$, verify: $\text{Ver}_{pk}(H(m), \sigma) = \text{true}$ | Authenticates the participant (supplier, transporter, retailer) | | Consensus Threshold | $f < \frac{n}{3}$ (Byzantine Fault Tolerance) | Maximum faulty nodes a BFT consensus can tolerate in a consortium of $n$ nodes | | TPS (Theoretical) | $\text{TPS} = \frac{\text{Block Size (tx)}}{\text{Block Time (s)}}$ | Throughput capacity of the supply chain ledger | | Latency | $L = t_{\text{finality}} - t_{\text{submit}}$ | Time from event to ledger finalization; critical for cold-chain monitoring | | Provenance Length | $P = \{B_1, B_2, \dots, B_k\}$, length $= k$ | Number of custody hops recorded for an asset | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Blockchain-SCM is actively deployed in: - **Food Safety:** Walmart + IBM Food Trust — traced mangoes from farm to store in **2.2 seconds** (previously took **6 days, 18 hours**). - **Pharmaceuticals:** MediLedger — prevents counterfeit drugs entering the supply chain. - **Diamond Provenance:** Everledger (Tracr) — tracks conflict-free diamonds from mine to retail. - **Logistics:** Maersk TradeLens — global shipping document automation. - **Automotive:** MOBI (Mobility Open Blockchain Initiative) — vehicle history and parts provenance. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping SCM Requirements to Blockchain Features > [!NOTE] > The following analytical matrix is **routinely tested in KTU ESE** as a 7-mark sub-question. Memorize the mapping logic. | **SCM Requirement** | **Required Blockchain Property** | **Justification** | |---------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------| | Provenance tracking | **Append-only ledger + timestamping** | Each transfer event is permanently recorded with a tamper-proof timestamp | | Authenticity verification | **Digital signatures (PKI)** | Each stakeholder signs their transaction with their private key, non-repudiable | | Multi-party trust | **Consensus mechanism (PBFT/Raft)** | No single party controls the ledger; trust is distributed | | Process automation | **Smart contracts (chaincode)** | Conditional releases (e.g., payment on delivery) execute automatically | | Counterfeit prevention | **Unique digital twin / NFT per physical asset** | A 1:1 cryptographic token represents the physical item | | Real-time visibility | **Event-driven architecture + push notifications** | Subscribers receive updates the moment a block is committed | | Regulatory compliance | **Immutable audit trail** | Auditors can replay the entire history of an asset | ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Cryptographic Provenance Chain Let us derive the **mathematical structure of a provenance chain** as expected in a 7-mark KTU question. ### Setup Let a physical asset $A$ (e.g., a pharmaceutical batch) pass through a sequence of $k$ custodians: $$A \rightarrow C_1 \rightarrow C_2 \rightarrow C_3 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow C_k \rightarrow \text{Consumer}$$ Each custodian $C_i$ creates a transaction $T_i$ with the following fields: $$T_i = \{ \text{batchID}, \text{sender}, \text{receiver}, \text{location}, \text{ts}_i, \text{hashPrev} \}$$ ### Step 1 — Transaction Construction by Custodian $C_1$ At time $t_1$, $C_1$ (manufacturer) records the manufacturing event: $$T_1 = \{ \text{batchID} = B, \text{sender} = \text{Manufacturer}, \text{receiver} = C_1, \text{location} = L_1, \text{ts}_1 = t_1, \text{hashPrev} = \emptyset \}$$ ### Step 2 — Hash Generation The transaction payload $T_1$ is hashed using SHA-256: $$H(T_1) = \text{SHA-256}(T_1)$$ This hash acts as the **unique fingerprint** of $T_1$. Even a single bit-change in $T_1$ would produce a completely different hash. ### Step 3 — Digital Signature $C_1$ signs the hash using their private key $sk_1$: $$\sigma_1 = \text{Sign}_{sk_1}(H(T_1))$$ The signed transaction becomes: $$T_1^{\text{signed}} = (T_1, \sigma_1)$$ ### Step 4 — Block Construction A block $B_1$ contains $T_1^{\text{signed}}$ plus the previous block hash. For the genesis block, $\text{hashPrev} = 0$: $$B_1 = (T_1^{\text{signed}}, H_{B_0})$$ The block hash is computed as: $$H_{B_1} = \text{SHA-256}(H_{B_0} \Vert \text{MerkleRoot}(B_1) \Vert t_1 \Vert \text{Nonce})$$ ### Step 5 — Propagation and Consensus $B_1$ is broadcast to all peers in the consortium. Endorsing peers verify the signature: $$\text{Ver}_{pk_1}(H(T_1), \sigma_1) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{true}$$ If valid, the block is added to the ledger after consensus. ### Step 6 — Subsequent Custodians For the second custodian $C_2$, the transaction includes the hash of the previous transaction to create an **unbreakable chain**: $$T_2 = \{ \text{batchID} = B, \text{sender} = C_1, \text{receiver} = C_2, \text{location} = L_2, \text{ts}_2 = t_2, \text{hashPrev} = H(T_1) \}$$ ### Step 7 — Provenance Verification A consumer with $pk_1, pk_2, \dots, pk_k$ (public keys) and $H(T_k)$ can verify the entire chain by: - Verifying $\sigma_k$ using $pk_k$. - Confirming $\text{hashPrev} = H(T_{k-1})$. - Recursively verifying all previous signatures. This recursive verification guarantees **end-to-end provenance** in $O(k)$ time. ### Step 8 — Final Provenance Expression The complete provenance of asset $A$ after $k$ hops is: $$P_A = \{ (T_1, \sigma_1), (T_2, \sigma_2), \dots, (T_k, \sigma_k) \}$$ $$\text{with} \quad \forall i \in [2,k]: \text{hashPrev}(T_i) = H(T_{i-1})$$ ## 3.3 Derivation — Throughput vs. Scalability Trade-off (Analytical) For a blockchain-SCM platform, the maximum sustainable throughput is: $$\text{TPS}_{\max} = \frac{\text{Block Size (bytes)}}{\text{Tx Size (bytes)} \times \text{Block Time (s)}}$$ For a typical Hyperledger Fabric deployment in supply chain: - Block Size: **2 MB** (configurable) - Average Transaction Size: **2 KB** (e.g., shipment event) - Block Time: **0.5 seconds** Therefore: $$\text{TPS}_{\max} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes}}{2 \times 10^3 \text{ bytes/tx} \times 0.5 \text{ s}} = \frac{10^3}{0.5} = 2000 \text{ TPS}$$ > [!TIP] > This is a **favourite KTU numerical**. If asked "Calculate the TPS of a supply chain blockchain with block size X, transaction size Y, and block time Z," apply this formula directly. ## 3.4 Step-by-Step — Smart Contract for Automated Payment on Delivery A common SCM smart contract logic is **"payment-on-delivery"**. Let us derive its chaincode logic step-by-step: ### Contract State - `mapping(bytes32 => Shipment) public shipments;` - `Shipment` contains: `buyer, seller, amount, status` ### Function: `confirmDelivery(bytes32 shipmentID)` **Step 1:** Verify caller is the buyer. $$msg.sender \stackrel{?}{=} shipments[shipmentID].buyer$$ **Step 2:** Verify status is `IN_TRANSIT`. $$shipments[shipmentID].status \stackrel{?}{=} \text{IN\_TRANSIT}$$ **Step 3:** Mark delivery as confirmed. $$shipments[shipmentID].status := \text{DELIVERED}$$ **Step 4:** Transfer payment from escrow to seller. $$\text{balance}[seller] \mathrel{+}= shipments[shipmentID].amount$$ **Step 5:** Emit event for downstream ERP integration. $$\text{emit DeliveryConfirmed}(shipmentID, \text{ts}_{now})$$ This chaincode executes **deterministically** across all endorsing peers, ensuring trustless automated settlement. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Supply Chain Flow on Blockchain > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a physical asset (e.g., a coffee bean batch) is tracked through the supply chain with each custody change written to the blockchain. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Farm Origin
IoT sensor records GPS and harvest time] A2[Quality Testing Lab
Cert uploaded as digital document] A3[Distributor Warehouse
Batch tagged with QR code] A4[Transport Vehicle
Cold-chain temperature logged] A5[Retail Outlet
Shelf date and price recorded] A6[End Consumer
Scans QR to verify provenance] BC1[(Blockchain Ledger
Permissioned Network
Hyperledger Fabric)] A1 -->|Tx1 recordShipment| BC1 A2 -->|Tx2 addCertificate| BC1 A3 -->|Tx3 receiveGoods| BC1 A4 -->|Tx4 logTransit| BC1 A5 -->|Tx5 markRetail| BC1 A6 -->|Query provenance| BC1 BC1 -->|Event notification| A1 BC1 -->|Event notification| A2 BC1 -->|Event notification| A3 BC1 -->|Event notification| A4 BC1 -->|Event notification| A5 BC1 -->|Provenance response| A6 ``` ## 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Layered View of Blockchain-SCM System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Layer5[Application Layer] L5A[Consumer Verification App] L5B[Stakeholder Dashboards] L5C[Regulatory Audit Portal] end subgraph Layer4[Integration Layer] L4A[REST and gRPC APIs] L4B[ERP Connectors SAP Oracle] L4C[Middleware Hyperledger Composer] end subgraph Layer3[Blockchain Layer] L3A[Smart Contracts Chaincode] L3B[Consensus Service PBFT Raft] L3C[Distributed Ledger] end subgraph Layer2[Data Capture Layer Oracles] L2A[IoT Sensors Temperature GPS] L2B[QR NFC RFID Readers] L2C[Mobile Data Entry Apps] end subgraph Layer1[Physical Asset Layer] L1A[Goods in Transit] L1B[Warehouse Inventory] L1C[Retail Stock] end L1A --> L2A L1B --> L2B L1C --> L2C L2A --> L4A L2B --> L4A L2C --> L4A L4A --> L3A L4B --> L3A L4C --> L3A L3A --> L3B L3B --> L3C L3C --> L5A L3C --> L5B L3C --> L5C ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow — Transaction Lifecycle in Supply Chain Blockchain ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Step 1: Physical Event Occurs
e.g., shipment dispatched] P2[Step 2: IoT or Operator Captures Data] P3[Step 3: Data Signed by Oracle] P4[Step 4: Chaincode Triggered
recordShipment invoked] P5[Step 5: Endorsing Peers Execute and Sign] P6{Step 6: Consensus Achieved
