$$P_{\text{ETH,liq}} = \frac{0.80 \times 1{,}500}{2} = \frac{1{,}200}{2} = \$600$$

**Step 5 — Compute Drop Required.**
$$\Delta P = \frac{2{,}000 - 600}{2{,}000} \times 100\% = 70\%$$

> **Conclusion:** A 70% crash in ETH price triggers Alice's automatic liquidation by the Aave smart contract — no human bank officer required.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Blockchain Architecture for Which Banking Use-Case?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to *justify* a blockchain architecture choice. Use this matrix.

| Use-Case | Recommended Architecture | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Retail CBDC (e.g., e₹-R) | **Permissioned, hybrid (indirect model)** | Scalability, central bank control, AML/CFT compliance |
| Wholesale CBDC (e.g., e₹-W) | **Permissioned DLT (Hyperledger Fabric)** | High TPS, privacy between banks, regulator oversight |
| Cross-Border Remittance | **Public + HTLC bridges** (e.g., Ripple, Stellar) | Open access, atomic swaps, low fees |
| Trade Finance | **Permissioned consortium** (R3 Corda, Hyperledger) | Confidentiality of trade terms, known participants |
| DeFi Lending | **Public + smart contracts** (Ethereum, Solana) | Composability, no intermediaries |
| Securities Settlement | **Permissioned with ZK-proofs** | Privacy + regulatory auditability |
| KYC Sharing | **Permissioned consortium** | GDPR compliance, shared infrastructure |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cross-Border Payment — Traditional (SWIFT) vs Blockchain

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** comparing the legacy SWIFT correspondent-banking model with a blockchain-based remittance. Note the elimination of intermediary hops in the blockchain model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sender Bank India] --> B1[SBI Correspondent NY]
    B1 --> C1[Citi Correspondent London]
    C1 --> D1[Receiver Bank USA]
    D1 --> E1[Beneficiary Customer]

    A2[Sender Wallet] --> B2[Blockchain Network]
    B2 --> D2[Receiver Wallet]
    D2 --> E2[Beneficiary Customer]

    classDef legacy fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef modern fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    class A1,B1,C1,D1,E1 legacy
    class A2,B2,D2,E2 modern
```

**Reading the diagram:** The red (top) path is the traditional SWIFT chain — 4 hops, each with reconciliation delays. The green (bottom) path is the blockchain path — only 2 hops, settled in one block.

### 4.2 DeFi Lending Architecture (Aave-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lenderA[Lender A] --> poolA[Liquidity Pool USDC]
    lenderB[Lender B] --> poolA
    poolA --> borrowerX[Borrower X]
    poolA --> borrowerY[Borrower Y]
    poolA --> interest[Interest Bearing Token aUSDC]
    interest --> lenderA
    interest --> lenderB
    collateralX[Collateral ETH] --> borrowerX
    oracleA[Pyth Chainlink Price Oracle] --> poolA
    liquidationA[Liquidation Engine] --> poolA
```

**Key interactions:**
- Lenders deposit USDC, receive aUSDC (interest-bearing receipt).
- Borrowers lock ETH as collateral, borrow USDC at variable APY.
- The **Chainlink/Pyth oracle** feeds real-time ETH/USD price to the pool.
- The **liquidation engine** automatically seizes collateral if CR < 80%.

### 4.3 CBDC Two-Tier Architecture (RBI e₹ Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RBI1[Central Bank RBI] --> dist1[Distributor Banks]
    dist1 --> wallet1[User Digital Wallet]
    wallet1 --> merchant1[Merchant]
    wallet1 --> p2p1[Peer to Peer Transfer]
    wallet1 --> atm1[ATM Cash Conversion]
    RBI1 -.issues CBDC.-> dist1
    dist1 -.validates KYC.-> wallet1
    RBI1 -.monitors ledger.-> dist1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The *indirect (two-tier) CBDC* model preserves the role of commercial banks as distribution & KYC agents, but replaces physical cash and RTGS rails with a tokenized central-bank liability.

### 4.4 HTLC Atomic Swap Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice
    participant ChainX as Chain X
    participant Bob
    participant ChainY as Chain Y
    Alice->>ChainX: Lock AssetX with Hash H
    Alice->>Bob: Share preimage z
    Bob->>ChainY: Lock AssetY with Hash H
    Alice->>ChainY: Reveal preimage z claim AssetY
    Bob->>ChainX: Use revealed z claim AssetX
    Note over Alice,Bob: Either both swaps succeed or both refund. Atomicity guaranteed.
```

This is the cryptographic primitive behind trustless cross-chain and cross-border atomic settlements.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. Define Decentralized Finance (DeFi). List any two advantages over traditional finance.**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
> DeFi refers to a financial ecosystem built on public blockchains (primarily Ethereum) where **smart contracts** replace intermediaries such as banks, brokers, and clearing houses to deliver lending, borrowing, trading, and asset management services.
> 
> **Advantages over TradFi:**
> 1. **Permissionless access** — anyone with an internet connection can participate, with no geographic or KYC barrier.
> 2. **Transparency** — every transaction is on-chain and auditable 24×7 by anyone.
> 3. **Composability** — protocols can plug into each other like *Money Legos*.
> 4. **Censorship resistance** — no single entity can freeze or reverse a valid transaction.

---

**Q2. What is a Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC)? Differentiate between retail and wholesale CBDCs.**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
> A **CBDC** is the digital form of a country's fiat currency issued by the central bank and recognized as legal tender.
> 
> | Aspect | Retail CBDC (e₹-R) | Wholesale CBDC (e₹-W) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Users | General public | Banks & financial institutions |
> | Use-case | Daily payments | Inter-bank settlement |
> | Ledger | Often account-based or hybrid | Token-based on DLT |
> | Volume | High | Low but high-value |
> 
> Retail CBDC aims to complement cash; wholesale CBDC aims to replace RTGS.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students commonly **lose 3–4 marks** by writing generic definitions without **specific technical terms** (e.g., "HTLC", "atomic settlement", "consensus protocol", "DVP").
> - **Diagrams are mandatory for 7+ mark questions.** A well-labelled Mermaid-style or hand-drawn block diagram fetches **at least 1–2 extra marks** in KTU valuation.
> - **Always conclude with a comparison table or a real-world example** (RBI, JPMorgan, Ripple) — examiners reward applied knowledge.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]*

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of a blockchain-based cross-border remittance system. Compare it with the traditional SWIFT correspondent banking model.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Traditional SWIFT Model (Recap):**
The SWIFT (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication) network is a *messaging* layer, not a settlement layer. A remittance from Bank A (India) to Bank B (USA) typically passes through:
- Sender's branch
- Sender's correspondent bank (e.g., SBI NY)
- Beneficiary's correspondent bank (e.g., Citibank NY)
- Beneficiary's branch
- Each hop involves **nostro/vostro account reconciliation** with **T+1 to T+2 days** settlement.

**2. Blockchain-Based Model (Detailed):**
The architecture has the following layers:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Application Layer Mobile Wallet] --> L2[Smart Contract Layer HTLC Escrow]
    L2 --> L3[Consensus Layer PoS or PBFT]
    L3 --> L4[Network Layer P2P Gossip]
    L4 --> L5[Ledger Layer Append Only Chain]
    L5 --> L6[Settlement Layer Finality Engine]
```

**3. Step-by-Step Operational Flow (as already detailed in Section 2.2).**

**4. Comparison Table (High-Yield for marks):**

| Parameter | SWIFT (TradFi) | Blockchain (DeFi/Consortium) |
|---|---|---|
| Settlement Time | T+1 to T+2 days | T+0 seconds to minutes |
| Cost per Tx | \$25–\$50 | \$0.001–\$2 |
| Intermediaries | 2–4 correspondent banks | 0 (atomic swap) |
| Transparency | Bilateral only | Full ledger visibility |
| Operating Hours | Banker's hours | 24×7×365 |
| Reversibility | Reversible (chargebacks) | Irreversible (finality) |
| Regulatory Reach | Strong (centralized) | Variable (jurisdiction-specific) |

**5. Real-World Examples:**
- **Ripple (XRP Ledger):** Partners with 300+ banks including Santander, SBI Remit.
- **Stellar (XLM):** Used by MoneyGram for US–Philippines corridor.
- **RBI Project:** The *e₹-W* pilot between 5 Indian banks for inter-bank call money market.

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of Smart Contracts and HTLCs in enabling trustless cross-border settlements.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Smart Contract — The Self-Executing Agreement:**
A smart contract is computer code stored on a blockchain that automatically executes the terms of an agreement when predefined conditions are met. In finance, smart contracts enable:
- **Escrow without a trusted third party**
- **Programmable collateral release**
- **Conditional payments (e.g., pay on delivery)**

**2. HTLC (Hash Time-Locked Contract) — The Atomicity Primitive:**
An HTLC is a special smart contract that locks funds using two cryptographic conditions:
- **Hashlock:** Funds can be unlocked only with the disclosure of the preimage $z$ of a hash $H = \text{SHA256}(z)$.
- **Timelock:** If not claimed within time $T$, the funds are refunded to the originator.

**3. Worked Cryptographic Walkthrough:**

- **Setup:** Alice wants to send ₹10,000 to Bob via XRP/ETH bridge.
- **Step A:** Alice generates a secret $z$, computes $H = \text{SHA256}(z)$.
- **Step B:** Alice locks ₹10,000-equivalent on Chain X with hashlock $H$ and timelock $T = 24$ hours.
- **Step C:** Bob sees the lock, locks equivalent value on Chain Y with the same $H$ and a *shorter* timelock $T' = 12$ hours.
- **Step D:** Alice reveals $z$ on Chain Y to claim Bob's locked value.
- **Step E:** Bob now has $z$ and claims Alice's lock on Chain X.
- **Failure path:** If $z$ is not revealed, both contracts refund after their respective timeouts.

**4. Atomicity Guarantee — Why It Works:**
Either *both* transfers succeed or *both* refund. There is no state where one party has paid and the other has not. This is **mathematically guaranteed** by the hash function's preimage resistance.

**5. Applications in Banking:**
- **Ripple's Interledger Protocol (ILP):** Generalization of HTLC for any two ledgers.
- **Lightning Network (Bitcoin):** HTLCs enable off-chain payment channels.
- **CBDC Bridges:** Cross-border CBDC pilots (mBridge by BIS) use HTLC-like commitments.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Decentralized Finance (DeFi) lending protocol. Compute the liquidation price of a position: 5 ETH collateral, 2,500 USDC borrowed, liquidation threshold 80%, current ETH price \$1,800.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. DeFi Lending Architecture (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2).**

**2. Working Mechanism:**
- **Liquidity Pools:** Lenders deposit assets (USDC, DAI) into a shared pool.
- **Borrowers** over-collateralize (typical CR > 150%) to borrow.
- **Interest Rates** are algorithmically determined by the utilization ratio:
$$R = \frac{\text{Total Borrowed}}{\text{Total Supplied}} \times 100\%$$
The borrow APY $r_b$ follows a kink-model:

$$r_b = \begin{cases} R_{\text{base}} + R_{\text{slope1}} \cdot U & \text{if } U \le U_{\text{optimal}} \\ R_{\text{base}} + R_{\text{slope1}} \cdot U_{\text{optimal}} + R_{\text{slope2}} \cdot (U - U_{\text{optimal}}) & \text{if } U > U_{\text{optimal}} \end{cases}$$

where $U$ is the utilization, $R_{\text{slope1}} \ll R_{\text{slope2}}$ (steep beyond optimal).

**3. Worked Liquidation Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Collateral $V_c = 5$ ETH
- Debt $V_d = 2{,}500$ USDC
- Current ETH price $P = \$1{,}800$
- Liquidation threshold $L_T = 0.80$

**Step 1 — Current Collateral Value:**

$$V_c = 5 \times 1{,}800 = \$9{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Current Collateralization Ratio:**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{9{,}000}{2{,}500} \times 100\% = 360\%$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Liquidation Price $P_{\text{liq}}$:**

$$\frac{5 \times P_{\text{liq}}}{2{,}500} = 0.80$$

$$P_{\text{liq}} = \frac{0.80 \times 2{,}500}{5} = \frac{2{,}000}{5} = \$400$$

**Step 4 — Required Price Drop:**

$$\Delta = \frac{1{,}800 - 400}{1{,}800} \times 100\% = 77.78\%$$

> **Conclusion:** ETH must drop **77.78%** from \$1,800 to \$400 for this position to be liquidated.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) models. With an example, discuss India's e₹-R and e₹-W pilots.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. CBDC Design Spectrum:**

| Model | Ledger | Anonymity | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct** | Central Bank | High (like cash) | Bahamas Sand Dollar |
| **Indirect (Two-Tier)** | Distributed | Low (KYC) | India's e₹ |
| **Hybrid** | Both | Variable | ECB digital euro studies |

**2. The Two-Tier (Indirect) Model — Adopted by India:**

- **Tier 1:** RBI issues CBDC to authorized banks.
- **Tier 2:** Banks distribute CBDC to end users via wallets.
- **KYC/AML:** Banks handle verification; RBI sees the wholesale ledger.
- **Offline Capability:** India's e₹-R supports *offline* transfers via NFC-like proximity (token-based model).

**3. India's e₹-R (Retail):**
- **Launched:** Pilot December 2022; expanded to 5 million+ users by 2024.
- **Use Case:** Person-to-person (P2P), person-to-merchant (P2M).
- **Tech Stack:** Built on a **permissioned variant of Corda / custom DLT** with throughput > 100,000 TPS target.
- **Token vs Account:** RBI uses a **token-based** model for P2P anonymity, account-based for merchants.

**4. India's e₹-W (Wholesale):**
- **Use Case:** Inter-bank call money, government securities settlement.
- **Participants:** SBI, HDFC, ICICI, Yes Bank, IDFC First, Bank of Baroda.
- **Tech Stack:** **Hyperledger Fabric** permissioned blockchain.
- **Benefit:** Atomic settlement replaces the **T+1** leg of the current RTGS system.

**5. Key Advantages:**
- **Financial Inclusion:** Smartphone-based access for unbanked.
- **Reduced Transaction Cost:** Eliminates payment of interchange fees to private networks.
- **Monetary Policy Efficiency:** Real-time visibility of money circulation.

**6. Key Challenges:**
- **Cyber-security:** Centralized honeypot risk.
- **Bank Disintermediation:** Risk of deposit flight to CBDC.
- **Privacy:** Tension between traceability and anonymity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Finance and Banking:**

- ☐ **Three financial paradigms:** CeFi, DeFi, TradFi — know at least 3 differences for each pair.
- ☐ **CBDC vs Cryptocurrency:** CBDC is a *central-bank liability*; cryptocurrency is *nobody's liability* (or a network's).
- ☐ **Merkle Tree Benefit:** Verification in $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ hashes — must derive or state this for full marks.
- ☐ **HTLC = Hashlock + Timelock** — used in atomic cross-chain swaps; mention "preimage resistance" explicitly.
- ☐ **Stablecoin Types:** Fiat-collateralized (USDC), crypto-collateralized (DAI), algorithmic (UST — *failed*) — know the difference.
- ☐ **DeFi Liquidation Formula:** $P_{\text{liq}} = \dfrac{L_T \times V_{\text{debt}}}{V_{\text{collateral}}}$ — practice at least 2 numerical problems.
- ☐ **RBI's e₹-R** uses **token-based** indirect model; **e₹-W** uses **Hyperledger Fabric** for inter-bank settlement.
- ☐ **SWIFT comparison:** Always state the cost (\$25–\$50) and time (T+2 days) to anchor the contrast.
- ☐ **Real-world examples to cite:** JPMorgan Onyx, Ripple, Aave, RBI e₹, BIS Project mBridge, TradeLens.
- ☐ **Draw a diagram for every 7-marker question** — Mermaid-style or hand-drawn block diagrams are mandatory.
- ☐ **TPS benchmark numbers to memorize:** Visa ≈ 1,700, Bitcoin ≈ 7, Ethereum ≈ 15–30, Solana ≈ 65,000.
- ☐ **Always end answers with a comparison table** — examiners (and KTU's OBE rubric) reward structured summarization.
- ☐ **Avoid these buzzwords without context:** "Web3", "metaverse", "DAO" — use only when relevant and define them.
- ☐ **Spell out acronyms first time:** HTLC, DVP, DLT, RTGS, KYC, AML, CBDC, SSI, DID — at least once in every answer.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Module 4 questions almost always combine a *conceptual* sub-part (definitions, comparisons) with an *analytical* sub-part (calculation, diagram). Allocate **4–5 minutes of your 14-mark answer** to the diagram and table — they are the highest ROI (marks-per-minute) elements in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insurance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT522 - BLOCKCHAIN AND CRYPTOCURRENCY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Insurance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Insurance (Blockchain Context)**: Insurance, in the context of distributed ledger technology, refers to a *risk-pooling financial contract* in which policyholders transfer the economic burden of a specified loss to an insurer in exchange for a **premium**, with the contractual terms, premium escrow, claim verification, and payout execution being automated, cryptographically secured, and immutably recorded on a blockchain network.

In the traditional sense, insurance is a contractual mechanism of **risk transfer** and **risk pooling** among a group of entities, governed by an *insurer* who underwrites policies, collects premiums, and disburses claims when predefined *perils* (loss events) occur. The KTU 2024 syllabus contextualizes this classical definition within **blockchain ecosystems**, where decentralized consensus, smart contracts, and cryptographic primitives replace the role of intermediaries.

**Key Vocabulary Anchors (Exam-Worthy Terms):**
- **Policyholder** — The entity (individual or organization) purchasing risk coverage.
- **Premium** — The monetary consideration paid by the policyholder to the insurer.
- **Insurer / Underwriter** — The risk-bearing entity assuming the liability in exchange for the premium.
- **Claim** — A formal demand by the policyholder for compensation against a covered loss.
- **Sum Assured** — The maximum liability the insurer agrees to pay upon a successful claim.
- **Peril** — The cause of loss (e.g., flood, fire, accident, smart-contract exploit).
- **Deductible (Excess)** — The portion of loss borne by the policyholder before insurance kicks in.
- **Actuary** — A professional who statistically models risk exposure and computes premiums.
- **Parametric Trigger** — An objective, data-driven event (e.g., rainfall $> 50$ mm in 24 h) that mechanically activates a payout.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Village Rice Bank"

Imagine a small Kerala coastal village prone to monsoon flooding. Every household contributes a fixed amount of rice each month to a communal granary. If one household's hut collapses, the village collectively compensates them from the granary, following a *vetted, transparent* protocol decided during the *grama sabha* (village council).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is precisely what insurance is — a **mutual risk-pooling agreement**. Blockchain-based insurance simply replaces the village council with a *smart contract*, the granary with a *multi-signature on-chain treasury*, and the verbal consensus with *immutable on-chain audit trails*.

Theft, cheating, and bureaucratic delay (which plague the *traditional granary model*) are mitigated by the cryptographic and game-theoretic guarantees of the blockchain.

## 1.3 Why Blockchain for Insurance?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Statement**: *"Blockchain technology addresses three structural inefficiencies of legacy insurance — opacity, claim-processing latency, and intermediary-driven fraud — by enabling trustless automation, real-time settlement, and shared ledger transparency."*

The traditional insurance industry suffers from:
1. **Information Asymmetry** — Insurers know more than policyholders (and vice versa), leading to *adverse selection* and *moral hazard*.
2. **High Operational Cost** — Up to **25 % – 30 %** of every premium is consumed by administrative overhead in classical insurers.
3. **Slow Claims Settlement** — Average settlement windows extend from **30 to 90 days**.
4. **Fraud Vulnerability** — The FBI estimates insurance fraud costs **>\$40 billion annually** in the U.S. alone.

Blockchain disrupts all four vectors through:
- **Smart Contracts** (self-executing policy logic),
- **Decentralized Oracles** (verifiable external data feeds),
- **Tokenized Premiums & Payouts** (programmable money),
- **Public Auditability** (anyone can verify premium inflows and claim outflows).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Insurance in the Blockchain Era

Blockchain does not invent a new *type* of insurance; it **re-architects the delivery mechanism**. The classical product taxonomy is preserved:

### 2.1.1 By Risk Nature
- **Life Insurance** — Covers mortality / longevity risk; longest contract horizons.
- **Health / Medical Insurance** — Covers hospitalization and treatment costs.
- **Property & Casualty (P&C)** — Covers assets (homes, vehicles, crops).
- **Liability Insurance** — Covers legal liabilities of the insured.
- **Specialty Insurance** — Cyber, marine, aviation, satellite, etc.

### 2.1.2 By Architectural Delivery
- **Centralized Insurer on Blockchain** — A traditional insurer uses a private/permissioned ledger to streamline its internal record-keeping (e.g., *B3i — Blockchain Insurance Industry Initiative*).
- **Hybrid (InsurTech) Models** — Insurer + smart contract triggers + oracles.
- **Decentralized / Peer-to-Peer Insurance** — No central insurer; members pool into a *mutual DAO* (e.g., *Nexus Mutual, Etherisc, Lemonade's protocol layer*).
- **Parametric Insurance** — Payout is automatic upon the occurrence of a *verifiable parameter* (rainfall index, flight delay, earthquake magnitude), eliminating subjective claim assessment.

## 2.2 Operational Workflow — Classical vs. Blockchain Insurance

The traditional 7-step claim lifecycle can be compressed into 4 steps under a blockchain-based parametric model.

| Step | Traditional Insurance | Blockchain-Based Insurance |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Quote | Manual underwriting, paper forms | Smart contract quotes risk from on-chain data |
| 2. Premium Payment | Bank transfer, check, cash | Crypto / stablecoin transfer to escrow |
| 3. Underwriting | Manual risk scoring by actuaries | Algorithmic underwriting via risk-pool smart contract |
| 4. Policy Issuance | PDF document, e-mail | NFT or on-chain policy record (ERC-721 / ERC-1155) |
| 5. Loss Verification | Field agent, surveyor, medical report | Decentralized oracle (Chainlink, UMA, Witnet) |
| 6. Claim Approval | Internal adjuster + manual review | Smart contract auto-execution on oracle trigger |
| 7. Payout | Bank wire, 30–90 day SLA | Atomic on-chain transfer, settlement in **< 5 minutes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight**: Whenever a KTU question asks *"How does blockchain reduce insurance fraud?"*, always anchor your answer to **immutability of the policy record + deterministic smart-contract claim triggers + oracle-driven verification**. These three pillars collectively make fraudulent claims cryptographically and economically infeasible.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Insurance Premium & Risk Math

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are universally used in actuarial science and have direct relevance to blockchain-anchored insurance pools. Master these for both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 2.3.1 Pure Premium (Risk Premium)

$$
P_{\text{pure}} \;=\; \text{Expected Loss} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot S_i
$$

where $p_i$ is the probability of loss event $i$ and $S_i$ is the severity (payout) of that event.

### 2.3.2 Loaded Premium (Commercial Premium)

$$
P_{\text{loaded}} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{pure}}}{1 - L - E}
$$

- $L$ = **Loading factor for insurer profit** (e.g., $0.10$ for 10 % target margin).
- $E$ = **Expense ratio** (administrative + acquisition costs, e.g., $0.20$).

### 2.3.3 Loss Ratio & Combined Ratio

$$
\text{Loss Ratio} \;=\; \frac{\text{Claims Incurred}}{\text{Premiums Earned}}
$$

$$
\text{Combined Ratio} \;=\; \text{Loss Ratio} + \text{Expense Ratio}
$$

> A **Combined Ratio > 1.00** indicates an underwriting *loss*; the insurer is paying out more than it earns in premiums.

### 2.3.4 Reinsurance Pro-Rata (Quota-Share) Formula

$$
\text{Ceded Premium} \;=\; \text{Gross Premium} \times c
$$

$$
\text{Ceded Loss} \;=\; \text{Gross Loss} \times c
$$

where $c$ is the quota-share cession fraction, $0 < c < 1$.

### 2.3.5 Parametric Insurance Payout

$$
\text{Payout} \;=\;
\begin{cases}
S_{\max}, & \text{if } X \ge X_{\text{trigger}} \\
\alpha \cdot (X - X_{\text{base}}), & \text{if } X_{\text{base}} \le X < X_{\text{trigger}} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

- $X$ = observed parameter (e.g., rainfall in mm, earthquake magnitude).
- $X_{\text{base}}$ = baseline below which no payout occurs.
- $X_{\text{trigger}}$ = threshold above which full sum $S_{\max}$ is paid.
- $\alpha$ = graduated payout slope.

### 2.3.6 Capital Pool Solvency (Risk-Pool DAO)

$$
\text{Solvency Ratio} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Pool Capital}}{\text{Open Liability}}
$$

A DAO treasury must maintain **Solvency Ratio $\ge 1.5$** to remain capital-efficient and avoid bank-run dynamics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: Students often write $P_{\text{loaded}} = P_{\text{pure}} \times (1 + L + E)$ in the wrong direction. The correct form is a **division** because the loaded premium must *cover* the pure premium *and* leave room for profit and expenses *on top*. Memorize the **1 - L - E** denominator.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Insurance is foundational to engineering risk management. In *civil engineering*, parametric crop insurance (indexed on satellite rainfall) protects farmers. In *mechanical engineering*, warranty insurance tokenized on blockchain enables machine-as-a-service models. In *computer science*, **smart-contract cover** (Nexus Mutual) insures DeFi users against protocol exploits. In *electrical engineering*, parametric renewable-energy insurance hedges wind-farm output shortfalls.

A blockchain-anchored insurance policy is, in effect, a **smart contract with financial risk semantics** — and therefore sits at the intersection of *distributed systems*, *cryptography*, and *actuarial science*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section builds three worked exam-style derivations that a KTU examiner can adapt for a 14-mark Part B question. Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown, and every line carries an English-language *translation* of the math.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pure Premium from Empirical Loss Data

**Problem Setup (KTU-Style):**
A Kerala-based crop insurer observes the following monsoon-related loss data across 10,000 policies over a 5-year period:

| Loss Severity Band (₹) | Number of Policies Affected | Total Loss (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (no loss) | 9,200 | 0 |
| 1 – 10,000 | 500 | 2,500,000 |
| 10,001 – 50,000 | 250 | 6,250,000 |
| 50,001 – 200,000 | 50 | 5,000,000 |

The blockchain-based parametric product is being designed. Compute the **pure (risk) premium per policy**.

### Step 1 — Total Policies and Total Loss

$$
N_{\text{total}} \;=\; 9200 + 500 + 250 + 50 \;=\; 10{,}000 \text{ policies}
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} \;=\; 0 + 2{,}500{,}000 + 6{,}250{,}000 + 5{,}000{,}000 \;=\; 13{,}750{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

*Translation*: We are simply summing the rows to aggregate the dataset before computing averages.

### Step 2 — Frequency and Severity Components

The pure premium can be decomposed using the **frequency-severity method**:

$$
P_{\text{pure}} \;=\; f \times \bar{S}
$$

where $f$ is the **loss frequency** (fraction of policies that experienced any loss) and $\bar{S}$ is the **average severity** (mean loss *given* a loss occurred).

### Step 3 — Compute Frequency $f$

Number of policies with any loss:

$$
N_{\text{loss}} \;=\; 500 + 250 + 50 \;=\; 800
$$

Frequency:

$$
f \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{loss}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \;=\; \frac{800}{10{,}000} \;=\; 0.08
$$

*Translation*: 8 % of policies experienced a loss event over the 5-year observation window.

### Step 4 — Compute Average Severity $\bar{S}$

$$
\bar{S} \;=\; \frac{L_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{loss}}} \;=\; \frac{13{,}750{,}000}{800} \;=\; 17{,}187.5 \text{ ₹}
$$

*Translation*: When a loss did occur, the average payout was ₹17,187.50 per policy.

### Step 5 — Compute Pure Premium

$$
P_{\text{pure}} \;=\; f \times \bar{S} \;=\; 0.08 \times 17{,}187.5
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{pure}} \;=\; 1375 \text{ ₹ per policy per year}}
$$

*Translation*: To break even on claims alone, the insurer must collect at least ₹1,375 per policy per year.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Loaded Premium with Expense and Profit Loading

**Problem Setup:**
Suppose the insurer targets a **profit margin** $L = 0.10$ and **operational expense ratio** $E = 0.20$. Using the pure premium from §3.1, compute the **loaded commercial premium**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Denominator

$$
D \;=\; 1 - L - E \;=\; 1 - 0.10 - 0.20 \;=\; 0.70
$$

*Translation*: Out of every rupee collected, 70 paise must go to *paying claims*; the remaining 30 paise covers profit and overhead.

### Step 2 — Apply the Loaded Premium Formula

$$
P_{\text{loaded}} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{pure}}}{1 - L - E} \;=\; \frac{1375}{0.70}
$$

$$
P_{\text{loaded}} \;=\; 1964.29 \text{ ₹}
$$

### Step 3 — Sanity Check (Reverse Decomposition)

$$
\text{Claims Share} \;=\; 1964.29 \times 0.70 \;=\; 1375.00 \text{ ₹} \quad\checkmark
$$

$$
\text{Profit Share} \;=\; 1964.29 \times 0.10 \;=\; 196.43 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{Expense Share} \;=\; 1964.29 \times 0.20 \;=\; 392.86 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{Sum} \;=\; 1375.00 + 196.43 + 392.86 \;=\; 1964.29 \text{ ₹} \quad\checkmark
$$

*Translation*: The math is internally consistent. The blockchain-based smart contract would be coded to forward ₹1,375.00 to the *claim pool*, ₹196.43 to the *underwriter profit wallet*, and ₹392.86 to the *operational treasury*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway**: A blockchain smart contract can enforce this exact split *deterministically* via `msg.value` distribution logic, removing any possibility of accounting fraud or misappropriation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parametric Crop Insurance Payout Curve

**Problem Setup:**
A parametric monsoon-crop insurance product is designed for Thrissur district farmers. The smart contract parameters are:

- Baseline rainfall: $X_{\text{base}} = 30$ mm
- Trigger rainfall: $X_{\text{trigger}} = 150$ mm
- Maximum payout: $S_{\max} = \text{₹}50{,}000$
- Graduated slope: $\alpha = 416.67$ ₹/mm
- Observed weekly rainfall: $X = 120$ mm

Compute the **automatic payout**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Region of the Payout Function

We must check the case:

$$
X_{\text{base}} \le X < X_{\text{trigger}} \;\Longrightarrow\; 30 \le 120 < 150
$$

*Translation*: The observation falls in the *graduated* band — partial payout applies.

### Step 2 — Apply the Graduated Payout Formula

$$
\text{Payout} \;=\; \alpha \cdot (X - X_{\text{base}})
$$

$$
\text{Payout} \;=\; 416.67 \times (120 - 30)
$$

$$
\text{Payout} \;=\; 416.67 \times 90
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Payout} \;=\; 37{,}500.30 \text{ ₹}}
$$

*Translation*: A farmer in Thrissur who experienced 120 mm of rainfall in a monitored week will receive an automatic smart-contract payout of ₹37,500.30 — *with no adjuster, no paperwork, and no 30-day waiting period*.

### Step 3 — Verify Boundary Behaviour

If $X = 30$ mm:

$$
\text{Payout} \;=\; 416.67 \times (30 - 30) \;=\; 0 \text{ ₹} \quad\checkmark
$$

If $X = 150$ mm:

$$
\text{Payout} \;=\; 416.67 \times (150 - 30) \;=\; 50{,}000.40 \text{ ₹} \approx S_{\max} \quad\checkmark
$$

*Translation*: The payout function is monotonically non-decreasing, capped at $S_{\max}$ — a desirable no-arbitrage property for any insurance contract.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Smart Contract State Transitions

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix maps the *states* of a blockchain insurance smart contract to the *events* that drive state transitions. This is a high-yield visual that examiners love for 14-mark questions.

| State | Trigger Event | Transition Logic | Next State |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Quoting` | User submits risk parameters | `computePremium(riskScore) → P` | `AwaitingPayment` |
| `AwaitingPayment` | User sends premium to escrow | `escrow.deposit(msg.sender, P)` | `Active` |
| `Active` | Oracle reports `X` | `evaluatePayout(X) → Payout` | `Triggered` or `Expired` |
| `Triggered` | Payout > 0 | `treasury.transfer(policyholder, Payout)` | `Settled` |
| `Expired` | Policy period elapsed | `escrow.refundSurplus()` | `Closed` |
| `Closed` | None (terminal) | — | `Closed` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout**: When writing smart-contract pseudocode on the exam, students often confuse *payable* functions with *view* functions. Premium deposits MUST use `payable`; oracle read functions MUST be `view` or `pure`. Mixing these will cause reverts at deployment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture — Blockchain Insurance Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer1[LAYER 1 - PROTOCOL]
        nodeA[Policyholder Wallet]
        nodeB[Insurer / DAO Treasury]
        nodeC[Smart Contract Engine]
    end

    subgraph Layer2[LAYER 2 - ORACLE & DATA]
        nodeD[Chainlink Oracle Node]
        nodeE[Satellite Rainfall Feed]
        nodeF[IoT Sensor Stream]
        nodeG[Off-Chain Medical API]
    end

    subgraph Layer3[LAYER 3 - VERIFICATION & TRIGGER]
        nodeH[Parametric Trigger Module]
        nodeI[Multi-Sig Claim Validator]
        nodeJ[Fraud Detection Heuristics]
    end

    subgraph Layer4[LAYER 4 - SETTLEMENT]
        nodeK[Stablecoin Payout Engine]
        nodeL[Reinsurance Bridge Contract]
        nodeM[Regulatory Audit Log]
    end

    nodeA -->|premium tx| nodeC
    nodeB -->|capital lock| nodeC
    nodeC -->|query| nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeD -->|verifiable data| nodeH
    nodeH -->|trigger event| nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ -->|approve| nodeK
    nodeK -->|atomic transfer| nodeA
    nodeK -->|ceded share| nodeL
    nodeC -->|immutable log| nodeM
```

*Interpretation*: This five-layer architecture illustrates the full data and value flow of a blockchain-based parametric insurance product. The **Policyholder** sends a premium to the **Smart Contract Engine** (Layer 1). The engine queries the **Chainlink Oracle Node** (Layer 2) which aggregates heterogeneous data sources — satellite rainfall, IoT sensors, off-chain medical APIs. Layer 3 performs parametric trigger evaluation, multi-signature validation, and fraud detection. Layer 4 executes the payout via stablecoins, routes the ceded share to the **reinsurance bridge contract**, and writes an immutable audit log for regulators.

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Parametric Insurance Claim Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PH as Policyholder
    participant SC as Smart Contract
    participant OR as Oracle Network
    participant TR as DAO Treasury
    participant AU as Auditor Node

    PH->>SC: 1. submit risk params
    SC-->>PH: 2. quote premium
    PH->>SC: 3. pay premium (stablecoin)
    SC->>TR: 4. escrow deposit
    SC-->>PH: 5. policy NFT minted
    Note over OR: Continuous monitoring
    OR->>SC: 6. trigger event confirmed
    SC->>SC: 7. evaluatePayout function
    SC->>TR: 8. request payout funds
    TR->>PH: 9. atomic stablecoin transfer
    SC->>AU: 10. emit ClaimSettled event
    AU-->>SC: 11. acknowledge audit receipt
```

*Interpretation*: This sequence diagram captures the temporal flow of a claim. Note the **atomic payout** at step 9 — the stablecoin transfer happens within the same blockchain transaction as the escrow release, eliminating partial-failure states. The **ClaimSettled event** at step 10 is the immutable on-chain receipt that any auditor (or downstream reinsurer) can query.

## 4.3 State Machine — Policy Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Draft
    Draft --> Quoted : risk params submitted
    Quoted --> Funded : premium received
    Funded --> Active : oracle confirm
    Active --> Triggered : parametric event
    Active --> Expired : time elapsed
    Triggered --> Settled : payout executed
    Expired --> Refunded : surplus returned
    Settled --> [*]
    Refunded --> [*]
```

*Interpretation*: The policy exists in exactly one of seven states at any given block height. Transitions are governed by the smart contract's *modifier logic* (e.g., `onlyAfter(block.timestamp)`, `onlyOnOracleConfirmation`). The terminal states are `Settled` (claim paid) and `Refunded` (no claim, surplus returned to pool).

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Centralized vs. Decentralized Insurance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD[TRADITIONAL INSURANCE]
        nodeT1[Policyholder] --> nodeT2[Insurance Agent]
        nodeT2 --> nodeT3[Insurer Core System]
        nodeT3 --> nodeT4[Claim Adjuster]
        nodeT4 --> nodeT5[Bank Payout]
    end

    subgraph BLOCK[BLOCKCHAIN INSURANCE]
        nodeB1[Policyholder] --> nodeB2[Smart Contract]
        nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Oracle]
        nodeB3 --> nodeB2
        nodeB2 --> nodeB4[Atomic Stablecoin Payout]
    end
```

*Interpretation*: The traditional model has **five actors and four intermediaries**; the blockchain model has **four actors and zero intermediaries**. The smart contract *is* the underwriter, the claims processor, and the payout engine — collapsed into a single deterministic code path.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
> Define the following terms in the context of blockchain-enabled insurance: **(i)** Parametric Insurance, **(ii)** Solvency Ratio, **(iii)** Reinsurance.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(i) Parametric Insurance**: A form of insurance where the payout is *automatically triggered* upon the occurrence of a *predefined, objectively measurable parameter* (e.g., rainfall exceeding 150 mm, or an earthquake above magnitude 5.0) — eliminating subjective claim assessment.
- **(ii) Solvency Ratio**: The ratio of a risk pool's total capital to its open (unsettled) liabilities. A ratio $\ge 1.5$ indicates the pool can absorb plausible worst-case losses. Mathematically,
$$
\text{Solvency Ratio} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Pool Capital}}{\text{Open Liability}}
$$
- **(iii) Reinsurance**: The practice by which an insurer *cedes* a portion of its risk to a secondary insurer (the *reinsurer*) in exchange for a *ceded premium*. On blockchain, this is automated via cross-chain *bridge contracts*.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
> List any **three advantages** of using blockchain technology in insurance claim processing over the traditional model.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Reduced Settlement Time** — Smart-contract-driven parametric payouts settle in minutes instead of 30–90 days.
2. **Fraud Mitigation** — Immutable on-chain policy records and oracle-driven verification make fraudulent claims cryptographically and economically infeasible.
3. **Lower Administrative Cost** — Elimination of intermediaries (agents, adjusters, bank processors) reduces the expense ratio from 20–30 % down to 2–5 % in mature decentralized pools.

*(Any three valid advantages with one-line justification = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note**: As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, *exactly one* of the following two questions will appear in the question paper. Both are provided for full preparation.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Explain the **architecture of a blockchain-based parametric insurance system** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of **smart contracts**, **oracles**, and **stablecoins** in the workflow. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A parametric insurance smart contract has the following parameters:
> - $X_{\text{base}} = 40$ mm, $X_{\text{trigger}} = 200$ mm, $S_{\max} =$ ₹80,000
> - Graduated slope $\alpha = 500$ ₹/mm
> - Observed rainfall $X = 160$ mm
>
> Compute the automatic payout. Justify which branch of the payout function applies. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Architecture (7 Marks)

A blockchain-based parametric insurance system is structured into **four logical layers**:

1. **Policy & Premium Layer** — The policyholder connects a Web3 wallet (e.g., MetaMask) and submits risk parameters (location, asset value, coverage period). A smart contract computes a premium using on-chain actuarial formulas and quotes it. Upon premium payment (in stablecoins like USDC), the contract mints a **policy NFT** (ERC-721) representing the active policy.

2. **Data & Oracle Layer** — Continuous external data (rainfall, temperature, flight status, IoT readings) is fed by **decentralized oracle networks** (Chainlink, UMA, Witnet). Oracles aggregate data from multiple independent node operators and produce a *cryptographically signed* on-chain report.

3. **Trigger & Validation Layer** — The smart contract's `evaluatePayout(parameter)` function executes upon receiving the oracle report. If the parameter crosses the trigger threshold, the contract initiates a multi-signature validation sub-routine to prevent single-point manipulation.

4. **Settlement Layer** — On approval, the DAO treasury releases the payout in stablecoins *atomically* within a single transaction. The `ClaimSettled` event is emitted to the public ledger for regulator and reinsurer auditability.

**Roles Recap:**
- **Smart Contracts** — Encode policy logic, premium math, and trigger conditions deterministically.
- **Oracles** — Bridge off-chain real-world data to on-chain logic without trusting a single data source.
- **Stablecoins** — Provide price-stable settlement, avoiding crypto-volatility risk for the policyholder.

*(Diagram from §4.1 of these notes may be redrawn on the answer sheet for full marks — 2 marks for the diagram, 5 marks for the textual explanation.)*

##### Part (b) — Payout Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1** — Identify the applicable branch of the payout function.

We must check whether:

- $X < X_{\text{base}} = 40$ → *No payout* branch.
- $X_{\text{base}} \le X < X_{\text{trigger}}$ → *Graduated payout* branch.
- $X \ge X_{\text{trigger}} = 200$ → *Maximum payout* branch.

Given $X = 160$ mm:

$$
40 \le 160 < 200
$$

The observation falls in the **graduated payout band**.

**Step 2** — Apply the graduated formula.

$$
\text{Payout} \;=\; \alpha \cdot (X - X_{\text{base}})
$$

$$
\text{Payout} \;=\; 500 \times (160 - 40)
$$

$$
\text{Payout} \;=\; 500 \times 120
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Payout} \;=\; 60{,}000 \text{ ₹}}
$$

**Step 3** — Verification.

Sanity-check the boundary: at $X = 200$ mm, payout would be $500 \times 160 = 80{,}000 = S_{\max}$ ✓. At $X = 40$ mm, payout is $0$ ✓. The function is monotonically increasing and capped — economically sound.

**Conclusion:** The Thrissur-based farmer's smart contract will auto-execute a stablecoin payout of **₹60,000** with no human intervention.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **limitations and risks** of blockchain-based insurance systems. Compare **decentralized peer-to-peer insurance (DAOs)** with the **traditional centralized insurer model** across at least four dimensions. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An insurer collects an annual pure premium of ₹2,000 per policy. The expense ratio is 0.18 and the target profit loading is 0.07. Compute the **loaded commercial premium** and show the **reverse decomposition** into claims, profit, and expense shares. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Comparison & Risks (7 Marks)

**Comparison Matrix:**

| Dimension | Traditional Insurer | Blockchain DAO / P2P |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Model** | Trust in regulator + brand reputation | Trustless — enforced by code and consensus |
| **Capital Efficiency** | Low (locked in centralized reserves) | High (capital deployable across DeFi yield protocols) |
| **Transparency** | Opaque — internal ledgers not public | Full transparency — every tx on public chain |
| **Claim Speed** | 30–90 days | Minutes (atomic smart-contract payout) |
| **Scalability** | High (centrally managed) | Limited by blockchain throughput (L2 scaling needed) |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Established (IRDAI, FEMA, GDPR) | Evolving — uncertain in many jurisdictions |
| **Fraud Resistance** | Vulnerable to insider fraud | Cryptographically enforced immutability |

**Limitations & Risks:**
1. **Oracle Risk** — If the oracle feed is compromised or stale, parametric triggers may activate spuriously or fail to fire.
2. **Smart Contract Bugs** — A code vulnerability (reentrancy, integer overflow) can drain an entire pool.
3. **Regulatory Uncertainty** — Many jurisdictions have not yet classified on-chain insurance pools as legal insurance vehicles.
4. **Scalability Bottleneck** — Layer-1 chains (Ethereum mainnet) process ~15–30 tx/sec, insufficient for high-frequency micro-insurance.
5. **Custodial Risk** — Policyholders must self-custody private keys; loss of keys = loss of policy benefits.

*(Allocate 3 marks for the comparison matrix and 4 marks for the limitations discussion.)*

##### Part (b) — Loaded Premium Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1** — Identify the inputs.

$$
P_{\text{pure}} \;=\; 2000 \text{ ₹}, \quad L \;=\; 0.07, \quad E \;=\; 0.18
$$

**Step 2** — Compute the denominator.

$$
D \;=\; 1 - L - E \;=\; 1 - 0.07 - 0.18 \;=\; 0.75
$$

**Step 3** — Apply the loaded premium formula.

$$
P_{\text{loaded}} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{pure}}}{1 - L - E} \;=\; \frac{2000}{0.75}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{loaded}} \;\approx\; 2666.67 \text{ ₹}}
$$

**Step 4** — Reverse decomposition.

$$
\text{Claims Share} \;=\; 2666.67 \times 0.75 \;=\; 2000.00 \text{ ₹} \quad\checkmark
$$

$$
\text{Profit Share} \;=\; 2666.67 \times 0.07 \;\approx\; 186.67 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{Expense Share} \;=\; 2666.67 \times 0.18 \;\approx\; 480.00 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5** — Verification.

$$
2000.00 + 186.67 + 480.00 \;=\; 2666.67 \text{ ₹} \quad\checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The insurer must quote a commercial premium of **₹2,666.67** per policy. Of this, ₹2,000.00 funds the claim pool, ₹186.67 is profit, and ₹480.00 covers administrative costs. A blockchain smart contract can enforce this split deterministically.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Diagram Missing in (a)**: Students who write only text in architectural questions lose 1–2 marks. Always include a *neat labelled block diagram* — even a hand-drawn one — alongside the explanation.
> 2. **Wrong Branch Selection**: In parametric payout questions, the *first step must be* identifying which branch of the piecewise function applies. Examiners award 2 marks just for that branch identification, before any arithmetic.
> 3. **Unit Omission**: Always write units (₹, %, mm) in your final answers. A numerically correct value without units is docked **0.5 marks** per occurrence.
> 4. **Formula Mis-Memorization**: Mixing up $1 - L - E$ with $1 + L + E$ is the single most common error in premium calculations. Drill the *denominator* form repeatedly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember — High-Density Revision**

- ☐ **Insurance is risk pooling**, not risk elimination. Blockchain enhances *delivery*, not the *math*.
- ☐ The **three pillars** of blockchain insurance are: **Smart Contracts + Oracles + Tokenized Money**.
- ☐ The **Pure Premium** equals *expected loss* = frequency × severity.
- ☐ The **Loaded Premium** formula is $P = P_{\text{pure}} / (1 - L - E)$ — memorize the **denominator** form.
- ☐ **Parametric insurance** uses an *objective, verifiable trigger*; subjective claim assessment is eliminated.
- ☐ The piecewise payout function has **three branches**: zero, graduated, and capped.
- ☐ **Combined Ratio** = Loss Ratio + Expense Ratio; values $> 1.00$ mean underwriting loss.
- ☐ **Reinsurance** is *risk-of-risk* — insurers insure themselves against catastrophic claim spikes.
- ☐ **Solvency Ratio** for a DAO insurance pool should remain $\ge 1.5$ for capital adequacy.
- ☐ **Key risks**: Oracle manipulation, smart-contract bugs, regulatory uncertainty, key-custody loss.
- ☐ **Real-world examples**: *Nexus Mutual* (smart-contract cover), *Etherisc* (flight-delay parametric), *Lemonade* (InsurTech hybrid), *B3i* (industry consortium).
- ☐ **Settlement time compression** is the headline value proposition: 30–90 days → minutes.
- ☐ Always show **boundary verification** in parametric payout questions (check the function at $X_{\text{base}}$ and $X_{\text{trigger}}$).
- ☐ **Stablecoins** (USDC, DAI) are used in payouts to avoid the volatility of native crypto assets.
- ☐ A policy can be tokenized as an **ERC-721 NFT**, making it tradable, composable, and auditable.
- ☐ **Mermaid diagram nodes** must never use reserved words like `end` as IDs — use `node1`, `stepA`, etc.
- ☐ For 14-mark answers, always include a *neat diagram* + *labelled explanation* + *worked numerical example*.
- ☐ Examiner gold phrases: *"immutable audit trail"*, *"trustless automation"*, *"atomic settlement"*, *"oracle-driven verification"*, *"decentralized risk pool"*.
- ☐ Module 4's "Insurance" topic bridges Module 3 (smart contracts) with Module 5 (decentralized finance) — know the *cross-module linkages*.

---

*End of Module 4 — Insurance (KTU 2024 Scheme, PECCT522).*

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a "Case Study" in the Context of Blockchain?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> A **Blockchain Case Study** is a structured, analytical examination of a real-world or simulated distributed ledger implementation, focusing on its architecture, consensus mechanism, transaction flow, governance model, performance metrics, and business value. In the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 4, a case study integrates the technical primitives learned in Modules 1–3 (hashing, Merkle trees, digital signatures, consensus) and evaluates their application in a production-grade system such as Bitcoin, Ethereum, Hyperledger Fabric, or an enterprise DLT deployment.

## 1.2 Intuition — The "Engineering Audit Report" Analogy

Imagine you have just been appointed as a **Junior Blockchain Engineer** at a consultancy firm. Your manager hands you the complete technical documentation of a system that *already exists in production* — a cross-border payment network, a hospital records vault, or a public cryptocurrency — and asks: *"Explain to a non-technical board of directors why this system works, what makes it secure, where it is slow, and what trade-offs were made."*

That is exactly what a **case study** is in this module: a **post-implementation forensic analysis** of a blockchain system. It is *not* a new design; it is a **deconstruction** of a working system using the theoretical tools you have already learned.

| Real-World Role | Mapping to Blockchain Case Study |
| :--- | :--- |
| Architectural blueprints | Block structure, transaction format, state model |
| Electrical wiring diagram | P2P gossip protocol, consensus algorithm |
| Building inspector's report | Performance metrics (TPS, latency, finality) |
| Tenant complaints | Identified limitations, attack vectors |
| Renovation history | Hard forks, governance upgrades |

## 1.3 Why Case Studies Matter in the KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (NEP 2020 Alignment)**
> Module 4 case studies are designed to achieve the following Course Outcomes (COs):
> - **CO4**: *Analyze real-world blockchain implementations and evaluate their architectural trade-offs.*
> - **CO5**: *Apply distributed ledger concepts to domain-specific problems and articulate the business, security, and performance implications.*
>
> The KTU valuation scheme explicitly tests the student's ability to **synthesize** (Bloom's Level 5) and **evaluate** (Bloom's Level 6) — case studies are the primary instrument for this.

## 1.4 The "Three Pillars" Framework of a Blockchain Case Study

Every case study, regardless of domain, can be decomposed into three pillars:

1. **Technical Pillar** — Consensus, data structure, cryptography, network topology.
2. **Business Pillar** — Use case motivation, stakeholders, token economics, ROI.
3. **Governance Pillar** — Who decides upgrades? How are disputes resolved? Is the system permissioned or permissionless?

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic**
> If a KTU answer on a case study covers **only** the technical pillar, it will be capped at ~60% of the marks. A full-marks answer must address **at least two of the three pillars** with equal depth.

## 1.5 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) You Must Memorize

These standard metrics appear in nearly every blockchain case study and are **bold-marked** for memorization:

- **TPS (Transactions Per Second)** — throughput.
- **TTF (Time to Finality)** — time until a transaction is irreversible.
- **Block Time** — average interval between block generation.
- **Energy Consumption (kWh/year)** — relevant for PoW systems.
- **Decentralization Index** — Nakamoto Coefficient, Gini coefficient of stake.
- **Cost per Transaction (USD)** — total fees paid by the user.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Universal 7-Layer Case Study Analysis Model

When a KTU question asks you to "Discuss the architecture of a blockchain case study", use this exhaustive 7-layer model. It is the **gold standard** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE answers.

### Layer 1 — Application Layer
- Smart contracts (Solidity, Chaincode, Move, Rust).
- DApp front-ends (Web3.js, Ethers.js).
- Oracles (Chainlink, Band Protocol) for off-chain data.

### Layer 2 — Execution Layer
- **EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine)** for Ethereum.
- **WASM (WebAssembly)** for Polkadot, Near.
- **Deterministic execution** guarantees.

### Layer 3 — Consensus Layer
- **PoW** (Bitcoin, pre-merge Ethereum).
- **PoS** (Ethereum 2.0, Cardano).
- **PBFT / Raft** (Hyperledger Fabric, Quorum).
- **PoA** (VeChain, Kovan testnet).

### Layer 4 — Networking Layer
- **Gossip protocol** (Bitcoin, Ethereum).
- **Gossip-vote** (Tendermint).
- **Peer discovery** (Kademlia DHT).

### Layer 5 — Data Layer
- **Block structure**: header + transaction counter + transactions.
- **Merkle Patricia Trie** (Ethereum state).
- **UTXO model** (Bitcoin) vs **Account model** (Ethereum).

### Layer 6 — Incentive Layer
- Block reward (subsidy + fees).
- Slashing conditions (PoS).
- Tokenomics (inflation, burn mechanisms).

### Layer 7 — Governance Layer
- **Off-chain governance** (Bitcoin BIPs, Ethereum EIPs).
- **On-chain governance** (Tezos, Polkadot OpenGov).
- Foundation-based (Ethereum Foundation), DAO-based (Uniswap, MakerDAO).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula, equation, and metric** you are expected to recall for case study questions in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Used In Case Study |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **SHA-256 Hash** | $H(M) = \text{SHA-256}(M)$ | $256$ bits, hex output | Bitcoin, most chains |
| 2 | **Block Hash** | $H_{block} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(Header))$ | $256$ bits | Bitcoin |
| 3 | **Mining Difficulty** | $D_{new} = D_{old} \times \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{target}}$ | dimensionless, re-adjusted every $2016$ blocks | Bitcoin PoW |
| 4 | **Target Threshold** | $T = \frac{T_{max}}{D}$ where $T_{max} = 2^{224} - 1$ | large integer | Bitcoin |
| 5 | **ECDSA Signature** | $(r, s)$ where $s = k^{-1}(H(m) + r \cdot d_A) \mod n$ | $512$ bits | All major chains |
| 6 | **Merkle Root** | $R = H(H(A \Vert B) \Vert H(C \Vert D))$ | $256$ bits | Bitcoin block header |
| 7 | **Block Reward (Halving)** | $R_n = 50 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\lfloor n / 210000 \rfloor}$ BTC | halves every $210000$ blocks ($\approx 4$ years) | Bitcoin |
| 8 | **Total Bitcoin Supply** | $S_{total} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 50 \times 210000 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} = 21000000$ BTC | cap $\approx 21$ million | Bitcoin |
| 9 | **TPS (Theoretical)** | $TPS = \frac{\text{Block Size}}{\text{Block Time} \times \text{Tx Size}}$ | e.g., $\frac{1 \text{ MB}}{600 \text{ s} \times 250 \text{ B}} \approx 7$ | Bitcoin |
| 10 | **PoS Staking Yield** | $Y_{annual} \approx \frac{R_{emission}}{S_{total\_staked}}$ | typically $4$–$8\%$ | Ethereum 2.0 |
| 11 | **Nakamoto Coefficient** | $N_c = \min \{ n \mid \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Stake}_i > 50\% \}$ | higher = more decentralized | All PoS chains |
| 12 | **PBFT Fault Tolerance** | $f = \lfloor (n-1) / 3 \rfloor$ | tolerates $f$ Byzantine nodes out of $3f+1$ | Hyperledger Fabric |
| 13 | **Gas Used by Tx** | $G_{tx} = \sum_{op} G_{op}$ where $G_{op}$ is opcode gas cost | measured in **gwei** | Ethereum |
| 14 | **Transaction Fee** | $F_{tx} = G_{tx} \times P_{gas}$ where $P_{gas}$ is gas price in gwei | ETH | Ethereum |
| 15 | **Finality Time (PBFT)** | $T_{finality} = 2 \times \Delta$ where $\Delta$ is network RTT | milliseconds to seconds | Hyperledger |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formatting Note**: In the table above, the symbol `\Vert` is used instead of the vertical pipe `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In your exam scripts, you may freely use `|` for concatenation.

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Layers

> [!TIP]
> The reason case studies feel overwhelming to freshers is that they try to memorize features. Instead, ask **why** each layer exists.

- **Why an Execution Layer?** Because consensus only agrees on *bytes*; an execution layer interprets those bytes as state transitions.
- **Why a separate Incentive Layer?** Because in a permissionless network, the only way to make honest behavior rational is **cryptoeconomics**.
- **Why governance is "Layer 7"?** Because without an upgrade mechanism, the protocol is a **digital fossil** — unable to adapt to new threats or requirements.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 7-layer model is not academic theatre. **Real industry adoption frameworks** such as the *Linux Foundation's Hyperledger Design Philosophy*, *Ethereum Enterprise Alliance's Reference Architecture*, and the *NIST Blockchain Technology Overview (NISTIR 8202)* all use similar layered decompositions. A KTU student who masters this model can immediately read any whitepaper, audit any smart contract system, and contribute to enterprise DLT projects.

## 2.5 Comparative Analysis: Public vs Permissioned Case Studies

| Dimension | Public (Bitcoin, Ethereum) | Permissioned (Hyperledger, Corda) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Participants** | Anonymous, open | KYC-verified, invited |
| **Consensus** | PoW / PoS (Sybil-resistant via resource) | PBFT / Raft (Sybil-resistant via identity) |
| **Throughput** | $7$–$3000$ TPS | $1000$–$20000$ TPS |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic (Bitcoin: $\approx 6$ blocks) | Deterministic (immediate after block commit) |
| **Privacy** | Pseudonymous, transparent | Confidential channels, zero-knowledge proofs |
| **Energy** | High (PoW) / Moderate (PoS) | Low |
| **Use Case Fit** | Currency, public DeFi | B2B supply chain, interbank settlement |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivations Required for KTU 2024 Case Study Questions

### 3.1.1 Derivation: Bitcoin's Total Supply Cap of 21 Million

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** that combines a geometric series with the halving rule.

**Step 1: Establish the halving schedule.**
The Bitcoin protocol halves the block reward every $210000$ blocks, which corresponds to roughly every $4$ years (since the target block time is $10$ minutes).

$$
R_n = 50 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n \quad \text{(BTC, where } n \text{ is the halving epoch)}
$$

**Step 2: Number of blocks per halving epoch.**
With a target block time of $10$ minutes, i.e., $600$ seconds, the number of blocks in four years is calculated as:

$$
N_{epoch} = 4 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 60 \times \frac{1}{10} = 210384 \approx 210000 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3: BTC produced per epoch.**
The total BTC mined in epoch $n$ is the product of the reward and the number of blocks per epoch:

$$
B_n = R_n \times N_{epoch} = 50 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n \times 210000 \text{ BTC}
$$

**Step 4: Sum over all halving epochs.**
The total supply is the infinite geometric sum:

$$
S_{total} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} B_n = 210000 \times 50 \times \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n
$$

**Step 5: Apply the geometric series formula.**
Using $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n = \frac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2}} = 2
$$

**Step 6: Final evaluation.**

$$
S_{total} = 210000 \times 50 \times 2 = 21000000 \text{ BTC} = 21 \text{ million BTC}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common student error is to forget that the geometric series converges because $\vert r \vert = 0.5 < 1$. If you stop the sum at epoch $n = 32$ (when $R_n$ rounds to zero), you correctly get the practical supply of $\approx 20\,999\,999.98$ BTC. Always state the convergence condition explicitly.

### 3.1.2 Derivation: Merkle Root Construction

This is the data-structure backbone of every Bitcoin-style case study.

**Step 1: Take an even number of transaction hashes.**
Let $H_A, H_B, H_C, H_D$ be the SHA-256 hashes of four transactions $A, B, C, D$.

**Step 2: Hash pairs in the first level.**

$$
H_{AB} = \text{SHA-256}(H_A \Vert H_B)
$$

$$
H_{CD} = \text{SHA-256}(H_C \Vert H_D)
$$

**Step 3: Hash the pair of pairs to form the root.**

$$
R = \text{SHA-256}(H_{AB} \Vert H_{CD})
$$

**Step 4: Generalization to $N$ transactions.**
For $N$ transactions, the height of the Merkle tree is $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$. The verification path (Merkle proof) for any one transaction is then exactly $\log_2 N$ hashes, which is why SPV (Simplified Payment Verification) is possible.

$$
\text{Proof size} = \log_2 N \text{ hashes} = 32 \cdot \log_2 N \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a block with $3000$ transactions, the Merkle proof is $\log_2 3000 \approx 11.55$, so it requires $12$ hashes — only **$384$ bytes** to prove inclusion. This is why light clients can run on mobile phones.

### 3.1.3 Derivation: Difficulty Adjustment Calculation

A frequent KTU numerical: given the time taken for the last $2016$ blocks, find the new difficulty.

**Given:** Target time = $2016 \times 10 \text{ min} = 20160 \text{ min}$ (i.e., $2$ weeks). Actual time taken = $T_{actual}$ minutes. Old difficulty = $D_{old}$.

**Formula:**

$$
D_{new} = D_{old} \times \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{target}}
$$

**Bounded** to a factor of $4$ in either direction to prevent extreme volatility:

$$
\frac{1}{4} \cdot D_{old} \le D_{new} \le 4 \cdot D_{old}
$$

**Example:** Suppose $T_{actual} = 30240$ min (the network was slow, taking $3$ weeks).

$$
D_{new} = D_{old} \times \frac{30240}{20160} = D_{old} \times 1.5
$$

The new target threshold becomes $T_{new} = T_{old} / 1.5$, meaning the mining puzzle becomes $50\%$ harder, restoring the $10$-minute average.

### 3.1.4 Derivation: Nakamoto Coefficient for Stake Distribution

**Given** a PoS network with $10$ validators holding stake percentages $[28, 22, 15, 12, 8, 6, 4, 3, 1, 1]$.

**Step 1: Sort in descending order (already sorted).**
**Step 2: Accumulate until the sum exceeds $50\%$.**

$$
28 \rightarrow 28\%; \quad 28 + 22 = 50\%; \quad 50 + 15 = 65\% > 50\%
$$

**Step 3:** The minimum number of validators required to exceed $50\%$ is $3$. Hence:

$$
N_c = 3
$$

A Nakamoto coefficient of $3$ indicates a relatively centralized network, since only $3$ validators could collude to halt the chain.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: The "Big Five" KTU Case Studies

This is the **master comparison table** that you should reproduce from memory in any KTU 14-mark case study question. It is the single highest-yield artifact of Module 4.

| **TPS (Theoretical)** | $\approx 7$ | $\approx 30$–$100$ (L1), $\approx 3000$ (L2 rollups) | $\approx 3000$–$20000$ | $\approx 1000$ | $\approx 65000$ |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic ($\approx 60$ min) | $\approx 12$–$15$ min | Deterministic, $\approx 2$ s | Deterministic | $\approx 5$–$10$ min (checkpoint) |
| **Data Model** | UTXO | Account-based | Key-value + CouchDB | UTXO-like (states) | Account-based (EVM-compatible) |
| **Smart Contracts** | Script (limited) | Solidity, Vyper, Yul | Chaincode (Go, Java, Node.js) | CorDapp (Kotlin/Java) | Solidity (EVM-equivalent) |
| **Privacy** | Pseudonymous | Pseudonymous | Private channels | Confidential (need-to-know) | Pseudonymous |

| **Nakamoto Coef. (2024)** | $\approx 4$ (mining pools) | $\approx 3$ (Lido + exchanges) | $\approx 2$ (consortium) | $\approx 3$ (notaries) | $\approx 5$ |

## 3.3 Worked Example: A Complete KTU-Style Case Study Walkthrough

**Problem:** *"Analyze the Bitcoin blockchain as a case study, covering its architecture, consensus, transaction lifecycle, and at least two limitations."* (14 marks)

**Step 1 — Introduction (2 marks).**
Bitcoin, proposed by Satoshi Nakamoto in $2008$ and launched in $2009$, is a decentralized peer-to-peer electronic cash system. It is the first practical solution to the **double-spend problem** without a trusted central authority.

**Step 2 — Architecture (3 marks).**
Apply the 7-layer model: Application (wallets, block explorers), Execution (Script — limited, no loops), Consensus (Nakamoto PoW), Network (gossip over TCP/8333), Data (block header + Merkle root + tx list), Incentive (block reward + fees), Governance (BIP process + Bitcoin Core maintainers).

**Step 3 — Consensus (3 marks).**
PoW: Miners iterate the **nonce** in the block header such that $\text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(Header)) < T$ where $T$ is the target. The difficulty $D$ adjusts every $2016$ blocks as derived in §3.1.3.

**Step 4 — Transaction Lifecycle (3 marks).**
- **Creation:** User signs a transaction with their private key $d_A$ via ECDSA: $(r, s) = \text{Sign}(d_A, H(m))$.
- **Broadcast:** Sent to neighboring nodes via gossip.
- **Mempool:** Each node validates (format, signatures, UTXO existence) and holds in a memory pool.
- **Mining:** A miner selects transactions, assembles a Merkle tree, and searches for a valid nonce.
- **Confirmation:** On finding a valid PoW, the block is broadcast. Receivers accept the block if all transactions are valid. Six confirmations ($\approx 60$ min) provide probabilistic finality with probability of reversal $< 0.001\%$.

**Step 5 — Limitations (3 marks).**
- **Throughput bottleneck:** $TPS \approx 7$ versus Visa's $\approx 65000$.
- **Energy consumption:** $\approx 200$ TWh/year — comparable to the annual electricity use of Poland.
- **Privacy weakness:** All transactions are publicly traceable; chain-analysis firms (Chainalysis) routinely de-anonymize users.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfall:** Students often list limitations without quantifying them. A KTU examiner rewards **specific numbers** ($7$ TPS, $200$ TWh, $6$ confirmations) over vague statements ("Bitcoin is slow"). Always include numbers.

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Cross-Chain Bridge Lock-and-Mint Model

Many enterprise case studies (Polkadot, Cosmos, WORMHOLE) use a **lock-and-mint** bridge. The formula for the bridge's solvency invariant is:

$$
L_{source} = \sum_{i} M_{i, \text{wrapped}}
$$

where $L_{source}$ is the total tokens locked in the source-chain bridge contract, and $M_{i, \text{wrapped}}$ is the supply of wrapped tokens issued on destination chains. A bridge is **solvent** if and only if:

$$
L_{source} \ge \sum_{i} M_{i, \text{wrapped}}
$$

If this invariant is violated (e.g., the $2022$ Wormhole exploit of $\$\,320$ million), the wrapped token de-pegs and the bridge fails.

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end flow** of a transaction through the $7$ layers of a typical case study (Bitcoin/Ethereum/Enterprise). It is a "block-level functional architecture" rather than a physical drawing, exactly as the KTU specification permits for complex systems.

```mermaid
    consL -->|6. Order Txs into Block| incentL[Incentive Layer: Reward + Fees]
    incentL -->|7. Append Block to Chain| govL[Governance Layer: BIP EIP Voting]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Transaction Lifecycle State Machine (Ethereum Case Study)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unknown: Wallet Created
    Unknown --> Pending: Tx Signed and Broadcast
    Pending --> Queued: In Mempool Awaiting Validator
    Queued --> Pending: Re-broadcast or Fee Bumped
    Queued --> Included: Selected by Block Proposer
    Included --> SoftConfirmed: 1 Block Confirmation
    SoftConfirmed --> Finalized: 2 Epochs ~ 12.8 min
    Finalized --> [*]: Irreversible
    Pending --> Dropped: Underpriced or Replaced
    Dropped --> [*]: Tx Discarded
    Included --> Reverted: Smart Contract Revert or Reorg
    Reverted --> [*]: State Rolled Back
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the state diagram:** Each arrow is a state transition. A transaction can transition back from `Queued` to `Pending` if it is **re-broadcast with a higher gas price (Replace-By-Fee)**. After `Finalized`, the transaction is **cryptographically irreversible** under Casper FFG finality rules.

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Hyperledger Fabric Transaction Flow (Enterprise Case Study)

Hyperledger's **execute-order-validate** architecture is one of the most frequently asked case studies. The diagram below shows the **3-phase commit**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    client[Client App] -->|1. Tx Proposal| endorse[Endorsing Peers<br/>Execute Chaincode]
    endorse -->|2. Endorsement Response| client
    client -->|3. Submit Tx with<br/>Endorsements| orderer[Ordering Service<br/>Kafka / Raft]
    orderer -->|4. Batch into Block| committer[Committing Peers<br/>Validate and Commit]
    committer -->|5. Tx Valid| ledger[Shared Ledger + State DB]
    committer -->|6. Tx Invalid| reject[Tx Marked Invalid]
    ledger -->|7. Event Notification| client
```
