# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions are short-answer or definition type. They test Bloom's Levels 1–3 (Remember, Understand, Apply). Each answer should be 3–5 sentences with at least one numerical or formulaic reference.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a blockchain case study. List the three pillars used to analyze any blockchain implementation.**

**Model Answer:**
A blockchain case study is a structured analytical examination of a real or simulated distributed ledger system, evaluating its architecture, performance, security, and business value. The three pillars are: **(1) Technical Pillar** — covering consensus, data structures, and cryptography; **(2) Business Pillar** — covering use case, stakeholders, and token economics; **(3) Governance Pillar** — covering upgrade mechanisms, dispute resolution, and permissioning model. A complete KTU answer must address at least two pillars with equal depth to secure full marks.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the Nakamoto Coefficient? With an example stake distribution, compute it.**

**Model Answer:**
The Nakamoto Coefficient $N_c$ is the minimum number of top validators whose combined stake (or hash power) exceeds $50\%$ of the total. It quantifies decentralization. For example, with stake distribution $[35\%, 25\%, 20\%, 10\%, 5\%, 5\%]$, the cumulative sum is $35\% \rightarrow 60\%$ after the second validator, so $N_c = 2$. A coefficient of $1$ means a single entity controls the network (centralized); a value $> 5$ is considered robustly decentralized.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries $14$ marks and is split into sub-parts typically worth $7 + 7$. Students must answer **either** OR. Internal choice requires two completely independent, non-overlapping alternatives.

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a suitable case study, explain the **7-layer reference architecture** of a public blockchain. Discuss the role of the consensus layer in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the Bitcoin network. The last $2016$ blocks took $25200$ minutes to mine. Given the target time of $20160$ minutes, compute the new difficulty. If a miner with $0.1\%$ of the network hash power attempts a block, what is the probability of finding it within $10$ minutes? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

The **7-layer reference architecture** decomposes any blockchain case study as follows:

1. **Application Layer** — User-facing DApps, wallets, and block explorers.
2. **Execution Layer** — Runtime that interprets transactions (e.g., **EVM** in Ethereum, **Script** in Bitcoin).
3. **Consensus Layer** — Agreement protocol (PoW, PoS, PBFT).
4. **Network Layer** — P2P **gossip protocol** for transaction and block propagation.
5. **Data Layer** — Block structure, **Merkle trees**, state database (UTXO or account model).
6. **Incentive Layer** — Block reward, transaction fees, and slashing conditions.
7. **Governance Layer** — Off-chain or on-chain decision-making for protocol upgrades.

**Detailed Role of the Consensus Layer:**
The consensus layer ensures that all honest nodes maintain an **identical, append-only ledger** despite the presence of malicious or faulty nodes. In Bitcoin's **Nakamoto Consensus** (PoW), miners compete to solve a cryptographic puzzle. The first miner to find a nonce $n$ such that $\text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(Header)) < T$ broadcasts the block. Other nodes **accept the longest valid chain** (with most accumulated work), providing probabilistic finality. The consensus layer is the **trust anchor** of the entire system; without it, double-spending would be trivial.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1: Compute the new difficulty.**
Given: $T_{actual} = 25200$ min, $T_{target} = 20160$ min.

$$
D_{new} = D_{old} \times \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{target}} = D_{old} \times \frac{25200}{20160} = D_{old} \times 1.25
$$

**Step 2: Check the $4\times$ bound.**
Since $1.25 < 4$, the new difficulty is **valid** and equals $1.25 \cdot D_{old}$.

**Step 3: Compute the miner's expected time per block.**
A miner with $0.1\%$ of the hash power finds a block with probability $p = 0.001$ per network block. The network's average block time is $10$ minutes. Therefore, the miner's expected time per block found is:

$$
E[T_{miner}] = \frac{10 \text{ min}}{0.001} = 10000 \text{ min} \approx 6.94 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4: Probability of finding a block within $10$ minutes.**
In a memoryless process (geometric distribution), the probability of at least one success in $k = 10$ minutes ($\approx 1$ network block) is:

$$
P(\text{success within } 10 \text{ min}) = 1 - (1 - p)^k = 1 - (0.999)^1 = 0.001 = 0.1\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the miner's hash power with the *cumulative* probability. The correct framework is **Poisson process**: $P(X \ge 1 \mid \lambda t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$, where $\lambda$ is the miner's block-finding rate.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Analyze **Hyperledger Fabric** as a case study. Explain its **execute-order-validate** architecture, channel concept, and why it is preferred for enterprise B2B use cases over Ethereum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A supply chain consortium uses Hyperledger Fabric with $5$ organizations, each running $2$ peer nodes, and the **Raft** consensus with a tolerance of $f = 1$ faulty node. Compute the total number of nodes in the network, the maximum Byzantine nodes tolerated, and the practical TPS limit if each peer can process $4000$ TPS. Discuss one privacy advantage Fabric offers over public chains. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Hyperledger Fabric** is a permissioned, modular blockchain maintained by the Linux Foundation. Unlike Ethereum's order-execute model, Fabric uses an **execute-order-validate** architecture, which decouples transaction execution from ordering — solving the **scalability bottleneck** of public chains.

**Execute-Order-Validate Phases:**
1. **Execute Phase:** The client sends a transaction proposal to **endorsing peers**, which simulate the chaincode execution and return an **endorsement signature** along with the read-write set. Crucially, *no ledger state is mutated yet*.
2. **Order Phase:** The client submits the endorsed transaction to the **ordering service** (Kafka or Raft), which sequences transactions into blocks. The ordering service is a **crash-fault-tolerant** (CFT) cluster by default; BFT is optional.
3. **Validate Phase:** All **committing peers** independently validate the endorsements, the version dependencies (to detect conflicts), and apply the state changes to the ledger.

**Channel Concept:**
A **channel** is a private sub-network within Fabric that allows a subset of organizations to transact confidentially. Each channel has its **own ledger**; a peer can join multiple channels. This enables multi-lateral B2B networks where competitors share infrastructure but not data.

**Why Preferred Over Ethereum for B2B:**
- **Permissioned access** with KYC/identity controls.
- **Confidential transactions** via private data collections and channels.
- **High throughput** ($3000$–$20000$ TPS vs Ethereum's $\sim 30$ TPS).
- **Pluggable consensus** (Raft, Kafka, BFT) versus Ethereum's monolithic PoS.
- **No volatile cryptocurrency** — no gas fees, no miner front-running.

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Step 1: Total number of nodes.**
With $5$ organizations, each running $2$ peer nodes:

$$
N_{peers} = 5 \times 2 = 10 \text{ peer nodes}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum Byzantine nodes tolerated.**
For Raft (CFT) with $f = 1$, the cluster size is $2f + 1 = 3$ orderer nodes. Raft tolerates **crash faults** only. If BFT consensus (e.g., SmartBFT) is used, then $f = \lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$ Byzantine nodes can be tolerated among the orderer cluster.

**Step 3: Practical TPS limit.**
If each peer can process $4000$ TPS, and the endorsement policy requires endorsement from $2$ peers per transaction (a common default), then the throughput is bounded by the **slowest of**:

- Endorsement throughput: $10 \text{ peers} \times 4000 \text{ TPS} / 2 \text{ endorsers} = 20000 \text{ TPS}$.
- Ordering throughput (Kafka cluster): typically $\approx 10000$ TPS.
- Commit throughput: limited by the ledger DB write rate.

The **bottleneck** is the ordering service, so the practical TPS limit is $\approx 10000$ TPS.

**Step 4: Privacy advantage.**
Fabric supports **Private Data Collections (PDCs)**, which allow a transaction to be shared among a specified subset of organizations **without** including the data in the channel-wide ledger. The data is disseminated via the **gossip protocol** only to authorized peers. This is fundamentally different from public chains where *all* data is replicated to *all* nodes. A practical example: a manufacturer can share unit pricing with a specific supplier while keeping it hidden from competitors on the same channel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In Q.A(b), if a student computes $D_{new} = D_{old} \times (20160/25200)$, they have **inverted the fraction** — this is a frequent error. The new difficulty must **increase** when the network is slow, i.e., when $T_{actual} > T_{target}$.
> - In Q.B(b), students often forget that the **endorsement policy** is the real bottleneck, not raw peer capacity. Mentioning "endorsement policy" or "read-write conflict detection (VSCC)" is essential to score full marks.
> - **Common mark-loss areas:** (i) Forgetting to mention **channels** in Fabric; (ii) calling Fabric "Ethereum for enterprises" (it is **not** EVM-compatible by default); (iii) confusing the **orderer** with the **committer**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Case Study (KTU 2024)**

- [x] A case study is a **post-implementation analysis**, not a new design. Always use the **7-layer model** (Application, Execution, Consensus, Network, Data, Incentive, Governance).
- [x] The **three pillars** are Technical, Business, and Governance. Covering only Technical caps marks at $\sim 60\%$.
- [x] **Bitcoin's $21$ million cap** is derived from the geometric series $\sum 50 \cdot (1/2)^n \times 210000 = 21\,000\,000$ BTC. Memorize this derivation.
- [x] **Merkle proof size** for a block with $N$ transactions is $32 \cdot \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ bytes.
- [x] **Difficulty adjustment** formula: $D_{new} = D_{old} \times (T_{actual}/T_{target})$, bounded to $[D_{old}/4, 4 \cdot D_{old}]$.
- [x] **Nakamoto Coefficient** $N_c$ = minimum validators whose combined stake exceeds $50\%$.
- [x] **PBFT** tolerates $f$ Byzantine nodes out of $3f+1$ total.
- [x] **Hyperledger Fabric** uses **execute-order-validate** (not order-execute like Ethereum).
- [x] **Channels** in Fabric provide multi-lateral privacy; **Private Data Collections** go further by sharing data only with specific organizations.
- [x] **Ethereum's EVM** is **Turing-complete** (with gas limits); Bitcoin Script is **intentionally not** Turing-complete.
- [x] **Finality types:** Bitcoin = probabilistic ($\sim 60$ min); Ethereum = $\sim 12$–$15$ min; Fabric = deterministic ($\sim 2$ s).
- [x] **Energy:** Bitcoin $\sim 200$ TWh/year; Ethereum post-merge $\sim 0.01$ TWh/year.
- [x] **Bridge solvency invariant:** $L_{source} \ge \sum_i M_{i, \text{wrapped}}$. Violation = de-peg (Wormhole 2022: $\$\,320$M).
- [x] In **enterprise** case studies, emphasize **identity, permissioning, confidentiality, and regulatory compliance** — not decentralization.
- [x] In **public** case studies, emphasize **cryptoeconomics, censorship resistance, and open participation**.
- [x] Always quantify with **numbers**: TPS, block time, energy, fee, confirmation count.
- [x] A KTU 14-mark answer should have **at least one diagram** (a labeled Mermaid-style block diagram) for full marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:**
> *"Layers for structure. Pillars for depth. Numbers for credibility. Diagrams for clarity. Trade-offs for realism."*
> Memorize this $5$-point mantra — it is the difference between a $10$ and a $14$ on a KTU Part B case study question.
